entries.initial_entry:
Had a call with Sami from the Aqaba branch about the delayed container shipments. Third time this month. He kept circling around the issue without giving me a straight answer about what went wrong on their end. I told him I need the incident report by Thursday and he agreed, but I could tell he was just saying what I wanted to hear.

Spent most of the afternoon reviewing Q1 numbers with Dina. She's been solid this quarter — her team's turnaround times are the best in the division. Told her I'd put her forward for the efficiency award at the next leadership meeting. She seemed surprised, which is odd. She should know she's earned it.

Picked up the girls from piano. Layla has her recital next week and she's been anxious about it — keeps saying she'll mess up the second movement. Sat with her after dinner and ran through the piece twice. She nailed it both times. She's better than she thinks she is but she won't believe me when I tell her.
entries.nudge_text: What's actually going on in Aqaba?
entries.response_text: Honestly I think Sami's lost control of his warehouse team. Two of his senior guys left in January and he promoted people who weren't ready. But if I say that out loud it becomes my problem to fix, and I've got enough on my plate.

entries.initial_entry:
Second time opening this today. Just got off the phone with my mother. She wanted to know if Fadi is "settling in okay" which is her way of asking whether I'm watching out for him. He's fine. More than fine — he closed the Zarqa supplier deal on his own last week and didn't even tell anyone until the contract was signed. I called him into my office this morning to tell him I noticed. The look on his face. Like he didn't expect anyone in management to actually see what he's doing day to day.

That's the thing that gets me. I remember being twenty-seven at my first real job and my manager barely knew my name. I told myself if I ever ran a team I'd make sure people felt seen. Not in some corporate poster way but actually noticed. Fadi's doing good work and he should hear it from someone who matters in the building.

Noor texted me a photo of Rania's math test — ninety-eight percent. Already thinking about how to celebrate at dinner. Maybe we do kunafa from Abu Salem's.
entries.nudge_text: Why does that matter so much to you?
entries.response_text: Because nobody did it for me. I had to figure everything out alone when I started. My first manager couldn't even remember which project I was on. I just don't want that for people under me.

entries.initial_entry: Got an email from HR about the VP role. They want me to "onboard" the new guy next week. Show him around, introduce him to the team leads. My team.

People keep asking me how I feel about the VP situation like they expect me to be bitter about it. I'm not bitter. The man hasn't started yet and I'm already being asked to hold his hand through orientation. Fine. I'll do it. I'll do it well because that's what professionals do and because everyone will be watching how I handle it.

Had the onboarding meeting today. His name is Khalil. MBA from London, five years at Maersk. He's polished. Asked good questions. I answered them thoroughly and introduced him to every team lead personally. Dina told me afterward she was impressed by how generous I was with my time. That word — generous. I don't know if that's what it was. I know how things work. The person who trains the new boss controls the narrative.

Fadi called me tonight to ask about a contract clause he wasn't sure about. Spent twenty minutes walking him through it. He thanked me three times. I told him to stop thanking me and just get it right.
entries.nudge_text: Was it generous or strategic?
entries.response_text: Both. I genuinely want the transition to go well for the company. But I'm not naive — if I'm the one who made Khalil's first week smooth, that positions me a certain way. I don't see anything wrong with that.

entries.initial_entry: Friday at home. Noor's at the clinic for a half day so I took the girls to Teta's house. My mother made mansaf and spent the whole lunch telling Rania she needs to eat more.

Khalil scheduled a "division alignment" meeting for Monday without telling me first. Found out from Dina who got the calendar invite. She assumed I already knew. I didn't. It's a small thing but it's the kind of small thing that tells you how someone sees the org chart.

Had lunch with Amjad from finance. He mentioned casually that there are rumors about restructuring — possibly merging operations and logistics under Khalil. I kept my face neutral but inside something tightened. I've built this team from twelve people to forty-two. The processes, the KPI framework, the vendor relationships — those are mine.

Came home and Noor could tell something was off. She asked and I said work stuff, the usual. She didn't push. She knows when to give me room. After the girls went to bed I sat on the balcony for a while thinking about what I'd actually do if they folded my division. Started going through my LinkedIn for the first time in months. Not seriously. Just looking.
entries.nudge_text: How serious is the LinkedIn thing really?
entries.response_text: It's not serious yet. I'm not updating my profile or messaging anyone. But I want to know what's out there in case I need to move fast. I refuse to be caught off guard.

entries.initial_entry:
Fadi came to me today looking shaken. Khalil pulled him into a meeting and started questioning the Zarqa deal — asking why procurement didn't go through the new approval workflow that apparently Khalil set up without consulting me or my team. Fadi didn't know what to say. He did everything by the book, by the process that existed when he closed the deal.

I went to Khalil's office and told him directly that if he's changing procurement workflows he needs to loop in operations first because my people are the ones executing these contracts. He smiled and said he appreciated the feedback. Appreciated the feedback. Like I'm filing a suggestion at a hotel.

I'm angry but what's worse is I feel like I let Fadi down. He came to me because he trusts me and I brought him into this company. I should have anticipated that Khalil would start making changes without understanding the existing structure. I should have shielded him from this. Now Fadi's second-guessing himself over a deal he should be proud of.
entries.nudge_text: Could you have actually prevented that?
entries.response_text: Probably not. Khalil was going to assert himself no matter what. But I could have warned Fadi, prepared him for the possibility that new leadership means new rules. I just didn't think it would happen this fast.

entries.initial_entry: Layla asked me at breakfast why I've been "grumpy." I told her I wasn't grumpy, just busy. But she's right. I've been bringing it home and that's not fair to them.

The restructuring rumor turned out to be exactly that — a rumor. CEO called me in on Thursday and told me operations stays independent, reporting to him, same as before. He also said he relies on my institutional knowledge and wants me on the quarterly strategy committee starting next month. I nodded, said thank you, kept it professional. Walked out of his office and called Noor immediately.

Took the whole family to Abu Salem's on Friday night to celebrate — didn't tell the girls why, just said we deserved it. Fadi texted me that evening asking for advice on a lease negotiation for his apartment. Nothing to do with work. I spent thirty minutes on the phone with him going through the clauses. Noor rolled her eyes but she gets it. Family is family, even at nine p.m. on a Friday.

My mother-in-law rearranged Sami's entire nap schedule without asking me. She told Tariq it was better for the baby to sleep earlier so she could take him to the park in the afternoon. Nobody asked what I thought. I had finally gotten him into a rhythm that gave me two hours in the evening to work on a logo project. That's gone now.

I stood there in the kitchen holding a cup of tea and I just didn't say anything. Tariq looked at me like he expected me to agree. His mother was already moving the crib blanket to the other room. I could feel my jaw clenching but I smiled. I hate that I smiled. Something about the way everyone just decided felt like they forgot I'm his mother, not a guest in this arrangement.
entries.nudge_text: You smiled but didn't want to. What stopped you?

entries.initial_entry: Sami had a fever all night. I was up every two hours. Tariq slept through it. His mother came in at 5am and took over, told me to rest. I should be grateful but it felt like being dismissed. I'm the one who knows what dose of medicine to give, what temperature means we go to the clinic. I didn't rest.
entries.nudge_text: What would the right kind of help look like?
entries.response_text: If she had asked. Just asked. "Can I sit with him so you can sleep?" instead of walking in and lifting him out of my arms. It's not the help. It's that nobody checks if I want it first.

entries.initial_entry:
Sami's fever broke. Normal day otherwise. Did laundry, made lentil soup, swept the hallway because nobody else notices the dust. Tariq came home and asked what's for dinner. I said soup. He said his mother already made something. So the soup is in the fridge now, I guess.

Finished a draft of the logo I've been working on. The client wants revisions but at least the base concept is solid. I had to work on it in the bathroom because Sami was asleep in the bedroom and the living room was occupied. Sat on the edge of the bathtub with my laptop balanced on the hamper. It's ridiculous but it's the only place I can close a door without anyone asking what I'm doing.

Can barely keep my eyes open. Sami is teething and it's been three nights of broken sleep. I had to email the client and ask for an extension on the logo revisions. Felt pathetic typing that out. My mother called to say my cousin Rania is getting engaged and when am I bringing Sami to visit. Everything requires something from me and I have nothing left to give right now.

Eid preparations are consuming the entire house. Tariq's mother has been cooking since dawn. She made ma'moul and knafeh and keeps sending me to the store for things she forgot. I don't mind the errands but she keeps correcting how I arrange the pastries on the tray. Told me that's not how they do it in their family. It's been two years, I still arrange things wrong apparently.

Tariq gave me money to buy a new outfit for Eid. I picked something I liked -- a dark green dress with embroidery on the sleeves. When I showed it to his mother she said it was pretty but maybe something more "appropriate for a young mother" would be better. I don't even know what that means. I put the dress in my closet anyway. I'll wear it.

My own mother sent a voice note about how she wishes I could spend Eid at their house this year. First Eid is always with the husband's family, she knows that, but she said it anyway. So now I feel guilty about being here and guilty about not being there. Sami won't remember any of this. I'm doing all of it for people who have opinions about trays and dresses.
entries.nudge_text: You said you'll wear the dress anyway. What's riding on that?
entries.response_text: It's just a dress. I liked it, I bought it, I'll wear it. Not everything has to be a statement.

entries.initial_entry: Later. Wore the dress. Tariq said I looked nice. His mother didn't comment. My sister-in-law Huda actually asked where I got it. Sami grabbed the embroidery and pulled a thread loose, which honestly made me laugh. Small thing but I felt like myself for the first time in weeks. I held Sami on my hip and I felt steady.

Got the payment for the logo project. Not a lot -- 45 JD -- but I earned it. Put it in the envelope I keep in my underwear drawer. I have about 200 JD saved now. I don't know what I'm saving for exactly. Maybe a better laptop. Maybe just proof that I can do something that isn't cooking and wiping noses.

Tariq doesn't know about the envelope. I'm not hiding it because I think he'd be angry, more because he'd want to "help manage it" and then it wouldn't feel like mine anymore. My father always handled my mother's money. She'd get her teacher's salary and hand it over and he'd decide what the household needed. She never complained about it. I think about that a lot. I don't want to not complain about it. I want a reason not to need to.
entries.nudge_text: What changes if Tariq finds that envelope?
entries.response_text: He'd probably be fine with it. Supportive even. But then it becomes a shared thing, a family decision. Right now it's the only thing in this house that belongs to just me. I'm not ready to give that up. Maybe that's selfish. I don't care.

entries.initial_entry:
Took Sami to the park near the mosque this morning. He's getting better at walking on his own, barely needs my hand now. He kept crouching down to look at ants. We stayed for almost two hours. I didn't bring my phone on purpose.

Ran into Um Fadi from the building. She asked if I was working these days, meaning a real job. I said no, just home with Sami. She nodded the way people nod when they think they're being polite but their face says everything. I used to let that bother me. Today I just picked Sami up and pointed at a bird and we moved on.

Tariq's mother made mansaf for dinner and I helped her roll the jameed. She showed me her mother's technique with the wooden spoon and for once it wasn't corrective, it was like she was sharing something. I actually paid attention. Her hands move fast for someone her age. The rice came out perfectly and she said "you're learning" and I think she meant it warmly. These moments confuse me because they make me forget why I'm frustrated. Or maybe they're exactly why I stay patient through the rest of it.

Friday. Tariq took the day off and we went to his parents' village with Sami to visit his grandfather. The old house has a courtyard with a lemon tree that Tariq's grandmother planted. His grandfather told Sami the same story he tells everyone about building the house stone by stone. Sami just chewed on a lemon leaf.

I sat with the women while the men did their thing. Tariq's aunt made sage tea the old way, on a brass tray over coals. It was good. Quiet. I sketched some patterns on a napkin -- the tile work in the courtyard is beautiful, these geometric interlocking shapes I've been wanting to use in a design project. Nobody noticed me drawing, which was the whole point.

New client reached out through Instagram. Wants a logo for a women's fitness studio in West Amman. Paying 80 JD. I said yes before thinking about when I'd actually do the work. Sami's not sleeping well again and the house is never empty. But 80 JD is 80 JD. I'll figure out the hours somehow. I always do.
entries.nudge_text: You keep saying you'll figure it out. Figure out what exactly?
entries.response_text: How to be two people at once. The one who takes care of Sami and the house and keeps everyone comfortable. And the one who wants to make things and get paid for it and have something that's mine. There's no schedule that fits both. I just keep squeezing one into the gaps of the other.

entries.initial_entry:
Tariq and I had an actual conversation last night. Not a fight. He asked why I've been staying up so late and I almost lied but I was too tired to come up with something so I told him about the freelance work. All of it. The clients, the envelope, the bathroom laptop sessions.

He was quiet for a long time. Then he said he wished I'd told him sooner. Not because he wanted to control it but because he felt stupid not knowing. That stung. I told him it wasn't about trust, it was about needing something that was just mine. He said he understood that but it still felt like I didn't trust him. I don't know if he's wrong.

We didn't resolve anything exactly. He said he'd talk to his mother about giving me more space during the day so I don't have to hide. I told him I don't want him to fight that battle for me. He looked confused. I think he genuinely doesn't understand why I'd rather struggle alone than have him fix it. Honestly, I'm not totally sure I understand it either. But the thought of his mother knowing about the work and having opinions about it makes my chest tight. Some things I need to handle my own way, even if my way is harder.
entries.nudge_text: He offered help and you said no. What are you protecting?
entries.response_text: Can we not do this right now. I already spent all night turning this over in my head. I know it doesn't make total sense. I just need to sit with it for a bit.

entries.initial_entry: Same day, later. Tariq's mother asked me at dinner if everything was okay between us. She must have heard us talking last night. I said everything was fine. Tariq backed me up. She didn't push it. But the way she looked at me -- not unkind, just knowing -- made me feel exposed. I keep building walls in a house made of thin ones.

Today was my parents' 40th wedding anniversary, and I spent most of the afternoon at their villa helping coordinate everything. My mother had very specific ideas about the flower arrangements and the seating chart — cousin Layla absolutely cannot be seated near aunt Fatima after what happened at Eid — and I found myself nodding along, rearranging the entire dining room twice to meet her vision.

The funny thing is, I didn't mind. There's something calming about fitting into the role everyone expects. My father pulled me aside after the guests arrived and thanked me for "being the reliable one," and I felt this warmth in my chest. My younger brother Samir flew in from London but spent most of the party on his phone, and I could see the disappointment in my mother's eyes. I don't want to be the source of that look.

But there was this moment, late in the evening, when the older relatives were doing their formal toasts, and I looked across the room at my wife Nadia. She caught my eye and did this little mock-yawn behind her napkin, and I had to bite my cheek not to laugh. We'd both rather have been at that new seafood place on the Palm with a bottle of wine, just the two of us. Instead, I sat through two hours of my uncle's stories about the old days.

I think I'm okay with it. The party was beautiful. Everyone was happy. And Nadia and I are going to that restaurant next weekend anyway — I already made the reservation. You can do both, I think. Be the person your family needs and still carve out space for the things that make life sweet.

I'm so frustrated I could scream. We had our quarterly review today and Hamza — my direct report who I've mentored for two years — threw me under the bus in front of the regional director. He presented his supply chain optimization idea as if it was entirely his own, when we literally developed it together over coffee last month. I was the one who told him to run the numbers on the Oman route!

The worst part is I just sat there. Smiled. Nodded along. When Ahmed, my boss, looked at me after the presentation, I said something supportive about Hamza's initiative. What else was I supposed to do? Create a scene? Interrupt him mid-presentation to claim credit? That's not how things are done. You don't embarrass a colleague publicly, especially one you outrank. It would look petty. Undignified.

But God, I wanted to. I wanted to say, "Actually, that was our idea, developed during our Tuesday coffees." Instead I shook his hand after and told him he did well. He had the grace to look slightly uncomfortable, at least.

Nadia says I should have spoken up privately to Ahmed afterward. She's probably right. But I keep thinking about how my father handled workplace conflicts — with patience, with letting things play out, with trusting that people eventually see the truth. Maybe Hamza will acknowledge it later. Maybe Ahmed already knows whose brain that idea came from.

Or maybe I'm just telling myself stories to feel better about being a coward. I don't know. I do know that I came home and ate an entire box of those pistachio sweets from that place in Sharjah, and I don't regret it one bit. Sometimes you just need something good after a bad day.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned Nadia's suggestion and your father's approach as two different paths. Which one feels more true to who you want to be?
entries.response_text: Honestly? I think I'm overthinking this. It's one meeting. Hamza is young, he got excited about presenting something that worked. The idea will get implemented either way, and that's what matters for the team. I'd rather focus on the fact that we're on track for a really good Q4, and I'm taking Nadia to Bahrain for New Year's. Some things just aren't worth the energy of picking apart.

entries.initial_entry:
Can't sleep. It's 2am and I'm sitting on the balcony with my laptop because Nadia's breathing is usually soothing but tonight it's just reminding me that everyone else can rest except me.

My mother called today and mentioned — casually, as if she was discussing the weather — that cousin Layla's husband just got made partner at his firm and isn't that wonderful, they're buying a bigger house in Abu Dhabi. Then she asked when Nadia and I are going to "settle down properly" and have children. I'm 41. We've been married six years. She knows we've been trying. She knows about the appointments with Dr. Khalil. But she still asks, every single time, like maybe if she reminds me enough, my body will cooperate.

I didn't say anything. I made the appropriate congratulatory noises about Layla's husband. I changed the subject to the marina and the boat engine I'm having fixed.

What I wanted to say was: we might not be able to have children, and it's breaking Nadia apart, and I feel like a failure every time she cries in the bathroom thinking I can't hear her. But you don't burden your mother with things like that. You don't make her worry. You don't disrupt the pleasant fiction that everything is fine and her eldest son has everything figured out.

The thing is, I don't even know if I want children anymore. Is that terrible? I used to. I had the whole picture in my head — teaching my son to fish, taking my daughter to her first day of school. But lately I've been thinking about what our life looks like without them. The freedom. The travel. The late Sunday mornings. Is that selfish? It feels selfish. But it also feels honest.

I should go back to bed. Nadia's got that look lately when I stay up too late, like she's worried about me. I don't want to give her another thing to worry about.
entries.nudge_text: You described the picture you used to have and the one you're starting to imagine now. What would it take to talk about this with Nadia?
entries.response_text: That's the thing — I'm not sure I can. We've never actually said out loud that it might not happen. We talk around it. We talk about appointments, and procedures, and "next steps." But we don't talk about what it means if there are no next steps. I think I'm afraid that if I tell her I'm starting to be okay with the idea of just us, she'll think I've given up on her. On us. On the future she wanted. And I haven't given up. I'm just... adapting? Protecting myself maybe? I don't know. I think I need to figure out what I actually feel before I bring it to her. Right now it's all just noise in my head at 2am.

entries.initial_entry: Bahrain trip was good. Nadia and I finally talked about the fertility stuff. She cried but also seemed sort of relieved, I think? We're going to take a break from the treatments for a few months.
entries.nudge_text: That sounds like a meaningful conversation to have had together. How are you feeling now that it's out in the open?
entries.response_text: You know, mostly I'm just glad to be back at work tomorrow. The holiday period always feels long. We have the Q1 planning meeting on Thursday and I want to make sure the Oman route data is properly credited this time. Fresh start to the year.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the entire afternoon at the community center helping Fatima with her citizenship application paperwork. Her English is getting so much better but the legal language still trips her up, and honestly it trips me up too. We went through every section three times to make sure nothing would get flagged. By the end my eyes were burning from staring at that tiny print.

But there was this moment when we finished and she grabbed my hands and just held them for a second without saying anything. Eight months ago she arrived here with two suitcases and three kids and now she's applying to become a citizen. I drove home feeling like maybe I actually did something that mattered today. The dishes were still in the sink and Mia needed help with her history project but for once I didn't feel bad about where my time went.
entries.nudge_text: What usually makes a day feel like it didn't matter?
entries.response_text: The days blur together sometimes. Errands, laundry, driving kids places. I know it matters, I know keeping a household running matters. But when I lie down at night after a day like that, I can't point to anything and say this moved the needle. This made something better. It's hard to explain without sounding ungrateful for the life I have.

entries.initial_entry:
Conference at Mia's school tonight about the AP Environmental Science curriculum and I'm still replaying this one exchange in my head. Another parent, Deborah something, stood up and said the class was spending too much time on "doom and gloom climate stuff" and that her son was coming home anxious. And I wanted to say something, I had the words right there, but I just sat there.

The teacher looked uncomfortable and sort of backpedaled about how they try to balance the science with "solutions" and "hope." And I thought about Mia last week telling me she doesn't want to have kids because what's the point. And I thought about all the reports I've read. And I said nothing. Just sat there nodding like everyone else while Deborah congratulated herself for protecting her son from being sad about the world actually ending.

I should have spoken up. I teach my kids to speak up. But I was worried about making things awkward, worried about being that parent, worried about the car ride home with Mia being embarrassed. Now I'm sitting here at midnight wishing I could do it over.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you in that moment?
entries.response_text: I think I was scared of my own anger. If I started talking I wasn't sure I could keep it civil. And there's this voice in my head that says people like Deborah can't be convinced anyway so why bother, why make yourself a target. But that's just an excuse isn't it. The teacher needed backup and I let her down.

entries.initial_entry:
Recycling day. Pulled out three bags of perfectly recyclable materials that neighbors just threw in with their regular trash. Aluminum cans, glass jars, stuff that takes thirty seconds to rinse out. I sorted through it all anyway because what else am I going to do, let it go to the landfill?

Tom thinks I take this too seriously. He said as much when I came back inside smelling like someone's old salsa jar. He's not wrong that I can't fix everyone else's habits but he's also not standing in the garage at 7am picking yogurt containers out of a trash bag. Sometimes I wonder if I'm the crazy one, if normal people just throw things away and sleep fine at night and don't think about where it all ends up.

The kids have learned to separate everything correctly, at least. That's something. But then I think about the scale of it, the millions of tons, the plastic islands, and my little neighborhood project feels like putting a band-aid on a hemorrhage. And I keep doing it anyway because what's the alternative.
entries.nudge_text: You said 'what's the alternative.' Do you actually have an answer to that?
entries.response_text: Give up and stop caring, I guess. Which isn't really an alternative at all.

entries.initial_entry:
Daniel's soccer game this morning, then dropped Mia at her study group, then grocery run, then made dinner for a family from the resettlement center whose mom just had surgery. Pretty standard Saturday honestly. I'm beat.

Noticed while shopping that they stopped carrying the fair trade coffee I usually get. Asked the manager and he said it wasn't selling enough. Ended up buying the regular kind because I needed coffee and didn't have time to go somewhere else. Small thing but it's been nagging at me all evening.
Mia asked if she could join me at the refugee center next week. Said she wanted to help with the kids' tutoring program. Didn't push her for a reason, just said yes.

My sister called to catch up and somehow we ended up in this whole thing about how I spend my time. She asked when I was going to "go back to real work" since the kids are older now. As if what I do isn't work. As if coordinating thirty households' recycling and showing up for families who have no one else isn't real.

I didn't say any of that obviously. Just said I'm happy with how things are, which is mostly true. But later Tom asked what was wrong and I snapped at him for no reason. I know she doesn't mean it as a criticism, she just doesn't get it. Not everyone measures their life by a paycheck. I'm doing what feels right even if it looks like nothing on a resume.
entries.nudge_text: What part of her question stung the most?
entries.response_text: That she might be right. That maybe I'm hiding behind volunteer work because I'm scared I couldn't cut it in a real job anymore. That eight years out of the workforce, who would even hire me now. Easier to tell myself I'm making a difference here than to test whether I still could somewhere else.

entries.initial_entry:
Back from the Mekong research trip with my students. Three weeks of river sampling, village visits, and improvised lessons under thatched roofs. My body is wrecked—mosquito bites on my ankles, a persistent cough from the humidity, and I think I pulled something in my shoulder carrying equipment. But here's the thing: I feel more awake than I have in months.

The kids were incredible. There was this moment when we were testing water quality near a fishing village, and one student started asking the local fishermen about changes they'd noticed over the years. No prompting from me. She just did it. That kind of spontaneous curiosity—you can't manufacture that in a classroom with fluorescent lights and standardized tests.

Now I'm back at my desk, and already I can feel the walls closing in. Tomorrow's faculty meeting will be about exam preparation timelines and classroom decoration guidelines. I know I should be grateful for the stability of this position, but part of me is already googling "environmental education programs hiring abroad."

I need to sleep for about a week. But I also know that if I stay still too long, this feeling of being alive will fade, and I'll be left with the same restlessness I've been running from since I was thirty.
entries.nudge_text: What happened at thirty?

entries.initial_entry: Faculty meeting was worse than expected. Three hours on compliance paperwork. Considered walking out twice.
entries.nudge_text: What almost made you walk?

entries.initial_entry: Got coffee with an old colleague who left teaching to do documentary work—she's filming a piece on coral restoration in Indonesia, asked if I'd be interested in consulting. Said I'd think about it but we both knew that meant yes.
entries.nudge_text: So why say you'd think about it?
entries.response_text: Habit, I guess. I've learned not to show my cards too quickly. People get concerned when you seem too eager to drop everything. My mother used to call it "chasing the wind"—not a compliment. So I've gotten good at pretending to deliberate while my mind is already packing bags.

entries.initial_entry:
The coral project is moving forward. My colleague Mei-Hua sent over preliminary details: two months in Sulawesi, starting in March, helping design educational components for local schools. The pay is modest but includes accommodation and meals. My current contract here allows for unpaid leave if approved by the department head.

I spent most of Sunday creating a detailed proposal for why this should be considered professional development rather than abandonment. Listed educational outcomes, skill acquisition, potential for future curriculum integration. The proposal is twelve pages long. I know Principal Huang will see through it—he's commented before that I seem to have "one foot out the door"—but I need the formality to protect my position in case I want to come back.

The strangest part: I'm not even sure I do want to come back. But I keep building these safety nets anyway. Maybe it's the years of my father telling me that a respectable career means staying in one place, building a reputation, becoming someone people can count on. Every time I leave, I hear his voice asking when I'll finally settle down.

Submitted the proposal this afternoon. Now I wait.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take to not build the net?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Maybe if I had enough money that coming back wasn't a financial necessity. Or maybe if I stopped caring about what my family thinks. But I'm 48 years old and I still want my father's approval, even though he's been dead for six years. That's probably the most honest thing I've written in months.

entries.initial_entry:
Principal Huang approved the leave request—but with conditions. I have to document everything, submit weekly reports on "educational outcomes observed," and return with a "concrete implementation plan for classroom integration." Basically turning an expedition into a bureaucratic exercise.

And yet I said yes immediately because two months in Indonesia is two months not here, not in this building with its humming fluorescent lights and scheduled bathroom breaks for students and the same conversations about test scores over and over—

My colleague Li-Na said she envied me at lunch today. Said she wished she could just pick up and go like I do. I wanted to tell her it's not freedom, it's compulsion. I don't choose to leave; I have to leave, or something in me starts to suffocate. But that sounds dramatic and she has two kids in elementary school and a mother-in-law with dementia, so I just smiled and said "you should try it sometime" which felt like a lie and a truth at the same time.

The approval notice is pinned to my corkboard. March feels like forever away. I'm already looking at flights, making lists, thinking about what equipment I'll need. The anticipation is almost as good as the going. Almost.
entries.nudge_text: You said compulsion, not choice. What's the difference for you?
entries.response_text: Choice implies I could stay if I wanted to. That staying is a real option I'm evaluating. But when I'm honest with myself, staying was never on the table. The only question was whether I'd leave with permission or without it. Compulsion is when the leaving isn't optional—it's just a matter of how much I'll sacrifice to do it.

entries.initial_entry: My sister called to say I'm being selfish again. That our mother is getting older and needs family nearby. That I "can't keep running away from real life."
entries.nudge_text: What did you say back?
entries.response_text: I hung up. But what I wanted to say was: this IS my real life. The running isn't away from something—it's toward something. The problem is I've never been able to explain what that something is, not even to myself. All I know is that when I'm moving, when I'm somewhere new, when I don't know what tomorrow looks like—that's when I feel like I exist. Staying in one place feels like slowly disappearing. But try explaining that to someone whose definition of a good life is stability and proximity to family.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the morning at the travel clinic getting vaccinations for Indonesia. Typhoid, hepatitis boosters, antimalarials. The nurse asked if this was my first time traveling to the region and I laughed—it was rude but I couldn't help it. She looked at my vaccine record and said "oh, you're one of those."

One of those. I know what she meant. The perpetual travelers, the people who seem allergic to staying put. She probably sees us all the time in a travel clinic. I wanted to ask her what else she's noticed about "people like me" but that seemed like a conversation neither of us wanted to have.

Afterward I walked through the night market near my apartment, the one I've been to a hundred times. Normally it bores me—same vendors, same food, same crowds. But today I looked at it differently, knowing I'll be gone in six weeks. The elderly woman selling scallion pancakes. The teenagers playing games on their phones while manning their parents' stalls. The specific quality of the light through the plastic tarps.

I do this every time I'm about to leave: suddenly appreciate what I've been ignoring. Then I leave anyway.

Mei-Hua called with updates on the Indonesia project. The funding came through, the local partners are confirmed, and there's even been interest from a university in Singapore about documenting our work for a larger research initiative. This is everything I wanted.

So why do I feel this undertow of anxiety?

I've been sitting with this feeling all day, trying to name it. It's not fear of the unknown—the unknown is the part I crave. It's something else. Maybe it's that I've built my entire identity around chasing the next horizon, and I'm starting to wonder what happens when I stop. Not if, but when. Because at some point, the body slows down. The opportunities shrink. The world gets smaller whether you want it to or not.

My mother is 76 this year. My sister was right about one thing: she won't be here forever. And I've spent most of my adult life being somewhere else, chasing experiences I can't even fully remember now. I have photo albums from twenty countries, but I couldn't tell you the name of my neighbors.

I don't know if this is wisdom finally arriving or just fatigue dressed up as self-awareness. But for the first time in a long time, I'm not sure the leaving will fix what the staying breaks.

I'll go to Indonesia anyway. Of course I will. But I'm writing this down so I remember: there was a moment, in January 2025, when I wondered if there might be another way to feel alive.
entries.nudge_text: What would that other way look like?
entries.response_text: I genuinely don't know. That's the terrifying part. My whole life has been organized around movement—every decision, every relationship, every job has been filtered through "will this let me keep moving?" I've never seriously tried to find out what the alternative would feel like because it seemed like death. But maybe it's not death. Maybe it's a different kind of life that I've never given a fair chance. I just don't know how to start when standing still has always felt like drowning.

entries.initial_entry:
Closed the Morrison deal today and the whole thing went exactly how I wanted it to. No compromises on timeline, no scope creep clauses they tried to sneak in, kept the IP ownership clean. Their legal team pushed back twice and I just waited. Didn't budge. They came around because they knew I'd walk and they needed me more than I needed them.

It's weird though, sitting here with the signed contract and a good whiskey, and I'm already thinking about what's next. Not in a restless way—more like I'm finally at cruising altitude and can look around. Eight years of building this thing on my own terms. Nobody telling me which clients to take or how many hours to bill. Worth every sleepless night in the early years.

Had dinner with my sister and her husband. Derek spent half the meal explaining how I should restructure the business, hire a COO, "scale properly." Like he's some genius because he climbed the ladder at a healthcare company and has a pension waiting for him. He doesn't get it. He'll never get it.

What really got under my skin was when Rachel nodded along with everything he said. She used to understand why I left corporate. Now she just sees the gray in my hair and the fact that I'm still single and makes assumptions. I'm not broken. I'm not running from anything. I just don't want someone else's version of success stapled to my forehead.
entries.nudge_text: What would your version of success actually look like right now?
entries.response_text: I mean, I already have it mostly. The Morrison deal, the freedom. Just gets old having to defend it every family dinner. Whatever.

entries.initial_entry:
Finally told Gary I won't be renewing the Nextera contract. Three years of dealing with their approval committees and change request bureaucracy—I'm done. The money was steady, but every time I had to sit through one of their "alignment meetings" I could feel my brain cells dying.

He didn't take it well. Called it impulsive. Said I was "burning bridges" and "thinking with my ego." Maybe. But I walked out of that call and felt like I could breathe for the first time in months. That has to mean something. I started this company so I'd never have to sit in another meeting where ten people discuss whether we need a meeting. Not going back to that. Not for any client.

Met with two potential clients this week. Turned down the first one—fintech startup with a founder who kept interrupting me to explain my own expertise back to me. Not worth the aggravation.

Second meeting went well. Small biotech company, three founders who actually listen. They want custom lab management software and gave me full creative control over the architecture. Already sketching out the data models in my head. This is the kind of work I got into business for. Clean problem, smart people, no corporate layers. Said yes on the spot.
entries.nudge_text: What made saying yes on the spot feel right?
entries.response_text: They treated me like an expert, not a vendor. Asked my opinion and actually wrote it down. No committee approvals, no "we'll circle back." Just three people who knew what they needed and trusted me to build it. That's rare.

entries.initial_entry: Spent most of the day reading about database architecture I could've just asked someone about. Old habit—figuring things out myself even when it's slower. Not sure if that's stubbornness or something else.
entries.nudge_text: What's the "something else" you're hinting at?
entries.response_text: Pride, probably. The satisfaction of solving it on my own. When I ask someone else, even if it's faster, it feels borrowed. Like I didn't really earn the solution.

entries.initial_entry:
Made solid progress on the biotech project. Finished the database schema and got their sign-off on the first prototype wireframes. They're easy to work with—send questions when they have them, otherwise leave me alone to build.

This is exactly the rhythm I've been craving. Wake up, coffee, work on something that actually matters, nobody pinging me about status updates or asking for a "quick sync." I control the timeline. I decide when to push hard and when to step back.

Took a long walk this afternoon just to clear my head and found myself actually smiling. Not at anything specific—just at the fact that this is my life, that I built it, that nobody handed it to me or approved it for me. I know that sounds simple but sometimes you need to step back and notice it. Eight years in and I'd still rather do this with less money than go back to a cubicle with more.

Ordered Thai food and worked until midnight because I wanted to, not because anyone was expecting it. That distinction matters.

Haven't slept more than four hours in three days. Biotech project hit a wall—their legacy system is a mess and integrating with it is turning into a nightmare. I said I'd have a working prototype by January 5th and now I'm not sure that's realistic.

Part of me wants to call them and reset expectations. That would be the smart thing. But another part of me—the part that's been driving this whole operation—hates the idea of admitting I underestimated the scope. I quoted the timeline. I own it. Even if it means working through the holidays.

Caught myself snapping at the barista this morning over nothing. That's not who I want to be. But I also can't shake this feeling that if I ask for more time, or ask for help, or admit I can't do it alone... I don't know. Something shifts. The whole story I tell myself about being the guy who figures things out—maybe that story has a cost I don't usually see.

Rachel called to ask if I'm coming for Christmas dinner. Told her I'd try. We both knew I was lying. I could hear the disappointment but also something else. Resignation, maybe. Like she's stopped expecting anything different.
entries.nudge_text: What would it cost you to ask for help right now?

entries.initial_entry:
Back from the chaos. Prototype works—not perfect, but works. Sent it over this morning and they loved it. Called it "exactly what they envisioned" which, honestly, made the last two weeks worth it.

But I've been thinking a lot about what happened in the middle there. The exhaustion, the snapping at people, missing Christmas. I pulled it off, sure, but I pulled it off in a way that felt familiar in a bad way. Like I was back in my twenties, proving something to nobody in particular.

I called Rachel yesterday. Apologized for Christmas. She said she gets it, which is worse than if she'd yelled at me. Told her I'm going to try to make January different. Not sure what that means yet. Maybe it means being okay with asking for help sometimes. Maybe it means picking projects where the timeline has some breathing room. Maybe it just means sleeping before I become someone I don't recognize.

The thing is, I know why I do it this way. When I solve the hard problem myself, when I stay up three nights to hit an impossible deadline, there's this feeling—like I'm proving I can still do it. That nobody else is required. That the kid who walked out of that corporate meeting made the right call. But maybe proving it over and over is its own kind of trap. Maybe at 41 I could occasionally let myself be helped without it meaning I failed.

Anyway. Project's done. Taking the rest of the week off. Might actually answer the phone when Rachel calls.
entries.nudge_text: What would "being helped" look like if it didn't mean failure?
entries.response_text: I think it would look like having someone else in the room. Not an employee, not a contractor—someone who actually shares the weight. A partner, maybe. I've always said I don't want a co-founder because I don't want to compromise, but maybe what I'm really afraid of is depending on someone. Trusting someone enough to let them in. The business is the easy part. The loneliness is harder to admit.

entries.initial_entry:
Stayed late at the lab again. Got home after the kids were asleep. Dina didn't say anything but I could tell from the way she left my dinner on the counter instead of the table. That's her signal.

The thing is I know I should've been home earlier. Layla had her first swimming lesson and I told her this morning I'd try to make it. "Try" is doing a lot of work in that sentence. I didn't try. I got pulled into running another simulation batch and the hours just disappeared. And now my kid learned to float without me there and I can't get that back. I keep telling myself the PhD is for them, for our future, but some of these moments don't wait for dissertations to be finished.
entries.nudge_text: Which moments are you most afraid of missing?
entries.response_text: Honestly all of them. But the ones where they stop asking me to come. That's what scares me.

entries.initial_entry:
Monday again. Advisor meeting at 10, went fine I think, he wants the literature review chapter revised by Friday which is tight but manageable. Spent the afternoon in the lab calibrating the shake table sensors. Boring but necessary work, nobody else was going to do it.

Dina took Omar to the pediatrician because he's had this cough for two weeks. She texted me the update: nothing serious, probably allergies, they gave him a prescription. I should've gone with her but Mondays are impossible with the advisor meeting and the TA prep for Tuesday's section. She handled it fine. She always handles it fine. That's partly the problem I guess, she's so capable that I lean on her for everything outside the lab and at some point that stops being partnership and starts being something else.

Called my mother in the evening. She asked when I'm defending. When I said probably next spring she went quiet then started talking about my cousin Faisal who just got promoted to department head at the ministry back home. He's two years younger than me. She didn't say it outright but the comparison was loud. Told her I had to go because Omar needed a bath, which was true but also convenient.
entries.nudge_text: What does it start being?
entries.response_text: Just imbalance I think. Not worth spiraling about right now.

entries.initial_entry:
Second time writing today which is unusual. Just got back from putting the kids to bed. Layla wanted me to read to her and I did three chapters of that Junie B. Jones book she's obsessed with. Omar fell asleep on my lap halfway through. Small thing but I needed it.

Dina and I actually talked after. Not about logistics for once. She told me about this cooking class she wants to take on Saturdays, Palestinian dishes from her grandmother's recipes. Her eyes lit up describing it. I told her she absolutely should and I'd handle the kids Saturday mornings. She looked surprised, like she wasn't expecting me to just say yes. That bothered me a little because it means she's gotten used to me being unavailable.

But I said yes and I meant it. Saturday mornings are mine with the kids and hers for the class. This is something I can actually hold onto. The dissertation won't collapse because I take three hours on a Saturday. And Dina deserves to have something that's just for her, same way the PhD is my thing. Though calling the PhD "my thing" feels strange when it's supposed to be for all of us. Whatever, the point is tonight felt right. I was present and it didn't cost me anything I'll regret.

Pulled two all-nighters this week to get the chapter revision done. Submitted it at 4am. Body is wrecked. Dina asked if I was okay and I snapped at her, said I was fine when I obviously wasn't. Feel awful about that. She doesn't deserve my stress dumped on her.
entries.nudge_text: What were you actually feeling when you snapped?
entries.response_text: Guilty. Because she was being caring and I resented it somehow. Like her concern reminded me I'd been neglecting everything that wasn't the dissertation.

entries.initial_entry:
Apologized to Dina this morning properly. Not just a mumbled sorry but sat down with her during the kids' cartoons and told her I knew I was being unfair. She said she knows the pressure I'm under but she needs me to not take it out on her. Fair. Completely fair.

Made pancakes for everyone after. Layla put chocolate chips in hers in the shape of a smiley face. Omar ate his with his hands and got syrup everywhere. Normal family morning and I was actually there for it instead of hunched over my laptop. Advisor can wait until tomorrow.

New month. Advisor approved the revised chapter with minor edits. Felt good to get that confirmation after the crunch. Started outlining the methodology chapter, should be more straightforward since it's describing work I've already done.

Dina started her Saturday cooking class last week. She came home with maftoul and it was better than my mother's, which I will never say to my mother. The kids and I went to the park while she was gone. Taught Layla to pump on the swing by herself. Omar mostly ate sand. Standard Saturday. This arrangement is working and I want to protect it.

Lab mate Chen got accepted to present at the ASCE conference in November. Good for him. His advisor fast-tracked the submission. Mine hasn't even looked at my abstract draft from three weeks ago. I know I shouldn't compare timelines. Chen doesn't have kids, doesn't have a family depending on him, works weekends without guilt. Different situation entirely. But sitting in the lab watching him celebrate while I'm still grinding through Chapter 3 revisions, I couldn't help doing the math on how far behind I am.

Dina asked how my day was and I said fine. Didn't mention Chen. Didn't want to sound petty about it.
entries.nudge_text: What does 'behind' mean to you here?
entries.response_text: Just timeline stuff. Conference papers, publications. The CV metrics that actually matter for the job market.

entries.initial_entry: Layla asked me tonight if I'm going to be a student forever. She wasn't being mean, just genuinely confused. Told her almost done. But the question rattled around in my head after. Am I taking too long? Am I dragging this out because finishing means facing what comes next?
entries.nudge_text: What comes next that worries you?

entries.initial_entry:
My mother called and dropped the news that my father's been having chest pains. He went to the doctor last week apparently and nobody told me until now. When I asked why, she said they didn't want to "worry me during my studies." I'm 38 years old and they're still treating me like I'm a kid who can't handle bad news.

I'm angry but I also know exactly why they did it. Because every time I talk to them I'm stressed about the PhD, about money, about the kids. So they stopped telling me things. They shielded me and now I feel like I failed them by being so consumed with my own problems that they couldn't even lean on me. Dina found me sitting in the kitchen at midnight staring at my phone. She just sat with me and didn't say anything for a while. Then she said we could look into flights for winter break if I wanted to visit. She's already thinking about logistics while I'm stuck in my own head.

I told my advisor I might need to go to Amman for a few weeks. He said it's fine as long as I keep writing. Of course that's what he said. Everyone needs something from me and I can't figure out how to be enough for all of them at once.

Spent the morning reviewing proposals from two potential investors. One offers more capital but wants a board seat and quarterly reviews. The other is smaller but completely hands-off. Everyone keeps telling me the bigger check is the obvious choice. But I keep thinking about three years ago when I walked away from a salary that would make most people's eyes water, all because I couldn't stand someone else dictating my calendar.

Maybe I'm being naive. Maybe smart founders take the bigger check and learn to play the game. But every time I imagine sitting in those quarterly reviews, justifying my decisions to people who've never built anything themselves, something in my chest tightens.
entries.nudge_text: What does that tightening in your chest usually signal for you?
entries.response_text: It's the same feeling I had in my last corporate job. Before I quit. Like I'm slowly being boxed in. When I was younger I thought I'd outgrow it, learn to compromise. But if anything it's gotten stronger. I think that feeling is my compass now—when it shows up, I know I'm about to betray something important about how I want to live.

entries.initial_entry: Took the smaller investment. Signed papers yesterday. Celebrated with Layla at that new Persian restaurant near the Marina. Felt right.

My mother called from Tehran today. She wanted to know when I'm getting married, when I'm having children, when I'm going to "settle down and stop playing." I'm almost forty. I've heard these questions my whole adult life. Usually I brush them off. Today it got under my skin more than usual.

I think it's because part of me wonders if she has a point. Not about marriage specifically—I'll figure that out when I figure it out. But about what I'm building toward. I work hard, yes, but I also make sure to enjoy the fruits of that work. Weekends in Oman. Good wine. A nice apartment. She sees all of that as frivolous, as avoiding responsibility. In her world, you sacrifice everything for family, for the future, for some distant reward.

But I've watched people sacrifice their entire lives for that distant reward and then die before they get there. Or they get there and realize they forgot how to enjoy anything. I don't want that. I refuse that. And yet—today, after we hung up, I sat on my balcony for an hour just staring at the water, wondering if my version of a good life will leave me alone at sixty with nothing but nice memories.
entries.nudge_text: It sounds like you're weighing two different visions of what a meaningful life looks like. Which one feels more true to who you are right now?
entries.response_text: The one where I'm present. Where I'm actually living my days instead of deferring them. I've tried the other way—grinding through miserable weeks for some hypothetical payoff. It made me sick, literally. Migraines, insomnia. My body rejected it. So I know what's true for me. I just wish knowing made it easier to hear my mother's disappointment.

entries.initial_entry:
Layla and I got into it last night. She thinks I work too much. I think that's rich coming from someone who doesn't understand what it takes to run your own thing. When you're employed, you clock out and your problems stay at the office. When it's YOUR company, it follows you everywhere.

But then—I don't know. She made this comment about how I used to be more fun. More spontaneous. And it stung because she's not wrong. The last few months I've been grinding harder than usual, trying to prove to myself that taking the smaller check was the right call. Trying to show I can grow this thing without giving up control.

I hate that I'm becoming the person I swore I wouldn't become. The one who's always "busy."
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned you 'hate becoming the person you swore you wouldn't become.' What would that person look like if you fully became them?
entries.response_text: I don't want to think about that right now. I just needed to get this out. I know what I need to do—I need to take her somewhere nice for the holidays and actually be present. That's the plan.

entries.initial_entry:
We went to Muscat for three days. Left my laptop at home—Layla's one condition. First day I was restless, kept reaching for my phone. By the third day I had forgotten what I was even anxious about.

We hiked to this natural pool in the mountains. Cold water, complete silence except for birds. I floated on my back and watched clouds move and thought: this is why I built my own thing. Not for the money, not for the status. For moments like this, whenever I want them. The ability to disappear for three days without asking permission.

I need to protect this. The business should serve the life, not the other way around.
Back from holiday. January pipeline already overwhelming. Three new client requests, two urgent. I said yes to all of them because I'm still in growth mode. Already regretting it.
entries.nudge_text: When you say you're already regretting it, what specifically is showing up for you?
entries.response_text: The familiar dread. Knowing the next two months will be meetings and deadlines and no room to breathe. Knowing I just had a beautiful reset and I'm already overcommitting. It's a pattern. I recognize it. I just don't know how to break it when saying no feels like leaving money on the table.

entries.initial_entry:
My old colleague Faisal reached out. He's now a VP at a big consulting firm, wanted to know if I'd consider coming back to corporate. "Senior role, name your salary, you'd have a team of twenty."

I said no, obviously. But then he made this comment—"You're still doing the solo thing? I thought that was a phase." And I felt this flash of anger. Like I needed to justify myself. Started listing my clients, my revenue, my growth numbers. Trying to prove I wasn't some hobbyist.

Later I wondered why I cared so much. Why his opinion mattered. I left that world for a reason. I don't owe anyone an explanation.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think Faisal's comment touched on that made you want to prove yourself?
entries.response_text: Maybe the fear that I'm fooling myself. That everyone else sees something I don't—that I gave up a real career to play entrepreneur, and one day it'll all fall apart and I'll have nothing to show for it. I know that's not rational. My business is healthy. But there's this voice that sounds a lot like my father, saying I'm not serious enough. That real men build empires, not lifestyles. I thought I'd silenced that voice. Apparently not.

entries.initial_entry:
Funny thing happened today. One of my clients—a resort chain—asked if I'd relocate to Bahrain for six months to oversee an opening. Great money, interesting project. My immediate reaction was no. Not because of the work, but because I imagined six months without my Thursday dinners at that Lebanese place, without my morning runs along the creek, without the routines that make my life here feel like mine.

Then I caught myself and thought—wait, am I becoming rigid? I built this life precisely so I could be flexible. So I could say yes to adventures. But now I'm protecting routines like they're sacred.

Or maybe routines are sacred. Maybe I've earned the right to build a life I don't want to escape from. Maybe flexibility isn't about saying yes to every opportunity but about having the freedom to say no.

I don't know. I told them I'd think about it over the weekend.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned earning 'the right to build a life you don't want to escape from.' What did it cost you to earn that right?

entries.initial_entry:
Turned down the Bahrain project. Explained that I could consult remotely but wouldn't relocate. They accepted, which surprised me. I expected them to walk away. Maybe I've been underestimating my leverage. Maybe I've been saying yes to things out of scarcity when I could afford to negotiate.

Layla was relieved. She said, "You're finally acting like someone who knows what he wants." I think she's right. For years I was running from things—from my old career, from expectations, from boredom. Now I'm starting to run toward something specific. A life that actually fits. It's a subtle shift but it feels significant.

We're planning another trip for March. Somewhere new. The reward for good work isn't more work—it's more life.

Sat through the whole lab meeting biting my tongue while Felix presented my preprocessing idea as his own. Didn't say anything. Walked home in the rain and honestly the cold felt better than sitting in that room. At least I know the idea works — that's mine regardless of who gets credit.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from speaking up?

entries.initial_entry: People keep asking why I turned down Professor Hecht's placement like I owe them an explanation. It was a bad fit. I don't need to justify building something from scratch when the alternative is tweaking someone else's framework for two years. My project, my call.
entries.nudge_text: You sound sure, but you haven't told your family yet.
entries.response_text: Yeah, because telling them means a two-hour phone call where my mother explains how Professor Hecht's name on my CV would open doors. She's not wrong about that part. I just can't spend two years on work that doesn't feel like mine. I'll tell them once I have something to show for it — results shut down the lecture faster than arguments do.

entries.initial_entry:
Three days into building my own pipeline and I'm already behind on coursework. Stayed up until four last night debugging tokenizer issues that would've been solved if I'd just used the lab's existing toolkit. My officemate Nina asked if I wanted help and I said no before I even thought about it. That was stupid. She's good at this stuff and she wasn't going to steal the project.

I keep doing this — refusing help because I need it to be entirely mine, and then drowning quietly. The fellowship application is due Friday and I haven't started the personal statement because every time I open it I think about how I'm supposed to frame myself as a team player. I don't know how to write that without it feeling like a lie, but I also don't know how to explain that I work better alone without sounding like a problem.

Had to let Fadi go today. Three years we built this together, back when it was just the two of us sharing a desk in my cousin's spare room. But he kept going around me to the team, kept second-guessing my calls in front of everyone. The look on his face when I handed him the paperwork, like I had betrayed something sacred between us.

Maybe I did. I keep thinking about what my grandmother used to say about loyalty, about how a man is nothing without the people who stood by him when he had nothing. Fadi stood by me. He believed in me before anyone else did. But a company cannot have two captains, and every time he questioned me publicly, I could feel the team's respect slipping away, feel my grip loosening.

I called Amira after, hoping she would understand. She just went quiet, then said something about how I seem different lately, harder. I told her running a business requires hard decisions. She said there is a difference between hard and cold. I hung up first, which I never do. Now it is 2am and I am staring at the ceiling wondering if she is right, if I am becoming someone even I would not recognize.
entries.nudge_text: What exactly did Fadi say that made you feel your grip loosening?
entries.response_text: Last month he told Rami, our lead developer, that my timeline for the Saudi expansion was unrealistic, and that maybe we should slow down. Did not tell me first. I heard it from Rami, who looked at me differently after that. Like maybe Fadi was the reasonable one and I was just chasing something too fast. That moment, I knew I had already lost part of the team to him, even if they would never say it out loud.

entries.initial_entry:
The investor meeting did not go how I imagined. Omar Khalifeh, the one everyone said would write the check without blinking, spent the whole time asking about our advisory board, our governance structure, whether we had brought on a more experienced CEO. I told him I am the CEO. He smiled like I had said something charming.

I used to think the money would just follow if the numbers were good enough. Our numbers are excellent. But he kept looking at me like I was a kid playing at something serious. I mentioned Fadi leaving and he actually said that was concerning, that investors like to see stable founding teams. Walked out of there feeling smaller than I have in months. Started drafting a message to Fadi, then deleted it.
entries.nudge_text: What were you going to say to Fadi before you deleted it?

entries.initial_entry:
Woke up at 4am again. Instead of spiraling about Omar or Fadi or any of it, I went to the office early and just sat in the dark for a while. Watched the sun come up through the glass. Something about being there before anyone else, the whole floor quiet and mine.

Rami came in around seven, surprised to see me. We ended up talking for two hours about the product roadmap, just the two of us hashing things out like the old days. No posturing, no defending my position. He had ideas I would have dismissed a month ago, but today I actually listened. Some of them were good. Told him so. His whole face changed, like he had been waiting for permission to think out loud.

Strange how that felt more like control than anything I have done in weeks. Not control over him, but control over myself. Over the kind of leader I am choosing to be. Amira called in the afternoon and I told her about the conversation with Rami. She said it sounded like I was finding my way back to something. I think she might be right. Not softer, not weaker. Just more certain about what actually matters.

The coordinadora pulled me aside after class and told me my reading circle format doesn't align with the ministry's new standardized framework. She wants me to switch to the workbook model by next week. I said I'd look into it but I'm not changing what's working.
entries.nudge_text: What happens when next week comes and you haven't switched?

entries.initial_entry:
People keep acting like I'm being difficult but I sat through two years of watching kids at the private school fill out comprehension worksheets with dead eyes. My students here actually talk about the books we read. They argue about characters. Last week Sofia, who barely spoke in September, stood up and defended her interpretation of the ending of the story to the whole class. That doesn't come from a workbook.

I talked to the coordinadora again today and told her straight up that I'd document my students' progress however she needs but I won't gut the program to fit a checkbox. She didn't love it. But she didn't say no either. My mom thinks I should just comply and keep my head down, which is rich coming from someone who argued with every teacher I ever had.
entries.nudge_text: Your mom told you to comply. How did that land?
entries.response_text: It stung more than it should have. She's the reason I push back on things in the first place.

entries.initial_entry:
Took Camilo through practice problems for the ICFES all Saturday morning. He's getting better at the reading comprehension sections, still shaky on the quantitative reasoning but that's not really my wheelhouse. I set him up with a YouTube channel that breaks it down step by step and told him to message me if he gets stuck. His mom, my tia Elena, called from Medellin to thank me, which was nice.

Went to abuela's in the afternoon. She'd made sancocho even though it was just the two of us, enough for ten people as usual. We watched a novela together and I fixed the latch on her back door that's been loose for months. She seems okay. A little quieter than normal maybe, but she always gets like that around the anniversary of abuelo's passing.

Long day. Parent meetings ran until six and then I stayed to reorganize the classroom library because nobody else was going to. Camilo called with a question about a practice test but I was too fried to be useful so I told him we'd go over it this weekend.
entries.nudge_text: You couldn't help Camilo tonight. Does that bother you?
entries.response_text: Yeah, a little. He doesn't ask unless he's actually stuck.

entries.initial_entry:
Got an email from the district office about a new mandate requiring all language arts teachers to submit weekly lesson plans using their template. The template is basically a fill-in-the-blank thing with pre-set learning objectives and approved activities. I forwarded it to my colleague Patricia and she just shrugged and said she'd start using it. I get it, she has two kids and no energy to fight bureaucracy. But I can't just do that.

The thing is, I know how this looks. I know people see me as the one who always has to do things her own way, the difficult teacher, the one who won't just go along. And maybe there's some truth in it. But I've also seen what happens when you hand over every decision about your classroom to someone who's never met your students. The kids in Ciudad Bolivar don't need another generic worksheet. They need someone paying attention to what actually reaches them.

I'm going to keep using my own plans and submit the template version as a formality. I've done this before. It's more work but I'd rather do double the planning than teach something I don't believe in.
entries.nudge_text: You said maybe there's truth in being the difficult one. What's that like?
entries.response_text: Lonely sometimes. Patricia used to push back with me but she stopped. I don't blame her but I notice it.

entries.initial_entry:
Second time writing today because I can't stop thinking about something that happened after school. One of my students, Diego, waited around after everyone left and asked me if I could help him write a letter to his dad who's working in Chile. He said his mom doesn't have time and he doesn't know how to start. So we sat there for forty minutes and I helped him figure out what he wanted to say. He wanted to tell his dad about a soccer game he played in and about a dog that follows him home from school every day.

It was one of those moments where you remember why you're doing this. Not the curriculum fights or the template emails or the district mandates. Just a kid who needed someone to sit with him and say yeah, that's worth writing down, your dad would want to hear about the dog.

I keep thinking about how many kids like Diego just need someone to stop and listen for half an hour. And then I think about how stretched thin I already am and how I told Camilo I'd help him this weekend and I promised abuela I'd come by Sunday and I still haven't finished planning next week's classes because I was busy making the fake template version for the district. There's only so much of me to go around.

Spent the morning with Camilo going through the quantitative reasoning section. He's definitely improving. We worked through the problems he'd flagged and he got about seventy percent right on his own before I stepped in. Told him that's real progress and he actually smiled, which he doesn't do much these days since he's so stressed about the exam. His mom is counting on him getting into a public university because they can't afford private tuition, so the pressure is real.

After that I went to abuela's. She was in better spirits today. She'd been to the market and bought ingredients for arepas de choclo, so we made them together. She told me stories about when she and abuelo first came to Bogota from the campo and how they used to make arepas on a clay stove. I've heard the stories before but I don't mind. She seems to need to tell them.

On the bus home I sketched out a rough plan for next week's reading unit. I'm going to have the kids write letters, inspired by Diego. They'll pick someone they want to tell something to and we'll work on voice and structure through that. It fits the ministry objectives if you squint at it the right way, and it's actually something the kids will care about.
entries.nudge_text: Diego's letter became a whole unit. Does that happen a lot?
entries.response_text: More than I realize probably. The best ideas come from paying attention to what the kids actually need, not from a planning document.

entries.initial_entry: Tia Elena called tonight and said Camilo bombed his practice test today, the one right after our session. She wasn't blaming me but it felt like it. I keep wondering if I'm actually helping him or just making myself feel useful.
entries.nudge_text: Making yourself feel useful — what made you put it that way?
entries.response_text: I don't know, I'm just tired. He'll be fine, he's smart enough.

entries.initial_entry:
Had the tech lead conversation with Martijn today. He laid out what the role would look like — running standups, making architecture calls, representing the team in cross-department meetings. I said I was interested, which is true, but the whole time I was thinking about how I'd actually handle it. I build things well when people leave me alone to think. That's not what this role is.

But I also know I did the right thing saying yes. Everyone on the team expects it, Noor would think I was insane to turn it down, and frankly it would look odd to pass on a promotion you're being handed. I can figure out the rest as I go. Probably.
entries.nudge_text: What part of it actually interests you?

entries.initial_entry: Long week. Spent most of it pairing with the new hire, walking her through the codebase and our deployment pipeline. Didn't get to touch my own backlog at all but she's ramping up faster than the last person we onboarded so I'll take it.
entries.nudge_text: Was it worth not having your own time?

entries.initial_entry:
Sprint retro got a bit heated. Devika suggested we rethink the approval flow for deployments because she thinks it slows us down. She's right, technically — it does add a half-day lag. I had a whole alternative sketched in my head while she was talking, a lighter-weight check that would still catch the critical stuff.

Didn't say it though. The senior architects set up the current flow and they were in the call. Felt like bringing up a redesign right there would come across as me trying to show off, especially with the tech lead thing floating around. So I just nodded and said I could look into Devika's point offline. She seemed a bit deflated that nobody backed her up more directly. I get it.

Weird day. Started normal — coffee, standup, tickets. Then around lunch Pieter from platform team pinged me about a side project he's been hacking on, this internal tool for tracking deployment health across services. He wanted to know if I'd help build the backend. Not sanctioned work, just something he thinks would be useful and wants to pitch to leadership once it's built.

First thought was yes obviously, this is the kind of thing I actually want to spend time on, building something new without twelve layers of sign-off. Second thought was that Martijn would not love me spending cycles on an unsanctioned project right when he's evaluating me for lead. Third thought was that Pieter didn't ask anyone else, he asked me, and that felt good in a way I don't fully understand. Like someone saw the part of me that wants to just build.

I told Pieter I'd think about it over the weekend. Which is code for "I want to do it but I need to figure out if I'm allowed to want to do it." That sounds ridiculous written down. I'm a grown man deciding whether to write some code on the side. But it feels more loaded than that right now.

Told Pieter I'd help with the tool. We set up a repo and I spent an hour last night after Noor went to sleep scaffolding the API layer. Felt more engaged in that hour than I have all sprint on the actual work.

Today was back to normal though — reviewing PRs, answering Slack threads, sitting through a planning session where half the decisions had already been made before we walked in. The usual. I'm not complaining, it's fine. I just noticed the contrast, that's all.
entries.nudge_text: You said it's fine twice now. Is it?
entries.response_text: Yeah. It's work, it doesn't all have to be exciting.

entries.initial_entry:
Noor and I went to dinner with her parents in Rotterdam on Saturday. Her dad asked about the promotion and I gave the standard answer — it's in progress, I'm grateful for the opportunity, etc. He seemed pleased. On the train back Noor said she was proud of me and I felt genuinely good about it. Like I'm on a track that makes sense to the people I care about.

Then on Sunday I stayed up way too late working on Pieter's tool and got completely absorbed refactoring the data ingestion layer. Noor asked what I was doing and I said "just work stuff" which wasn't really a lie but wasn't the whole truth either. I don't know why I didn't just explain it. Maybe because explaining it would mean admitting how much more alive that project makes me feel, and then someone might ask what that says about everything else.
entries.nudge_text: What would it mean if you did explain it to her?
