He noticed, I think. Asked if everything was alright. I said I was just tired from the trip. Which was true but not the whole truth. The whole truth is that I don't know how to enjoy a beer on a Thursday evening without a running tally in the background telling me what it's costing.

Spent most of Saturday at the university library. Productive enough — reorganised my notes on the Bijlmermeer case study and drafted two pages that might actually survive the next revision. Not exciting but solid work.

Walked home through the park. The leaves are mostly gone now. Passed a man walking three dogs who looked happier than anyone I've seen in the department all month. Made a pasta with anchovies and garlic for dinner, the one I learned from an Italian colleague years ago. Watched half a documentary about post-war Dutch architecture that I'd been meaning to get to. Fell asleep on the sofa before it finished, woke up at two in the morning with a stiff neck.

Normal day. Nothing to report, really. Sometimes that's fine.
entries.nudge_text: When isn't it fine?

entries.initial_entry:
Today was a choice. My old colleague Hendrika, from the Rotterdam days, was in town and invited me for lunch at a place we used to go — Indonesian rijsttafel, the kind of meal that takes two hours and you leave feeling like you've been properly fed for the first time in months. I haven't seen her since I started the PhD. She's good company, sharp, funny, the sort of person who makes you remember you used to be someone other than a stressed-out student.

I didn't go. I told her I had a deadline, which is technically true in the way that there is always a deadline. What actually happened is I sat at my desk, revised three paragraphs, went to a supervision prep meeting that lasted forty minutes, and spent the rest of the afternoon reading a paper I could have read tomorrow or next week or never. The work was fine. It was useful. But it wasn't urgent, and I knew that when I made the choice.

By evening I was sitting with the familiar feeling of having picked the responsible thing and gotten nothing in return except a quiet kind of bleakness. Hendrika sent a photo of the rijsttafel with a message that said "you were missed." I looked at it for a long time. The food looked incredible. I ate a sandwich at my desk.
entries.nudge_text: What would have happened if you'd gone?
entries.response_text: I would have enjoyed it. That's the honest answer. I would have had a good meal with a good friend and come back to the same work in the same state it was in before. Nothing would have fallen apart. I know that. I just couldn't make myself believe it at the time.

entries.initial_entry:
December already. Dark by half four. Cycling home tonight the air had that cold sharpness that makes you feel awake whether you want to or not. Locked my bike, walked up the stairs, and instead of going straight to the laptop I did something I haven't done in weeks — ran a bath. Not a shower, a proper bath. Found some old bath oil under the sink that I don't even remember buying, probably left over from when Anneke was still visiting.

Lay there for maybe forty minutes. The water was too hot at first, then just right. I could hear the wind outside and the neighbours' television through the wall, some game show. My mind wandered but not to work, which surprised me. I thought about the flat I had in Rotterdam, the one with the balcony where I used to grow herbs. Thought about a holiday I took in Portugal years ago where I spent a whole day doing nothing except swimming and reading on a beach. It felt like remembering a different person.

Got out when the water went cold, put on clean clothes, made tea. Sat at the kitchen table and just drank it. The thesis will be there in the morning. Tonight, I think, this was enough. I don't say that often. I'm trying to notice when I do.

Had the investor update call this afternoon and Jake — the new associate from Ridgeline Capital — kept jumping in while I was walking through the Q2 numbers. Not even asking questions, just restating what I'd said like he was translating me for the rest of the room. At one point I was mid-sentence about the warehouse expansion and he cut me off to say "so what Marcus is getting at is..." and finished my own thought. Nobody on the call said anything. My COO just sat there.

I didn't say anything either. That's the part sitting with me. I just paused, let him finish, and kept going like it didn't happen. But it did happen. And now I'm replaying it thinking about how everyone on that call has Jake's version of my point in their heads instead of mine. I should've shut it down. I didn't. I hate that I didn't.
entries.nudge_text: Why didn't you shut it down?

entries.initial_entry:
Closed the partnership deal with Trail & Summit today. Six months of back-and-forth and I got the terms I wanted — exclusive distribution for their new hydration line, our branding on the co-marketed SKUs, and a revenue split that actually reflects who's doing the heavy lifting on fulfillment. Their VP tried one last push to lower our margin by two points on the call this morning and I held the line without raising my voice. Just said "that's not going to work for us" and waited.

He came back ten minutes later with the original number. Felt right. Not celebratory or anything, just the quiet satisfaction of knowing I read the situation correctly and didn't blink. Told my ops lead to start prepping the warehouse space. We'll need it by September.
entries.nudge_text: How'd you know he'd come back?

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday and I'm still in the office. Spent the morning reviewing next quarter's ad spend breakdown with the marketing contractor. Numbers look fine, nothing exciting, just the usual allocation across paid social and a small bump for the Trail & Summit launch. She had a few suggestions about shifting budget toward influencer placements and I told her to put together a one-pager for Monday. Standard stuff.

Grabbed a sandwich from the place downstairs and ate it at my desk while going through the lease renewal docs for the warehouse. Landlord wants a three percent bump which is within range. I'll sign it tomorrow. Just one of those days where everything is maintenance — nothing's on fire, nothing's broken, but nothing moves forward unless I push it. Tired but it's a normal tired.

Had coffee with an old college friend this morning, someone I haven't seen since before the company existed. She's a product manager at a mid-size tech firm now. We talked about work mostly. She described her day-to-day and it sounded structured in a way I don't think I could tolerate — standups, sprint reviews, a manager who approves her project scope. She seemed fine with it. Happy, even.

I kept thinking about how different the wiring is. She talked about "alignment meetings" and I nearly laughed. In my world, alignment means everyone gets on board with the direction I've set, not a group exercise where we vote on priorities. Neither way is wrong, I guess. But I couldn't do hers. The thought of someone else deciding what I work on each week makes my chest tight, and I don't think that's going to change.

Still thinking about the coffee with Lauren this morning. She made this offhand comment near the end — something like "must be nice to not have a boss, but also kind of lonely, right?" And I brushed it off in the moment, said something about how the tradeoff is worth it. But I've been sitting with it since.

It's not lonely. That's not the right word. I have a team, I have a board, I have vendors and partners calling every day. The thing is, none of those people are peers in the way she means. They're people I manage or people who manage expectations of me. And that's fine. I chose this. I'd rather be the one making the calls alone than share the wheel with someone who doesn't see what I see. That's not loneliness, that's just the shape of the job.
entries.nudge_text: What does sharing the wheel look like to you?
entries.response_text: Honestly it looks like the thing with my old co-founder. Two people pulling in different directions, every decision a negotiation. I don't want that again.

entries.initial_entry:
Needed approval from the city on the warehouse expansion permit. Filed it three weeks ago. Called the planning office this morning and got transferred twice before someone told me the reviewer assigned to our file is on vacation until Wednesday. So now I just wait. The contractor is ready, the materials are ordered, my ops lead has the install schedule mapped to the day — and the whole thing stalls because one person I've never met decided to take a week off with my application in their inbox.

I asked if someone else could review it and the answer was no, there's no process for reassignment. I thanked the woman on the phone because that's what you do, then sat here staring at the wall. I've built something that employs twelve people and I can't get a permit reviewed because of someone else's PTO schedule. The waiting is the worst part. I can handle problems. I can't handle sitting still.
entries.nudge_text: What's the worst part about sitting still?
entries.response_text: It's not deep. I just have a timeline and other people don't care about my timeline. That's the whole thing.

entries.initial_entry:
Permit came through yesterday, two days later than it should have but it's done. Contractor starts Monday. Spent this morning walking the warehouse floor with my ops lead, marking where the new racking goes, checking measurements against the floor plan. Everything lines up. The expansion adds forty percent more storage which means we handle the Trail & Summit inventory without renting overflow space. That saves roughly four thousand a month, which I calculated before the board even asked.

The board did ask, on this morning's call. One of the newer members — a guy Ridgeline brought in — questioned whether we needed the expansion at all or could just use a 3PL. I walked him through the numbers, showed the per-unit cost comparison, and explained I'm not outsourcing a core part of the operation to a third party I can't control. He backed off. But I could feel the room testing me, the way rooms do when someone new wants to make their presence felt by poking at decisions already made.

After the call I drove up to Lookout Mountain, which I do sometimes to reset. Sat in the car at the overlook for twenty minutes, windows down, watching the city below. From up there the warehouse is just a dot near the highway. I've built something real, it's growing on my terms, and moments like today — the permit landing, the expansion moving, the board falling in line — that's proof the way I run things works.
The gallery wants to change the whole color palette for the installation, said it's "too somber for the spring collection." Spring collection. Like my work is a handbag.
entries.nudge_text: What matters most to you about this piece?
entries.response_text: It's about plastic waste in the North Sea. If I make it cheerful and decorative, I'm just adding to the noise. The discomfort is the point.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the weekend at the refugee art workshop. We had twelve participants this time, up from eight last month. One woman, Fatima, has been coming consistently since October. She doesn't speak much Dutch yet, but her drawings are extraordinary - detailed botanical illustrations of plants from her home region in Syria that she does from memory.

I brought some of my old nature journals from when I was her age, thinking we might find common ground. We sat together for most of the afternoon, not really talking, just drawing side by side. At one point she reached over and corrected my shading technique on a leaf. I was the student.

The session ran two hours over. I had planned to work on my installation sketches this evening, but I sat in the car for twenty minutes before driving home, just thinking about those plants that no longer exist where she once lived. The commission deadline feels less urgent somehow, even though I know it shouldn't.
entries.nudge_text: What shifted for you in that car?
entries.response_text: I was thinking about permanence. My gallery piece will be dismantled in three months. Fatima's drawings preserve something real that's been destroyed. I started questioning whether my environmental commentary means anything compared to someone actually documenting loss.

entries.initial_entry:
Just got off a call with my sister. She asked when I'm going to "get serious" about retirement planning. I'm 52. I have a studio, commissions, work that matters. But because I don't have a pension and a holiday fund, I'm apparently not a real adult.

She means well. I know she worries. But every conversation becomes a referendum on my life choices. Her husband works in insurance, they have a nice house in Utrecht, the kids are in good schools. And I have paint under my fingernails and a second-hand car. Different doesn't mean wrong.
Later the same day. Maybe the call bothered me more than I admitted. I do think about money sometimes. Not having it, I mean.
entries.nudge_text: What does security actually look like to you?
entries.response_text: Not what my sister has. But maybe enough to not panic when a commission falls through. I've been pretending money doesn't matter because wanting it feels like a betrayal of why I do this.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. The gallery accepted my color choices after I sent them a three-page explanation of the concept. They didn't love it, but they agreed. Small victory. I should feel satisfied but mostly I feel tired.

The nonprofit board meeting ran three hours last night. We're losing our main funder and no one can agree on next steps. Half the board wants to chase corporate sponsors, half thinks that compromises our mission. I sat there thinking about how much simpler it was when I just showed up and ran workshops. Now there's strategy and stakeholders and "capacity building."
entries.nudge_text: What's getting squeezed out by all this?

entries.initial_entry: Saw the question about what's getting squeezed. I'm not avoiding it. I just don't have a neat answer. My studio time, obviously. Maybe that's fine for now.
Good day in the studio. Lost track of time, which hasn't happened in weeks. The installation is finally taking shape the way I imagined it.

Installation opens next week and I'm behind. Skipped the workshop on Saturday to finish the main panel, which was the right choice for the deadline but felt wrong in a different way. Fatima asked after me, apparently. One of the other volunteers said she seemed disappointed.

I've been telling myself that the artwork has meaning beyond just me, that it might change how someone sees the world. But sitting here at midnight with resin fumes and sore hands, I wonder if I'm just making excuses for choosing my career over the people I said I cared about.
entries.nudge_text: Is this a one-time choice or a pattern?
entries.response_text: Pattern. I know it's a pattern. The teaching job, my marriage, now this. I keep choosing work. I tell myself it's because the work matters, but maybe I just find people harder.

entries.initial_entry: Opening night went well. Several pieces sold. A journalist from NRC wants to interview me about the environmental themes.

Same day, later. The opening was a success by every external measure and I spent the last hour sitting in my studio in the dark.

A collector asked me to explain the piece about North Sea plastic. I gave my usual explanation - the scope of the problem, the research, the materials I used. He nodded, bought the piece, and then asked if I could do something similar but "more hopeful" for his holiday home. I smiled and said I'd think about it.

I should have said no. I should have said art isn't interior decoration. But I need the money, and he wasn't wrong that people want hope. And maybe I'm the one who's wrong, clinging to this idea that discomfort creates change when actually people just look away.

I keep thinking about what I said in the last question - that I choose work because people are harder. But tonight I chose people, in a way. I performed the version of myself that makes sales and connections. And I hate it, but I also felt this relief that it was working, that maybe I don't have to be so uncompromising about everything.
entries.nudge_text: What would uncompromising cost you right now?
entries.response_text: Probably the commission. Maybe more. But also - and I hadn't thought this through until now - it might cost me this idea that my choices are purely principled. Some of my stubbornness is just fear of doing it wrong in a different way.

entries.initial_entry:
Went back to the workshop today. Fatima was there. She showed me the drawing she finished while I was away - a complete botanical study of a flower I don't recognize, maybe something that grew near Aleppo. We didn't talk about my absence.

I'm tired in a good way tonight. The kind of tired from doing something rather than just worrying about all the things I'm not doing. I emailed the collector to say I'd think about his commission but couldn't promise "hopeful" - I could only promise honest. He hasn't replied. That's probably fine.

Can't stop thinking about today. There was this older man, Mr. Hernandez, and he reminded me so much of my tio — the way he called me "mija" and told me about his grandchildren back in Honduras. He needed his blood pressure medication but couldn't afford the copay, kept apologizing like he was asking for something unreasonable. And I just... I knew we have samples. I know where they are. I could have grabbed a month's supply and no one would have noticed or cared.

But I didn't. Followed protocol. Gave him the paperwork for financial assistance that takes six weeks to process. He thanked me anyway, squeezed my hand, said God would bless me for my kindness. Kindness. I filled out a form.

The rules exist for a reason, right? That's what my supervisor says. That's what I tell myself when I'm lying in bed at 3am staring at the ceiling instead of sleeping. The system works when everyone follows it. My abuela used to say you earn respect by doing things the right way, even when the right way is hard. She volunteered here for decades and never took a single bandaid home that wasn't accounted for.

But also — what is the system actually doing for Mr. Hernandez? He's 68, worked construction his whole life, and now he can't afford pills that cost the hospital pennies. I went into nursing because I wanted to help people. Real help. Not paperwork.

I keep telling myself I'll feel better about this tomorrow. I won't.
entries.nudge_text: What would your abuela have done?
entries.response_text: She would have followed the rules. She always did. But things were different then — there wasn't so much bureaucracy, people looked out for each other more. Anyway, it doesn't matter. I have to get ready for my shift.

entries.initial_entry: Told my mom I can't come home for Christmas this year because of work. It's not entirely a lie — I could have requested off earlier. Just didn't.
entries.nudge_text: Why didn't you?
entries.response_text: Because part of me wanted an excuse. Everyone will be there — tias, primos, the whole family asking when I'm getting married, why I'm not going to church anymore, if I'm eating enough. I love them but I come back feeling like I'm failing at being who they expect me to be. At least at the hospital I know what I'm supposed to do.

entries.initial_entry:
Good shift today. We had a Guatemalan family come in — the grandmother only spoke K'iche' and was terrified. I don't speak it fluently but I know enough from my own abuela to say the important things: you're safe, we're going to help you, your family is right outside.

She grabbed my hand during the IV and prayed. I prayed with her, quietly, in Spanish. Felt right. The daughter cried when I came out to update them. Said they'd never been treated with such dignity at a hospital before. That's the job. That's why I'm here.

I hate the new scheduling software. It's supposed to be "fair" and "optimized" but it has me working Christmas Eve AND Christmas Day, back to back twelve hour shifts. Meanwhile Sarah who has no family obligations gets the whole week off because she requested it first. Cool. Great. Love that for her.

Called my mom to complain and she just said "Ay mija, that's how it is. At least you have a good job." Which I know is true. I know I'm lucky. I know people would kill for this position. But I'm allowed to be annoyed, right? I haven't had a Christmas off in three years. Three years of sending gifts instead of being there. Three years of video calls while everyone eats together.
entries.nudge_text: What's the hardest part of not being there?
entries.response_text: Watching them on the screen and knowing they're making memories without me. My little cousin is seven now and she barely knows who I am. Last year she asked my mom if I was the one who sends the presents. Not tia Valentina. Just "the one who sends presents." I'm becoming a stranger to my own family and I can't do anything about it because someone has to cover the holiday shifts and I'm the one without kids.

entries.initial_entry: Worked sixteen hours. A man died on my floor — no family, no one to call. I held his hand at the end because no one should die alone. My family sent me a video of them opening presents and I couldn't watch it.
entries.nudge_text: You could be there for him but not for them?

entries.initial_entry: Everyone's going out tonight. I'm eating leftover tamales my neighbor brought me and going to bed early. Told myself I'd call my mom at midnight her time but I'll probably be asleep. Another year of choosing this life over the one I was supposed to have.
entries.nudge_text: What life were you supposed to have?
entries.response_text: Married by now, probably. Living close to home, seeing my family every Sunday, raising kids who speak Spanish as their first language and know how to make tamales and go to mass. That's what my mom did, what her mom did. Instead I'm alone in an apartment eating someone else's cooking and pretending this is enough because I'm "making a difference." I don't know if I'm brave for building something new or just selfish for leaving everything behind.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the whole afternoon with Abu Mahmoud at his farm outside Karameh. Three hours sitting on plastic chairs drinking tea while he showed me where the irrigation canal used to run before the allocation changed. His hands, the way he traced the old waterline in the dirt like drawing a map from memory. Forty years he worked that land. Now his grandson is driving trucks in Amman because there is nothing left to grow.

I recorded everything but the recording will not capture how quiet he got when I asked about the cooperative's petition. How he looked past me toward the orchards, half of them dried stumps now. Dr. Khalil wants regression models and policy impact matrices. What I have is Abu Mahmoud saying "we wrote letters until we ran out of paper." This is what I came here for. Not the degree, not the credentials. This.
entries.nudge_text: What made you leave engineering for this?
entries.response_text: Thirty years I designed systems to move water from where it was to where it was needed. Good work. Useful work. But always I was working for someone else's priorities, someone else's calculations about who matters. The last project, we diverted a whole tributary for a development that displaced six villages. I signed my name to those drawings. Came home and could not sleep for a week. Told my husband I needed to understand the other side of my own blueprints.

entries.initial_entry:
Committee meeting today. Dr. Khalil brought up timeline again, asked when I would have my methodology chapter finalized. I told him the fieldwork is taking longer because I am doing it properly, not rushing through interviews like they are survey forms. He gave me that look, the one he gives when he thinks I am being difficult. Said something about how at my stage of career I should know how to work within constraints.

At my stage. Meaning old. Meaning I should be grateful they let me in at all, should play by the rules without questioning. He does not say this directly but I hear it. The younger students in my cohort, they nod along, they revise when told to revise. Maybe that is smarter. Maybe I am wasting everyone's time insisting my approach matters. But every time I sit down to write the chapter his way, my hands just stop. Something in me refuses.
entries.nudge_text: What specifically about his way makes your hands stop?
entries.response_text: He wants me to treat the farmers as data points. Sample size, response rates, coding categories. I understand the logic, I am not stupid about methodology. But when I sit down to write it that way, I keep seeing Abu Mahmoud's face. He trusted me with his story. Reducing it to a variable in a regression feels like betraying that trust. Like I am doing the same thing I did as an engineer, just with different paperwork.

entries.initial_entry: Roommates had people over until 2am again. Studied at the library until they kicked me out, then sat in my car in the parking lot reading by phone light. Called Farshad and he laughed, said I am the most stubborn person he knows.
entries.nudge_text: What were you reading in the car?
entries.response_text: Papers on participatory action research. Found this scholar from Brazil who worked with fishing communities fighting industrial pollution. Her whole methodology is about the community being partners in the research, not subjects. Reading her citations like following a trail. If Dr. Khalil will not accept my approach, maybe I can build enough of a foundation that he cannot dismiss it as naive.

entries.initial_entry: Presented the participatory framework to the committee today. Dr. Khalil called it unconventional. Dr. Haddad said she found it compelling. Got approval to continue with modifications. Walked out of that room feeling like I had earned something that was mine.

Started the new term today. My Primary 3 class has 32 students this year, and already I can tell Jayden in the back row is going to be a handful. Had a meeting with Mrs. Tan, my mentor, about my classroom management plan.

She said my lesson on fractions last week was "creative but needed tighter pacing." I stayed until 7pm revising the slides. Arjun texted asking if we're still going for Korean BBQ on Saturday - I said yes but I'm already thinking about how much marking I need to clear.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned saying yes to Korean BBQ but your mind went straight to marking. What's making you think about work when you're making weekend plans?

entries.initial_entry:
I've been thinking about something Mrs. Tan said during our feedback session. She mentioned that I spend more time on "fun activities" than other beginning teachers and asked if I was "prioritizing student engagement over curriculum coverage." It stung more than it should have.

The thing is, my kids actually look forward to my lessons. Aisha told me yesterday that math is her favorite subject now because of my fraction pizza activity. But I also notice the other teachers leaving at 5pm while I'm still cutting out laminated shapes at 6:30. Maybe I'm doing this wrong. Or maybe they're just faster because they've been doing it longer. I don't know.
entries.nudge_text: That comment from Mrs. Tan clearly landed hard. What is it about her feedback that got under your skin?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I think I'm just tired. It's the end of term and everyone's stressed. She probably didn't mean anything by it - she's been teaching for 20 years and has seen a hundred new teachers come through. I'll focus on pacing next term and it'll be fine.

entries.initial_entry:
Last day of term. I just got home and it's 9pm. End-of-year reports took forever because I insisted on writing personalized comments for all 32 students instead of using the template phrases. Mrs. Tan actually complimented my reports today - said the parents would appreciate the detail. Small win, I guess.

But I'm so tired. Arjun and I were supposed to go to the Christmas market at Gardens by the Bay tonight but I cancelled. He was understanding but I could hear the disappointment. This is the third thing I've cancelled this month. Last week was my cousin Meera's birthday dinner, and before that it was supposed to be a spa day with my college friends.

Everyone keeps telling me the first year is the hardest. My colleague Rachel, who's in her second year, said she barely saw her husband for six months. Is that supposed to make me feel better? Mum called yesterday and when I told her I was exhausted, she said "but this is what you wanted, no?" And yes, I did want this. I love being in the classroom. Today during our class party, watching the kids exchange cards and eat cookies, I felt so happy. Dinesh, my quietest student, actually came up and gave me a handmade card that said "Best Teacher."

I just wish I could have both - be good at this job AND have a life outside of it. Right now it feels like I'm choosing one over the other and somehow still failing at both.
entries.nudge_text: You said it feels like you're failing at both work and life outside it. But you also mentioned getting compliments on your reports and a handmade card from a student. What would 'not failing' actually look like to you?
entries.response_text: I think not failing would mean... being present. When I'm at work, fully at work without guilt about what I'm missing. And when I'm with Arjun or my friends, actually being there instead of mentally going through my to-do list. Right now I'm always half somewhere else. The card from Dinesh was beautiful but even in that moment part of me was thinking about how I still needed to input grades into the portal.

entries.initial_entry:
Christmas Eve. Arjun and I are at his parents' place in Johor for the long weekend. His mum made mutton biryani and there's way too much food as usual. His younger sister Kavitha is back from Melbourne so there's been a lot of catching up.

We went to a night market yesterday and I bought these ridiculous matching couple keychains shaped like curry puffs. Arjun pretended to be embarrassed but I caught him showing his sister later. Today we just lazed around - watched a movie, played cards with his grandma, ate leftovers for lunch. His parents have this swing seat in their garden and I spent an hour there just reading a novel. An actual novel, not a teaching resource.

The contrast from last week is stark. I haven't thought about school once today. Well, except when Kavitha asked about my job and I ended up talking about Dinesh's card for ten minutes. But that was nice talking, not stressed thinking.

Arjun asked if I was feeling better. I said yes. He said "good, you looked really worn out" and honestly, I hadn't realized how bad it had gotten until I stepped away from it. Tomorrow we're driving to Malacca for the day to try this famous chicken rice ball place his colleague recommended.

Back in Singapore. The Malacca trip was lovely - we walked through Jonker Street, ate cendol, took photos by those colorful murals. Arjun said we should do more spontaneous trips like this. I agreed, but already my mind is drifting to school reopening next week.

I've been thinking about what "presence" means. Over the past few days I felt like myself again - laughing at dumb jokes, being curious about things that aren't work-related, enjoying food without rushing. But now that I'm home, sitting in my apartment with my laptop open, I can feel the familiar anxiety creeping back. I haven't opened my email yet but I know there will be things waiting. Part of me wants to check "just to know what's there." Part of me wants to protect this feeling a little longer.
entries.nudge_text: You're sitting with the choice of whether to open your email right now. What do you think would happen if you waited until tomorrow?
entries.response_text:
Probably nothing catastrophic. School doesn't officially start until next Wednesday. But there's this voice that says I'll be behind, that other teachers are already preparing, that Mrs. Tan might have sent feedback I need to act on. The rational part of me knows one more day won't matter. The anxious part is already composing a to-do list.

I'm going to close the laptop. Tomorrow is soon enough.

entries.initial_entry:
First week back and I'm already feeling the pressure. Today Mrs. Tan called me into her office to discuss my "development goals" for the year. She brought up the MOE promotion framework and asked where I saw myself in five years. I panicked and said "senior teacher track" because that's what you're supposed to say, right?

But sitting here now, I'm not even sure that's true. The senior teachers I see are in meetings constantly, doing administrative work, mentoring others. They're hardly ever in classrooms with kids anymore. And isn't that the whole point? Isn't that why I became a teacher?

I called my friend Meilin who teaches at another school. She's the same age but already coordinating the Science department's enrichment programs. She sounded excited about it - talked about "visibility" and "building a portfolio." When I told her about feeling conflicted, she said "Priya, you can't stay a classroom teacher forever. That's just how the system works."

So now I'm confused and also a little annoyed. Why can't I just be good at teaching? Why does everything have to be about climbing some ladder? I keep thinking about Dinesh and how he's finally starting to participate in class. That matters to me. More than being on some committee or getting a title.

But then there's Mum and Papa, who video called on New Year's Day asking about my "career progression." And Arjun's parents, who are so proud that both their kids have "professional jobs." And Mrs. Tan, who genuinely wants to help me succeed by her definition of success.

I feel like I'm disappointing everyone by wanting something simpler.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned feeling like you're disappointing everyone by wanting something simpler. But who exactly decides what 'success' means for your career - them or you?
entries.response_text: That's a loaded question. In theory, me. In practice, there are real consequences to not playing the game - slower pay progression, fewer opportunities, being seen as lacking drive. It's not as simple as "follow your heart" when the system is designed around a specific path.

entries.initial_entry:
Something good happened today and I want to remember it. We started a new unit on creative writing and I let the kids write about anything they wanted. Most wrote about their holidays, pets, favorite games. But Aisha - the one who said math was her favorite subject - wrote this whole story about a girl who could fly but chose to walk because she liked how flowers smelled at ground level.

I pulled her aside after class to tell her how much I loved it. She got so shy and happy at the same time. Her mom picked her up today and I mentioned the story - she looked surprised, said Aisha usually hates writing.

Moments like this are why I teach. Not for reviews or promotions or what it looks like on paper. For a 9-year-old discovering she can create something beautiful with words. I don't know how to put that in a "development goal" but it's the truest thing I know about myself right now.
Long Sunday. Arjun and I had brunch at Atlas then walked around Bugis. Nice day but my mind kept wandering to tomorrow, to meetings, to whether I'm doing enough.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned wondering if you're doing enough. Enough for what, or for whom?
entries.response_text: For myself, I think. Or the version of myself I thought I'd be by now. When I was in NIE, I imagined being this effortlessly great teacher who also had time for friends and hobbies and travel. Instead I'm constantly choosing between being mediocre at everything or good at one thing while the rest falls apart. Today was supposed to be relaxing but I couldn't stop calculating how many hours until Monday. That's not who I want to be.

entries.initial_entry:
Finally had a day off after six days straight. Spent the whole afternoon at Kite Beach with Nadia and Sara. We found this spot near the food trucks where the sand was perfect, not too hot, and just lay there listening to the waves. Nadia kept trying to get us to go paddleboarding but I told her I just wanted to stay horizontal and feel the sun on my face.

There was this moment when I looked up at the sky and realized I hadn't thought about the hospital in hours. No beeping monitors, no worried parents, no charts to update. Just the sound of the water and my friends laughing about something on their phones. I needed that. I really, really needed that.

Second entry today because we ended up at this new Persian restaurant Nadia found. The tahdig was crispy and perfect and I may have eaten three servings of kashk-e bademjan. My stomach hurts in the best way possible.
entries.nudge_text: What made tonight special beyond the food?
entries.response_text: It was just nice, you know? Good food, good company. Sometimes that's enough.

entries.initial_entry:
Back-to-back twelve-hour shifts. A child in the oncology ward took a turn for the worse and I spent most of yesterday holding her mother's hand while the doctors explained options. I got home at 9 PM both nights and barely had energy to shower before collapsing.

My mother called today asking when I'm going to visit Beirut. I told her maybe in November but honestly I don't know if I can face the family gatherings right now. Everyone will ask about marriage, about why I'm "wasting my best years" in Dubai. I just want to rest without having to justify my choices to anyone.
entries.nudge_text: What specifically about the family gatherings feels hard to face?
entries.response_text: The usual stuff. Lebanese aunties being Lebanese aunties. It's fine, I'll deal with it.

entries.initial_entry:
Charge nurse Fatima made another comment today about my "lifestyle." We were in the break room and she was talking about how she works double shifts to save for her daughter's education, and then she looked at me and said "some of us have responsibilities." As if I don't work hard. As if nursing isn't exhausting whether you stay for eight hours or sixteen.

I don't understand why taking care of myself is seen as selfishness. I give everything to my patients when I'm on shift. I held that mother's hand for three hours on Tuesday. I stayed late to comfort a seven-year-old who was scared of his IV. But because I don't sacrifice my entire existence to work, I'm somehow less committed?

My father worked himself into a heart attack at fifty-two. I watched him in that hospital bed, gray and diminished, and I swore I would never let a job steal my life from me. Fatima doesn't know that. Nobody at work knows that. They just see a young woman who goes to the beach and assume she doesn't take anything seriously.
entries.nudge_text: What was it like watching your father in that hospital bed?
entries.response_text: Terrifying. He looked so small. He was always the strongest person I knew, the one who fixed everything, and suddenly he couldn't even lift a glass of water. I remember thinking: this is what happens when you give everything to work and keep nothing for yourself. I was nineteen. I had just started nursing school. I promised myself I would never end up like that.

entries.initial_entry: Booked a weekend trip to Muscat with Sara. We found a resort with an infinity pool overlooking the Gulf. I've been counting down the days.
entries.nudge_text: What are you most looking forward to about this trip?

entries.initial_entry:
Back from Muscat. We spent Saturday morning at the fish market in Muttrah, watching fishermen unload their catch while the call to prayer echoed across the harbor. Sara haggled with a vendor for some dried limes and we both got these beautiful silver bangles from an old souk.

The afternoon we did nothing. Absolutely nothing. Lay by the pool with our books, ordered room service, watched the sunset turn the water gold and pink. Sara said she felt guilty for not "doing more" while we were there, but I told her this is doing something. Rest is doing something. Enjoying the moment is doing something. I think she actually believed me by Sunday.
entries.nudge_text: Why was it important to you that Sara believe that?
entries.response_text: Because I see myself in her. The guilt, the feeling like you should always be productive, always achieving something. I used to feel that way too before I decided to stop. If I can help her let go of that even a little, maybe she won't burn out the way I almost did.

entries.initial_entry:
My cousin Rania got engaged. The family WhatsApp group exploded with congratulations and my mother immediately called to tell me, her voice dripping with that particular tone she uses when she's comparing me to someone else. "Rania is younger than you, habibi. Just something to think about."

I am thinking about it. I'm thinking that Rania is marrying someone her parents introduced her to after three months of knowing him. I'm thinking that she quit her job because he wants her to focus on "starting a family." I'm thinking that none of that sounds like the life I want.

But I didn't say any of that. I just said congratulations and asked about the wedding date. Because what's the point of explaining? They don't understand that some of us want different things. That not wanting what they want doesn't mean I'm broken or confused or going through a phase.

There's a new resident at the hospital, Dr. Faris. Young, intense, the type who reads medical journals in the break room. We got to talking today during a slow hour and he asked me what I do for fun. When I mentioned the beach and restaurants and travel, he looked at me like I was speaking a foreign language.

He said he hasn't taken a vacation in three years. Three years. He seemed almost proud of it, like suffering is a badge of honor. I wanted to ask him what the point of becoming a doctor was if he's never going to enjoy being alive, but I just nodded and changed the subject.

It made me think about how different people measure their lives. Some people count achievements, promotions, publications. I count good meals, beautiful sunsets, moments of genuine laughter with people I love. Neither is wrong, I guess. But I know which one I want on my deathbed.
entries.nudge_text: What made you so certain about what you want on your deathbed?
entries.response_text: I don't know if I'm certain. It's just how I try to live. Thinking too much about dying feels morbid.

entries.initial_entry:
Lost a patient today. Eight years old. Leukemia. I've been doing this for five years and it never gets easier. You tell yourself not to get attached but how can you not when you're adjusting her pillows and bringing her extra apple juice and watching her try to be brave for her parents?

I went straight to the beach after my shift. Sat there in my scrubs watching the waves until the sun went down. I don't know what else to do with grief except let it sit next to me for a while. The water doesn't ask anything of you. It just keeps moving.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think she would have wanted for you after today?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Probably to go home and eat something. She was always asking if I ate lunch.

entries.initial_entry:
Nadia's birthday dinner last night. We went to this rooftop place in JBR with the Dubai Marina glittering below us. Thirteen of us around a long table, sharing mezze and laughing so loud the tables around us kept looking over. I don't even remember what we were laughing about. Just the feeling of being surrounded by people who like being alive.

After dinner we walked along the marina and Nadia said this was the happiest she'd been in months. Work has been crushing her and she almost canceled tonight because she felt guilty taking time for herself. I told her what I always tell people: the work will always be there. The moments won't. I don't know if I'm wise or just stubborn, but I believe it.
entries.nudge_text: Where did you learn to see it that way?
entries.response_text: From watching my father after his heart attack. He said the worst part wasn't almost dying. It was realizing how much of his life he'd missed while he was busy working. He told me to never make the same mistake. I listened.

entries.initial_entry: Long week. Flu season starting early. I'm off for the next three days and I have zero plans except sleeping in and maybe getting a massage. That's the whole plan. That's enough.

Went home to Beirut for a long weekend. Faced the aunties. Survived. Actually more than survived.

Something shifted this time. When my aunt Huda started the usual questions about when I'm settling down, I just smiled and said I'm very happy with my life as it is. She looked confused, like she didn't have a script for someone who isn't apologizing or getting defensive. My mother was watching and I could tell she wanted to jump in and explain me, but she didn't. She just let me be.

Later my father pulled me aside on the balcony while everyone was eating. He said he's proud of me. That I figured out something at thirty that took him fifty years and a heart attack to understand. That life is for living, not for impressing people who won't remember you anyway. Coming from him, that meant everything.

I flew back to Dubai this morning and went straight to the beach. Watched the sunset alone this time. Felt full in a way I can't quite describe. Not because anything big happened, but because I'm finally living the life I actually want, and the people who matter most are starting to see it too.

- Finally shipped the offline-sync feature for the rural schools module
- Took three sprints longer than planned but it actually works on 2G now
- Ravi sir from the Hubli pilot sent a voice note saying the kids were using it during lunch breaks
- That voice note is doing more for me right now than any sprint review ever has
entries.nudge_text: What was it about that voice note specifically?
entries.response_text: He sounded surprised. Like he genuinely did not expect it to work. And the kids laughing in the background, just messing around with the app like it was a game. That gap between what we built and what they turned it into, I don't know, it reminded me of when Suresh sir first showed me a terminal in college.

entries.initial_entry: Priya got her campus placement offer today. It is a decent product company in Hyderabad with good benefits. Amma cried at lunch, the happy kind, and then immediately started worrying about Priya living alone. I told her Priya would be fine. Honestly I am just relieved one less thing is on the table financially.

Coming back to this because something has been sitting with me since the afternoon. When Priya showed me the offer letter I noticed the salary. It is nearly the same as mine, fresh out of college. I am not jealous of her, that is not it. She earned it completely. But it made me do the math I keep avoiding.

I have been at this startup for three years and the increments have been modest. The work is the most fulfilling thing I have done but I also know Amma's knees are getting worse and the consultation she needs is not cheap. Priya's new salary helps but she will have her own rent and expenses. I keep circling back to the same question without finishing the thought.
entries.nudge_text: What's the question you keep not finishing?

entries.initial_entry: Had coffee with Nikhil today. He moved to a fintech company six months ago, says the work is mostly CRUD but the pay let him put his parents in a nicer flat. He was not bragging. He looked tired actually. I kept nodding and thinking about Amma's knee and the 2G kids in Hubli at the same time.
entries.nudge_text: Can you have both or is that the problem?
entries.response_text: I think the honest answer is I don't know yet and that scares me more than either option. If I leave for more money I know I'll resent sitting in standups about payment conversion funnels. But if Amma needs that surgery and I am still making idealistic-startup money, then what was all of it for? I keep framing it like a binary but maybe the part I can't say out loud is that I'm afraid whichever I pick I'll feel like I failed someone.

entries.initial_entry:
Came back to write more because I could not sleep after that last entry. Nikhil also mentioned that his old manager from the gaming company reached out to me apparently. Said they are looking for someone to lead backend on an accessibility project, adaptive interfaces for visually impaired users. The pay would be significantly better than what I make now and the work would not feel empty the way pure fintech might.

But here is the thing. If I leave the startup, the offline-sync project does not have a clear owner. Karthik could take it over eventually but he joined four months ago and the codebase is, frankly, held together with duct tape and my institutional memory. The Hubli pilot is supposed to expand to three more districts by January. If that rollout stumbles because of a handoff gap, actual kids lose access.

I know I am not irreplaceable in some cosmic sense. People leave jobs and the world continues. But the timing feels like it matters. Priya's placement means I could take a risk on staying. Or it means I finally have the breathing room to go somewhere that pays properly. Same fact, two completely different stories depending on which direction I face. Lying here staring at the ceiling fan trying to figure out which story I actually believe.

Amma asked me at breakfast why I have been quiet lately. I said work stress, which is not a lie but is not the whole thing either. She said she hopes I am not overworking myself for her sake. The way she said it made me think she knows more than she lets on. That made my chest tight for the rest of the morning.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think she actually knows?

entries.initial_entry: The Saturday bootcamp kids presented their projects today. One of them, Farah, built a bus-route finder for her neighborhood because her grandmother takes the wrong bus sometimes. It is buggy and the UI is rough but she demoed it to the group with so much confidence. I almost lost it in front of twelve teenagers.
entries.nudge_text: What got to you about Farah's project?
entries.response_text: She reminded me of why I started this in the first place. She was not building it for marks or a portfolio. She built it because her grandmother needed help and she figured out she had the skills to do something about it. That is the whole thing, right there. That is the part I keep forgetting when I am up at night running salary comparisons.

entries.initial_entry:
- Got home from the bootcamp feeling good then opened LinkedIn and saw three more recruiter messages
- One of them literally said "stop leaving money on the table"
- Closed the app but the phrase is stuck in my head
- Maybe I should just turn off notifications for a while, I cannot think clearly with all this noise

The kids go back to school tomorrow and I should be relieved because summer has been exhausting, nonstop snacks and arguments and sunscreen reapplication, but instead I'm sitting on the balcony at midnight feeling like something got taken from me. Which is ridiculous because I was complaining about this exact summer two weeks ago.

It's the mornings I'll miss. Tariq leaves early anyway so it was just me and the kids, slow, no rushing. I'd make coffee the way I like it, with cardamom, and we'd eat fruit on the balcony before it got too hot. Nobody asking me to be anywhere. Nobody checking if I'd signed a permission slip. Just sun and sticky fingers and that particular quiet you get before the city wakes up fully.

Now it goes back to the schedule. Pack lunches at 6:30, iron uniforms, stand at the gate making small talk with other mothers who want to discuss tutoring. I know it sounds ungrateful. I know plenty of mothers would love to be home at all. But I keep thinking about those mornings and how I didn't appreciate them enough while they were happening.
entries.nudge_text: What made those mornings feel different from the rest of summer?
entries.response_text: No agenda. That's really it. Nobody needed anything from me yet.

entries.initial_entry:
First day back and it went fine, objectively. Hana was thrilled about her new teacher. Sami less so but he's nine, everything is beneath him now apparently. I got them there on time, uniforms pressed, lunches packed with the little notes they pretend to hate.

Came home to an empty house and immediately felt like I should be doing something productive. Laundry, organizing closets, something. Instead I sat on the couch and ate leftover knafeh for breakfast and watched a cooking show for two hours. Kept glancing at the door like someone was going to walk in and catch me. Catch me doing what? Sitting in my own house? I don't know why I can't just enjoy things without the running commentary in my head.
entries.nudge_text: Whose voice is that commentary?
entries.response_text: My mother's, probably. She never sat down during the day. Still doesn't.

entries.initial_entry: Made the cardamom coffee again this morning after drop-off and drank it slowly on the balcony by myself. Twenty minutes of nothing. It felt like reclaiming something small.

Tariq's mother called to say she thinks Sami should start Quran tutoring on Saturdays. Saturdays are the one day we have no obligations and now I'm supposed to give that up too. I didn't say no but I didn't say yes either and that silence is going to cost me later.
entries.nudge_text: What does that silence usually lead to?
entries.response_text: A longer phone call next week where she explains what a good mother would do.

entries.initial_entry:
Friday lunch at my parents' house again. Same food, same seating arrangement, same conversation about my brother's new car. Hana spilled grape juice on the couch and my mother gave me that look, the one that says I should have been watching more closely, even though there were fifteen adults in the room.

I don't hate Fridays. The food is good and the kids love seeing their cousins. But by the time we get home I feel like I've been performing for six hours straight. Smiling, helping in the kitchen, making sure the children behave, agreeing when my aunt says I'm lucky I don't have to work. Lucky. Sure. I came home and ran a bath so hot it turned my skin red and that was the best part of the entire day.
entries.nudge_text: What would a Friday look like if you designed it?

entries.initial_entry:
Something good happened and I want to write it down before I forget the feeling. Tariq came home early today, which never happens, and he said let's take the kids for ice cream. Not the place near the house, the one across town with the pistachio gelato and the little garden out back where they have jasmine growing along the wall.

We drove with the windows down and Hana sang the whole way, completely off key, and Sami was reading in the back seat which meant he was content. We sat outside and the light was doing that golden thing it does in September and I had two scoops and didn't think about anything else. Not the laundry, not Saturday tutoring, not what my mother-in-law thinks.

Tariq looked at me at one point and said "this is what matters, right?" and I almost cried because yes. This. Exactly this. A warm evening and good ice cream and my kids being happy and nobody asking me to be anything other than a person eating gelato in a garden. I want to hold onto this feeling but I already know tomorrow I'll wake up and the list will be there waiting.
Spent money on a nice candle and a book I've been wanting and I don't need to justify it. Tariq didn't say anything about it. Nobody said anything about it. I'm the only one keeping score.
entries.nudge_text: What would not keeping score look like?
entries.response_text: I'll let you know when I figure that out. The candle smells incredible though.

entries.initial_entry:
The Saturday tutoring started. Tariq's mother won, of course. Sami was fine with it, which somehow makes it worse because I can't even claim I'm protecting him from something. He came home and recited what he'd learned and everyone on the family group chat sent clapping emojis and heart emojis and I felt like the walls were closing in.

It's not about the tutoring specifically. It's that every open space in the week gets filled. Every pocket of time that could be for something unstructured, something that just feels good, gets assigned a purpose by someone else. I keep losing these little windows and I don't know how to say that out loud without sounding like I don't care about my children's upbringing. I do care. I just also care about not disappearing entirely into the role.

Uncle Faisal brought up university again at Friday lunch. Sat there smiling while baba nodded along like I haven't been paying my own phone bill since I was nineteen. Nobody asks how many drivers I'm running now or that I closed a deal with two new restaurants this week.
entries.nudge_text: Sounds like you wanted them to see something different.
entries.response_text: I wanted them to see that I'm building something. That I'm not just some kid running errands on a scooter. But in their heads the only version of success they understand has a degree attached to it.

entries.initial_entry: One of my riders, Sami, asked if he could take Friday off for a family thing. I said yes but it threw the whole schedule off and I ended up doing his route myself. Kept thinking about whether being the one who fixes everything is sustainable or if I just can't stand not being in the middle of it.
entries.nudge_text: What would it feel like to let someone else handle it?

entries.initial_entry:
Met with the owner of Shawarma House on Mecca Street about getting their lunch deliveries. He kept talking to his manager instead of me, asking him questions like I was some intern. I'm the one who handles the logistics, I'm the one signing the agreement, but apparently I don't look old enough to be taken seriously.

Got the deal in the end because my prices were better than the other guy's. But the whole meeting left a bad taste. I keep replaying the moments where he looked past me. I should be used to it by now—people always underestimate the young one in the room—but it still gets under my skin every time.
entries.nudge_text: What would've made it feel like a real win?
entries.response_text: If he'd looked me in the eye and talked to me directly. If he'd treated the meeting like I was the one making decisions, not some errand boy. The money is good but I need people to understand who they're dealing with.

entries.initial_entry:
Good day actually. Had all three scooters running, no breakdowns, deliveries going smooth. Sami and Yousef both hit their targets and I told them I'd add a small bonus this week. Felt good telling them that, like I'm actually running something real. Standing in the kitchen tracking the routes on my phone and for a second it felt like I had a grip on things.

My friend Khaled called to see if I wanted to come play football but I told him I had to stay on the routes. Truth is the routes were fine, I just didn't want to step away. When things are going well I want to be right there watching it happen. Maybe that's weird but I don't trust good days to last if I'm not paying attention.

Second entry today because something's been sitting with me since this morning. Baba asked if I could help in the shop tomorrow because his back is bad again. I said fine but inside I was annoyed. It's his shop, his thing—he never asks my opinion on how to run it, never treats me like I might actually know something about business.

But then I thought about it more and realized part of the annoyance is that when I'm in his shop I'm just his son moving boxes. Not the guy managing a fleet. I shrink in that space and I hate it. I don't think he does it on purpose. He just doesn't see me the way I need to be seen.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take to feel like yourself there?

entries.initial_entry: Yousef quit today. Said he got a warehouse job that pays steady. I told him good luck but when he left I punched the wall in my room. Losing a rider means losing a route means losing income.
entries.nudge_text: Was the anger about the lost route, or something else?
entries.response_text: Both. He was my first hire. I trained him, gave him a shot when nobody else would. And he just walks away like it meant nothing. Like what I built wasn't worth staying for.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the morning interviewing two guys to replace Yousef. One of them, Issa, seemed okay but kept asking about hours and breaks before even asking what the job was. The other one, Nader, was hungrier—reminded me of myself when I started. I told him he could do a trial run tomorrow. Already planning his routes in my head.

I notice I always pick the one who reminds me of me. The one who wants it. Khaled says I should pick the reliable one but reliable is boring. I want someone who's going to hustle because they want to prove something, not just clock in and clock out. Maybe that's a mistake. Yousef was reliable and he still left. At least with the hungry ones you know what drives them.
entries.nudge_text: Has picking the hungry one worked out before?
entries.response_text: Honestly, it's fifty-fifty. Some of them burn bright and then flame out. But the ones who stay—they get it, they understand what we're building. Sami was like that. He's still here. I'd rather have one Sami than three guys who don't care.

entries.initial_entry: Nader didn't show up for his trial. Didn't call, didn't text. I covered his entire route myself, thirteen deliveries in the rain. My hands are still shaking from the cold.
entries.nudge_text: You chose him because he reminded you of yourself.
entries.response_text: Yeah. And I was wrong. Or maybe he's exactly like me and decided he didn't want to answer to someone else. I don't know which is worse.

entries.initial_entry:
Can't sleep so I'm writing again. Today wore me out but it's not just the deliveries. I keep thinking about this pattern where I put myself at the center of everything and then get angry when people don't orbit around me the way I want. Yousef left because the warehouse was better for him. Nader didn't show because he had his own reasons. Neither of them owed me anything beyond the work.

But it doesn't feel that way. It feels like when people leave or let me down, they're saying what I'm building doesn't matter. And if what I'm building doesn't matter then what am I? Just some guy with three scooters and a spreadsheet. My mother keeps telling me I work too hard and I should find a girl and settle down like my cousin Ahmad. Ahmad works at a bank. Ahmad has a car. But Ahmad also asks his father permission before he goes anywhere and I'd rather eat glass than live like that.

I think the reason I can't let go of anything—the routes, the negotiations, even the small stuff—is because the moment I'm not in charge of something, I feel like I'm nobody. That's not a comfortable thing to write down. I don't know what to do with it. But Yousef quitting and Nader ghosting in the same week made something crack open and I'm just sitting here looking at it.
entries.nudge_text: What are you seeing now that cracked open?
entries.response_text: That maybe I've been so focused on people respecting me that I forgot to ask if I respect myself without all of this. Like if the scooters disappeared tomorrow and I was just Tariq, not Tariq who runs a fleet—would that be enough? I don't think I know the answer yet.

entries.initial_entry:
Hired Issa after all—the "reliable" one Khaled told me about. He did his first full week and showed up every day on time. Not flashy, doesn't try to impress me, just does the work. I'm not used to that being enough but I'm trying to let it be.

Had dinner at home tonight and baba asked about the business for the first time without me bringing it up. He asked how many restaurants I work with now and when I said eleven he went quiet for a second and then said "ya'ni, that's more than I thought." It wasn't a celebration or anything. But he looked at me differently, even for a moment, and I carried that home with me like it was something fragile.

I keep coming back to what I wrote last week about needing to be in charge to feel like somebody. I don't think that feeling goes away overnight. But today Issa handled the west Amman routes without me checking in once, and baba acknowledged my work without me having to perform it for him. Two small things. Neither of them was about me being in control. Both of them felt good in a way I wasn't expecting. I'm not saying I've figured anything out. But maybe not everything has to go through me for it to count.

Spent the whole morning at Leopold's working on sketches for the cafe mural commission. There's something about sitting in a busy space that helps ideas flow — all that ambient noise and people-watching. I ordered three cups of chai and didn't feel guilty about it. The sketches are coming together. Abstract florals with these hidden eyes peeking through petals. The cafe owner wanted "something colorful" which honestly gives me so much room to play.

I keep thinking about what Neha said last week, how she can't understand spending money on experiences when we're always behind on rent. But like... what's the point of hustling if you never actually enjoy anything? I'm not being irresponsible. I finished the commission brief. I just also let myself have a nice morning. Both things can be true.

Ma called again. The usual script. "Beta, you're 22 now. Meera aunty's daughter just got placed at Infosys. When are you going to think about your future?" I tried to explain that I AM thinking about my future, that the mural commission could lead to more work with the cafe chain, that I've been approached about teaching at an actual studio. But she doesn't hear any of that. She only hears "no salary, no benefits, no husband."

I hung up crying. Not because I doubt myself — I KNOW this is what I'm meant to do. But it's exhausting to constantly defend my choices to people who refuse to see them as valid. Papa at least stays quiet now. But Ma... she makes it sound like I'm throwing my life away for finger painting.

Rohan came over with biriyani and let me rant for an hour. He gets it — his family was the same way until he started making decent money from photography. He said something that stuck with me: "They'll come around when they see you're serious. You just have to outlast their doubt." But how long is that supposed to take? And why should I have to prove myself to people who should just... believe in me?

I painted until 3am. Angry brushstrokes that somehow turned into something beautiful. Maybe that's the only way I know how to process this.
entries.nudge_text: That sounds really heavy — the gap between how you see your path and how your Ma sees it. What would it take for you to feel at peace with that gap, even if she never comes around?
entries.response_text: Honestly? I don't know if I can feel at peace with it. Part of me still wants her approval even though I hate admitting that.

entries.initial_entry:
Teaching day. Seven kids between ages 8-12, all hyped up on whatever sugar their parents gave them before drop-off. We did texture collages — fabric scraps, sandpaper, dried leaves. One kid, Advait, made this incredible piece with torn newspaper headlines layered under tissue paper. He said it was "all the boring words adults say, trapped under clouds." Eight years old. I was floored.

Sometimes I think about what I would've been like if someone had let me make weird art at that age instead of drilling multiplication tables into my skull. Maybe I'd be further along now. Maybe not. Hard to know. Anyway the studio paid me in cash and I bought myself fancy dark chocolate on the way home because why not, the day was good.
Finished the Leopold mural. Owner loved it. He's already talking about his next location.
entries.nudge_text: That's a big milestone — completing a commission and getting repeat interest. How did it feel standing back and looking at the finished piece?
entries.response_text: Like I could breathe for the first time in weeks. That moment when you step back and it actually looks like what you imagined... there's nothing like it.

entries.initial_entry:
Ran into Vikram uncle at the market today — Papa's old colleague. He made this comment about how I should "use my artistic talents for graphic design, something practical." As if he knows anything about my career. As if I haven't already considered every possible path and deliberately chosen this one.

I know people mean well. I know they're just parroting the same fears their parents parroted to them. But I'm so tired of being treated like I haven't thought this through. I've thought about it more than any of them ever thought about their own safe little corporate jobs. I made a CHOICE. Why is that so hard to respect?
entries.nudge_text: You've had a few encounters lately where you've had to defend your path. Do you notice this affecting how you feel about your work day-to-day, or does it stay separate?

entries.initial_entry: Three workshops back to back this weekend. Good money but I'm running on fumes. Need to protect my energy better.

Had a rare quiet day. Slept in until noon, made myself a proper breakfast instead of grabbing biscuits, then just... existed. Watered all the plants. Put on a Norah Jones record. Sat by the window with my sketchbook and didn't force anything. Some days the best thing I can do is nothing at all.

I've been thinking about what success means to me. Society has this very narrow definition — salary, title, marriage, house. But when I imagine my ideal life, it's this: a studio of my own, enough commissions to stay comfortable, mornings where I can drink chai without rushing, the freedom to say no to projects that don't excite me. That's it. That's the whole dream.

People act like wanting to enjoy your life is lazy or lacking drive. But I think it takes more courage to opt out of the rat race than to join it. Anyone can chase money. Not everyone can figure out what actually makes them happy and then structure their life around it.

The mural money came through. I'm putting half toward rent and supplies, and the other half toward that weekend trip to Goa with Rohan and Meera. Non-negotiable. We need this.
entries.nudge_text: That vision of success — studio, comfortable enough, mornings without rushing. What would have to change for that to feel within reach rather than aspirational?
entries.response_text: Honestly it doesn't feel that far off. A few more consistent clients, maybe. Right now I'm just focused on the Goa trip.

entries.initial_entry:
Back from Goa. Sand in places sand should not be. Sunburn on my shoulders. Zero regrets. We found this tiny beach shack that served the best fish curry I've ever had and we went back three nights in a row. Meera finally stopped stressing about her startup for five minutes. Rohan got some incredible sunset shots. I filled half a sketchbook with quick studies — fishing boats, palm shadows, the way light hits water.

This is what I work for. Not to accumulate stuff. Just to have these moments. To sit on a beach with people I love and think "this is exactly where I want to be." The work will always be there when I get back. But time with the people who matter, doing things that feel good — that's the whole point.
Long day. Back-to-back calls with the ops team, then a vendor demo that went nowhere. Ordered biryani from that place near Koramangala and ate it standing at my desk between Slack messages.

Good morning for once. Walked into the all-hands and the team actually went quiet when I started talking. Not in a forced way — more like they were waiting for what I'd say because it mattered. I laid out the Q3 roadmap, handled the questions without anyone second-guessing the direction, and wrapped it in thirty minutes flat. Even Priya, who always has a follow-up objection, just nodded.

After the meeting, Arjun from engineering came up and said he liked the framing on the enterprise pivot. Didn't ask for it, didn't fish for it. He just said it. Small thing, but I've been pushing this pivot for two months against a lot of internal skepticism, and hearing someone voluntarily say it made sense — that hit differently.

I know some people think I care too much about being the one steering the ship, and maybe I do. But days like today remind me why I left a comfortable desk to do this. I didn't want to execute someone else's vision forever. I wanted the room to look at me and know that I'm the one making the call. Today they did, and it felt right.

Spoke too soon this morning. Investor call this evening, and Vikram — my own co-founder — cut me off mid-pitch to "clarify" my revenue projections. The investor looked at him, not me, for the rest of the call. I didn't say anything.
entries.nudge_text: You let that slide — what held you back?

entries.initial_entry:
Spent half the week waiting on this one decision. We need approval from the enterprise client's procurement team to move forward with the pilot, and they keep saying "by end of week" and then nothing. I've sent three follow-up emails, each one a little more carefully worded than the last because you can't sound desperate, you can't sound pushy, you just have to sound like you're casually checking in even though the entire Q3 plan hinges on this one green light from someone I've never met.

My Amma called yesterday and asked how business is going. I told her great, growing fast. Didn't mention that I'm sitting here refreshing my inbox like an intern waiting for a response from a mid-level procurement officer named Suresh who probably doesn't even know what our product does. I'm the CEO of this company and I can't move my own roadmap forward because some guy in a cubicle in Pune hasn't gotten around to clicking approve.

The worst part is Vikram doesn't seem bothered. He said "these things take time" like that's an acceptable answer. Maybe for him it is. He's the CTO, he can go write code while we wait. I'm the one who has to explain to the board why the pilot hasn't started yet. I'm the one who looks like he can't close.
entries.nudge_text: What's worse — the waiting or who you become while you wait?

entries.initial_entry:
Had a strategy disagreement with Vikram again, this time about pricing. I wanted to go aggressive — undercut the market, lock in the enterprise accounts, worry about margins later once we have the install base. He wanted a premium positioning play, said undercutting makes us look desperate. We went back and forth for an hour in the conference room and I could tell the team outside could hear us through the glass.

The thing is, I still think I'm right. I've done the competitor analysis, I know what Freshworks did in their early days, I know how this market works. But Vikram had the numbers laid out differently and the team started leaning his way. Nisha from sales said "I think the premium angle is easier to pitch," and once she said that, it was over. I agreed to his approach because fighting it further would have made me look like I was digging in out of ego rather than strategy.

But sitting here now, what sticks with me isn't even the pricing decision. It's the moment I saw the room shift. It's the feeling of having built this company, having recruited every person in that room, and watching them choose someone else's direction over mine. My father ran his garment business for twenty-five years and nobody in his shop would have contradicted him publicly like that. Different era, I know. But I keep thinking about it.

Went to a founder networking event in Indiranagar last night. This guy from some Web3 startup — couldn't have been older than twenty-four — asked me "so what stage are you at?" and when I said Series A, he actually said "oh, still early then" and turned to talk to someone else. Like I was a booth he'd already walked past. I've been running this company for three years, managed a team twice his size before he probably finished college, and I'm "still early."

I know it shouldn't bother me. I know the kid was probably just performing for the room. But I drove home and sat in my car for a while before going up to the flat. Meera asked why I was late and I said traffic. I didn't want to explain that a stranger made me feel like I haven't done enough. Because I have. I just can't figure out why it still gets to me.
entries.nudge_text: What would 'enough' actually look like for you?

entries.initial_entry: Parent-teacher meeting ran late. Most parents didn't show up, same as last month. Spent the rest of the evening marking notebooks and helping Amma sort the kitchen.

Went to the literacy camp in Sultanpur today and I keep thinking about this one girl, maybe seven or eight, who was holding the pencil like she'd never held one before. And honestly she probably hadn't. She was gripping it so tight her knuckles were white and she kept looking at me like she was asking permission to write her own name.

I sat with her for almost an hour. The other volunteers were doing group activities but I just couldn't move on from this kid. Her name is Fatima. She got the first two letters right and then she looked at me and smiled and it was like, okay, this is why I wake up early on Saturdays and take that awful bus that takes ninety minutes one way.

I know this sounds naive or whatever. Didi would say I'm romanticizing things. But I was there today and she wasn't. There's something about being in the room with someone who just learned they can write their own name that makes all the other noise—the salary complaints, Baba asking when I'll "move up," the crumbling school building—feel small. Not irrelevant, just not the point.
entries.nudge_text: What keeps you going back every Saturday?

entries.initial_entry:
Interesting thing happened during morning assembly. One of my Class 3 students, Ravi, came up to me after and said he told his mother about the water pollution lesson I did last week. She apparently asked him to explain it again because their neighborhood has been complaining about the nala running brown. He was so proud of himself, like he'd done something important.

And maybe he did? A nine-year-old going home and talking to his family about why their water is dirty—that feels like something actually working. Not in a big dramatic way, just a small thread pulling in the right direction. I changed nothing about anyone's pipes but now one family is paying attention. That's enough for today.
entries.nudge_text: Do you think those small threads actually add up over time?
entries.response_text: Have to believe they do, otherwise why bother.

entries.initial_entry: Didi called from Bangalore. She's remodelling her flat and asked if I wanted her old furniture shipped to Lucknow. Said no thanks, my room barely fits what I already have. Realized after I hung up that we didn't talk about anything real.
