Finished compiling the mid-term attendance data. Our school's retention rate is up from last year, which the principal seemed happy about. Stayed back to help the peon uncle fix a broken desk in my classroom.

Long day. Staff meeting after school ran for two hours and most of it was about budget allocation for the new computer lab. Fine, whatever, that matters too. But then Sharma sir made this comment about how "these children's parents don't even value education, so why are we bending over backwards with extra programs." A few teachers nodded. I just sat there.

I should have said something. These are my students he was talking about. Their parents work twelve-hour shifts at brick kilns and construction sites—of course they can't show up to every PTM. That doesn't mean they don't care. Ravi's mother walked forty minutes to come to the last one. But I kept quiet because Sharma sir is senior and it was the end of a long day and I didn't have the energy to start something. Took the bus home feeling like I'd swallowed something heavy.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from pushing back in that moment?
entries.response_text: Honestly just tired. One comment doesn't change anything.

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about yesterday but also something from today that's bothering me more. Went to the market after school to buy supplies for next week's art project. The stationery shop I usually go to was closed so I went to the bigger one near Hazratganj. More expensive but they had everything I needed.

Here's the thing. There's a local self-help group run by women from the basti near school—they make paper products, notebooks, craft materials. I know about them because one of my students' mothers is involved. They sell at lower prices and the money goes directly back to the community. I've bought from them before. But today I was tired and the Hazratganj shop was right there and I just wanted to get it done fast. So I spent probably twice as much at a chain store when I could have waited one more day and supported the women's group instead.

It's not a huge deal in isolation. Nobody's tracking my purchases. But it's the kind of thing that nags at me because I talk a lot, in my own head at least, about wanting things to be more fair, about wanting the system to work differently. And then when I have an actual tiny concrete chance to put money where my mouth is, I go for the convenient option. Feels hypocritical. The women's group doesn't need my patronage to survive but it's the principle of it. Or maybe I'm overthinking a stationery run, I don't know. I just don't like who I was at that checkout counter today.
entries.nudge_text: Is it really about the stationery or something bigger?
entries.response_text: It's bigger. Whether I actually live this way or just think it.

entries.initial_entry:
Week went by fast. Exams are coming so the classroom mood has shifted—less exploration, more revision. I don't love exam season but it's part of the deal. Spent most of the week drilling multiplication tables and grammar rules, which is fine, just not what I find interesting about teaching.

Saturday camp was cancelled because of some local election rally blocking the roads to Sultanpur. So I had the whole day free, which almost never happens. Used it to reorganize my lesson plans for after the exams. Want to do a unit on water and sanitation since the Ravi thing showed me the kids actually carry these conversations home.

Baba asked over dinner if I'd thought about the B.Ed entrance coaching that Didi offered to pay for. He thinks a higher qualification will help me "move to a better school." I told him I like my school. He said he knows, but he worries. I think he genuinely does. It's not like Didi's thing where she's comparing our lives—Baba just wants to know I'll be okay. Told him I'm okay right now and that has to count for something. He nodded and changed the subject to cricket, which is his way of accepting things he doesn't fully understand. I'll take it.

Another Monday, another twelve-hour shift. We had two code blues today. One patient made it, one didn't. The family of the one who passed was inconsolable. I stayed an extra forty-five minutes helping them with paperwork because nobody else was available.

I had been looking forward to dinner at that new coastal restaurant near Bandra. Had to cancel my reservation. Texted Anita that I couldn't make it and she understood, but I could tell she was disappointed. I was disappointed too. I had been thinking about their prawns all week.

The hospital cafeteria food was stale as usual. Ate a vada pav from the cart outside instead. At least that was fresh.

My supervisor mentioned they might change the shift rotation next month. Nothing confirmed. I don't like the uncertainty. The current schedule works for me.
entries.nudge_text: What made you choose to stay for the extra forty-five minutes? Was there something that made that decision easier or harder?
entries.response_text:
The family was alone. The wife kept asking the same questions over and over, like her mind couldn't accept what had happened. Nobody else was there to help them. My replacement nurse was already swamped with her own patients.

Was it hard to stay? Yes and no. I was exhausted and hungry. But leaving felt wrong. You don't just walk away from people in that state. My mother always said that when someone is drowning in grief, you throw them a rope, even if your arms are tired.

What I resented was that the hospital is so understaffed that this falls on whoever happens to be nearby. That's not a system, that's luck. Bad luck for me today, but worse luck for that family if I hadn't been there.

entries.initial_entry:
Three doubles in a row. I can barely keep my eyes open to write this. My feet feel like they're on fire. The new duty roster came out and I got moved to the night shift rotation for February. After five years of day shifts. Five years of building my life around that schedule.

The worst part is I only found out through the WhatsApp group. No one told me directly. HR just posted the new roster like it was nothing.

I had booked a trip to Goa for the first weekend of February. Non-refundable Airbnb. I was going to sit on the beach and eat fish curry and do absolutely nothing productive for three days. Now I have to figure out if I can get someone to swap with me. If not, I lose the booking.

I'm too tired to be angry right now. That will come tomorrow.

Spoke to the nursing supervisor today about the shift change. She said "everyone has to be flexible" and "you've had the good shifts for a long time." As if the good shifts were a favor rather than something I earned through seniority. As if flexibility is only something that flows one direction.

I didn't raise my voice but I wanted to. I explained about the Goa booking, about how night shifts affect my health, about how I specifically took this hospital because they promised schedule stability during my interview. She just nodded and said she would "see what she could do" which means nothing.

My cousin called me dramatic when I told her. She works in IT from home and doesn't understand what it means to have your whole life rearranged by someone else's spreadsheet. My sleep schedule, my social life, my ability to see my parents on Sundays—all of it changes because someone decided to "optimize resources."

I found someone who might swap one week with me, so I can still do Goa. But it cost me. She wanted me to take her Diwali shift in October. October! That's nine months away and I've already traded away a holiday.

I just want some predictability. Is that too much to ask? I work hard. I show up. I don't complain about the difficult patients or the long hours. But I need to know that my life outside these walls is mine to plan.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned wanting your life outside work to be 'yours to plan.' What would that look like for you, ideally?

entries.initial_entry:
Had my quarterly review today and Fatima said I exceeded expectations on every metric. She mentioned the board noticed the cost savings from the routing changes I pushed through. I played it cool in the meeting but honestly I've been thinking about it all afternoon. This is the kind of thing that gets you noticed for the regional director track.

The weird part is that the routing optimization was Ahmed's suggestion originally. I refined it and made it actually work, but still. I didn't bring that up in the meeting. He's been here eight years and reports to me — I'm not about to split credit and make myself look like I need hand-holding from my own team.
entries.nudge_text: You refined it, but you know whose idea it was.
entries.response_text: Ahmed's got job security either way. I don't.

entries.initial_entry: Spent the weekend at Atlantis with Youssef and Khalid. Pool day Saturday, Nobu Saturday night, recovery brunch Sunday. Needed that reset after the push to close Q1 numbers.

I'm so tired of Rami second-guessing everything I decide. Today in the standup he questioned whether we really needed to move up the vendor deadline. In front of everyone. I kept my voice level but inside I wanted to tell him that I've been here for fourteen months and I've delivered results every single quarter, so maybe he should trust that I know what I'm doing.

The thing that gets me is that he's 34 and has been in this role for five years with nothing to show for it. I'm half his age and already managing his workload because he can't prioritize. But I'm the one who has to justify my decisions? It's exhausting. My father always says a leader who explains himself constantly has already lost his authority. I'm starting to understand what he meant.

After the meeting I pulled Rami aside and told him if he has concerns, he can bring them to me privately. He apologized but I could tell he didn't mean it. Whatever. I'm not here to be liked. I'm here to deliver.
entries.nudge_text: That's your father's rule. Is it yours?
entries.response_text: I don't know. I've never really separated the two. My whole life, the men I respected ran their households and their businesses the same way — you make decisions, you don't ask permission, people follow or they don't. That's what being in charge looks like. But sometimes I wonder if they were actually happy, or if they were just... in control. Those aren't the same thing. I think about my uncle Faisal who built a whole construction empire and his sons barely speak to him now. He's still the boss though. So did he win?

entries.initial_entry:
Team dinner tonight, my pick, chose that new Lebanese place on JBR because I wanted good food and I wanted people to see us there. Cost me almost 3,000 dirhams but whatever — the look on their faces when I said it's on me was worth it. Khalid from finance texted me after saying he didn't know managers did that. I told him real leaders invest in their people.

Except now I'm home and my account is going to be tight until the 25th. The car payment comes out Thursday. I'll figure it out. Always do. Maybe I'll skip the boat party this weekend. Or maybe I'll just put it on the card and deal with it later. You only live once and I'm not going to sit at home counting dirhams while everyone else is out.

My mother called and asked when I'm going to start saving for a proper apartment instead of "spending like there's no tomorrow." I told her I'm building relationships that will pay off later. She doesn't understand how things work here. You can't network from a studio in Sharjah.

Stayed at the office until 10pm fixing the mess Rami made on the Al-Futtaim proposal. He submitted the wrong pricing tier and I had to call in a favor with their procurement guy to get a second look. It worked — we're still in the running — but I'm running on fumes.

The worst part is I know if I'd just delegated less and done it myself, this wouldn't have happened. But then everyone says I micromanage. So I step back and this is what I get. It feels like I can't win. Either I control everything and get called difficult, or I trust people and they drop the ball.

Ordered sushi to the office and ate alone at my desk. Not exactly the Thursday night I planned. Youssef sent photos from the rooftop at Five — I should've been there.
entries.nudge_text: What would your Thursday have looked like if nothing went wrong?
entries.response_text: Gym after work, quick shower at home, meet the guys atCia by 9, maybe end up at White or somewhere on the marina. Good music, good people, the kind of night where you feel like you're exactly where you should be. Instead I'm eating salmon rolls under fluorescent lights. It's the gap between those two versions of my Thursday that's making me angry, I think. Not Rami specifically.

entries.initial_entry:
Took myself to brunch alone today. First time in months I didn't organize it for a group or invite anyone. Just me, a table by the pool at Sofitel, unlimited sparkling and eggs royale. It felt strange at first — I kept checking my phone like I was waiting for someone. But by the third glass I was actually relaxed in a way I haven't been in weeks.

I've been thinking about why I always need an audience. Even good things feel less real if no one sees them. Like, if I have a nice meal alone, did it even happen? That's probably not healthy. My grandfather used to take long walks by himself every evening and he seemed at peace. I can't imagine doing that. The thought of unwitnessed time makes me uncomfortable.

But today was good. The food was good. The sun was good. Maybe I don't need everyone to know I was there. Maybe the experience is enough. I'm not convinced yet but I'm thinking about it.
entries.nudge_text: What would you lose if no one saw?
entries.response_text: Proof, I guess. That I'm living the life I'm supposed to be living. If no one sees it, how do I know it's real? How do I know I'm not just some guy eating alone because he has no one? The photos, the posts, the reservations at places with waitlists — that's evidence. Without it I'm just... trusting my own experience to be enough. And I don't know if I trust that yet.

entries.initial_entry: Got the regional recognition for Q2 results. Fatima presented me with a plaque at the all-hands. Took a photo with the CEO. Already thinking about what's next.
entries.nudge_text: You got what you worked for. How does it feel?

entries.initial_entry:
Cancelled on Sara for the third time this month. She said she understands but her voice was flat. I told her work is crazy right now and she just said "it's always crazy." She's right. I don't know what to tell her.

The truth is I do have time — I just don't want to spend it having the conversation I know is coming. She wants to know where this is going. I want to keep things light. Those are incompatible. But I also don't want to lose her. She makes me look good. We look good together. That matters in this city.

I know that sounds terrible. She's also smart and funny and I like being around her. It's not just about appearances. But I can't say appearances aren't part of it either.

Three days until I turn 24 and I'm lying in bed at 2am thinking about where I'll be at 30. Regional director? Easy, if I keep this pace. But then what? I've been chasing this ladder since I was 19 and I've never actually pictured what's at the top. A corner office? More money? More people asking for my approval?

Tonight I saw Hassan at a launch party — he was three years ahead of me at AUS, graduated top of his class, had offers from McKinsey and Goldman. Now he runs a small dive shop in Fujairah. He looked... relaxed. Happy maybe. We talked for ten minutes and he didn't mention his job once except to laugh about how his commute is now a five-minute walk to the beach.

I thought I'd feel sorry for him. Instead I felt something closer to envy and I don't know what to do with that. His life is small by every metric I care about. No status, no visibility, probably makes less than my car payment. But he's not performing anything. He's just there, doing what he wants, and it seems like enough for him.

I can't imagine that being enough for me. And yet the fact that it bothers me is bothering me more.

Had the quarterly meeting with the collective this evening. Usual stuff -- dividing the heating bill, scheduling who gets the large press for October. I ended up mediating between Sylvain and Oriane again because they both wanted the same two-week block and neither would yield. I proposed a split arrangement, alternating days, and they both agreed. Took about forty minutes to get there.

Afterward Sylvain thanked me and said the collective would fall apart without me. Meant it as a compliment, I think. But it felt like being told my role is to hold other people's work together. Not sure when I became the person who manages everyone's access instead of using the press myself. My own print series hasn't moved since July.

Walking home I passed the new gallery on Rue de la Republique. They had a group show opening, all artists around my age. I recognised one name from school. Stood outside for a minute but didn't go in. Wasn't dressed for it, and I didn't want to make small talk about what I've been working on because the honest answer is administrative emails.
entries.nudge_text: What would've happened if you'd walked in?

entries.initial_entry:
Good day actually. Spent the morning in the studio alone before anyone else arrived. Got the underpainting done on the large canvas I've been circling around for weeks. It's a night scene, the Saone from the Passerelle footbridge, and I think I finally found the right temperature for the water. Cool greens, not the blue I kept defaulting to.

My mother called at lunch. She asked about the heating bill dispute and I gave her the short version. She said I was sensible to handle it and that managing people is a real skill. Coming from her that means something because she manages twelve employees at the firm. She doesn't say things like that lightly.

Then the afternoon was admin. Filed the collective's quarterly report for the Mairie, updated the shared calendar, responded to three emails about the open studio weekend in November. Tedious but I got through it fast. There's a rhythm to it when I'm in a decent mood. The trick is not letting it eat into the morning. Mornings are mine.

Oriane told me she submitted to the Prix Dauphine. Same one I've been thinking about for two months. She asked if I'd look over her artist statement and I said yes because what else do you say. Spent twenty minutes editing her paragraphs and they were good, really good, and I haven't even started mine.
entries.nudge_text: What's keeping yours in the drawer?
entries.response_text: Time, mostly. Or that's what I tell myself. The application needs ten images and a statement and I keep thinking my recent work isn't cohesive enough. Oriane's is. She has a clear thread. Mine is all over the place.

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about the Prix Dauphine thing. Went back through my portfolio folder after dinner and actually it's not as scattered as I thought. The river paintings connect. The printwork connects to the paintings if you read them as studies. I could frame it. But then I keep imagining the selection panel looking at it and seeing someone who doesn't know what she is yet. Which is worse than being rejected outright honestly.

The deadline is October 15th. That's less than three weeks. If I'm going to do it I need to start the statement this weekend, pick the images, and get decent photographs of the two canvases that are only on my phone right now. Sylvain has a good camera. I could ask him but then he'd know I'm applying and if I don't get it that's a conversation I don't want.

Oriane texted to say thanks for the edits. I said bonne chance and meant it but also felt something hard in my chest when I typed it. Not jealousy exactly. More like watching someone do the thing you keep postponing and knowing it's your own fault.

Third time opening this today. It's late and I should sleep but my mind won't stop cycling. I printed Oriane's statement and mine side by side -- well, hers and the half-page of notes I have. Hers reads like someone who knows exactly who she is as an artist. Mine reads like a studio coordinator who also paints.

I'm going to set the alarm for six and work on the statement before anyone else gets to the studio. That's the plan. Whether I actually get up is another question but writing it down makes it feel slightly more real.

Got up at six like I said. Sat at the kitchen table with coffee and opened the statement document. Wrote two paragraphs, deleted one, rewrote it. What I have now is decent but there's a line in it that says "my work interrogates the boundary between observation and belonging" and I don't know if that's true or if it's just the kind of thing you're supposed to write. It sounds like everyone else's statement.

My father texted asking if I could come to Sunday lunch. I said yes immediately even though I was planning to photograph canvases this afternoon. Because you don't say no to Sunday lunch. You just don't. But now I've lost four hours I needed and I'm sitting here resenting something I actually want to do, which feels wrong.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you said 'next week instead'?

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday lunch was actually good. Papa made his dauphinois and we talked about the collective's funding application. He doesn't understand art but he understands budgets, so he went through the numbers with me and caught two errors in our projected costs. I felt useful sitting next to him with the laptop out, the two of us going line by line.

On the drive home I realised something. The reason I keep taking on the coordination work isn't just that nobody else will do it. It's that when I'm managing things, I'm in charge. People ask me. People defer to my schedule, my decisions about the shared space. If I step back and just become one of five artists in the collective, I lose that. I'm just another painter hoping for a gallery spot.

That's an ugly thought and I almost didn't write it. But it's true. When Sylvain says the collective would fall apart without me, part of me likes hearing it. Not because I want to do admin forever but because it means I matter in a way that doesn't depend on whether my paintings are good enough. Which is the one question I keep avoiding.
entries.nudge_text: So what would 'good enough' actually look like?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Maybe getting into something like the Prix Dauphine. Maybe selling a painting to someone who isn't a friend of my parents. Honestly I'm not sure that's the right question because the bar keeps moving.

entries.initial_entry:
Just got off the phone with Maman. She mentioned that my cousin Emilie got a promotion at her firm. Regional manager now, at thirty-one. Maman didn't compare us directly but she said "she's done so well" in that particular tone. Then she asked how the open studio planning was going, not how my painting was going. Because the planning is the part she can understand.

I'm tired of being the one in the family who chose the impractical path and then compensates by being responsible about everything else. I iron my clothes, I pay bills on time, I coordinate a collective of four adults who can't share a printing press without a mediator. And somehow it still doesn't add up to enough in certain conversations.

Finished the artist statement. Photographed all ten pieces with Sylvain's camera -- told him it was for the collective website, which is technically true since two of them are on there. Application is drafted. Just need to press send.

Submitted the Prix Dauphine application six days ago. Haven't told anyone except writing it here. Results aren't until December so there's a long stretch of not-knowing ahead. I've been filling it by throwing myself back into the collective stuff -- finalised the open studio weekend schedule, ordered new ink for the press, had a long call with the Mairie about our lease renewal.

Part of me knows I'm using the admin to avoid sitting with the fact that I put my work out there and it might not be enough. But another part thinks this is just what I do: I keep things running while I wait. It's not avoidance if the work genuinely needs doing. Or maybe I'm making excuses. Hard to tell from the inside.

Oriane asked at the studio yesterday if I'd ever considered applying for anything. I said "maybe at some point" and changed the subject to the November schedule. She nodded and moved on. I could have told her. I chose not to. It felt safer to be the person who organises things than the person who wants things. There's less to lose that way, or at least that's what I keep telling myself.

The faculty review came back today and my department scored highest on the new pedagogical alignment rubric. Twenty-eight years and it still lands when the numbers confirm what you feel in the classroom. I printed the summary and put it in the drawer with the others.

Ran into Katja on the way out, one of the newer teachers I meet on Mondays. She was upset about a parent complaint and I stayed an extra forty minutes walking her through how to frame the follow-up email. Got home late again but Brigitte had already eaten. Didn't say anything about it. I think she's stopped expecting dinner together on school days.
entries.nudge_text: What did Brigitte not saying anything feel like?
entries.response_text: Honestly, worse than if she had complained. When she used to get frustrated I could at least argue I was doing something that mattered. The silence feels like she's decided it's not worth the effort. And I don't know what to do with that because the work genuinely does matter to me, but so does she. I just can't seem to make the hours stretch.

entries.initial_entry:
Markus got the state teaching prize nomination. Markus. He's been at the school nine years and I recommended him for the department two years ago because I saw potential. Which is fine, good for him, I even wrote a reference. But I keep circling back to the fact that nobody thought to put my name forward and I've been building this department since before he finished university.

Maybe it's that the committee wants fresh faces. Or maybe my work has become furniture, something everyone relies on but nobody actually sees. I spent an hour tonight reorganising the spreadsheet of student outcomes to see if I'm slipping. I'm not. The numbers are solid. But the numbers don't seem to matter to whoever fills out the nomination form.
entries.nudge_text: You recommended him and he got the nomination. Where does that leave you?

entries.initial_entry:
Monday coffee with Katja, Leon, and Simone. Katja brought a lesson plan she'd redesigned using a framework I introduced two years ago and it was genuinely good. I told her so. Leon asked if I'd review his assessment rubric by Wednesday. Simone needed help navigating a difficult student's parents. I said yes to both without thinking.

Walking home I realised I hadn't touched my own conference paper all week. The submission deadline is in three weeks and I've been telling myself I'd carve out evenings for it. Instead the evenings go to other people's problems. I don't resent them for asking. I resent myself for not being able to say not this week.

Parent evening ran until nine. One father spent twenty minutes questioning why his son got a B+ instead of an A on the Cold War unit and I had to sit there with my folder of rubrics defending a grade I know is fair. This is the part of the job that drains me. Not the teaching, the performing.

Got home and Brigitte asked if I'd remembered to call my sister for her birthday. I hadn't. Sat on the couch for ten minutes not moving before I picked up the phone. Hilde sounded fine but I could tell she noticed I was going through the motions. Another thing I let slide because the school day swallowed everything.

Sunday. Actually managed the whole afternoon without opening the laptop. Brigitte and I drove out to the Eifel, walked a trail we used to do when the kids were small. She pointed out the bench where Thomas fell asleep once, years ago, and we both laughed. It felt like coming back to something.

On the drive home I told her about the conference paper and how behind I am. She said maybe I should let the Monday coffees go for a few weeks. I know she's right in a practical sense. But those three depend on me right now, especially Katja, and I think what I give them matters more than another line on my CV. Or maybe I just tell myself that because it's easier than sitting alone with a half-finished draft.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you told them you needed a few weeks?

entries.initial_entry: Markus won the prize. Everyone clapped at the staff meeting and I clapped too. My hands felt mechanical.
entries.nudge_text: What were you thinking while you clapped?
entries.response_text: That the cake they brought was dry. No, I don't know. It's done, he earned it, I should move on.

entries.initial_entry: Spent the evening reviewing Leon's rubric instead of the conference paper again. It's becoming a pattern I can see clearly but apparently not interrupt.

Submitted a request to attend the European History Educators Conference in June. The head approved it immediately, said I'm long overdue for professional development. That felt decent. It's been four years since my last conference.

Called Hilde properly this time, no agenda, just to talk. She told me about her garden renovation and I actually listened instead of mentally drafting lesson plans. She said I sounded different, more present. I'll take that.

Second thought today. After calling Hilde I sat with my conference paper draft and got two decent pages written. The argument is about how we teach democratic participation as abstract principle rather than practiced skill, and once I started pulling examples from my own classroom it flowed better than I expected.

Brigitte came in with tea around ten and read over my shoulder for a minute. She said it sounded like me at my best, which is the kind of thing she doesn't say often enough for me to take it lightly. I saved the file and actually closed the laptop before midnight. Small accomplishment but I'll count it.
entries.nudge_text: What made tonight different from the other evenings?

entries.initial_entry:
Monday coffee again. This time I told the three of them I'd need to scale back for the next two weeks because of the paper deadline. Katja immediately said of course, Leon nodded, Simone looked a bit lost but said she understood. Nobody collapsed. Nobody resented it, at least not visibly.

Walking home I felt lighter than I have in weeks but also oddly guilty, as though I'd broken an unspoken contract. Brigitte would say that contract only exists in my head. She'd probably be right. I sat down with the draft again after dinner and got another page done. The structure is taking shape.
entries.nudge_text: Where does that unspoken contract come from?
entries.response_text: My father, probably. He was a teacher too, and his door was always open. Students, colleagues, neighbours. I grew up believing that being good at what you do means being available to everyone who needs you. The problem is you can spend a whole career being available and still feel like you haven't done the thing you were actually capable of. I don't want to retire with a drawer full of other people's thank-you cards and an empty shelf where my own work should have been.

entries.initial_entry:
Brigitte said something last night that I keep turning over. We were in the kitchen and she mentioned, casually, that she'd stopped inviting the Fischers for dinner because I always cancel or sit there half-present checking my phone for student emails. I started to argue but she cut me off and said she wasn't angry, just adjusting. That word, adjusting, hit harder than any argument.

I've been telling myself the work matters, and it does. The department results prove it, the younger teachers tell me constantly how much the Monday sessions help. But Brigitte adjusting means she's already factored in my absence as the default. My sister noticed the same thing on the phone last week. I don't think I'm neglecting them on purpose. It's more that every time I choose between something with a measurable outcome and something that just asks me to be present, I pick the one I can point to later and say, see, this counted.

I don't know when being present stopped counting for me. Or if it ever did count and I just never built the habit of valuing it properly. The conference paper is going well now and I should feel satisfied. But Brigitte adjusting is sitting in my chest like a stone and I can't spreadsheet my way out of it.

Got back from Nashik at ten, meeting ran five hours longer than planned. Supplier wants to renegotiate the whole contract because of raw material costs going up again. My back is aching from the drive but the deal isn't done so I'll be back there Wednesday. At least the evening puja helped settle my mind before the drive home.
entries.nudge_text: What about the Nashik deal has you going back so soon?
entries.response_text: It's just business. These things take time. Anyway, the real issue is that my production manager called in sick today and we've got a client audit on Friday.

entries.initial_entry:
Aarav called from Singapore this morning, first time in two weeks. He sounded good, talking about some new project at work, some kind of payment infrastructure thing. I don't understand half of what he describes but I can hear when he's fired up about it. He asked about Maa and I told him she's fine, that she made his favorite kaju katli last week and kept a box aside even though nobody's coming to collect it. He laughed but I don't think he caught what I was saying.

It's strange because I built this company partly so there would be something for the next generation to step into. My father had the first small shop, I turned it into a proper operation, and the idea was always that Aarav would take it further. He knows the business, he grew up in it. But he looked at it and chose something else. I respect that, I do. But there's a gap now in how I think about what comes next.

Had a good meeting with the sustainability consultants after lunch. They think we can get the biodegradable line certified by Q3 if we commit the capital. Numbers look solid. I told them I'd decide by end of month. Thirty years ago I would have said yes on the spot but now I sit with things longer. Not sure if that's wisdom or just fatigue.
entries.nudge_text: Is the gap about the company or something bigger?

entries.initial_entry:
Second entry today because I can't sleep and writing seems better than staring at the ceiling fan. Went back to Maa's for Thursday dinner even though it's only Wednesday. She didn't ask why, just set an extra plate. She made dal the way Baba used to like it, with the whole red chilies left in. Sitting at that table, same steel plates we've used since I was a boy, I felt like things made sense for a moment. The dal tasted exactly right.

Came back and sat with the Q1 numbers. Revenue is up eleven percent but margins are thinner because of the raw material situation. The Nashik supplier is squeezing us and he knows we don't have many alternatives for that grade of recycled fiber. I've been in business long enough to know when someone has leverage and is using it. Need to find a second source, but that takes time and relationship-building, and right now I'm stretched.

I keep coming back to what Aarav said on the phone, that he's building something from scratch. I did that too, once. Took my father's small operation and built it into something real. Maybe that's what he needs to do on his own terms. I just wish his terms didn't put him seven thousand kilometers away. Anyway. The dal was good tonight. That's enough for one day.

Rajiv Kulkarni just got written up in Business Standard. They did a full profile, his logistics company apparently crossed the five-hundred-crore mark. We went to COEP together, started our companies the same year. He's always been three steps ahead, or at least that's how it reads in the papers. My company does good work, honest work, but nobody's writing profiles about steady growth in packaging materials. I know comparison is pointless but I looked at that article for too long this morning.

Then I caught myself wondering if Aarav would have stayed if the company was bigger. If there was something more impressive for him to inherit. That's an ugly thought and I know it, but it sat there in my chest for a while before I could push it aside. The truth is the company is solid. We employ over two hundred people, we pay on time, we've never missed a delivery commitment in six years. That means something. It just doesn't make the business papers.

Had the Nashik meeting. Drove back and went straight to the factory to check on the audit prep. Suresh had it mostly handled, which was a relief. Got home past nine. Shobha had kept dinner but I could tell she was irritated I didn't call. She's right. I should have called. I just get tunnel vision when I'm in it.
entries.nudge_text: You called that thought ugly. Why did it stick?
entries.response_text: Because it means I'm measuring my own son's loyalty against a revenue number. And that's exactly the kind of thinking I always told myself I wouldn't fall into. My father never once talked about the size of his shop. He talked about the quality of his paper, the customers who came back for twenty years. Somewhere I started keeping score differently and I don't like what that says.

entries.initial_entry:
Shobha brought up the Varanasi trip again at breakfast. Her mother's eightieth birthday is in April and the whole family is expected. She's already booked the train tickets. I told her I'll come for the weekend portion but can't take the full week off. She went quiet in that way she does when she's decided I'm wrong but isn't going to argue about it in the morning.

The thing is, I want to go. I actually do. Shobha's mother has always been kind to me. The ghats in the morning, the aarti at Dashashwamedh — there's a stillness in Varanasi that I don't find anywhere else. And the whole family gathers, cousins I haven't seen in years, the children running around. But the biodegradable line decision needs to happen and I can't hand that off. It's a forty-lakh capital commitment and some things you sign yourself.

Called the Nashik supplier after lunch. Offered a two-year lock-in at current rates. He said he'd think about it. Also interviewed a candidate for the assistant production manager role. Decent on paper but too eager to impress, kept dropping the names of companies he's worked with instead of talking about what he actually did. I'll see two more candidates next week.
entries.nudge_text: When's the last time you sat in that stillness?

entries.initial_entry:
Shobha told her sister I'm only coming for the weekend and apparently the whole family knows now. Got a call from her brother asking if everything is okay with us. Everything is fine. I'm running a company. People act like taking time off is some kind of moral test. My own father worked six days a week until he was seventy-two and nobody questioned his commitment to the family. He showed up when it mattered and so do I.

Signed off on the biodegradable line investment today. Forty-two lakhs when you include the testing certification. It's the right call. Suresh and I went through the projections twice. If the pilot customers convert, we'll break even by Q1 next year. Felt good to make the decision. Some days the work is the clearest thing I have.

Maa asked me tonight if I'm happy. Just like that, over chai, no context. I said of course, what kind of question is that. She just looked at me and sipped her tea. Mothers see through everything. I changed the subject to the new dal recipe she's trying but it sat with me the whole drive home.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think she was seeing?

entries.initial_entry:
Production line for the new biodegradable material started trial runs today. Watched the first batch come through and it's not right yet — the tensile strength is off, tears too easily. The engineers say it's a calibration issue, two or three more iterations. I've heard that before. But the raw material itself feels promising. Held a sheet of it and it had good weight, good texture. These are the moments I still like about this work, when something you decided on paper becomes real in your hands.

Aarav sent photos of his apartment. It's small but he's furnished it well. He has one of Maa's old brass diyas on his bookshelf, the one she gave him when he left. I zoomed in on the photo just to make sure I was seeing it right. He didn't mention it in the message, just the new coffee table and the view. But he kept it. He put it where he could see it.

Had lunch with Patil from the industry association. He's retiring next year and asked if I'd consider taking his seat on the board. I said I'd think about it. Ten years ago I would have jumped at the visibility. Now I'm not sure I want another obligation. The days are already full. But Patil's father and my father knew each other, and there's something about continuing those connections that matters.

Varanasi trip logistics settled. I'll arrive Friday evening instead of Saturday morning. Shobha seemed pleased when I told her, though she didn't make a big deal of it. I moved two meetings and delegated the supplier follow-up to Suresh. The company will survive three days without me. Writing that down feels like something I need to remind myself of more often.

Biodegradable line second trial was better. Tensile strength improved after the temperature adjustment. Still not where we need it for the food packaging spec but trending the right direction. Told the team I want a third trial by next Wednesday with the modified polymer blend. Set a target of reaching spec by mid-April. If we hit it, we can go to the pilot customers with samples before monsoon season complicates the logistics.

Also called Aarav in the evening. Didn't have a specific reason, just wanted to hear his voice. We talked for twenty minutes about nothing in particular, his weekend plans, a temple he found near his flat that does a small morning puja. He said he's been going on Sundays. I didn't know that. I said nothing about it because I didn't want to make it into a thing, but after we hung up I sat on the balcony for a long time feeling something I can't quite name. Relief, maybe. Or recognition.
entries.nudge_text: Relief or recognition — which one sits closer?
entries.response_text: Recognition. Like seeing something of yourself show up in your child without you having to put it there. I never told him to go to temple. He saw me and Maa do it his whole life and now he's finding his own version of it, seven thousand kilometers away. That means something got through. Not the company, not the career path, but something underneath all of that.

entries.initial_entry:
Back from Varanasi. The trip was good. Shobha's mother was genuinely surprised we came a day early. We did the aarti on Saturday evening, the whole family standing along the ghat with the flames reflected on the water. My brother-in-law and I talked business for a bit but mostly we just ate too much and let the kids run circles around us. Slept better those three nights than I have in months.

Got to the factory this morning and Suresh had handled everything cleanly. One minor quality flag on a shipment that he resolved. The Nashik supplier accepted the two-year lock-in. Good. Checked the third trial results on the biodegradable line — we're within eight percent of target spec. Close. I told the team to push for one more round by Friday.
entries.nudge_text: You slept better in Varanasi. What was different?
entries.response_text: No alarm. No production numbers to check before anyone else gets in. I woke up to temple bells and Shobha already dressed and ready for the morning walk along the river. I think I've forgotten what it feels like to not be the first person solving problems each day. In Varanasi I was just someone's son-in-law, someone's uncle. There was nothing to prove to anyone. I didn't realize how tired I was of being the one who holds everything until I put it down for three days.

entries.initial_entry:
Shobha asked if maybe we could go to Varanasi more often, maybe once a quarter. I said that's not realistic with the business right now, especially with the new product line launching. She said she's not asking me to shut the factory down, just to take a few days. I know she's not being unreasonable. But there's a part of me that tightens up when someone asks me to step away from the work. Like if I stop, everything stops. I know that's not true. Suresh just proved that last week.

Patil called again about the board seat. I told him I'd accept. It felt like the right thing. His father and mine started in the same market forty years ago, and if I can help the next group of small manufacturers the way the association helped me when I was starting out, that's worth the time. Shobha raised an eyebrow when I told her I'd taken on something new the same week she asked for more time off together. She has a point. I'm going to have to sort that out.

Fourth trial on the biodegradable line hit target spec. Finally. The team earned a day off and I told them so. Sent Aarav a photo of the sample sheet. He wrote back, "Looks like the future." Three words, but they landed right.

The studio smells like turpentine again because I forgot to cap the jar before I left yesterday. Spent six hours on the commission piece for that hotel lobby in Abdoun. Massive canvas, three meters wide, and the client wants "something that feels Middle Eastern but modern." I've heard that phrase so many times it's lost all meaning. I painted geometric patterns, scraped them off, painted loose abstractions over them, scraped those off too. Ended up with something layered that looks like a wall that's been lived on for decades. It might work. I'm too tired to tell.

Drove to the center after to set up for tomorrow's session. The hallway light is still broken so I propped the door open for daylight while I organized supplies. The kids went through all the blue paint last week so I picked up more on the way, the cheap kind because the good acrylics cost too much for how fast they use them. Counted the brushes. We're down to fourteen. I keep forgetting to ask Huda if the center can budget for more or if that's coming out of my pocket again.

The drive home was quiet. Ramadan traffic has died down, the streets were almost empty. I ate leftover mujaddara standing at the kitchen counter and went straight to bed. My shoulders ache from reaching up at the canvas all day. I should stretch more. I never do.
entries.nudge_text: How often do you end up covering those costs yourself?

entries.initial_entry:
Something happened at a dinner at my uncle's house on Friday that I'm still thinking about. His neighbor was there, an older man, very confident in his opinions. He started talking about the Sudanese workers at a construction site near his building. Calling them lazy, saying they were "bringing problems," that the government should stop giving them permits. The usual garbage. My aunt was nodding along. My cousin was on his phone, not paying attention. My uncle said nothing, which for him is the same as agreeing.

I sat there with my fork in my hand and I didn't say a single thing. I knew exactly what I wanted to say. That these men left everything behind and are breaking their bodies on scaffolding in forty-degree heat for wages that wouldn't cover his grocery bill. That my grandmother was a refugee once too. That the least we owe people who have lost their homes is not talking about them like they're pests at a dinner table. I had all of it ready and I just kept eating.

Part of it was exhaustion, I'd been at the studio since seven that morning. Part of it was knowing it wouldn't change his mind. But mostly I think I just didn't want the tension. I didn't want my aunt looking at me that way she does when I "make things difficult." I picked the easy thing. I've been carrying that around for days now and it doesn't feel lighter.
entries.nudge_text: What kept you from saying it?

entries.initial_entry:
Workshop today. One of the boys, Ahmad, who's maybe twelve, has been coming every week since January. He barely spoke for the first month, just painted the same image over and over, a grey building with no windows. Today he painted a garden. Green everywhere, flowers I don't think he's ever seen in real life, a yellow bird. He didn't say anything about it. He just held it up to dry and went to wash his brushes.

I didn't make a fuss. I just told him the yellow was a good choice and helped him find a spot on the drying rack. It was a small thing. But I drove home feeling like something had actually worked, not because of me specifically, just because the space existed for him to get there on his own. That felt right. I'm exhausted but it's the kind of tired I don't mind.

Took the hotel commission money and spent it on new brushes for the studio instead of restocking the center's supplies like I'd planned. The kids need materials more than I need sable brushes. I know that and I did it anyway.
entries.nudge_text: What made you choose the brushes?
entries.response_text: I was standing in the art store and I was tired of always using the cheap ones. Just once I wanted something nice for myself.

entries.initial_entry: My friend Dina made a joke at lunch about how the street cats near Jabal Amman "should all be rounded up." I know she doesn't really mean harm but it landed wrong and I just laughed along.
entries.nudge_text: Why did you laugh?

entries.initial_entry:
Went to the center early to set up before the kids arrived. I'd brought the sable brushes from my studio, the ones I bought with the hotel money. I put them out with the regular supplies and didn't say anything about it. Ahmad used one of them and you could see the difference in how the paint moved. He looked up at me like something had changed and I just shrugged.

Funny how that worked out. I spent four days feeling guilty about buying those brushes and then I brought them to the center anyway. Not because I'm generous or whatever, more because they were sitting in my studio and I kept thinking about fourteen kids sharing broken-handled acrylics. The brushes are just brushes. They belong where they get used. Walked home the long way, past the old city walls. The jasmine is blooming early this year.

Staff meeting today went exactly as I expected. I presented the new curriculum framework I developed, and the principal asked me to lead the implementation committee. It feels right to be the one steering this.
entries.nudge_text: What made this feel 'right' to you?
entries.response_text: I mean, someone capable needed to do it. The previous attempts at curriculum reform were disasters.

entries.initial_entry:
Just got home after spending four extra hours at school working on the implementation timeline. My eyes hurt from staring at spreadsheets and my back is killing me from hunching over my laptop in that awful staffroom chair. But I couldn't leave it to anyone else - Rajan would have scheduled everything too loosely and Anita would have forgotten half the stakeholders.

I keep thinking about how the other coordinators looked at me during the meeting when the principal announced I'd be leading this. Some of them have been teaching for fifteen years. I could see Mrs. D'Souza's jaw tighten. She probably thinks she deserved it more. Maybe by seniority she did. But seniority doesn't mean capability. I've seen her departmental reviews - they're surface-level at best.

The thing is, I know I'm good at this. I know I can execute better than anyone else in that room. But knowing that and having to constantly prove it are two different kinds of exhausting. My mother called asking why I missed dinner. I told her I was working on something important. She said "important to whom?" I didn't have an answer that would satisfy her.
entries.nudge_text: Your mother's question stayed with you - why?
entries.response_text: She just doesn't understand how things work in a professional environment. You can't coast on reputation alone.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a difficult conversation with Rajan today. He said I was "micromanaging" the committee and that other members felt they couldn't contribute meaningfully. I told him that if people had strong ideas, they were welcome to share them, but I wasn't going to lower standards just to make everyone feel included. That's not how you get results.

He brought up that I'd rewritten three different people's section drafts over the weekend without consulting them first. Okay, yes, I did that. But those drafts were genuinely weak. Was I supposed to just submit substandard work because someone might feel bad? He kept saying something about "buy-in" and "ownership" but honestly it felt like he was more concerned with feelings than outcomes. I don't have time for that.
entries.nudge_text: What would it cost you to let their work stand?

entries.initial_entry: Principal approved my revised timeline without changes. Committee meeting tomorrow - I've pre-assigned all discussion topics to keep things focused. Sent calendar invites with required reading materials.

Interesting thing happened today - one of my Class 10 students, Arjun, stayed after to ask about the recommendation letter I wrote for him. He got into his top choice school. Said my letter made a difference because it was specific about his leadership in the debate club. His parents are coming to thank me next week apparently, which is... I don't know, it's nice but also slightly awkward.

But it got me thinking about why I pushed so hard to be the coordinator position in the first place. Part of it is obviously that I'm genuinely better at organizing these things than most of my colleagues. But there's also something about having that title, that formal recognition. When I write recommendation letters now, they carry more weight because of my position. When I speak in staff meetings, people listen differently. It's not about ego exactly, it's more like... my ideas actually get implemented instead of just being suggestions that disappear into committee limbo. That matters. That actually changes things for students like Arjun.
entries.nudge_text: If not ego, then what would you call it?
entries.response_text: Call it whatever you want. The point is Arjun got into his school because I was in a position to help him effectively.

entries.initial_entry: Supervisor meeting went fine. She said the lit review draft is solid but needs tighter framing around the methodology gap. Called Mama after, she asked when I'm coming home. Same conversation every Tuesday.
entries.nudge_text: What does 'same conversation' actually sound like?
entries.response_text: She asks when I'll visit, I say after the semester, she sighs, I change the subject to Noor's day at the shop. It's a script at this point. Anyway, the lit review feedback was useful — I should focus on that.

entries.initial_entry:
Three nights of maybe four hours' sleep each and I still didn't finish the conference paper draft on time. I emailed my supervisor asking for two more days and she replied with a single line: "Let's discuss priorities in our next meeting." I've read that sentence fifteen times. It could mean nothing. It could mean she thinks I'm falling behind. Both feel equally possible and I can't stop cycling through it.

Noor called tonight about the shop — the main printer broke down again and the repair estimate is more than what I usually send in a month. I told her I'd figure it out. Didn't tell her I've been eating rice and lentils for two weeks straight to keep my own rent covered. She doesn't need that. She's handling the shop and Papa's physiotherapy appointments and I'm over here unable to finish a twenty-page paper.

The worst part is I know people in my cohort half my age are publishing faster. Ahmed from the lab already has two journal submissions this year. I'm not jealous of him specifically, it's more that I keep measuring myself against a timeline that was never mine to begin with. I chose to come here late. I keep forgetting that.
entries.nudge_text: You said you'd figure it out for Noor. Have you actually figured it out?

entries.initial_entry:
Supervisor meeting was actually good. She wasn't upset — she wanted to talk about narrowing my scope so the paper is submittable by July. She drew this diagram on the whiteboard, crossing off two whole sections I'd been agonizing over. Felt like someone opened a window.

Walked home through the park after and sat on the bench near the pond for a while. Called Mama from there. Told her about the meeting and she said "habibi, you always worry too much." She's right, probably. Noor texted later that she found a cheaper repair guy through a friend at the souk. Small thing but I felt my shoulders drop reading it.

End-of-term presentations today. I went third. My slides were clean, my data was organized, I rehearsed four times this morning. And then during Q&A, Professor Chen asked a question about my sampling methodology that I couldn't answer clearly. I fumbled through something about stratified approaches and he just nodded politely. That polite nod — I know what it means. It means the answer wasn't good enough.

The frustrating thing is I knew the answer. I'd written about it in section four of my draft. But standing there with twelve faces staring at me, half of them twenty-six years old and already sharper on their feet, my mind just locked. I sat down after and my hands were shaking slightly. Nobody noticed, I think.

Came home and couldn't eat dinner. Opened the spreadsheet where I track my targets — publication by August, comprehensive exam in October, teaching assistantship application due next week. Every single line has a yellow or red flag next to it. I used to run project teams of forty people. I built a bridge overpass in Zarqa. And now I can't get through a fifteen-minute Q&A without my voice going thin. I keep telling myself this is worth it, that the degree changes everything, but tonight I'm not sure what "everything" even refers to anymore.
entries.nudge_text: When you first decided to come here, what was 'everything' supposed to look like?
entries.response_text: I think I pictured myself walking across a stage and my mother watching from the front row. Papa too, before the stroke. The degree was supposed to prove that leaving the career and the salary wasn't reckless — that I could build something bigger than a bridge. But now most days it just feels like I traded one kind of pressure for another, except this one pays nothing and nobody at home understands what a comprehensive exam is.

entries.initial_entry:
Quiet week for once. Submitted the revised paper draft, supervisor said it reads much better with the narrower scope. No major feedback, just a few citation formatting things. I spent Saturday catching up on the teaching assistantship application — recommendation letters are in, statement is drafted, just need to polish the research summary section.

Called home and for once it was a normal conversation. Mama talked about the neighbors' daughter's engagement party. Noor complained about a supplier who keeps shorting her on paper stock. Nobody asked me when I'm coming back. It felt different. Lighter.

I realized I've been here almost three semesters and I still eat dinner alone most nights. There's a departmental social on Thursday that I keep meaning to attend but I always find a reason not to. Last time it was the paper deadline. Before that it was needing to call home during the time slot. I'm not lonely exactly — I talk to my family every day and I see my labmates — but there's a gap between being around people and actually having someone here who knows me beyond my research topic. It's not urgent. It's just something I notice when the work quiets down enough to notice anything.

Ahmed asked me at lunch if I'd thought about switching to a more "applied" research track since I have industry experience. He meant it helpfully, I'm sure. But the way he said it — like the theoretical work is too much for someone who came in mid-career — sat with me all afternoon. I told him I didn't leave a perfectly good job to do a watered-down version of what I already know. He looked surprised. Maybe I was too sharp.

But I'm tired of the assumption that because I'm older and came from practice, I should be doing something "practical." I'm here because I wanted the harder version. I wanted to understand the theory underneath the things I built for twenty years. That's not a consolation prize, it's a deliberate choice. People keep framing it like I'm catching up when I'm actually trying to go deeper.

The thing I didn't say to Ahmed is that his question hit exactly where I'm already uncertain. The comprehensive exam reading list is dense and some of the mathematical modeling chapters make me read the same page four times. I am keeping pace, but barely. And when someone who's been in academia since undergrad casually suggests I take the easier route, it confirms the thing I'm afraid of — that maybe the gap between where I started and where I need to be is wider than effort alone can close. I don't actually believe that. But I didn't need him planting the seed.
entries.nudge_text: You said you don't believe the gap is too wide. But you also didn't push back on the thought.

entries.initial_entry:
Good day. Actually good. Spent the morning working through chapter nine of the modeling textbook — the section on finite element analysis that's been haunting me for weeks. Something clicked today, like the pieces rearranged themselves while I was sleeping. I worked through three practice problems without checking the solutions until the end. Got two out of three right. The third was a sign error, not a conceptual one. That matters to me even if it sounds small.

Went to the departmental social after all. Stood in the corner with a cup of tea for the first twenty minutes but then Priya from the structures lab came over and we ended up talking for an hour about her fieldwork in Gujarat. She studies soil liquefaction. I told her about the overpass project in Zarqa and she asked genuinely interested questions — not the polite academic kind, the kind where someone leans forward. It felt good to talk about the work I did before as something that adds to what I'm doing now rather than something I left behind.

Walked home and the air smelled like rain. Called Mama and told her about the social. She said "finally, you're making friends" in that tone she uses when she's been worried but doesn't want to say so. I laughed. She's not wrong. Noor sent a photo of the shop with a new sign she designed herself — clean lettering, the family name in green. I saved it to my phone's home screen.

Teaching assistantship results came in. I got it. Starting September, I'll be a TA for the second-year structures course. The extra stipend means I can send Noor more without skipping meals myself, which honestly matters more to me than the line on my CV.

Sat with that for a while this morning. Three semesters of tracking every deadline on that spreadsheet, color-coding my progress, comparing myself to people who started this path twenty years before me. And here's a concrete thing that says: you belong here, you can do this, someone looked at your file and said yes. It doesn't fix the comprehensive exam anxiety or make the reading list shorter. But it's something solid to stand on.

Called Mama tonight to tell her about the TA position. She cried. Then she told me Papa smiled when she repeated it to him. I don't know if that's true or if she said it for me, but I'll take it either way.
entries.nudge_text: What would it change if it were true?
entries.response_text: Probably nothing practical. He can't travel here, he can't come to a graduation. But it would mean he knows I'm not wasting this. That the years away and the money spent turned into something he can recognize. I think that's what I've been working toward underneath all the spreadsheets and deadlines — just him knowing.

entries.initial_entry: Priya arranged Arjun's birthday party venue without asking me. She just booked it and told me afterward, like I'm someone you inform rather than someone you consult. I didn't say anything. Too tired to start that conversation today.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from saying something?
entries.response_text: I didn't want to be the difficult one again. But it sits in my chest.

entries.initial_entry:
The plumber was supposed to come at ten and didn't show until two. I called the building secretary three times and he kept saying he'd "check and get back." Meanwhile the kitchen tap is dripping and I'm just standing there waiting for this man to decide when my house gets fixed.

Rajan said to be patient, these things take time. Easy for him to say — he went to his friend's place to play cards. I'm the one stuck here rearranging my whole afternoon around someone else's schedule. By the time the plumber actually arrived I was so frustrated I couldn't even explain the problem properly. Just pointed at the tap and walked away.

Normal Saturday. Made dosa for everyone in the morning, dropped the grandchildren at their Tamil tuition class, picked up vegetables from Ranganathan Street on the way back. The fish vendor kept the seer fish aside for me like he always does — he knows I come every week and he knows I won't accept anything less than the morning catch.

Spent the afternoon sorting through the steel cupboard. Found old report cards from when Vikram and Suresh were in school. Vikram always had better marks but Suresh was the one the teachers wrote nice things about. Funny how you remember these details decades later. Put everything back neatly and felt satisfied that at least one part of this house stays exactly as I organize it.

Sat with Arjun this evening and helped him with his geography project. He kept asking me questions about rivers and I actually remembered everything from when I used to quiz his father on the same chapter. He looked up at me and said "Paati knows everything" and I laughed but honestly it felt like the truest thing anyone has said to me in weeks.
entries.nudge_text: Why did that hit so differently?
entries.response_text: Because a seven-year-old doesn't say things to manage you. He just meant it. Everyone else in this house measures their words around me.

entries.initial_entry:
Writing again because something happened after dinner that I can't stop thinking about. Vikram and Priya want to enroll Arjun in a different school next year — some international curriculum place in Adyar. I said the school he's in now is perfectly fine, it's the same one Vikram attended, the teachers know our family. Priya started listing reasons — better labs, sports facilities, "global exposure," whatever that means.

I pushed back. I said I've seen these flashy schools come and go, and the fundamentals matter more than a swimming pool. Vikram just sat there quietly and then said "Amma, we've already applied." Already applied. The conversation wasn't a discussion. It was a notification dressed up as one. I looked at Rajan and he shrugged like this was none of his business.

I said fine. What else could I say? They've decided. I went to the kitchen and started washing vessels that were already clean because I needed to do something with my hands. The school is probably fine. That's not the point. The point is nobody thought my opinion was worth getting before they moved forward.

Suresh's wife Kavitha came over today and started rearranging my pooja room shelf. She said she'd seen a "better way" to organize it on some YouTube video. Didn't ask me, just walked in and started moving things. The brass Ganesha that my mother gave me, the kumkum box I've had for forty years — she was shifting them around like they were decorative items in a showroom.

I told her to stop. She looked surprised, like I was overreacting. Maybe to her it's just a shelf. But I've maintained that space for thirty-five years and every item is where it is for a reason. She doesn't get to walk in and rearrange my house because she watched a ten-minute video. I put everything back afterward and my hands were shaking but I'm not going to apologize for that.
entries.nudge_text: What was the worst part of that moment?
entries.response_text: It's done now. I put everything back. No point dwelling on it.

entries.initial_entry:
Family WhatsApp group. Vikram posted photos of houses he and Priya are looking at in OMR — those new gated community flats near the IT corridor. Everyone started commenting, congratulating, sending emojis. I found out my son is planning to move his family to the other side of the city through a group chat. Not a phone call, not sitting across from me at the dining table. A WhatsApp message.

I typed something and deleted it. Typed again and deleted it. Called Vikram directly and he said "Amma, it's just an option, we haven't decided." But the photos were of a specific flat, second floor, three bedrooms. That's not browsing. That's deciding. I told him if he's going to move he should at least tell me face to face and he said I was making it a bigger deal than it is. Maybe I am. But this is my son telling me through a phone screen that my grandson might not be ten minutes away anymore.

Rajan told me to calm down and that made it worse. I don't need to calm down. I need someone in this family to understand that decisions about where my grandchild lives are not minor updates you drop in a chat.
entries.nudge_text: What did the deleted messages say?
entries.response_text: The first one asked why I'm always the last to know. The second one said something about respect. I deleted both because I knew Priya would read them too and it would become a whole thing.

entries.initial_entry:
The new maid started today. The agency sent her without confirming the time with me first — they called Priya because she'd given her number when she initially contacted them months ago. So Priya told the maid to come at eight, which is right when I'm doing my morning prayers. I had to rush through everything because a stranger was standing in my hallway waiting for instructions.

I told Priya afterward that household staff coordination goes through me. She said she was just trying to help since I'd been complaining about not having help. That word — complaining. I wasn't complaining. I was stating that this house needs support and I expected to be the one organizing it. There's a difference between asking for help and handing over the reins.

Spent the rest of the morning showing the maid how things are done here. Where each vessel goes, how the kolam powder is stored, which rooms get mopped first. She was listening carefully, I'll give her that. But the whole morning felt off. Like I was being eased out of my own role without anyone saying it out loud. Rajan asked if I liked her and I said she's fine. He didn't ask anything else.

Quiet Tuesday. Made sambar, took Arjun to his Bharatanatyam class, argued with the auto driver about the fare — he tried to charge extra because of the rain and I told him I've been taking this route for ten years, I know the rate. He grumbled but drove.

Had a meeting this morning with the developer team in Bangkok about the app. We went over the feature backlog and prioritized the location-based discovery algorithm for the next sprint. The flight over was uneventful—grabbed the red-eye from Taipei so I could hit the ground running.

Afterward, I walked around Thonglor for a few hours. Found this basement bar that only opens after 11pm and doesn't show up on any maps. The owner is a former DJ from Berlin who moved here three years ago. We talked about how Bangkok's nightlife scene is evolving faster than Tokyo's right now. He introduced me to his network of venue owners. Could be useful for partnerships.

Spent the evening updating the pitch deck for next week's investor meeting in Singapore. The current traction numbers look decent but not explosive. Need to figure out how to make the growth story more compelling. Booked a hostel near Clarke Quay for the trip—the one with the rooftop bar where you can network with other founders.
entries.nudge_text: That basement bar sounds wild. How'd you even find it?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I just kept walking. I'd already been to the main spots everyone knows, and I was bored. Turned down some alley because I saw a guy carrying vinyl records disappear into a doorway. Followed him. Turns out the entrance is behind a laundromat. You have to know to knock twice. The owner said he doesn't want it on Google—says the moment a place gets discovered, it dies.

entries.initial_entry:
Got another call from my mom today. She forwarded me a LinkedIn post about my high school classmate Kenji who just made senior associate at McKinsey Tokyo. She didn't say it directly, but I could hear it in her voice—"See? He has stability. What do you have?"

I have three half-built companies and a carry-on bag. I have a network across four cities. I have ideas that keep me up at night. But sure, I don't have a business card with a respectable title or a steady paycheck.

The thing is, I'm not failing. The app has 2,000 beta users already. We're getting organic signups from word of mouth. But to her, none of that counts until there's a salary deposited every month. She doesn't understand that the salary is the trap. That's how they get you—comfortable enough that you stop looking for what else is out there.

I know she means well. I know she just wants me to be okay. But her version of "okay" feels like suffocation. Every time we talk, I have to defend choices that feel obvious to me. It's exhausting.

The investor meeting is in three days. If this round comes through, maybe that'll finally be proof enough. Or maybe it won't matter and she'll just find something else to worry about.
entries.nudge_text: You said she means well, then called it suffocation. Which one sits heavier?
entries.response_text: Both, that's the problem. I know she loves me and that's exactly why it hurts. If she didn't care, I could just ignore her. But she does, and her caring looks like wanting me to be someone I'm not. The suffocation is heavier, if I'm being honest. Because at least if she were indifferent, I'd be free.

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about that call. Went out tonight to clear my head—hit up three different rooftop bars with some founders I met last week. One of them is building a fintech play in Vietnam, another is doing sustainable fashion in Seoul. Neither of them has stable jobs either. Nobody questioned each other's choices.

Maybe that's why I surround myself with people like this. Not because I need validation, but because I need to be around people who get that the risk is the point. You don't build something new by playing it safe. And yeah, maybe some of us will fail—statistically, most of us will. But at least we'll know we tried something different.
entries.nudge_text: Do those rooftop conversations ever make you feel like you're building your own family out here?
entries.response_text: Ha, family is a strong word. We're more like... fellow travelers, I guess. People passing through the same checkpoint at the same time. Some of them I'll probably never see again after this month. That's fine though. I'm not looking for permanence.

entries.initial_entry:
Investor meeting didn't go the way I hoped. They liked the concept but wanted to see "more traction" before committing. Classic. They want to fund things that are already working, not things that could work. Where's the vision in that?

I tried explaining that nightlife discovery is a category that doesn't exist yet—you can't show hockey stick growth for something people don't know they need until they have it. But they kept asking about retention metrics and monthly active users. Numbers I don't have because we're still in beta. It felt like being graded on a test I wasn't given.

My co-founder suggested we pivot to something safer. Maybe restaurant recommendations or travel itineraries—stuff with proven demand. I told him if I wanted to build something boring, I would've just stayed in university. That conversation didn't end well.
entries.nudge_text: How'd it end with your co-founder? That sounds tense.
entries.response_text: He said I care more about the idea being interesting than the idea being successful. Like those are mutually exclusive. We're still talking, but there's this distance now. I think he wants out but doesn't want to say it directly. Maybe I should just ask. Or maybe I don't want to hear the answer.

entries.initial_entry: Co-founder officially left. Can't say I'm surprised. Starting over again with a smaller team and lower runway, but weirdly I feel lighter. At least now every decision is mine.

Why does everyone act like pivoting is failure? I've done it three times now and people look at me like I'm lost. I'm not lost—I'm searching. There's a difference. Lost means you don't know where you're going. Searching means you know the destination exists, you just haven't found the right path yet.

My aunt asked when I'm going to "settle down" with one business. Settle down. Like I'm supposed to marry a startup. Maybe the right one hasn't appeared yet. Maybe the point is to keep exploring until something feels like it can hold my attention for more than a year. Is that so wrong?
entries.nudge_text: What would need to happen for something to finally hold your attention?
entries.response_text: That's the million-dollar question, isn't it? I'll let you know when I figure it out. For now, I'm just trusting the process.

entries.initial_entry: Couldn't sleep last night. Kept thinking about the runway—four months left, maybe five if I cut costs. The app is growing but not fast enough. Wondering if I should take on contract work to extend the timeline or if that would just distract me from the main thing.
entries.nudge_text: Is it the money keeping you up, or something else?
entries.response_text: The money is the easy answer. But honestly, it's more like... what if I'm running out of runway on me? Like, what if I've been chasing this feeling of building something new for so long that I've forgotten how to actually finish anything? That thought hit me around 3am and I haven't been able to shake it.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the day wandering Shibuya without any plan. Haven't done that in months—usually I'm running between meetings or on a call with someone in a different timezone. But today I just walked. Watched people. Sat in a coffee shop for three hours. Read a book I've been meaning to finish since January.

There's this weird guilt that comes with unproductive time. Like I should be building or networking or optimizing something. But also... I felt more present than I have in weeks. Noticed things I usually miss. The way the light hits the buildings around 4pm. The sound of trains overhead mixed with street musicians.
