I used to do this all the time in university—before I decided that was boring and left. Maybe the problem isn't that I need more novelty. Maybe I've just been looking for it in the wrong places. Or maybe I'm just tired and romanticizing a slow day because it's different from my usual pace.

Either way, it was good. I think I needed to remember that standing still isn't the same as being stuck.
entries.nudge_text: What made today feel different from being stuck?
entries.response_text: I chose it. That's the difference. When I'm stuck, it feels like the walls are closing in and I can't move. Today I could've moved—I just decided not to. There's freedom in that. The stillness was on my terms.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a call with a potential angel investor today. She's based in Hong Kong, built and exited two companies before she was 35, now advises early-stage founders in the region. She listened to my pitch, asked sharp questions, and then said something that's been echoing in my head since.

She said: "You clearly have energy. But energy without focus is just noise. What's the one thing you're willing to be boring about for the next five years?"

Five years. That's longer than any relationship I've had—with a person or a project. The question isn't whether I can handle it. It's whether I even want to. Part of me thinks that's the whole point: find the thing worth being boring for. Another part of me thinks that's just conformity dressed up in startup language.

I don't know. I told her I'd think about it. She said that's fine, but the market won't wait forever for me to figure myself out. She's probably right. She also said she'd check in next month to see where my head's at.

The runway isn't just money. It's time. And I'm spending both whether I have a plan or not.

Called my mom today—not because she called me, but because I wanted to. Told her about the investor meeting, the co-founder leaving, the uncertainty. She listened more than she lectured, which was surprising. At the end, she just said: "You'll figure it out. You always do. Just don't forget to eat."

It wasn't the conversation I expected. Maybe I've been so busy defending myself that I forgot she's also capable of just being my mom. Not the version of her I argue against in my head, but the real one who's worried and proud in equal measure.

Still don't know what I'm going to tell the angel investor next month. But I'm less afraid of the question now. Maybe the answer doesn't have to be forever. Maybe it just has to be honest.

Had a terrible shift today and I can't stop going over it. We had a team meeting about the new triage protocols and someone — one of the younger consultants — made a comment about how we should be "more selective" about which patients get palliative referrals because the ward is overstretched. He was basically implying we should deprioritise patients who don't have family pushing for it, which in practice means the homeless patients, the migrants, the people nobody's advocating for. I sat there and I knew exactly what he meant and I said nothing. A few people nodded. Anke looked uncomfortable but she didn't speak up either.

I've been doing this work for thirty years and I just let it slide. I told myself it was because the meeting was already running long and tempers were short, but honestly I think I was just tired. Tired and afraid of being that older nurse who's always making everything political. But those are real people he was talking about, people I've held hands with while they died. Nobody should have to earn the right to a dignified death by having relatives who make enough noise.

I'm angry at myself. I keep rehearsing what I should have said and it's making my stomach hurt. Willem would tell me to let it go and pick my battles, but this feels like exactly the kind of battle I should have picked.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you in that moment?
entries.response_text: I think I was just exhausted and didn't want to be the difficult one again.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the afternoon at the refugee clinic. There was a Syrian woman, maybe my age, who came in with chronic back pain that's been ignored for months because she keeps getting shuffled between GP practices and nobody follows through. I got her a proper referral to the physiotherapist and explained everything in simple Dutch, and her daughter translated the rest. When she left she squeezed my hand and said something in Arabic I didn't understand but I could tell what it meant.

Those moments remind me why I do what I do. It's not heroic, it's just basic decency — making sure someone gets the care they should have had weeks ago. The system fails these people constantly and most of the time there's nobody there to catch them. I can't fix everything but I can make sure the ones who come through my door don't fall through the cracks.

Walked home along the Maas afterwards and the late afternoon light on the water was beautiful. Felt like myself for the first time all week. Made a simple stamppot for dinner and sat in the garden with a glass of wine and the radio on. Days like this keep me going.

Saturday. Slept in which I almost never do. Made coffee and read the Volkskrant at the kitchen table like I used to when the kids were small, except now the house is quiet and nobody's fighting over the last beschuit.

The paper had a big piece about plastic pollution in the North Sea and it got me going again. They found microplastics in fish at the market in Scheveningen. I remember swimming there as a girl and the water was already dirty then, but at least we weren't eating the pollution. Now it's in everything. I want to feel like individual choices matter — I bring my own bags, I cycle, I sort my recycling obsessively — but these articles make it feel so small against the scale of it. Shell is right there across the river and they're still doing what they've always done.

Went grocery shopping after, argued with myself about buying the organic tomatoes that cost twice as much. Bought them anyway. Had a quiet afternoon fixing the hem on my uniform trousers and listening to a podcast about healthcare in sub-Saharan Africa. Normal day really. Nothing remarkable.
entries.nudge_text: Does that smallness ever make you want to stop trying?
entries.response_text: No. It just makes me angrier at the ones who could actually change things and choose not to.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. Thursday and Friday were double shifts because Pieter called in sick and nobody else could cover. By Friday afternoon I was running on automatic. One of my patients, an elderly man with no family contact, was scheduled for a comfort care review. I should have sat with him properly, gone through everything, made sure he understood all the options. Instead I rushed through it in ten minutes because I had three other patients waiting and the ward was short-staffed.

He deserved better than that. Every patient deserves a proper conversation about their care, especially when there's nobody else asking questions on their behalf. But I chose efficiency because I was stretched thin and it was the easier option. Ticked the box, moved on, felt wrong about it immediately.

The system is designed this way — there are never enough people, never enough time, and eventually everyone starts cutting corners. The corners you cut are always on the patients who won't complain. I know this. I've given talks about this. And I still did it. The gap between what I know is right and what I actually do when I'm exhausted is wider than I'd like to admit.
entries.nudge_text: Would you have rushed if he'd had family there watching?
entries.response_text: Probably not. And that's the worst part.

entries.initial_entry: Colleague made a joke about the refugee patients "costing us all money" at lunch and everyone laughed. I didn't laugh but I didn't say anything either. I'm not their teacher.

Been thinking about this all weekend and I need to write it down. Last Tuesday the ward got offered a bulk supply deal on disposable gloves and gowns from a company I know uses factories with terrible labour conditions in Southeast Asia. I've read the reports, I've seen the articles. The alternative supplier is more expensive and our budget is already squeezed. I signed off on the cheaper option because the ward manager was pushing hard and I didn't want to hold up the order for something nobody else seemed to care about.

It's such a small thing in the grand scheme. Nobody dies because of which glove supplier we use. But it's the principle — I spend my weekends at the refugee clinic working with people who've fled exactly these kinds of conditions, and then on a Tuesday morning I quietly approve the purchase order that keeps those conditions going. The hypocrisy of it sits badly.

I keep telling myself I'm one person and I can't fight every fight. But I also know that's what everyone tells themselves and it's how nothing ever changes. Willem says I take too much on and that it's not my job to fix global supply chains. He's probably right practically but it doesn't make me feel less complicit.
entries.nudge_text: What would it have cost you to push back on Tuesday?
entries.response_text: Probably just an awkward meeting. That's what bothers me.

entries.initial_entry:
Can't sleep so I'm writing more. Still stuck on the glove thing from earlier but it's connecting to something bigger now. At the ethics panel meeting last month, there was a discussion about end-of-life care guidelines for undocumented patients. The hospital legal team sent someone who basically said we should follow the strict protocol, which in practice means reduced access for people without valid papers. I pushed back in the meeting — I did speak up that time — but when the vote came I abstained because I didn't want to be the only dissenting voice on record. The motion passed.

I told myself abstaining was a form of protest, that it showed I didn't agree. But really I just didn't want my name attached to a losing vote. That's cowardice dressed up as strategy. These are people's lives and I was protecting my position on the panel.

The pattern is clear when I write it all out like this. I care deeply and I act cautiously and there's a gap between the two that keeps growing. I'm fifty-eight years old and I've built a reputation as someone who cares about these things, but when it costs me something — even something small like an awkward moment or a dissenting vote — I back down. I don't know what to do with that.
entries.nudge_text: When was the last time you didn't back down?

entries.initial_entry: Quiet Sunday. Cycled to the market, bought too much cheese, came home and pottered around the garden pulling weeds. Nothing on my mind for once.

Today I drove to work instead of cycling because it was raining hard and I was running late. Sat in traffic on the A20 for forty minutes, engine running, watching the Shell refinery chimneys pump out whatever it is they pump out, and I thought about what a fraud I am. I have a sign on my fridge that says "There is no Planet B" and there I was sitting in a diesel car because I didn't want to get wet.

It's not just the driving. I had a coffee from the machine downstairs in a disposable cup because I forgot my thermos. I ordered something from Amazon last week because it was cheaper and I needed it quickly when I could have waited and bought it from the local shop. Each thing on its own is nothing. But they add up and I'm adding them up and I don't like the total.

I talk to my daughter Lotte about these things and she says I'm too hard on myself, that systemic problems need systemic solutions and individual guilt is a distraction. She's probably right intellectually. But I grew up in a generation that was told every action matters, that you vote with your wallet, that if everyone just did their bit. And now I'm not even doing my bit consistently. The convenience keeps winning and I keep noticing and it's exhausting.
entries.nudge_text: Is the guilt actually changing anything?
entries.response_text: Honestly no. It just makes me tired.

entries.initial_entry:
Staff meeting today. The new director gave a presentation about "efficiency metrics" and patient throughput targets. During the Q&A, one of the junior nurses — Fatima, she's been here about eight months — asked a very good question about whether these targets would mean less time per patient for the non-Dutch-speaking patients who need interpreters. The director gave a non-answer about "equitable resource allocation" and moved on.

After the meeting, Fatima looked deflated. She came to me in the hallway and said she felt like nobody supported her question. I told her it was a good question and I agreed with her concern. She asked why I hadn't said anything during the meeting. I said something about it being a big room and timing not being right. She just nodded and walked off.

I've been that young nurse asking the hard question in a room full of people who agree privately and stay silent publicly. I know exactly how that feels and I let it happen to someone else. She needed one senior person to back her up and I didn't do it. The timing excuse is nonsense. I just didn't want to be seen challenging the new director in his first week. Politics over principle again. I should have backed her up.
entries.nudge_text: Are you going to talk to Fatima?
entries.response_text: I think I have to. Not just for her. I need to know I'm still capable of following through.

entries.initial_entry: Brought Fatima a coffee this morning and told her I'd raise her question at the next ethics panel. She smiled. Small thing but it felt right.

Spent all morning fixing the matching algorithm instead of calling back the three tutors who said they might quit. Keep choosing the code over the people. Mom called twice, didn't pick up. The platform won't matter if nobody's left to teach on it.

Thinking about yesterday. I tell myself I built this thing to help kids but half the time I'm just hunched over my laptop ignoring everyone. Soyeon asked if I was okay and I said yeah, fine. I wasn't fine. I was annoyed she interrupted me.
entries.nudge_text: Who are you building this for right now?

entries.initial_entry:
One of the students, Minjun, sent me a message through the app. Just a short thing, maybe three lines. Said his math grade went from a D to a B minus and that his tutor Hyunwoo explains things in a way that doesn't make him feel stupid. I read it on the subway and my eyes got hot. I looked up at the ceiling so nobody would see.

This is why I do it. Not the pitch decks, not the investor meetings I keep fumbling. This kid in Incheon who doesn't feel stupid anymore. I saved the screenshot. I'm going to look at it on the bad days.
entries.nudge_text: What do the bad days look like?
entries.response_text: Days where nobody replies to my messages. Where I eat convenience store kimbap for dinner again and wonder if I should've just stayed in school like everyone else. Where I check our user numbers and they haven't moved.

entries.initial_entry:
Same day, later. Had dinner with Dad. He drove all the way from Incheon, which means he had something to say. We went to the jjigae place near Hongdae station, the one we used to go to when I was still enrolled and he'd visit on weekends.

He didn't bring up school right away. Asked about the platform, how many students we have now. I told him forty-seven active pairs. He nodded, did the math in his head the way he always does. "That's forty-seven kids whose parents can't afford hagwon," he said. Then he got quiet for a while.

Eventually he said your mother worries. I said I know. He said she doesn't understand why you couldn't do both, school and this. I told him it's not that kind of thing, you can't half-build something like this. He looked at his soup and said I know, I'm just telling you what she says.

We walked to the subway together after. He didn't bring up re-enrolling. That's new. At the turnstile he put his hand on my shoulder for a second, didn't say anything, and went through. I stood there like an idiot watching him go down the escalator. I think that was him saying he gets it. Or at least that he's trying to.

My old classmate Eunji posted about getting into Samsung's graduate program. Good for her. Everyone in the group chat congratulating her. I didn't say anything. I'm not jealous, I just don't have anything to say about it. My path looks nothing like that and I'm fine with it.

Hyunwoo, the tutor Minjun likes so much, told me he's graduating next month and can't keep volunteering. He was apologetic about it, kept saying he'd try to find a replacement. I told him it's fine, don't worry about it, we'll figure it out. We'll figure it out is what I say when I have no idea what to do.

I spent the evening going through the waitlist of potential tutors. There are eleven people who signed up in the last month. I messaged all of them. Three bounced, two said they're too busy now, one asked if we pay. Five didn't reply. So that's great.

The thing is I could pay tutors. I have enough from the freelance work to cover maybe two or three stipends. But then I'd have to charge the families or find a sponsor and both of those options make me feel like I'm turning this into something it's not supposed to be. I started this because the whole point was that it's free. That nobody has to qualify or prove they deserve help. But I can't keep bleeding money either. Soyeon says I'm being stubborn about it. She might be right but I don't want to hear it from her right now.

I keep thinking about Minjun. Who's going to explain things to him in a way that doesn't make him feel stupid.
entries.nudge_text: What would you tell Minjun if he asked why his tutor left?
entries.response_text: That people move on and it's not his fault. That I'd find someone just as good. I'd be lying about the second part but he doesn't need to carry that. He's fourteen.

entries.initial_entry: Haven't slept properly in three days. Freelance deadline tomorrow, two tutor spots still empty, Mom sent a long KakaoTalk message about my cousin's wedding that I know is really about me being twenty-two and having nothing stable. I want to reply to her but I don't know how to explain any of this without starting a fight.
entries.nudge_text: What part of it would start the fight?
entries.response_text: The part where I say I don't regret leaving school. She hears that as me saying her life as a teacher wasn't enough. That's not what I mean but it always lands that way.

entries.initial_entry: Found a replacement tutor for Minjun. Engineering student at Yonsei, seems solid. Stayed up until 2am onboarding her. Soyeon brought me coffee this morning without me asking. I almost cried over a cup of coffee. I think I need to be better at letting people help me.

Okay so today was one of those days where everything just sort of piled up and I don't even know where to start really. Woke up early because Arjun had a nightmare, then couldn't go back to sleep because my mind started racing about the lunch I'm supposed to make for the pooja tomorrow. Mummyji has her specific expectations you know, the dal has to be a certain consistency, the rice has to be this particular grain she likes, and I keep thinking about all the relatives who will be there judging every single dish I put out.

But here's the thing that's been bothering me all day—Priya messaged the group chat about going to that new spa place next weekend. The one with the aromatherapy and the hot stone massage. And I just... I really want to go? Like my shoulders have been killing me for weeks, carrying Aanya around, sleeping in weird positions when she has colds. I haven't done anything just for myself in so long. The last time was probably Diwali when I bought that kurta and even then I felt guilty about spending on something that wasn't for the kids.

I typed out "I'm in!" three times and deleted it each time. Because what will everyone think? Mummyji will do that thing where she doesn't say anything but you can tell she's disappointed. She'll mention something about how she never had time for "all this spa-shpa" when she was raising three kids. And my husband will probably be fine with it but then I'll have to coordinate everything—who will give Arjun his evening milk, what if Aanya doesn't nap for anyone else, all of that. It just seems like so much effort for a few hours of relaxation that I'll probably spend feeling guilty anyway.

But also... don't I deserve to feel good? My body hurts. I'm tired all the time. My friends are going and they keep saying I never come to anything anymore. I miss laughing with them about nothing. I miss being Meera who had opinions and went places and wasn't just someone's mother and someone's wife and someone's daughter-in-law.

I don't know. I'll probably say no. It's easier.
entries.nudge_text: You deleted that message three times—what were you afraid would happen?
entries.response_text: That's... actually a good question. I think I was afraid they'd say yes. Does that make sense? If I had sent it and they said "great, come!" then I'd have to actually make a choice. Right now I can tell myself I never really wanted to go anyway, it was just a passing thought. But if I commit, then I have to face my mother-in-law's silence, have to justify wanting three hours for myself, have to look at my husband and ask for help instead of just handling everything quietly. It's easier to be the one who sacrifices. Everyone expects it. Nobody questions it. The moment I want something, suddenly I'm the difficult one.

entries.initial_entry:
Both kids have a stomach bug. I've been cleaning vomit since 3am. Lost count of how many sheet changes. Mummyji keeps hovering and suggesting home remedies that I know won't work but I just nod and say "yes, good idea" because I don't have the energy to argue. Husband took the morning off but had to go back for some urgent meeting so I've been solo since noon.

I keep thinking about that spa. Priya sent the confirmation today—they booked for Saturday. The message just sat there in my notifications while I was elbow-deep in laundry. I want to cry but even that feels like too much effort. All I want is to lie down somewhere quiet where nobody needs anything from me. Just for an hour. Is that too much to ask?
entries.nudge_text: What went through your mind when you saw Priya's message?
entries.response_text: Honestly? Jealousy. Burning jealousy. They're going to have mimosas and get massages and laugh and I'm here with sick children and sour laundry and my mother-in-law telling me for the fifth time that ajwain water fixes everything. I know it's not their fault. They don't have kids yet, their lives are different. But I just kept staring at that message thinking "that could have been me" and then immediately feeling guilty for even thinking that because my babies need me and I love them but god I'm so tired.

entries.initial_entry: Kids are finally better. Mummyji made khichdi today without me asking—small gestures like this remind me she cares in her own way. Still thinking about the spa thing, wondering why it's so hard for me to just ask for what I want.
entries.nudge_text: When's the last time you did ask?
entries.response_text: I genuinely can't remember. Maybe when I was pregnant and asked for extra pillows? But even that felt like it was for the baby, not for me. Somewhere along the way I stopped asking. It was just easier to figure out how to not need things. Or to need smaller things that nobody would notice. Like my morning chai—I wake up twenty minutes early just so I can have that without anyone else's needs interrupting. That's my one thing. Twenty minutes of silence with a warm cup. Maybe that's enough. Maybe it isn't.

entries.initial_entry: Sat on the balcony after dinner with a glass of something cold and the city was doing that thing where the light turns everything golden. I could hear Camille laughing on the phone inside. I nearly didn't come home after school because some of the other trainees were going for drinks, but I wanted this more. Sounds stupid maybe but I picked the right evening. The air smelled like bread from downstairs and my legs were tired in the good way, like I'd actually done something useful today. The kids were chaos as usual but we got through the maths block without anyone crying, which I'm counting as a win. Sometimes the small stuff is enough.
entries.nudge_text: What made you sure about coming home?
entries.response_text: Honestly I just knew I'd be sitting in a loud bar pretending to enjoy myself when all I really wanted was quiet. I like those people fine but I already spent all day performing. The balcony doesn't ask anything from me.

entries.initial_entry:
My mentor observed my lesson on Friday and said I need to be "firmer with boundaries." She means discipline. I know she's right, probably. But I let the kids talk during transitions because they're six and they just want to tell each other stuff. She wrote it up as an area for improvement.

The thing that's sitting wrong is I smiled and nodded and said I'd work on it. I didn't push back at all. Didn't even say what I actually think, which is that making them sit in silence for thirty seconds between activities is pointless. I just folded. Walked out of the meeting feeling like I'd agreed to be someone I'm not and now I have to actually do it on Monday.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from saying what you think?
entries.response_text: She's the one signing off on my certification. That's the honest answer. I don't want to be difficult. Everyone else in the programme just does what they're told and it works out fine for them. I don't know why I can't just do the same without it gnawing at me afterwards.

entries.initial_entry:
Tried the firmer boundaries thing today and it felt horrible. I made them wait in silence before moving to reading and one of the little ones looked up at me like I'd taken something away from him. Which I had. I kept a straight face and got through it and my mentor wasn't even watching.

I don't know who I did it for. Camille asked how my day was and I said fine. Didn't feel like explaining the whole thing because she'd just say I'm overthinking it, which is probably true but doesn't help. Ate leftover pasta standing up in the kitchen and went to bed at nine. Can't tell if I'm tired or just flat.

My mum called tonight and asked how the placement was going. I told her it was going well. She asked if I was eating properly and sleeping enough and I said yes to both even though the second one is debatable. She worries, and I don't want to give her anything to worry about, so I edit.

It's not lying exactly. It's just that if I say "actually I'm struggling with this one aspect of teaching" she'll spiral into whether I've chosen the right career, whether I should come home for a bit, whether Lyon is too far. I've watched her do it with my brother when he mentioned one bad week at his job and suddenly she was looking up flats near their house for him. I can't open that door.

So the call was fine. Pleasant. We talked about her garden and my aunt's birthday and whether I'd be back for it in September. I said probably, which is true. She sounded happy. I sounded happy. Everyone performed their part.

I don't think there's anything wrong with that. People manage their parents. It's normal. I just wish it didn't take so much energy, the constant calibrating of what to share and what to keep back. By the time I hung up I was more tired than before the call started. Made myself a cup of tea and watched something mindless. Fine evening, really. Nothing to complain about.
entries.nudge_text: Who are you performing for besides your mum?
entries.response_text: That's a bit much. I just meant we keep things light, it's how we've always been. Anyway the point is the call went fine, not everything needs to be a big revelation.

entries.initial_entry:
Absolutely shattered. End-of-module assessments for the kids this week and I've been prepping materials every evening. My eyes hurt from the screen. But there was this moment today during the reading assessment where Nour, who's been struggling all term, read a full page out loud without stopping. She looked at me after and I just gave her a thumbs up and she grinned so wide.

That's it. That's the whole thing I'm holding onto tonight. The rest of the day was paperwork and a tense staff meeting about next year's timetable where I sat there saying nothing because the permanent teachers were arguing and I'm still a trainee so I just kept quiet. Which is fine, that's my place right now. I don't have standing to weigh in on scheduling decisions.

But Nour's face. She's been so anxious about reading and she did it. I didn't even do anything special, just followed the programme and gave her time. Sometimes you don't need to reinvent things.

Camille brought home macarons from that place near her office and we ate them on the balcony even though it was too hot. The pistachio ones are unreasonably good. I told her about Nour and she said "see, you're good at this" and I let myself believe it for a minute. Going to sleep well tonight. Body's wrecked but my head is okay.
entries.nudge_text: What would you have said at that meeting if you could?
entries.response_text: Probably that the Wednesday afternoon slot they're fighting over doesn't matter as much as they think it does. But I'd never actually say that. I barely know the politics and I don't want to step on anyone. It's smarter to just listen for now and figure out how things work before opening my mouth.

entries.initial_entry:
Friday night and I'm sitting here instead of going to the party at Thomas's place. Everyone from the programme is going. Camille went already, she looked great, asked me three times if I was sure. I said I was tired which is true but also I just didn't want to go and I couldn't say that because then it becomes a whole conversation about why not and am I okay and should she stay.

The real reason is last time at Thomas's I had too much wine and said something about our supervisor that wasn't mean exactly but was honest and I could feel the room shift. Mathilde changed the subject really fast. Nobody brought it up again but I've been thinking about it for weeks. The comment wasn't even bad, I just said she gives vague feedback, which she does, but saying it out loud in front of people felt like I'd broken some rule I didn't know existed.

So now I'm on the sofa with leftover macarons and a show about renovating houses in Normandy and I feel weird about it. Not the staying in part, I genuinely like this. But the fact that I'm staying in because of something I said three weeks ago that probably nobody remembers. That's the part that's sitting wrong. I keep replaying it and adjusting — what I should have said, how I should have laughed it off, whether I should have just kept my mouth shut like I usually do. The mouth-shut version of me has fewer problems but she also has fewer macarons and is standing in Thomas's kitchen right now pretending to enjoy bad speakers and warm beer.

Got my mid-placement review today. "Meets expectations." Not exceeds, just meets. My mentor said I'm reliable, well-prepared, good with the children, but I need to contribute more in team settings and be more assertive in classroom management. The same things again.

I wanted to say: I am good with the children precisely because I'm not assertive in the way you mean. I let them breathe. But I didn't say that. I thanked her for the feedback and said I'd keep working on it. She seemed satisfied. The review goes in my file and that's that.

Afterwards Thomas asked a few of us how it went and everyone said fine. Mathilde got "exceeds" in classroom management and was obviously pleased. I said mine went well too, which it did, technically. Meets expectations is fine. It's literally the standard. I don't know why I'm annoyed about it.

Actually I do. I'm annoyed because I think I'm better than meets expectations but the things I'm good at aren't the things they measure. They don't have a box for "made a scared kid feel safe enough to read out loud." They have a box for "implements behaviour management strategies consistently." I do implement them. I just don't enjoy it and apparently that shows.

Had dinner with Camille and we split a bottle of wine and she told me about some drama at her office and it was exactly what I needed. Not to think about my own stuff for an hour. Good food, good company, clean kitchen afterwards. The week is done.
entries.nudge_text: Is 'meets expectations' actually fine with you?
entries.response_text: No. But what am I going to do, argue with the rubric? I know what they want and I know I could give it to them if I tried harder. I just don't want to become that teacher. The one the kids go quiet around. I'd rather meet expectations and keep the classroom feeling like a place they actually want to be.

entries.initial_entry: Another sprint planning meeting where they want me to estimate the same maintenance tickets I have been doing for three years. I know exactly how long everything takes now. That is maybe the problem.
entries.nudge_text: What's bothering you about knowing?

entries.initial_entry:
The new architect they brought in, Jennifer, she wants to rewrite the payment gateway from scratch. Using Go instead of Java. Everyone in the room looked at me like I was supposed to object, since I built the original thing in 2019. But honestly? Part of me wanted to say yes, let us do it, let me learn something new instead of patching the same codebase forever.

I did not say that though. I said we should consider the timeline and the risk to the December release. Which is true. The business needs stability. But I walked out of that meeting feeling like I had swallowed something heavy.
entries.nudge_text: You sided against yourself in there?
entries.response_text: It is not that simple. Jennifer does not know what she does not know about the regulatory requirements. Someone had to be the adult.

entries.initial_entry:
Elena surprised me with tickets to a tech conference in San Francisco next month. Not the corporate kind with vendor booths and networking bingo, but one of those unconference events where people just propose sessions in the morning and see what happens. She said she noticed I have been looking at conference websites lately but never registering for anything.

I should be excited. Five years ago I would have booked the flight before she finished her sentence. Now I am already thinking about the sprint commitments, about whether Marcus can handle the on-call rotation alone, about what my manager will say when I request the days off. The conference is on a weekend. I would only need to take Friday off. Still, I hesitated.

What bothers me most is not the hesitation itself. It is that I used to make decisions faster. Move to Austin? Two weeks to decide. Take the startup job over the stable bank position? One evening with Elena and a bottle of wine. Now I research, I weigh, I consider all stakeholders. My mother would say I finally grew up. But it feels more like something got dimmer.

I will go to the conference. I already told Elena yes. I just wish the yes had come easier.
entries.nudge_text: When did decisions start feeling heavy?
entries.response_text: Maybe around the time my father got sick. 2018. Suddenly there were consequences everywhere I looked. Every choice felt connected to whether I would be there, whether I was reliable. He passed in 2019 and I thought it would lift, but it did not. The weight just found new things to attach to.

entries.initial_entry:
Long day. Production incident at 3 AM, the payment gateway again. Not the part Jennifer wanted to rewrite, ironically, but the legacy integration with the bank's SFTP server. I was on call so I got the page. Took four hours to trace it to a certificate expiration that nobody had on their calendar.

By 7 AM I had fixed it and documented everything. By 8 AM I was in the standup explaining what happened. By 9 AM I was getting messages from the VP asking how we let this happen. Not thank you for catching it. Not good job on the quick fix. Just how did we let this happen.

I did not let anything happen. I inherited this system. I have been asking for budget to modernize the certificate management for two years. But I did not say that either. I just said we would add better monitoring and put the renewal dates in a shared calendar. Safe answer. Team player answer.

Elena asked me at dinner why I looked so tired. I said work was busy. I did not tell her about the VP message because then she would get angry on my behalf and I would have to either agree with her anger or defend the company that made me feel small today. Both options felt exhausting.

I need to sleep but my mind keeps rehearsing the incident like I missed something. I did not miss anything. The certificate was not in any documentation I had access to. But I keep checking anyway.
entries.nudge_text: You keep swallowing things down lately.
entries.response_text: Maybe I do. It is easier than the alternative. Every time I push back, I become the difficult one, the senior engineer who cannot adapt, the old guy who thinks he knows better. So I absorb it. I fix things quietly. I make myself small so the team can function. It is what my father did at his factory job for forty years. I used to think he was weak for that. Now I understand he was just tired.

entries.initial_entry: The morning after a bad night is always strange. Everything looks normal. Coffee tastes the same. I just feel like I am watching myself go through the motions from somewhere slightly outside.
entries.nudge_text: What motions are you watching yourself do?
entries.response_text: The usual routine. Checking Slack before I get out of bed. Reviewing the overnight alerts. Logging into the standup. Answering questions I have answered a hundred times. It is not bad, exactly. It is just automatic. Like I am not really needed there, just my pattern.

entries.initial_entry:
Flew to San Francisco yesterday for the unconference. Staying at a hotel near the Embarcadero with a view of the bay. Weather is cold and gray but I brought my good jacket.

The conference format is interesting. You show up in the morning, people pitch sessions on index cards, everyone votes with stickers, and then the schedule emerges. No predetermined speakers, no approved slides. Someone proposed a session on rebuilding legacy systems with LLMs. Someone else wanted to discuss leaving tech after 50. I almost proposed something about maintaining software versus maintaining yourself, but I did not have the index card ready in time.

I attended four sessions. The legacy systems one was good, practical advice I could use. The leaving tech one was harder to sit through. A woman around my age talked about how she realized she had optimized her whole life for a company that laid her off in an afternoon. She cried a little. Everyone was respectful.

Tonight I walked along the waterfront instead of joining the dinner group. Watched the ferries come and go. Called my mother. She asked if I was eating well and I said yes, even though I had skipped lunch. Some lies are kindness.

Tomorrow is the last day. I feel different here. Not better or worse. Just different.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you with that index card?
entries.response_text: I was still figuring out what I wanted to say. It felt too personal for a room of strangers. Maybe next time.

entries.initial_entry:
Back from the conference for a week now and people at work keep asking how it was. I say it was great, very inspiring, lots of good ideas. Which is true. But when they ask what I am going to do differently, I do not have a good answer. Learn Go? Propose a rewrite? Those feel like someone else's moves.

My manager suggested I do a lunch-and-learn to share what I learned. I said I would think about it. I probably will not do it. Public speaking was never my thing, and honestly, the sessions that meant most to me were the ones I cannot really present in a corporate setting. Nobody wants a slide deck about existential uncertainty in late-career engineers.
entries.nudge_text: What did you actually bring back from there?
entries.response_text: Some business card contacts. A reading list. The usual conference stuff.

entries.initial_entry:
December sprint started with three people out sick and a scope increase nobody agreed to. I have been working ten-hour days trying to keep us on track for the holiday release. Jennifer is frustrated because her Go rewrite keeps getting deprioritized. I am frustrated because I am the one who has to explain why to her without making leadership look bad.

Had dinner with Elena's cousin Miguel who is visiting from Monterrey. He runs a small construction company there. He asked me about my job and I tried to explain sprint planning and stakeholder management and he just laughed and said it sounds like I spend a lot of energy managing people's feelings about work instead of actually working. He did not mean it unkindly but it stuck with me. Maybe he is right. Maybe that is what corporate software engineering becomes after a while.
entries.nudge_text: What would actually working look like for you?
entries.response_text: Building something. Seeing it run. Not writing status reports about why it is not done yet. But that is not realistic at my level, I suppose.

entries.initial_entry:
I finally did it. I proposed to my manager that I move to Jennifer's team for the Go rewrite project. Not as a lead, not as an architect. Just as a developer. Someone who writes code and learns the new language and maybe remembers what it felt like to not know everything about a codebase.

He looked at me like I had asked for a demotion. Which, technically, I had. He said he would need to think about it, talk to HR, consider the implications. He asked if everything was okay at home. I said yes, everything is fine, I just want to learn something new before I am too old to learn.

I do not know why I said it like that. I am not too old. But something in me wanted to say it out loud, the fear underneath. Like naming it would make it less powerful.

Elena was proud of me when I told her. She said she has been waiting for me to do something like this for years. I did not know she was waiting. I thought I was hiding the restlessness well enough. Apparently I was only hiding it from myself.

Now I wait to see what they decide. Maybe they say no and I stay in my maintenance role forever. Maybe they say yes and I flounder in a new language at 57. Both possibilities feel more alive than certainty.
They said yes. Contingent on finishing the holiday release first, but yes. I start on Jennifer's team in January. I have been studying Go tutorials at night, feeling like a student again.

Vikram got promoted to Staff Engineer today. Everyone's congratulating him on Slack, and yes, fine, he deserved it. But let's be honest—he's been here six months longer than me and his scope was smaller until Q2. I'm not bitter. I'm just aware of where I stand. My manager keeps telling me my time will come, but I've heard that before at my last company. You have to make your time come.

Called Appa tonight. He asked about work, and I told him everything was progressing well. No point worrying him with the details. He mentioned the temple renovation fund and I sent extra this month. These things matter. You take care of your family, you do what's expected—that's not a burden, that's just how it should be.
entries.nudge_text: What would 'making your time come' actually look like?
entries.response_text: Visibility. I need to be leading cross-functional initiatives, not just delivering my own sprint work. I've already started attending product roadmap meetings even when I'm not required. When the reorg happens in Q4, I want my name to come up naturally for the platform team lead role.

entries.initial_entry: Priya asked when I'm taking her to meet my parents. I said after Diwali, maybe. We both know I'm stalling but she didn't push.
Three nights of on-call rotations. The junior devs can't seem to handle basic incident triage without escalating to me. I don't mind being the one they depend on, but I need sleep.
entries.nudge_text: Is there a difference between being needed and being indispensable?
entries.response_text: Indispensable means they can't function without you. Needed just means they prefer having you around. I'd rather be indispensable, even if it costs me sleep. Nobody promotes the guy who's easily replaced.

entries.initial_entry:
Quarterly review feedback came in. My manager said I have strong technical skills but need to demonstrate more "executive presence." I asked what that meant specifically. She said it's about how I communicate upward—being more concise in leadership meetings, framing problems in terms of business impact rather than technical detail.

I've always believed that doing excellent work should speak for itself. That's what my father taught me—results matter, not showmanship. But apparently that's not how it works here. The people who get ahead are the ones who know how to package themselves.

Spent the evening reading articles about executive communication. Downloaded a book on stakeholder management. I don't like that I have to play this game, but I've never failed at something I committed to learning. If this is what they want, I'll figure it out.

What frustrates me is that nobody told me the rules changed. At IIT, you studied hard, you got the marks, you got the placement. Clear metrics. Here it's all subjective—"presence," "influence," "visibility." How do you measure that? How do you know if you're winning?
entries.nudge_text: What would it mean if excellent work alone was never enough?
entries.response_text: Then everything I was taught about how success works would be wrong. My father worked for the same company for thirty years and retired as a senior manager—never played politics, just delivered results. But maybe that's why he stayed senior manager. Maybe there was a ceiling I didn't see. I don't want that ceiling.

entries.initial_entry: Presented the Q3 roadmap to the leadership team today. Kept it to seven slides, used business metrics instead of technical specs. VP nodded twice. Small win.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel to communicate that way?

entries.initial_entry:
Priya's friends are getting engaged left and right. She showed me photos from another mehendi last night and I could feel her watching for my reaction. I said the bride looked happy. That's not the response she wanted.

It's not that I don't want to marry her. I do. But there's an order to these things. My parents expect me to be settled—flat, savings, stable position—before I take that step. Amma specifically said not to repeat what her brother did, rushing into marriage without foundation and then struggling for years. I'm building the foundation. Priya should understand that this is for us, not against us.

My cousin Karthik called to announce he's getting married. He's two years younger than me, works at an MNC doing standard IT support, lives in a rented flat in Coimbatore. And yet he's the one Amma spent twenty minutes congratulating on the phone tonight. "Such good news, beta! When will we hear from you?"

I'm the one who sends money home. I'm the one who got into IIT, who moved to Bangalore for better opportunities, who's actually building something. Karthik posts cricket memes and calls it a day. But because he's checking the marriage box, suddenly he's the responsible one?

I'm not jealous of Karthik. His ceiling is that MNC job until retirement. I want more. But I can't help feeling like the family scoreboard doesn't track the things I'm actually achieving. It's all marriage, children, temple visits. The professional stuff—they're proud, sure, but they don't understand it. To them it's just "computer work."

What if I never make them understand? What if I keep climbing and the only thing they see is the boxes I haven't checked?
entries.nudge_text: Whose approval matters more to you right now?

entries.initial_entry:
The platform team lead role opened up officially. My manager submitted my name. I've been preparing all week—reviewing my contributions, documenting the initiatives I've led, practicing how to frame my scope expansion.

Interview is next Thursday. Five panelists including the CTO. I've never presented to him before. My manager said he values people who "think like owners"—whatever that means. I'm guessing it means speaking about company strategy as if you have personal stake in it. I can do that.

Called Priya to tell her. She was supportive but also asked if this meant more late nights. I said probably yes, at least initially. She went quiet. I told her once I get this role, things will stabilize. She said I said that about the senior engineer promotion too. She's not wrong, but what does she expect? This is how you build something. You sacrifice now for position later.

Appa would understand. He always said the early years are for investment, the later years for enjoying the returns. I'm still investing.

Interview went well, I think. CTO asked how I'd handle a direct report who consistently missed deadlines. I said I'd have a direct conversation about expectations and consequences. He seemed to like that.
entries.nudge_text: What's making you unsure about how it went?
entries.response_text: It's not uncertainty, it's just being realistic. You never know what other candidates said or what factors matter to them. I did my part. Now I wait.

entries.initial_entry:
Got the role. Platform Team Lead. Officially starts August 15th. Vikram sent me a congratulatory message that was probably through gritted teeth. Doesn't matter. I'm ahead now.

Manager said the CTO specifically mentioned my clarity about accountability. Direct conversations about expectations and consequences—turns out that's exactly his management philosophy. Lucky guess, or maybe I just think the way leaders do. Either way, I'm in.
entries.nudge_text: What does being 'ahead' give you?

entries.initial_entry:
Called home to share the news. Appa congratulated me, asked what the new title means in terms of responsibilities. Explained about headcount, budget ownership, technical direction. He said it sounds like a lot of work. Then asked if this means I'll be too busy to come for Pongal.

I said of course I'll come for Pongal. I always come for Pongal.

Amma asked about Priya. I said we're doing well. She said Karthik's wedding is in November and the family will be asking questions. I said I know. She said maybe Priya could come for the wedding, meet everyone. I said I'd think about it.

After the call, I sat with this feeling I couldn't name. I achieved exactly what I set out to achieve six months ago. Platform Team Lead before 32. Higher salary, direct reports, visibility to the C-suite. But the call home felt like two different conversations—me trying to share a professional milestone, them trying to pull me back to the family timeline.

Maybe both things can be true. Maybe I can lead the platform team and also bring Priya to Karthik's wedding and also send money home and also make Pongal. But it feels like I'm running two races on parallel tracks, and I'm not sure if either one knows the other exists.
entries.nudge_text: What would it look like if both tracks knew about each other?
entries.response_text: I'd have to tell my parents what the promotion actually cost—the late nights, the stress, why I haven't proposed yet. And I'd have to tell Priya that family expectations aren't just background noise to me, they're load-bearing. I don't know if anyone wants that level of honesty. Maybe it's easier to keep the tracks separate.

entries.initial_entry:
Grandma called today and I let it go to voicemail. I don't know why I do that. It's not like I don't want to talk to her, I do, but every time I see her name on my screen I get this knot in my stomach. She's going to ask when I'm coming home for Christmas and I don't have an answer yet.

The thing is I could go. I have enough saved. Instacart's been decent with the holiday rush and I picked up extra shifts. But something about going back there makes me feel like I'm shrinking. Like all the space I've made for myself out here just collapses and I'm back to being the girl who doesn't know what she wants, getting looked at sideways by aunties who think I should've stayed and helped out.

But then I think about missing another Christmas without her cedar tea and her stories about the old times and I feel like the worst granddaughter alive. She's 74. How many more Christmases does she have? And I'm here worrying about my feelings.

I should call her back. I will. Tomorrow.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned feeling like you're shrinking when you go back, but also like the worst granddaughter for staying away. What would make going home feel less like losing the space you've built?
entries.response_text: I've been thinking about that. Maybe if I went back with like... something to show for myself? Not like a degree or a fancy job, but something that says I didn't just run away and waste these two years. I've been selling some of my beadwork online, just small stuff, earrings and keychains. It's not much but it's mine. Maybe if I could show her that I'm still connected to the things she taught me, even out here, it wouldn't feel so much like I abandoned everything. I don't know if that makes sense.

entries.initial_entry:
I finally called Grandma back and now I wish I hadn't. Not because it went badly, it didn't, she was sweet like always. But she told me that Uncle Ray is sick again and they're doing a ceremony for him next week and she said — she didn't guilt trip me, she never does — she just said it would mean a lot to him if family could be there.

And I'm sitting in my car in the Safeway parking lot after a delivery just crying like an idiot because I should be there. That's what you do. When someone's sick, when there's a ceremony, you show up. That's not even a question back home. You don't weigh it against your schedule or your bank account or whether you feel like it. You just go.

But I have three delivery shifts this week that I can't cancel without tanking my rating, and my car needs an oil change that I've been putting off, and the drive is six hours each way. And I'm sitting here making excuses like some white girl who doesn't understand that some things matter more than money.

My mom would've gone. Before she got sick, before everything, she would've dropped everything and driven home. And here I am, her daughter, calculating whether I can afford the gas.

I hate who I'm becoming out here.

I went. Took Friday off and drove home Thursday night after my last delivery. My Instacart rating dropped to 4.7 because I cancelled Saturday but I don't care.

The ceremony for Uncle Ray was... I forgot how it feels to be in that space. Everyone singing together, the smell of sage, the way time moves differently. My cousin Marcus drummed and he's gotten so good — last time I saw him he was still learning. Grandma held my hand the whole time and didn't say anything about me being away, she just squeezed my fingers when certain prayers came up.

I made her cedar tea Sunday morning before I drove back. She watched me do it without correcting me once, which means I remembered right. She gave me a bundle of sweetgrass to take with me and said "for your apartment, so it smells like home." I almost lost it right there.

The drive back felt different than usual. Like I was bringing something with me instead of running away from something. I hung the sweetgrass above my door as soon as I got in. The whole place smells like summer on the rez now.

I think maybe the answer isn't staying or leaving. It's finding ways to carry it with me.
entries.nudge_text: You described bringing something back with you this time. What would it look like to keep that feeling through the ordinary days here?
entries.response_text:
That's the part I haven't figured out yet. Like, it's easy when I just got back and the sweetgrass is fresh and I can still hear the songs in my head. But in two weeks when I'm grinding through deliveries in the cold and some customer yells at me about their missing bananas? I don't know.

Maybe that's why I keep the beadwork going even when I'm exhausted. It's not about the money — I make like $15 an hour if I'm lucky. It's about sitting there with the needle and the thread and doing something my grandmother's grandmother did. That's a thread too, right? Not just the physical one. Maybe I need more of those threads. Little rituals. I've been thinking about learning to make fry bread. Grandma's recipe. Something I could do on Sunday mornings that's mine but also ours.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a meeting with Dr. Villanueva about the dissertation chapter on extractive policy in Peru. For once he actually engaged with the argument instead of just redirecting me toward his framework. He pushed back on my case selection, which was fair, but he did it by asking questions rather than just telling me to change it. That felt different. I left his office thinking clearly for the first time in weeks.

Walked to the mercado afterward and sat with a coffee going through my notes. The chapter outline is solid. The theoretical framing is mine -- not his, not borrowed from the lab's usual line. I've been circling this argument for months and it finally has shape. There's a version of this that could become a standalone article, which matters because I need publications that aren't just appendages to his projects.

I forget sometimes that I came here with a decade of professional experience. I wasn't some kid who wandered into a program. I chose this. I left a salary and a title because the work I was doing in Bogota hit a ceiling I couldn't break through without the credential. Days like today remind me the trade-off has a point.
entries.nudge_text: That chapter outline is yours -- what usually pulls you away from it?

entries.initial_entry:
Lab meeting today was three hours of performative agreement. Villanueva presented his new grant proposal and everyone nodded along like it was revelation. Half the methodology section is recycled from a paper we all know didn't replicate well. I sat there biting my tongue because the last time I raised a concern in front of the group he reframed it as me "not understanding the nuance yet." In front of the first-years. That was six months ago and I still feel it.

Marina, the other senior student, caught my eye during his presentation. She sees it too but she's smarter about it than I am -- she just stays quiet, publishes on the side, and keeps her head down. I can't do that. Something in me won't let me just sit there when the reasoning is sloppy. But speaking up costs more than staying silent, at least in this lab.

Came home and opened the Peru chapter again. The contrast is sharp. In Villanueva's lab I'm a student who should listen. In my own work I'm building something that could actually matter for how these countries manage their mining conflicts. The gap between those two versions of me is getting harder to hold.
entries.nudge_text: What does Marina's approach cost her that yours doesn't?

entries.initial_entry:
Got an email from a former colleague in Bogota asking if I'd co-author a policy brief on lithium regulation for a think tank. Good money, my name on it, and the topic overlaps with my dissertation. I said yes immediately. Then spent the rest of the afternoon wondering if I should have checked with Villanueva first. The program technically doesn't require it, but there's an unspoken expectation that outside work gets cleared through him.

The thing that bothers me is the hesitation itself. I spent eight years writing policy documents without asking anyone's permission. Now I'm second-guessing a decision I'm fully qualified to make because someone with a title might disapprove. I don't know when I started treating his opinion as a gate I have to pass through, but I notice it more and more.

Same day, later. Called my mother in Cali to tell her about the policy brief. She was happy, asked how much it paid, then reminded me that my cousin Luis just got promoted to director at the ministry. She doesn't mean it as a comparison but it lands like one. Luis is 33, has a corner office, and never left the country. I'm 38, sharing a desk in a research lab.

But then she said something that settled me: "You always needed to understand why things work, not just that they work." She's right. That's why consulting felt hollow toward the end. I was writing recommendations I didn't fully believe in because the analysis was never deep enough. The PhD is slow and humbling but at least I'm doing the actual thinking now.
entries.nudge_text: Is the thinking deeper now, or just differently constrained?
entries.response_text: Different constraints, sure. But at least I chose these ones.

entries.initial_entry:
Villanueva told me today that he wants to be listed as co-author on the Peru chapter if I submit it to a journal. His reasoning is that the "intellectual environment of the lab" contributed to the work. I pushed back. Told him the chapter uses a different framework than anything the lab has produced, that the fieldwork contacts are mine from my Bogota years, that the data collection was funded by my own assistantship hours. He got quiet in that way he does -- not angry, just recalibrating.

He said we'd "revisit it later," which means he expects me to come around. I won't. This is the one piece of work in this entire program that is entirely mine. Every argument, every source, every late night rewriting the methodology section because his suggestions didn't fit. I'm not handing that over.

I know how this looks from outside. Grad student refusing to share credit with advisor. But this isn't about generosity. If I publish this with his name on it, it becomes part of his portfolio, his grant narrative, his legacy. My contribution disappears into his. I've watched it happen to Marina three times already. That's not going to be me.
entries.nudge_text: What happens if he doesn't let it go?
entries.response_text: I'll figure that out when it comes. Right now the chapter is mine.

entries.initial_entry:
Can't focus. Keep replaying the conversation with Villanueva. The way he leaned back in his chair. The pause before he said "revisit." I know that move because I used to do it in meetings in Bogota -- the deliberate silence that says I'm not done with this. He's not going to drop it. And the problem is he doesn't need to fight me directly. He just needs to delay things. Slow down feedback on my other chapters. Forget to sign off on conference funding. The machinery of a program has a hundred small levers.

Started the policy brief for the Bogota think tank. The writing came fast, like muscle memory. Two thousand words in three hours. It reminded me of what it feels like to work without someone standing over the output, deciding if it's good enough to carry their name.

Had lunch with Marina. She told me Villanueva asked her opinion about my "collaboration style." That's how he phrases it -- not that I'm difficult, just that my style needs adjustment. She told him I'm thorough. Diplomatic of her. We both know what he's doing. At least someone in that lab sees it clearly.
entries.nudge_text: What would it change if you and Marina pushed back together?

entries.initial_entry:
Later in the evening. Finished the first draft of the policy brief. Sent it to the think tank contact. Fourteen pages, clean argument, clear recommendations. Took me less than a week of evening work. The dissertation chapter, by contrast, has been in revision for four months because every piece of feedback from Villanueva sends me in a different direction.

Thinking about what Marina said at lunch, about his use of "collaboration style." It's a framing that puts the problem on me, on how I work, instead of on the fact that he's claiming credit for something he didn't build. Language like that is how people with institutional standing rewrite the narrative. I've seen it in government -- the person with the title gets to define the terms.

I need to decide soon whether to submit the Peru chapter independently or wait for whatever "revisit" means. Waiting gives him time to build his case or create friction. Submitting means the relationship might not recover. My mother would say "no pelees batallas que no puedes ganar." But I'm not sure this is a battle I can afford to lose either. The chapter is the best thing I've written. If it goes out with his name, the work is still good. But it won't be mine anymore.
entries.nudge_text: Which loss would sit with you longer?

entries.initial_entry: Spent twenty minutes at the pharmacy comparing two brands of the same medication because the cheaper one had packaging I didn't recognize. Walked out with the expensive one. Too tired to explain to myself why that matters but it does.
entries.nudge_text: What was it about the unfamiliar packaging?

entries.initial_entry: Went to the office, reviewed next quarter's staffing projections, had lunch with Faisal at the usual place. Came home, called my mother, watched half a documentary about shipping routes before falling asleep on the couch.

My cousin Nasser wants me to co-sign on a car loan for him. He's family, and I know he means well, but his track record with money is not great. I told him I'd think about it, which is the polite version of what I actually felt, which was a hard wall going up the second he asked.

Part of me wondered if I'm being too rigid. He's working now, he's trying. But I've spent three years building a buffer between myself and the kind of financial stress my parents dealt with when I was a kid, and the thought of someone else's payments showing up on my credit report makes my chest tight. I know how that sounds.
entries.nudge_text: That wall — protective or isolating?
entries.response_text: Both, probably. But I'd rather be isolated than exposed.

entries.initial_entry: Got asked in a team meeting if I'd volunteer for the new branch setup in Jeddah. It's a promotion track, everyone knows that. I said I'd need to review the terms first, which bought me time. Staying here is not playing small — it's just not gambling what already works.
entries.nudge_text: What would the terms need to say?

entries.initial_entry: Meal-prepped for the whole week on Saturday so I wouldn't have to think about food during work days. Reorganized the emergency drawer too — replaced the expired first aid supplies, checked the flashlight batteries. Tomorrow feels manageable because today I made it so.

Faisal told me today he thinks I overthink things. He said it casually, like it was nothing, but it stuck. He was talking about how I checked the office supply order three times before submitting it because last quarter they sent the wrong toner and it took two weeks to sort out. He thinks that's excessive. I think that's why we didn't run out of toner.

But then I'm sitting here at night and thinking about whether double-checking an office supply order is really about the toner or about something else. Whether I do it because it's smart or because the idea of something going wrong, even something small, makes me feel like I'm standing on a ledge. And I don't know. I honestly don't. But the toner was correct this time.
entries.nudge_text: Standing on a ledge over toner — your words.

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about what Faisal said. And it's not even the toner thing specifically, it's that he said it like it was obvious to everyone around me. Like they all see this thing I do and just tolerate it. That stings more than the actual criticism.

I called my sister after work and she said something similar once, years ago, that I hold on too tight to things. She meant relationships at the time. I don't think I hold on too tight. I think I hold on the right amount and other people let go too easily and then act surprised when things fall apart.
entries.nudge_text: What did your sister mean back then?
entries.response_text: She was upset I kept texting her ex to make sure he returned her stuff. She thought I was overstepping. I thought I was being thorough.

entries.initial_entry:
You know what, I'm not going to keep circling this. Faisal has lost his wallet twice this year. Twice. And both times I was the one who helped him cancel his cards because he didn't even know which bank to call. So maybe being careful is not the personality flaw everyone wants it to be.

I run a team of fourteen people. If I operated the way other people seem to think is normal, things would fall through constantly. The reason things don't fall through is because someone checks. Someone follows up. That someone is me and I'm tired of acting like that needs defending.

Slow day at work. We're between project cycles so mostly it was inbox cleanup and a couple of one-on-ones with junior staff. Had kabsa for lunch from the place near the office that's been there since before I started this job. The owner knows my order by now.

After work I went to the gym, did the same circuit I always do, and stopped at the grocery store on the way home. They moved the rice to a different aisle which threw me off for a second but otherwise everything was fine. Watched the news, texted my mother goodnight.

Couldn't sleep so I'm writing this. There's a rumor at work that our department might get restructured. Nothing official, just something Ahmed mentioned in passing, but now I can't stop running scenarios. What if they move me to a different team. What if the new manager doesn't operate the way I've set things up. What if they decide operations can be leaner and I'm the cut.

I have six months of expenses saved. I know that. I've checked. But the knowing doesn't quiet the thing in my chest that says six months isn't enough, that nothing is enough, that the floor could always open. I protected that buffer carefully and part of me wonders if I built it because I'm wise or because I've never really believed the ground would hold.
entries.nudge_text: Six months saved but still not enough — where does enough start?
entries.response_text: When I stop flinching at rumors. Haven't figured out how to save my way there yet.

entries.initial_entry: Good class today. The students actually debated about the new coastal development project instead of just waiting for me to give them the "right" answer. These moments remind me why I chose this path.
entries.nudge_text: What made them engage differently today?
entries.response_text: I think they're just more comfortable with the format now. We've been doing discussions for a few weeks.

entries.initial_entry:
Long day. Parent-teacher meetings are draining in a way that teaching never is. One parent spent twenty minutes explaining why her son shouldn't have to learn about "foreign problems" like ocean pollution. I tried to explain that the Arabian Sea is not foreign to Kerala, but I could see her eyes glazing over.

Then the principal pulled me aside afterward to suggest I "tone down the activism" in my lessons. Said some parents have been complaining. I nodded and said I'd think about it. Not sure what I'm supposed to think about, honestly.
entries.nudge_text: What did that nod cost you?
entries.response_text: Nothing. It's just politics. Part of working in any institution.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the weekend thinking about the principal's comment. I talked it over with Arjun and he made a good point - I can either fight every battle loudly and burn out, or I can be strategic. Pick what matters most and find ways to teach it that don't raise red flags.

I don't love the idea of softening my approach. But I also know that if I get pushed out, whoever replaces me will probably just teach straight from the textbook. At least I'm here. At least these kids are asking questions about their own coastline. That has to count for something.

Finished the last piece for the Jabal Amman gallery order today. Pulled each one out of the kiln myself, wrapped them in tissue, lined them up on the shelf. Four months of work sitting there looking exactly the way I wanted them to look.
entries.nudge_text: What hit you when you saw them all lined up?
entries.response_text: Relief, honestly. That this batch came out right and the gallery check will clear next week.

entries.initial_entry:
My cousin Fadi was in town from Dubai and we had dinner at my uncle's place. He kept going on about his new project management role, the relocation package, the company car. My aunt turned to me at one point and said, "You see, Nadia, that is what happens when you follow a plan." I smiled and ate my kibbeh and did not say anything.

The thing is, I do have a plan. I have the gallery contract through October, the workshop income, the private commissions spreadsheet. It just does not look like a plan to people who need a company name on it. I left dinner early and drove home with the windows down and felt fine about it. I did.
entries.nudge_text: Did you actually feel fine?
entries.response_text: No. I kept replaying my aunt's voice the whole drive home. It is not that she is wrong about Fadi doing well — he is. It is that she cannot see what I have built because it does not come with a business card.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent most of the morning at the studio doing inventory. Ran the numbers on clay and glaze stock for the next quarter and placed an order with the Turkish supplier. The exchange rate is worse this month, so I bought less than I wanted to. I will need to reorder before October if the gallery pieces go ahead.

Afternoon was a walk-in workshop — six women from a corporate team-building group. They made pinch pots and seemed to enjoy it. Easy money, low creative effort on my part. I have three more of these booked through August, which helps with the monthly numbers.

Back again. Could not stop thinking about what I wrote earlier, about the workshops. I want to be clear — I am not ungrateful. The workshop income is what lets me say no to design freelance gigs I do not want. It is a trade I chose and I would choose it again.

It is just that sometimes the choosing gets heavy. Every month is a little negotiation between what I want to make and what I need to sell. I keep both ledgers and they rarely agree. But they do not have to agree. They just have to coexist.

Had a call with Reem from the Wekalat gallery in Beirut. She wants to include two of my pieces in a group show in November. No artist fee, just exposure and potential sales. I said I would think about it, but what I was really doing was calculating shipping costs from Amman to Beirut in my head while she talked.

I decided yes. The pieces she wants are ones I made for myself, not on commission. Letting them go to a show where someone might actually connect with them — that matters more to me than the logistics headache. I will figure out the shipping.
entries.nudge_text: Which pieces did she pick?

entries.initial_entry: Signed a six-month lease renewal on the studio today. Landlord kept it at the same rate, which almost never happens here. Called my mother afterward and she said, "Good, at least something is fixed."
entries.nudge_text: What did she mean by 'at least'?

entries.initial_entry:
One of the corporate workshop pots cracked in the kiln. Not a client piece, just a leftover someone forgot. But I spent twenty minutes staring at it anyway because the glaze had done something unexpected on the broken edge — this deep copper-green that I have been trying to get for months on my own work and it just showed up on a throwaway pot by accident.

I cannot explain why that made me so frustrated. All the careful testing, the temperature logs, the glaze ratios written out in notebooks. And the thing I have been chasing appears on a piece that no one will ever claim. I scraped the glaze residue off and saved it in a jar. I will try to figure out what happened. That is all I can do.

Shipped the two pieces for the Beirut show today. Wrapped them myself in three layers — bubble wrap, then foam, then a wooden crate I built from scrap lumber in the back of the studio. The shipping guy looked at me like I was overdoing it, but those vessels took me five weeks each and I am not trusting them to someone else's packing job.

After they left, the studio felt different. Emptier, obviously, but also lighter. I have been holding onto those pieces since April, moving them from shelf to shelf, never quite sure what they were for. Now they have a destination and I can stop hovering. Reem sent a photo of the gallery space where they will be placed — a corner with natural light from a high window. They will look right there.

Spent the rest of the afternoon mixing a new glaze batch based on the copper-green residue from last week. I adjusted the iron oxide ratio and ran a small test tile. Will not know until tomorrow. The kiln schedule is full this week with workshop pieces, so I wedged my test into the last corner of the bottom shelf where the temperature runs slightly cooler. We will see.

The test tile came out close. Not exact — the copper-green is more muted than the accident, and it does not have that sharp metallic edge I saw on the broken pot. But it is in the neighborhood. I mixed a second batch with slightly more copper carbonate and will fire again Thursday.

My mother called this morning. She sounded tired. She asked about money, which she does when she is worried about her own but does not want to say so. I told her the gallery payment came through and that the workshop bookings are steady through September. She seemed relieved, or at least she stopped asking follow-up questions. I wired her some money through CliQ after we hung up — not a lot, but enough that she will not have to ask again for a while.

Sometimes I think the reason I track every fils so carefully is not really about the studio at all. It is about being able to pick up the phone and say "everything is fine" and have it be true. Or close enough to true. If my numbers are solid, I can carry more than just my own weight, and that is the part that lets me sleep at night.
entries.nudge_text: When did you start carrying that weight?
entries.response_text: When my father died and the bills started arriving at her door. I was twenty-six. I remember sitting at the kitchen table in Beirut doing the math and thinking, I need to always be able to do this math, no matter what.

entries.initial_entry: Workshop cancelled last minute — the group rescheduled to next month. That is 400 dinars I was counting on for September's clay order. I sat in the studio for an hour doing nothing, just staring at the schedule board and rearranging numbers in my head.
entries.nudge_text: What are you going to do?
entries.response_text: I will figure it out. I always do.

entries.initial_entry:
September already. Pulled up the spreadsheet this morning and went through everything — studio rent, clay orders, kiln fuel, the shipping costs from the Beirut pieces. After the cancelled workshop, the buffer is thinner than I like. Not dangerous, but I can feel the margin. I have two options: pick up a small freelance design job to bridge the gap, or book an extra workshop weekend and push the clay order back two weeks.
