I chose the workshop. Spent an hour writing a post for the studio's page, offering a weekend intensive for beginners. It is more of the same work I was just complaining about, but at least it is mine — my studio, my schedule, my terms. The freelance route would have paid better but I would have spent a week taking direction from someone else's brief, and I left that behind for a reason.

Reem messaged that the Beirut show opens next Friday. She said there has been some early interest in the vessels. I read her message three times, then put the phone down and went back to wedging clay. I do not want to get ahead of myself. If they sell, good — it changes the math. If they do not, the math still works, just tighter. Either way, I keep making the work.

Started doing the math again tonight. If I keep the delivery app running five days a week and pick up at least two handyman jobs on the weekends, I can cover everything with about twelve thousand pesos left over. That leftover is supposed to be for the motorcycle insurance renewal in May. I wrote it on a sticky note and put it on the fridge next to the old one that says "DO NOT touch savings."

Funny thing is, I spent Saturday at the parrilla with Cacho and Pocho like always, and the bill came out heavier than usual because we ordered that tira de asado. I didn't blink. Six hours of good food and good talk, what am I going to do, skip it? Some weeks the numbers work, some weeks they don't. But I'm not going to stop living just because the spreadsheet says I should.

My sister Graciela keeps calling to tell me I should go back to a regular job. She doesn't get it. I'm not going to sit in some warehouse again answering to a guy ten years younger than me who treats people like furniture.
entries.nudge_text: What is it about the idea of going back that feels impossible?
entries.response_text: It's not impossible, I just don't want to. There's a difference. Look, I know what I'm doing. I've been managing fine for five years this way. Graciela worries because she's Graciela. She married an accountant, everything in her life has a line item. Not everyone lives like that.

entries.initial_entry:
Bad week. The delivery app lowered the per-trip rate again, no warning, no explanation. Just opened the app Monday morning and the numbers were different. I did the same twelve deliveries I always do on a weekday and came home with almost twenty percent less. Twenty percent. That's not nothing. That's a week of groceries, that's Negrita's vet visit I've been putting off.

The handyman side is slower too because people are tightening up. I had three jobs lined up this week and two of them canceled last minute — one lady said she'd "wait until next month" to fix her bathroom faucet and the other one just stopped responding. So now I'm sitting here Thursday night with the calculator app open, moving numbers around like I'm playing some kind of sad puzzle game, trying to figure out where the motorcycle payment comes from.

The thing that really gets to me is the feeling. It's not just about the money, it's the not knowing. Every month is a guess. Every week I wake up and I don't know what I'm going to make. I used to think that was the trade-off for not having a boss breathing down my neck. Lately I'm not so sure the trade-off is worth it. But what am I supposed to do, crawl back and prove Graciela right? No. I'll figure it out. I always figure it out.
entries.nudge_text: You said you're not sure the trade-off is worth it anymore. What would it look like if you really sat with that thought?

entries.initial_entry: Got a call from Don Alfredo across the street — he needs his whole kitchen faucet replaced plus some tile work. That's a good-sized job, probably two days of work. Felt like I could breathe again for the first time all week.

Motorcycle started making a noise yesterday. A grinding thing when I brake. I took it to Raul's shop and he looked at it and said the brake pads are gone and the disc is warped, it needs replacing. He quoted me a number that wiped out everything I'd saved from the Alfredo job. Every peso.

I stood there in Raul's shop and felt my stomach just drop. Because it's not optional — the motorcycle is how I make deliveries. Without it I'm on foot or taking the subte, which means fewer trips, which means less money, which means next month's rent gets tight. It's like this chain where one thing breaks and everything behind it starts wobbling. I told Raul to do it, because what choice do I have. But I walked home and I couldn't even eat dinner.
entries.nudge_text: When you walked home, what was going through your head?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I was thinking about my old man. He worked at the same factory for thirty-two years. Hated it, but every month the check came in. He never had to wonder if the floor was going to fall out from under him. I used to think that was a sad way to live. Now I'm not so sure. He always had a little envelope in the dresser drawer with cash for emergencies. I don't have that envelope. I don't have anything between me and the next bad surprise. And that thought — that I'm one broken motorcycle or one bad month away from not being able to pay rent — that sat on my chest the whole walk home.

entries.initial_entry: Couldn't sleep. Opened the app at 1am to see if there were any late-night delivery runs. There were two. I took them both, rode the bicycle I borrowed from my neighbor. Came home at 3am with barely enough to cover tomorrow's gas.

Picked up the motorcycle from Raul this morning. It rides clean now, brakes feel solid. That's something. But I'm running on maybe four hours of sleep from the last few days and I can feel it in my eyes, in the way I'm slow to react at intersections. Almost clipped a bus mirror on Corrientes.

Saturday is coming and Cacho already texted about the parrilla. Part of me wants to skip it this week, save the money. But another part of me — the louder part — says that's the one thing that keeps me sane. You take away the asado with friends and what do I have left? A cat, a calculator, and a leaking faucet in my own bathroom that I haven't even bothered to fix.
entries.nudge_text: When you say the parrilla is the one thing that keeps you sane — what does it give you that nothing else does?
entries.response_text: It's the only time during the week where I'm not counting anything. Not counting deliveries, not counting pesos, not counting hours. I sit down, the meat comes, somebody pours the wine, and for a few hours I'm just Roberto having a good time with his friends. Nobody's asking me to fix something or deliver something or be somewhere in fifteen minutes. It's the closest thing I have to feeling like my life is actually mine.

entries.initial_entry: Don Alfredo recommended me to his daughter for some painting work at her new apartment. Three rooms, good rate, she even offered to pay half upfront. I bought Negrita the fancy wet food she likes to celebrate.

Graciela came over unannounced today while I was between deliveries. She brought empanadas, which was nice, except then she sat at my kitchen table and started doing that thing she does where she looks around the apartment cataloguing everything that's wrong. The peeling paint above the window, the chair with the broken leg, the fact that I still don't have a proper desk and eat on the couch.

Then she said — and I quote — "Roberto, you're almost fifty, you can't live like a college student." And I just stood there holding an empanada feeling like I was fourteen again getting lectured. She doesn't see that I actually like my life most of the time. I like picking my own hours. I like riding through Palermo on a Tuesday morning with the sun out and nobody telling me where to be. But she looks at this apartment and she sees failure. And the worst part is that sometimes, when she leaves, I start seeing it through her eyes too.
entries.nudge_text: You said sometimes you see it through her eyes. What do you see when that happens?
entries.response_text: A guy who's been treading water for five years and calling it swimming. Someone who left a stable situation because of pride and never figured out the next step. I see the broken chair and I think, why haven't I fixed that? I'm literally a handyman. And the answer is because when I come home I don't want to fix anything else, I just want to sit down and not move. And that scares me a little because my old man never left things broken. He always had the house perfect. I can't even keep up with my own apartment and I live alone with a cat.

entries.initial_entry:
Painting job at Don Alfredo's daughter's place is done. She was happy with it, paid the balance, even tipped me a little extra. So the money situation is stable for the moment. Rent is covered, motorcycle payment is covered, Raul is paid. There's a small cushion again, not much, but it's there.

Went to the parrilla Saturday like always. Pocho brought his nephew, a kid of about twenty-five who does the same delivery app I do but also drives for a rideshare service on the side. He was telling me about his "strategy" — how he works the surge hours, how he tracks which neighborhoods tip best, how he's saving for a down payment on his own place. The whole table was impressed. I just sat there eating my chorizo and feeling old.

It's not jealousy exactly. The kid has energy and optimism and a plan, and good for him. But I was that kid once. I had the energy to work sixteen-hour days and still go dancing with my ex-wife on Saturday night. Now I do eight hours of deliveries and my back locks up. I eat the asado and drink the wine and it's still good, it's always good. But there's this thin layer underneath the enjoyment now, like a film on old coffee. Like I'm enjoying it because I know this might not last. Like I should be saving what I'm spending here but I can't bring myself to stop because this is the part of my life that doesn't feel like survival.
entries.nudge_text: You described a thin layer underneath the enjoyment. When did you first start noticing it?

entries.initial_entry:
Fixed the chair today. The one Graciela noticed. It took me twenty minutes. I don't know why I left it broken for so long. I also patched the peeling paint above the window. Not because she said anything but because once I started with the chair I just kept going. Negrita watched me from the couch like she was supervising.

After I finished I sat in the chair and it felt solid. Stupid thing to feel good about but I did. I texted Cacho and told him I'd bring a bottle of the good Malbec tomorrow — the Catena, not the box wine we usually get. Sometimes you just want to do something a little extra for no reason. I've got the painting money still in my pocket. Next week is uncertain, it always is. But right now the chair is fixed, the wall looks clean, and tomorrow I'll eat well with people I like. That's not nothing.

Finally told Andrés he can't keep showing up twenty minutes late to install pieces. I've been letting it slide for weeks because he's good with his hands and the clients like him, but this morning he rolled in while a collector was already waiting and I had to cover for him, again. So I pulled him aside after she left and laid it out: one more time and I'm finding someone else.

He looked genuinely startled, which tells me nobody's ever drawn that line with him. Good. I can't have my space looking disorganized when there's money on the table. Felt solid doing it, too. Not angry, just clear. The gallery is mine and the standard is mine to set.
entries.nudge_text: Did the collector say anything about the wait?
entries.response_text: She didn't mention it directly, but she kept checking her phone while I was making small talk. Ended up buying the smaller piece instead of the one she'd asked about originally. Could be coincidence, could be she lost patience. Either way, that's exactly why I can't let things slide.

entries.initial_entry:
Long day finishing the commission for the Restrepo family. Got the last panel mounted and stepped back to look at the whole triptych together. It works. The proportions are right, the color transitions hold. I stood there for a minute just looking at it and felt something click into place, that rare moment when what I made matches what I saw in my head.

Called my mom after. She asked about the gallery rent, whether I'd paid on time. I had, obviously. I always do. She worries, but she doesn't need to — I've had the next three months covered since January. Told her the commission check clears Friday. She sounded relieved, which is enough for now.
entries.nudge_text: What was that click like?

entries.initial_entry: Met with a potential co-investor for the expansion into the second floor. He kept talking over me, pitching his own vision for the layout. I smiled and nodded through most of it instead of pushing back, and now I'm sitting here irritated at myself.

Walked through the Saturday market in Provenza this morning, not to buy anything but just to look at how other people display their work. The ceramicist on the corner has started using linen backdrops and better lighting — she's learning. A year ago her booth looked like a garage sale. Now people stop.

I do this thing where I watch who commands a space and who just fills it. The ceramicist commands hers now. It reminded me why I fought so hard to get the gallery out of the shared lease arrangement. When I had to negotiate every wall placement with Camilo, nothing felt fully mine. Now every corner of that space reflects a decision I made, and collectors can feel it. That matters more than people think.

Lazy Sunday, which almost never happens. Woke up late, made arepas with the leftover hogao from Friday. Sat on the balcony eating and watching the street below. The neighbor's kid was kicking a ball against the wall again, steady thunk-thunk-thunk rhythm that's almost calming when you're not trying to concentrate.

Started going through my supply invoices because even on a day off I can't leave the numbers alone. Paints are up twelve percent from last year which is absurd, and I'm running through the cadmium yellow faster than usual because of the Restrepo palette. Need to either find a new supplier or adjust my pricing. Probably both. I've been meaning to rework the pricing sheet anyway — the last revision was August and costs have shifted.

Nothing urgent. The accounts are fine. I just like knowing exactly where the money sits, what's coming in, what's going out. My mother would say I got that from my father, who used to keep a paper ledger for his tire shop even after they had a computer. Different scale, same instinct.

Spent the afternoon finalizing the March exhibition lineup and it felt like orchestrating something, which is the part of this work I love most. Three emerging artists, each one I handpicked. Two of them came to me through referrals from collectors I've built relationships with over years. The third found me through Instagram, sent a portfolio that was raw but had something. I'm taking a risk on her, but a calculated one — I've already soft-committed two buyers who tend to go for that aesthetic.

The thing is, when I curate a show, I'm not just hanging art. I'm deciding whose career gets a platform and whose doesn't. That responsibility sits well with me. I spent years being the one hoping someone would give me a shot. Now I'm on the other side. I've earned that.

Had a good call with Daniela, my accountant, beforehand. March projections are strong, the reserve fund is untouched, insurance renewal is handled. When the financial side is solid, I can actually think about the art. When it's shaky, everything else feels shaky too. I learned that the hard way during the first year after the split with Camilo, when I was burning through savings and couldn't focus on a single canvas.
entries.nudge_text: What was the something in her portfolio?
entries.response_text: Honestly, she reminded me of myself ten years ago. Same raw energy, same refusal to make the work palatable for easy sales. I almost didn't reach out because I don't usually go for sentimental reasons. But then I looked at her pieces again and they're genuinely good. I just didn't want to admit that the recognition was part of why I noticed her.

entries.initial_entry: The co-investor sent a revised proposal with his name listed as "co-director" of the new floor. I didn't agree to that. I wrote back saying we need to clarify terms before anything moves forward, but my hands were shaking when I hit send. Not from fear — from the idea that someone thinks they can just slide into a title I haven't offered.
entries.nudge_text: What were the hands shaking from, then?
entries.response_text: Just adrenaline. It's fine. The email's sent, the boundary is drawn. Moving on.

entries.initial_entry: March show opens Friday. All three artists delivered on time. Walked through the hung pieces this evening and everything is exactly where I placed it. Good feeling, knowing the room is set and the invitations went out to the right people.

Opening night went better than I planned. Sold four pieces in the first two hours, including one from the new artist — the one I took a chance on. A collector from Bogotá asked for my card and said he'd been hearing my name. That sentence alone made the whole month worth it. This is what building a reputation looks like, one show at a time, one handshake at a time.

Called my mom after closing up. She asked how much I made and I told her, which I don't usually do, but I wanted her to hear it. Wanted her to know the gallery isn't some reckless bet anymore. She said "qué bueno, mija" in that tone that means she's genuinely proud but won't say it bigger than that. That's enough. The numbers are good, the reserve is intact, and people are starting to know the name Villalobos in this city's art scene.

Someone on an art forum called my curation "commercially safe." I almost replied but closed the tab instead. My gallery, my choices, my track record. If making a living from art while keeping my lights on and my artists paid is "safe," fine. I'll take it.
entries.nudge_text: What were you going to type?
entries.response_text: Something about how I left a stable partnership to build this alone and nobody who's done that gets to be called safe. But then I realized I'd be arguing with a stranger who's never mounted a show or signed a lease. Not worth it. I know what I've built.

entries.initial_entry: The co-investor agreed to drop the co-director title but now wants approval rights on artist selection for the second floor. Told him I'd think about it. I won't give that up, but I also can't fund the expansion alone and the bank terms are worse. Feels like I'm slowly trading control for capital and I'm not sure where the line is.
entries.nudge_text: Where would the line be?
entries.response_text: I don't know yet. That's the whole problem. I'll figure it out before the next meeting.

entries.initial_entry:
Another Monday spent optimizing database queries for a billing system. The work pays well, certainly better than anything I could have imagined when I left the village. But today I walked past the construction site where migrant workers are building our new office tower, and I saw a child no older than ten carrying bricks. Her mother was mixing cement nearby.

I wanted to say something, to do something. Instead I walked to my air-conditioned desk and wrote code that helps rich hospitals bill patients more efficiently. The disconnect between my daily labor and what I believe matters is growing wider every year.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from saying something?

entries.initial_entry: Couldn't sleep last night. Kept seeing that girl's face. Called my mother in the morning and she reminded me that I too carried water from the well before school when I was her age.
entries.nudge_text: What did her saying that bring up for you?
entries.response_text: It made me feel ashamed in a different way. Like I've become so removed from where I came from that I've forgotten what survival looks like. My mother wasn't excusing the child labor, she was reminding me that I've had the privilege to forget.

entries.initial_entry: Sent the monthly transfer to the village school. Also enrolled for an online course on building accessible software for low-resource settings. Start next week.

The course is fascinating. We are learning about designing for users with limited internet, older phones, and varying literacy levels. It reminded me of my grandmother who never learned to read but could recognize patterns in kolam designs that would challenge any algorithm I could write.

I mentioned the course to Raghav at work today. He laughed and asked why I was bothering with "social service" projects when I could be learning AI tools that would increase my market value. I didn't have a good answer in the moment, but walking home I realized: the question itself reveals different assumptions about what work is for.

For Raghav, work is a transaction, skills exchanged for money. For me, at this stage in life, it feels like there should be more. Not everyone has thirty years of experience building systems. Maybe that expertise could serve people who actually need it, not just corporations looking to squeeze more margins. But then again, my daughter's tuition fees come from those same corporate margins. The math doesn't always work out cleanly.
entries.nudge_text: Has Raghav always seen things that way?
entries.response_text: He's younger, maybe mid-thirties. Grew up in a different India than I did, one where software engineers are heroes and startups are the new temples. I don't blame him. When I was his age, I was chasing the same things. The difference is I've reached them now and I'm asking what comes after.

entries.initial_entry:
Lakshmi suggested I should consider early retirement and volunteer full-time with some NGO. Easy for her to say when she has her own pension secured. I know she means well, but she doesn't understand how much of my identity is tied to being a working professional.

Also, and I didn't say this to her, there's fear. What if I give up the corporate world and realize I'm not actually useful to anyone? At least here, however meaningless the work feels, I have metrics proving my value. Pull requests merged, systems delivered, performance reviews completed.
entries.nudge_text: What would 'useful' look like outside of work metrics?
entries.response_text: I don't know. That's the honest answer. I've been measured by numbers for so long that I can't imagine what value looks like without them. Maybe helping someone learn to code who couldn't afford classes? But how would I know if I was actually making a difference?

entries.initial_entry:
Sprint planning ran until 7 PM. We're building features no one asked for because some VP read an article about what competitors are doing. I sat there for four hours nodding along, contributing where required, while part of me was calculating how many village school teachers' salaries could be paid with the cost of this one unnecessary feature.

I know this kind of thinking isn't productive. You can't compare everything to some moral baseline or you'll go mad. But today I'm too tired to pretend the comparison doesn't exist in my head.
entries.nudge_text: Why does that comparison keep coming back?
entries.response_text: Because I remember what it felt like to have nothing and know that luck was the only difference between me and so many others. Every rupee spent on nonsense feels like stolen opportunity for someone who could have used it better. I know that's not how economics works, but the feeling doesn't care about economics.

entries.initial_entry:
Video call with Priya tonight. She's doing well in Toronto, enjoying her research on climate modeling. She asked about my work and I found myself describing it with more cynicism than usual. She paused and then said something that stuck: "Appa, you taught me that every small action matters. Why don't you believe that for yourself?"

She's right, of course. I've always told her and her generation that individual choices ripple outward, that systemic change starts with personal conviction. But somewhere along the way I started feeling like my own choices were too small to matter against the scale of suffering I see.

The conversation reminded me of why I became an engineer in the first place. Growing up, technology represented possibility, a way out of predetermined fates. I wanted to build things that would make life better. When did I lose that thread? Or maybe I haven't lost it, I've just buried it under spreadsheets and sprint cycles.
entries.nudge_text: What would it look like to un-bury it?

entries.initial_entry: Same day, later. After the call I went for a walk and found myself outside the construction site again. This time the workers had finished for the night. Empty scaffolding and silence. Felt strangely peaceful.

Spoke with HR about reducing to four days a week. They said they would need to discuss with leadership but it might be possible with adjusted compensation. The money difference isn't trivial, but Lakshmi and I talked it through and we can manage.

The fifth day I want to dedicate to teaching coding at a community center in Koramangala that serves domestic workers and their children. I reached out to them last week and they welcomed the offer. First session scheduled for next Friday.

First teaching session did not go as planned. I had prepared carefully, slides on basic programming concepts, examples I thought would be relatable. But the students struggled with the mouse and keyboard basics I had taken for granted. One woman, Kamala, works as a housekeeper in my neighborhood. She spent fifteen minutes trying to type her name because she had never used a computer before.

I felt useless, like I had parachuted in with my fancy knowledge completely missing what they actually needed. By the end I had abandoned my lesson plan entirely and we spent the last hour just practicing how to open and close windows on the screen.

Walking home I felt deflated, then embarrassed for feeling deflated. Who did I think I was, expecting to transform lives in one session? This is going to be harder and slower than I imagined. The question is whether I have the patience and humility to stick with it.
entries.nudge_text: What would patience look like here?
entries.response_text: I don't know, showing up again next week I suppose. Starting where they are instead of where I expected them to be.

entries.initial_entry:
Kamala waved at me this morning. She was walking to work as I was leaving for office. She smiled and said, "Sir, I practiced. I can type my name now." Such a small thing, and yet.

Maybe this is what it looks like. Not grand gestures or systemic overhauls, but one person learning to type her own name for the first time at forty-five. My daughter was right. Every small action matters. The scale of the world's problems paralyzes me when I look at it whole, but zooming into this one interaction, this one victory, there's something real there. Something I helped create.
entries.nudge_text: What changed between yesterday and today?
entries.response_text: Nothing really changed. I just saw her smile and remembered why I wanted to do this in the first place.

entries.initial_entry:
Long day. There was a curriculum planning session this afternoon and I'd spent most of last weekend drafting a proposal for how we should restructure the Year 9 field trip programme. I'd mapped out logistics, costs, student learning outcomes - the whole thing. I started presenting it and maybe two minutes in, Henrik just cut straight across me. Didn't even raise his hand or wait for a pause. Just started talking about his own idea for partnering with some outdoor centre his friend runs.

And then everyone just... shifted attention to him. Elsa started asking him follow-up questions. The deputy head nodded along. My slides were still up on the projector and nobody even glanced at them. I sat there holding my notes feeling like I'd been physically pushed to the side of the room. I tried to redirect once - said something like "if we could go back to the cost comparison I'd started" - but Henrik just kept going, and the deputy head said we'd "circle back" to mine later. We didn't.

I know I should have pushed harder. I know I should have said "I wasn't finished." But something about the way everyone just moved on made me feel like if I insisted, I'd look petty or desperate. And that felt worse than being ignored. So I just sat there, pretending to take notes, while Henrik got credit for a half-baked version of what I'd already done better. I'm still angry about it and I don't know what to do with that.
entries.nudge_text: What felt worse - being overlooked, or the thought of how you'd look pushing back?
entries.response_text: I think it's the second one, honestly. If I'd just been overlooked that's annoying but I can deal with it. It was the calculation in my head - like, if I fight for this, will they see me as someone with conviction or someone who can't handle being interrupted? And the fact that I even had to think about it made me feel small. I shouldn't have to audition for basic respect.

entries.initial_entry:
Good day actually. Parent-teacher conferences tonight and I had back-to-back sessions for three hours straight. My voice is shot and I'm mainlining tea but I feel kind of... alive? There's something about sitting across from a parent and watching their face shift when I show them exactly where their kid improved, with specific examples, with the rubric breakdowns I put together. One mother said she wished all her daughter's teachers were as organised. Another father asked if I'd be willing to mentor his son through the IB application. I said yes before he even finished the sentence.

I don't know why these nights energise me when they exhaust everyone else. Elsa left after her first block looking like she'd run a marathon. But for me it's like - this is where it matters. This is the part where they see you actually know what you're doing. And when they see that, they trust you. And when they trust you, you can actually change things. I reorganised my class seating charts last week based on learning profiles I built myself and two parents tonight specifically mentioned that their kids are more engaged since the change. That's not nothing. That's me actually making a difference because I put myself in a position to.

Just got home. Feet hurt. Going to mark three essays and then collapse. But yeah. Good day.

Submitted the budget request for the Year 9 field trip two weeks ago. Still haven't heard back. I emailed the deputy head on Wednesday, she said it needs to go through the finance committee. I asked when the committee meets. She said "when they have quorum." So basically I'm just supposed to sit here and wait while my timeline collapses.

I've already done all the work. The costing is done, the risk assessments are done, the parent consent forms are drafted. Everything is ready to go and it's being held up by people who haven't even looked at the proposal yet. I followed up again today and got a one-line reply: "We'll get to it." As if my project is some afterthought they'll squeeze in between tea breaks. It's infuriating. If they'd just give me direct access to the budget approval process I could have this sorted in a day. But no, I have to wait for someone else to decide my work is worth looking at.
entries.nudge_text: Is it the delay that's getting to you, or something else?

entries.initial_entry:
Small win today. The head of department asked me to represent our faculty at the district curriculum review panel next month. It's normally a senior teacher slot and she specifically said she chose me because my documentation is the most thorough. Two colleagues with over a decade more experience got passed over for this. I don't feel bad about that. If anything it confirms what I already suspected - that being meticulous matters more than just being around for a long time.

I've already started preparing my presentation notes even though the panel isn't for three weeks. I want to walk in there and have the most polished submission in the room. If I do this well, the district coordinators will know my name. That opens doors. That's how you go from being "the young geography teacher" to someone people actually listen to.

Just came from a department meeting about the spring assessment schedule. I had a clear plan for staggering the exams but Karin pushed back hard, said it would create too much admin work, and the rest just went with her. I caved because I could feel the room turning, but her plan is worse and they'll see that in April when everything piles up.
entries.nudge_text: You said you 'caved.' What would it have taken to hold your ground?

entries.initial_entry:
New teaching assistant started today. She was assigned to support my Year 8 and Year 9 classes. Within the first hour she rearranged my resource station without asking, told a student a different method for the mapping exercise than the one I'd taught, and then during break she suggested to Elsa that my lesson pacing might be "a bit fast for some learners." Elsa told me this casually like it was helpful feedback.

I've been teaching these classes since September. I built the assessment rubrics, the differentiation frameworks, the whole progression structure. And someone who's been in the building for four hours is already second-guessing my methods to a colleague. I pulled her aside after lunch and explained how my classroom runs. She smiled and said "of course, I'm just here to help." But the damage is done - Elsa now thinks my pacing is a problem. I need to correct that impression before it spreads.

District panel prep is going well. Spent the weekend refining my presentation and I'm confident it's strong. Ran through it with the head of department on Friday and she said it was "exactly the level of detail the panel looks for." She also mentioned she'd put my name forward for a cross-school working group on assessment reform that starts after Easter.

The field trip budget finally got approved too - no changes to my original figures. So all that waiting was for nothing, they just rubber-stamped what I'd submitted weeks ago. Annoying but at least it's done. Year 9 students are already asking about it. One of them told me it's the first time a teacher has organised something like this for their year group. I didn't correct her but it's true - nobody else bothered to put the work in. I did.

Ran the departmental meeting today and everyone stayed on time, on topic. New teacher Camila tried to bring up a textbook she liked and I redirected before it derailed things. Small thing, but it matters — I set the rhythm of these meetings and when they run well, the whole week runs well.
entries.nudge_text: What was Camila's suggestion about?
entries.response_text: Not the point. We had an agenda and I kept us on it.

entries.initial_entry: Still thinking about that meeting. The truth is Camila looked a little deflated when I moved past her. But if I let every junior teacher pitch their favorite material mid-agenda we would never get through anything. The department works because someone holds the line. That someone is me and it has been me for fifteen years.
entries.nudge_text: She got to you a little, didn't she?
entries.response_text: She is new. She will learn how things work here.

entries.initial_entry:
Faculty meeting this morning, the big monthly one with the coordinadora and all department heads. I had prepared a ten-minute summary of our curriculum adjustments for next semester. Detailed slides, enrollment data by section, everything organized the way I like — no fluff, just facts and a clear recommendation.

I got three minutes in before the coordinadora cut me off. Said she wanted to "open the floor" for a more collaborative discussion. Then Profesor Ríos from the science department started talking about interdisciplinary projects and somehow my curriculum adjustments became a footnote to his pitch. He used my enrollment data — the numbers I had just shown — to support his own proposal. Nobody seemed to notice. Or maybe they noticed and did not care.

I sat there for the rest of the meeting contributing when asked but mostly just watching my work get absorbed into someone else's narrative. I wanted to say something. That those were my numbers, my analysis, my recommendation. But the coordinadora had already set the tone and pushing back would have looked petty. So I smiled. Nodded. Made a note in my planner to address it privately later.

Walking back to my classroom I could feel it in my jaw, the clenching. Fifteen years I have been building this department and Ríos, who has been here four years, gets to repurpose my work in real time while I sit there like I am attending someone else's meeting.

Spent the morning reorganizing the exam schedule for finals. Nobody asked me to — I just saw that the current draft from admin had conflicts that would affect my students. Fixed it, sent it to the vice-rector with my corrections marked in red, cc'd the relevant people. That is how you get things done around here. You do not wait for permission, you present the solution.
entries.nudge_text: Why the red marks and the cc's?

entries.initial_entry: Regular Wednesday. Classes went fine. The eleventh graders are reviewing for their exam on Independence movements and most of them actually did the reading, which is a first this semester. Ate lunch at my desk grading quizzes while the other teachers chatted in the sala de profesores.
entries.nudge_text: Why at your desk instead of the sala?
entries.response_text: I get more done alone. The sala is just gossip and complaints about students.

entries.initial_entry:
Coming back to this because something happened after classes. The vice-rector stopped me in the hallway and thanked me for the exam schedule corrections I sent last week. Said it saved them a headache and that she was going to mention it at the next admin meeting. Small recognition but I will take it. That is how it should work — you do good work, someone notices.

It reminded me of something my father used to say. He managed a textile warehouse in Medellín for twenty-something years and he always told me the man who controls the calendar controls the operation. I did not understand it when I was young. Now I live it. Every week I review the departmental calendar, the exam schedule, the committee meeting dates. I know what is happening before most people know it is happening. That is not obsession, that is just how you keep things from falling apart.

My wife Patricia says I should relax more at home, that I bring the school energy into the house. Maybe she has a point. But when I relax, things slip. I have seen it happen to other department heads. They let go of the reins and within a semester the curriculum is a mess, the exam dates are wrong, and everyone is scrambling. I will not be that person.
entries.nudge_text: What would actually slip if you let go?

entries.initial_entry: Third time writing today but Patricia's comment is still rattling around in my head. She is not wrong that I bring work home. But she married a man who takes charge of things. That is who I am at school and at the junta and at home. I do not know how to be half of that.

Sent the coordinadora an email Monday asking for approval to add a new elective on Colombian political history for next semester. It is Thursday and nothing. No reply, no acknowledgment. I know she read it because she responded to a facilities request I cc'd her on the same day. My proposal just sits there while I wait for someone who probably has not taught a class in a decade to decide whether my idea has merit.
entries.nudge_text: The silence is saying something to you?
entries.response_text: It is saying she does not respect the department heads enough to respond promptly. That is an organizational problem, not a personal one.

entries.initial_entry: The coordinadora finally replied. She likes the elective idea but wants to "co-develop" it with the social sciences department, meaning Profesor Ríos gets to put his fingerprints on my proposal. I wanted to push back but she framed it as collaboration and you cannot argue against that without looking difficult. So I said sounds good. It does not sound good. My idea is about to become a committee project where fifteen years of expertise get diluted into consensus.

Had a team standup today and Ravi brought up that our payment processing pipeline is rejecting a disproportionate number of transactions from certain Southeast Asian corridors. The data was right there in the dashboard. Mostly remittance flows, the kind migrant workers use to send money home. Something about the fraud model flagging certain patterns that are just... normal behavior for people sending small amounts frequently.

I knew what I was looking at. I've sat across the table from guys at the worker center who tell me how hard it is to send money back to their families, the fees they pay, the delays. And here's our system making it worse. But when Ravi raised it, the lead just said we'd revisit the thresholds next quarter and moved on. I could have pushed. I had the context, the stories, the data to make a case. But I just nodded and typed something in the chat about checking the logs.

Part of me says it wasn't the right moment, that I need to build a proper case first. But honestly I think I was just worried about being the guy who derails standup with a soapbox moment. My parents always told me not to stick out, keep your head down and do good work. Maybe that advice served them well in a factory town in Fujian. I'm not sure it serves me well here, sitting on information that could change something.
entries.nudge_text: What would you have said if you'd spoken up?
entries.response_text: Probably something about how the people on the other end of those rejected transactions aren't abstractions. They're the same guys I see on Saturday mornings trying to figure out medical claims. But saying that out loud at work feels like crossing a line I can't uncross.

entries.initial_entry:
Went to the worker center this morning. There was a new guy, Suresh, from Tamil Nadu. Fell from scaffolding three weeks ago and his employer hasn't filed the injury report. He showed me the bruises on his back, purple-yellow, still swelling around his ribs. I helped him draft a letter to MOM. My Mandarin-accented English and his Tamil-accented English, both of us stumbling through legalese neither of us was built for.

But we got it done. He shook my hand with both of his and said thank you in a way that made me want to cry in the stairwell afterward. I held it together. Barely.
entries.nudge_text: What was it about his thank you?
entries.response_text: It felt undeserved. All I did was help write a letter. He's the one living through it, sleeping on a bunk bed with broken ribs because he can't afford to stop working.

entries.initial_entry:
Normal Monday. Dropped Xiao Yu at school and she asked me why some of her classmates' parents drive them and we take the bus. I told her we like the bus. Which is half true. We can afford a car, we just choose not to, but explaining that to a seven-year-old is its own project.

Work was the usual sprint backlog. Nothing exciting, nothing soul-crushing. Had lunch with a colleague and we talked about the new condo launches. Everyone around me seems to be upgrading something, their flat, their car, their phone. I don't feel the pull the way I used to, but I notice it like background noise you can't quite tune out.
Same day, later. Ordered from Grab because I was too tired to cook. While eating I scrolled past an article about plastic waste in the Java Sea. Closed it, kept eating from the styrofoam container.
Rainy morning. The uncle at the kopi stall asked about my daughter. Drank my coffee and watched the wet road for a while before heading to the MRT.

Team dinner tonight. Quarterly thing, company card. The conversation turned to hiring and someone made a comment about how candidates from certain countries need more "handholding." A few people laughed. I didn't laugh but I also didn't say anything. Just moved the ice around in my glass.

The thing is I know exactly what that comment means. I've been on the receiving end of it, the assumption that because you come from a certain place you're somehow less. And I've watched the guys at the center deal with far worse versions of the same attitude every day. But at the table I just sat there because it was a team dinner and I didn't want to make it awkward. I'm so tired of choosing comfort over what I actually think.
entries.nudge_text: Is it always about comfort, or is something else at play?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Probably both. It's easier to blame it on not wanting confrontation than to admit I'm scared of being seen differently by people I work with every day.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week but today felt different. Took Xiao Yu to East Coast Park and she spent an hour picking up shells and bits of plastic from the sand. She didn't separate them at first, just collecting, and then she looked at her bucket and said the plastic ones aren't pretty. I asked what we should do with them and she said throw them away properly. We spent another thirty minutes walking to find recycling and trash bins.

It's a small thing. A kid picking up beach trash. But watching her make that distinction on her own, without being told, reminded me of why Mei Ling and I try to live the way we do. Not because we're going to fix the ocean or the climate or anything so grand, but because the habits matter. The noticing matters.

Came home and cooked dinner together, the three of us. Xiao Yu was in charge of washing vegetables. She takes the job very seriously, inspecting each leaf. Mei Ling and I exchanged a look over her head, the kind of look that says we might be doing okay at this. It's been an exhausting few weeks but tonight I feel settled in a way I haven't for a while.
entries.nudge_text: What made tonight different from the other exhausting days?

entries.initial_entry: Worker center morning. Helped two new guys file their claims. One of them brought me a packet of murukku his wife made. Sat on the bench outside eating it and feeling grateful for this strange second life I have on Saturdays.

Sprint deadline tomorrow and I'm at the office late. The PM asked me to cut corners on the data validation layer for the remittance feature, the same one Ravi flagged weeks ago. She said we'll circle back to it after launch. I know what "circle back" means. It means never.

I said okay. I could have pushed back, could have said the validation matters because real people's money is on the line and if we ship it broken the ones who suffer are the ones who can least afford it. But the deadline is real and I'm tired and sometimes you just want to go home. Chose what was easy for me. The people affected won't even know my name.

The remittance feature launched last week. Already seeing support tickets about failed transfers. Mostly from corridors serving Bangladesh and Myanmar. I pulled the data quietly and it's what I expected, the validation issues I was asked to skip are causing real failures for real people.

Brought it up in a 1:1 with my lead. He agreed it's a problem but said the next sprint is already planned and we can't squeeze it in. I nodded. Again. I keep telling myself I'll escalate next time, build the case, find the right moment. But the right moment keeps not arriving and meanwhile people can't send money to their families.
entries.nudge_text: How many right moments have passed now?
entries.response_text: Too many. I've lost count. At some point the excuse stops being about timing and starts being about me.

entries.initial_entry:
Xiao Yu asked me at dinner what I do at work. I gave the usual answer, daddy helps move money from one place to another so people can buy things. She asked if I help everyone and I said I try to. But sitting there with her looking at me I felt the gap between that answer and reality like a physical weight.

Mei Ling could tell something was off. She squeezed my hand under the table. Later I told her about the remittance tickets and she asked me what I was going to do. I said I didn't know. She said that's a start, at least you're not pretending you're fine with it. She's right. I'm not fine with it. I've been choosing convenience over doing what I know matters and each time it gets a little easier to justify, which is exactly what scares me.
entries.nudge_text: What's one thing you could do this week?

entries.initial_entry: The Hamburg hub finally hit ninety-six percent on-time and I didn't even get a mention in the quarterly call. Kramer presented the numbers like they were his. Sat there chewing my pen while the board nodded along.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from speaking up during the call?
entries.response_text: Politics. Kramer reports to the CEO directly. Picking a fight on camera would only hurt me.

entries.initial_entry:
Still stewing over the call. Lotte opened a bottle of that 2019 Brunello we'd been saving for something and I couldn't even enjoy it properly. Kept replaying the moment where Kramer said "the regional team" instead of my name. The regional team. Like the numbers came out of nowhere.

It's not just vanity, though people would say it is. When you run three sites and you restructure the nightshift rotation yourself and you push back on corporate for months to get the new sorting line funded, having your name attached to the outcome matters. It's how you move up. It's how people know who to call when the next VP seat opens.
entries.nudge_text: What happens if the VP seat opens and they don't think of you?
entries.response_text: Then six years of building this region into the best-performing in the company would have been a waste. And I'd have to seriously think about whether I stay or find somewhere that notices.

entries.initial_entry: Two of my warehouse leads called in sick on the same day and I spent six hours on the floor sorting parcels like it's 2003. My back is wrecked. Cancelled dinner reservations at that new place in Katendrecht because I couldn't face sitting upright for two hours.

Had the one-on-one with Kramer today. Brought up the quarterly call, kept it measured. He said he appreciated my "team-first attitude" and that everyone knows who does the real work. Classic deflection. He even patted me on the shoulder on the way out, which I found condescending but smiled through anyway.

Lotte says I should document everything from now on. She's probably right. She also said I need to stop letting work ruin my evenings, because last weekend I spent the entire Saturday sailing trip checking emails on my phone instead of actually being out there. She's definitely right about that one. I barely noticed the water.
entries.nudge_text: You said you barely noticed the water. How often does that happen — being somewhere you chose to be but not really being there?
entries.response_text: More than I'd like. But that's the deal when you run things, isn't it.

entries.initial_entry: Got the green light to hire two senior coordinators for the Antwerp hub. My budget, my picks, no committee. Finally something that feels like actual authority instead of managing upwards all day. Already have a shortlist.

Hosted twelve people for dinner last night. Lotte paired every course — she did the lamb with a Châteauneuf-du-Pape that made van der Berg's wife close her eyes when she tasted it. That's the kind of evening I live for. Good food, the right people, candles on the canal, Lotte in her element. Van der Berg even asked if we'd consider doing a supper club. I said maybe, but honestly I don't want to turn it into an obligation.

The thing is, half the guests were people from the industry. Not a coincidence. You build your network over a cheese course, not in a meeting room. Van der Berg sits on the advisory board at Port of Rotterdam. His wife runs procurement at Maersk Benelux. I like these people genuinely — I do — but I also know exactly what those relationships are worth. Lotte says I can never just have fun without an angle. She's not wrong, but I don't see why it can't be both.
entries.nudge_text: When Lotte says you always have an angle — does that bother you or does it feel like a compliment?
entries.response_text: Depends on the day. Sometimes I think she sees through me in a way that's useful. Other times it makes me feel like I can't just enjoy something without her questioning my motives.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. The Antwerp expansion hit a snag — permits delayed, which means the two coordinators I hired start in three weeks with nothing structured for them to do. I've been on calls with the municipal office, with legal, with the construction firm, trying to unstick things. None of it is technically my job. The site manager should be handling permits. But if I leave it to him we'll lose another month, and I can't afford that with the VP shortlist conversation supposedly happening in Q3.

Lotte and I were supposed to go to Bruges for the weekend. She booked a hotel with a rooftop terrace and a Michelin-star restaurant for Saturday night. I told her I'd need to work Saturday morning and she went quiet in that way she does. Not angry, just — recalibrating. She went anyway, took her friend Anneke. I stayed home and spent the day on a laptop in my bathrobe eating cheese toast. By four o'clock I realised the permit issue was probably going to take another two weeks regardless of whether I spent Saturday on it or not.

I could have been in Bruges. I could have been sitting on a rooftop with a glass of something excellent, watching the sun go down with the person I like most in the world. Instead I ate cheese toast. I don't know what to do with that information.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take for you to have gone to Bruges instead?

entries.initial_entry: Lotte showed me photos from Bruges. The terrace, the food, the canal at sunset. She wasn't rubbing it in — she just wanted to share. I looked at the photos and felt something I can only describe as stupid. Not sad. Stupid.

Permits came through. Both coordinators start Monday and the fit-out begins next week. I walked the Antwerp floor this morning and rearranged the staging layout myself because the site manager's plan was going to create a bottleneck at dock three. He didn't love me overriding him but he'll see the throughput numbers in two weeks and come around.

Took Friday off. Actually took it off — phone in a drawer, sailed out to the Grevelingenmeer with nobody but the cats in my head for company. Grilled fish on the dock afterwards, drank a cold Riesling, watched the light change on the water for an hour. Didn't think about Kramer or permits or throughput once. Well, maybe once. Lotte would be proud. Didn't tell her about the once.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned the phone in the drawer like it was a big deal. Was it?
entries.response_text: Yes. I check it probably forty times a day on weekends. Old habit from when I was on-call as a shift lead. Now it's just control.

entries.initial_entry:
VP shortlist leaked. Three names. I'm one of them. Kramer is not, which should make me feel vindicated but instead I just feel the weight of it. If I get this, I'm responsible for all five regions across the Benelux. Sixty direct reports through my managers. A seat at the executive table. Everything I've been manoeuvring towards for years.

But there's a clause in the offer framework that the role is based in Amsterdam and requires eighty percent in-office. Lotte's business is in Rotterdam. The canal house is in Rotterdam. The boat is twenty minutes from Rotterdam. I'd be commuting two hours a day or we'd need a second flat in Amsterdam, which means I'd be living half my life in a box near Zuidas eating takeaway alone while Lotte hosts wine tastings without me.

I keep doing the maths in my head. More status, more scope, more say in how the company runs. But also more time in transit, fewer evenings on the terrace, fewer dinners where Lotte pairs a wine so perfect it changes the conversation. I don't know how you weigh those things against each other. Every time I think I've decided, I flip. Lotte hasn't said what she thinks yet. I think she's waiting to see what I actually want before she tells me what she wants, and honestly that scares me more than the interview.
entries.nudge_text: If Lotte told you she wanted you to take it, would that make it easier or harder?
entries.response_text: Harder. Because then I couldn't blame the decision on protecting what we have. I'd have to admit I want it and deal with what that costs.

entries.initial_entry:
Something about today left me flat and I can't quite shake it. We had a cross-functional sync, the kind where everyone brings their updates and pretends to care about everyone else's. I'd prepared a slide on our Q4 handoff plan — tight, clean, the numbers right there. Got two sentences in and Marianne from commercial just started talking over me. Not even a segue, just launched into her own thing like I hadn't been mid-sentence.

The worst part is nobody said anything. Not Pieter, not Jan-Willem, nobody. The meeting chair just let it flow and asked her a follow-up question. My slide was still up on the screen. I sat there staring at it. I could have interrupted her back, could have said "I wasn't finished," but I was so caught off guard I just waited. And then the moment was gone. We moved to the next agenda item.

It's the kind of thing that shouldn't bother me this much but I've been replaying it all evening. I keep thinking about what I should have done. Maybe it's because I'm tired. Maybe I'm reading into it. But if it were someone else — if it were Thomas or Sven — nobody would have talked over them. I know that for a fact.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think made it so hard to push back?
entries.response_text: It wasn't about pushing back, really. I just think meetings like that should have a basic level of order. If I'm presenting, let me present. It's not complicated. The chair should have handled it.

entries.initial_entry:
Standard Friday. Had the weekly with my directs, went through capacity, flagged the resource gap on the warehouse integration project. Nothing dramatic. Sent a summary to Pieter afterwards like I always do. He replied with a thumbs-up emoji, which tells me absolutely nothing about whether he actually read it.

Lunch was fine. Grabbed a broodje with Stefan from finance. He mentioned they're hiring a new FP&A lead and wanted to know if I had any opinions on the JD. I gave him a few pointers — told him to make the reporting line clearer because last time the role was too ambiguous and nobody respected whoever sat in it. He seemed to take it on board. Small thing, but at least someone asks.
entries.nudge_text: Does it land differently when people actually ask?
entries.response_text: Of course it does. If someone values your input enough to come find you at lunch, that's real. A thumbs-up on Teams is just someone clearing their inbox. But it's not like I'm keeping score. Stefan and I go back a while, that's all.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent half of Thursday chasing a sign-off from legal on the new vendor contract. I'd sent the redlines Monday, followed up Wednesday, and then Thursday morning I walked over to Anneke's desk and she hadn't even opened the attachment. Just said she'd been "swamped" and would get to it "when she could." I asked if there was a way to escalate and she looked at me like I'd insulted her grandmother.

So now I'm sitting on a contract I can't execute, the vendor's account manager has emailed me twice asking for a timeline, and I have to go back to my team and tell them we're delayed because someone in another department can't open a PDF. The whole thing makes me feel like I'm running a team with no real authority over the things that actually affect our deadlines. I can plan all I want — doesn't matter if someone else holds the pen.

Really frustrated tonight. Had a project review with the steering committee — the one Pieter chairs, with Marianne, Thomas, and two of the directors. I'd built out the entire rollback scenario for the platform migration, spent the weekend on the risk matrix, mapped out every dependency. Came in ready.

Thomas disagreed with the timeline. Said we should push back the cutover by two sprints. I explained why that would blow up the downstream handoffs and showed the cascade impact. He just shrugged and said "better safe." The directors nodded along with him, didn't ask me a single follow-up. Pieter said "let's go with Thomas's view for now and revisit in January."

For now. Like my analysis was a suggestion. I didn't fight it because what's the point — they'd already decided. Thomas has been here longer, has a louder voice in those rooms, and people just default to his read. I sat through the rest of the meeting barely listening. Kept thinking: I did the work, I had the data, and none of it mattered because the room had already picked a side before I even opened my mouth.

Went home and just sat on the couch for an hour. Didn't even eat properly. It's not about the timeline itself — I can live with the delay. It's that feeling of preparing something thorough and watching it get waved away. Like the effort was irrelevant.
entries.nudge_text: Is it the outcome or being overruled that stings more?
entries.response_text: Look, I said it myself — the delay is fine. It's the process. If you're going to ask me to do the analysis, at least engage with it. Don't just nod at the person who says "better safe" and call it a day. That's not how you run a steering committee. But I suppose that's how things work around here.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a strange moment today. HR sent around the invitations for the annual leadership development programme — the one that feeds into the senior management pipeline. Last year I wasn't eligible because of tenure. This year I definitely am. But when the list of nominees came out, my name wasn't on it. Thomas was on it. Marianne was on it. Even Daan, who joined six months after me and runs a smaller team.

I asked my line manager about it and he said nominations go through "calibration" with the directors and he'd "look into it." Which is code for: I don't know and I won't push it. He said it as if it were a minor admin glitch, like a wrong room booking. I stood there nodding, said thanks, walked back to my desk.

I keep going back and forth. Part of me thinks it's a genuine oversight — maybe my name slipped through, maybe someone forgot to tick a box. But then I think about the steering committee last week, about Marianne talking over me, about how Pieter barely registers my updates, and it starts to feel like a pattern. Like somewhere along the line, the people who decide these things stopped seeing me as someone on the upward track.

I don't want to be the person who reads slights into everything. But I also don't want to be the person who just lets things pass and wakes up three years from now still in the same chair, wondering what happened.
entries.nudge_text: What would pushing on this actually look like?
entries.response_text: That's the thing I keep turning over. I could email the HR business partner directly, ask for the criteria, make a case. I've done that kind of thing before — when I wanted the title change, I didn't wait for anyone to offer it. I put together a one-pager and walked into my manager's office. But this feels different. The title was something I could argue on merit. This feels more like... I need people to already think of me a certain way, and if they don't, no one-pager is going to change that. It's the kind of thing you can't just demand. And that's what makes it worse, honestly.

entries.initial_entry:
Keep circling back to the meeting from Monday. Different meeting this time, ops standup. Clara was presenting the warehouse KPI dashboard she'd been working on — my idea originally, I flagged it in September and asked her to build it out. She did a good job, I'll say that. Clean design, right metrics. But when she presented it to the wider ops group, she introduced it as her initiative. "I noticed we had a gap in visibility so I put this together."

I didn't correct her. Maybe I should have. But it would have looked petty, right? Standing up in a standup and saying "actually, I told you to do that." Nobody wants to be that manager. So I just sat there, and people praised her for the initiative, and Pieter said something like "great ownership, Clara," and I thought: that was mine.

It's not about credit exactly, or maybe it is, I don't know. It's more like — I set the direction, I identified the problem, I allocated the resource, and the visible output gets attributed to someone else. And the thing is, Clara probably doesn't even realize she did it. She's not scheming. She just stood up and told the story from her perspective. But her perspective conveniently started after I handed her the brief.

Small thing. Shouldn't matter. But it does. Especially this week, after the nomination list and the steering committee and all of it. Feels like I'm becoming invisible in my own team.

Monday morning. Had a one-on-one with Daan — he's been struggling with a resource conflict between two of his workstreams and wanted to talk it through. I helped him map out the trade-offs, walked him through the prioritisation framework I use, and by the end of it he had a clear plan. He thanked me and said he wished more people explained things the way I do. That landed well.

Afterwards I realised that's the version of my job I actually like. Not the steering committees, not the nomination lists, not the politics of who gets heard in which room. Just someone coming to me with a problem, me helping them think it through, and both of us walking away with something useful. I know the other stuff matters — it does — but today reminded me there's a part of this I'm actually good at that has nothing to do with who's watching.
entries.nudge_text: Has it always been like that — the two sides?
entries.response_text: More or less, yes. The helping part has always come naturally. The other part — the positioning, making sure the right people notice — that's something I taught myself. I used to think I needed both to move forward. Maybe I still do. But today was a reminder that one of them actually fills me up and the other just drains me. Doesn't change what I need to do, but it's good to notice, I think.

entries.initial_entry:
Mummyji made a comment at lunch about the indigo stains on my fingernails. She didn't say anything directly, just that thing she does where she looks at my hands and then looks at the dal and raises her eyebrows. Ravi was right there and said nothing. I know he thinks the dyeing thing is fine, he's told me so, but he won't say it in front of her. So I'm the one who looks like I'm neglecting things.

The thing is, the kitchen was spotless. Lunch was on time. Arjun's tiffin was packed before school. I don't know what more she wants from me. Or maybe I do know and I just don't want to give it, which is this complete surrender of any time that's mine. Like the house should swallow me whole and I should be grateful for it.

I scrubbed the indigo off after lunch. Took ten minutes with lemon and baking soda. I shouldn't have had to do that. But I did it anyway and I'm not sure what that says about me.
entries.nudge_text: Why did you scrub them off?
entries.response_text: Because it was easier than sitting through another silent dinner with her staring at my hands. I pick my fights and that one wasn't worth it. But it felt like erasing something.

entries.initial_entry:
Arjun's parent-teacher meeting today. The class teacher mentioned he draws during free period instead of playing cricket with the other boys and asked if everything is okay at home. I wanted to say, yes, everything is fine, my son likes drawing, why is that a problem? But I smiled and nodded and said I'd encourage him to be more social.

Came home and told Ravi about it and he said maybe Arjun should join a sport, that it would be good for him. And I thought, here we go. Everyone has an opinion about what the boy should be doing except the boy himself.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from saying that to the teacher?
entries.response_text: I don't know. You just don't, do you? Not in that setting.

entries.initial_entry: Made aloo paratha for breakfast the way Appa used to, with extra ajwain in the dough. The smell filled the whole kitchen and for a second I was back in our old house in Mysore, watching him roll them out, flour everywhere.

Spent the whole morning at the Diwali mela helping set up the community puja. I did the rangoli at the entrance the way my grandmother taught me, rice flour with turmeric borders, none of the acrylic paint shortcuts the younger crowd uses. Three women came up and asked me to show them how. Felt good.

Later, back home, I worked on a new indigo resist pattern. Nobody was around to comment on it. Ravi had taken Arjun and Vihaan to the cricket match. Two hours of silence and my hands doing what they wanted to do. I didn't even check my phone.
entries.nudge_text: Which part of the day stuck with you more?

entries.initial_entry:
Mummyji wants me to start teaching Vihaan the Vishnu Sahasranama. He's seven. She says she learned it by six. I said I'd start next week, which I will, because it's important, Appa made sure I knew it by heart and I'm glad he did. But there's this edge to the way she asks, like she's testing whether I'm doing enough.

Ravi's cousin Priya called in the evening. She started a small catering business last year and wanted to know if I'd be interested in supplying hand-dyed table linens. I said I'd think about it. Didn't mention it to anyone at home yet.
entries.nudge_text: Are you going to tell Ravi about Priya's offer?

entries.initial_entry:
Sat down this morning to teach Vihaan the first twenty verses. He's quicker than I expected. He kept asking what the words meant and I found myself explaining them properly, not just the rote way I was taught but actually talking about what each name describes. He asked why Vishnu has so many names and I said because one name is never enough for something that big. He seemed satisfied with that.

It reminded me of sitting with Appa in the puja room, early mornings, the smell of camphor and sandalwood paste. He was patient. He never rushed me or made it feel like a chore. I want to give Vihaan that same feeling, not the pressure of getting it right, but the warmth of sitting together with something that matters. The sense that this is ours, this thread that connects us backward through generations, something nobody can take away or schedule over.

Mummyji passed by the room and listened for a while and actually smiled. She didn't say anything, didn't correct his pronunciation or mine. She just stood there and smiled. That was enough for today.

Spent the afternoon cataloguing my dye samples. I have fourteen shades of indigo now, ranging from almost-grey to a deep navy that I got by double-dipping with a fermentation vat I built from a recipe in an old Kalamkari manual. Labelled each one with the mordant used, the dip count, the fabric type. Cotton takes differently than silk, and I'm getting more consistent results with the cotton muslin I sourced from a weaver in Solapur.

Priya messaged again about the linens. She has a wedding order for February. Twelve table runners, twenty napkins. The money would be decent. I told her I need to think about scale. My terrace setup can handle maybe four meters of fabric at a time. I'd need to figure out drying space and timing around the kids' schedules.

Vihaan came up to the terrace after his homework and asked if he could dip his hand in the vat. I let him. His palm came out blue and he ran downstairs to show everyone. Mummyji was not pleased.
entries.nudge_text: So are you doing it?
entries.response_text: Haven't decided. Need to work out the logistics first.

entries.initial_entry:
Told Ravi about Priya's proposal over chai after the boys went to bed. He was more supportive than I expected. He said if I wanted to try it, he'd help me figure out the money side. He even suggested converting part of the back store room into a workspace. I hadn't thought about that.

What surprised me was my own hesitation. He was offering exactly what I thought I wanted, backing, space, permission. But when it was right there in front of me I felt this pull backward, like, who am I to run a business? My mother didn't. Mummyji didn't. All the women I grew up watching ran homes, not enterprises. And I love running this home. That's the part that gets lost when people hear I want to do something else. I don't want to leave, I want to also.
entries.nudge_text: What does 'also' actually look like day to day?
entries.response_text: Mornings with the boys, school drop-off, puja, cooking. Afternoons when they're in school or doing homework, that's my window. Three, maybe four hours. It's not a lot but it's mine. I just need people to stop treating those hours like leftover time that belongs to the house.

entries.initial_entry:
Mummyji brought up the store room idea at dinner, in front of Ravi's parents who were visiting. She said something like, "Meera is thinking of opening a factory in the house." She was smiling but it was the wrong kind of smile. Ravi's father asked what she was talking about and I had to explain the dyeing and the linen idea in front of everyone while trying not to sound like I was defending myself.

Ravi stepped in eventually and said it was just a small project. The word "small" stuck. I know he was trying to help but it felt like he was shrinking it to make it palatable. I didn't say anything after that. Finished dinner. Cleared the plates. Went upstairs.

Can't sleep. Ravi's parents left after dinner and everyone is being normal now but I keep replaying the table conversation. The worst part isn't what Mummyji said, it's that nobody asked me a real question about it. Nobody said, what kind of fabric, what's the process, how did you learn. They only wanted to know if it would interfere with anything.

I keep thinking about Appa's workshop in Mysore. He repaired watches. Tiny, patient, precise work. Nobody ever questioned whether he should be doing it. It was just what he did. I want that. Not the permission, not the approval. Just the quiet assumption that of course I do this, it's mine.
entries.nudge_text: What would Appa say about the indigo?

entries.initial_entry:
Took the boys to the Ganesh temple this morning for the monthly abhishekam. Vihaan recited the first twenty verses of the Sahasranama on the auto ride there, unprompted. I didn't even know he'd been practising on his own. The priest noticed and said something to me about keeping these things alive. I felt proud but also this strange defensiveness, like I needed to prove I wasn't only about the dyeing, that I still carry all of this too.

Why do I feel like I have to choose? Like every hour I spend with the indigo is an hour stolen from something else. Nobody told Ravi he has to choose between his consulting work and being a father. It's just assumed he can be both.
entries.nudge_text: Who's actually making you choose?

entries.initial_entry:
Called Priya back tonight and said yes to the February order. Twelve runners, twenty napkins, indigo with a kalamkari-inspired border block I've been working on. She sounded happy. I felt terrified and then calm, in that order, like jumping into cold water.

Haven't told Mummyji yet. Told Ravi, who said congratulations and then asked if I needed help setting up the store room this weekend. I said yes. Vihaan overheard and asked if he could help with the blue. I said yes to that too. I don't know if I'm doing the right thing. But I know I'm doing my thing, and right now that has to be enough.

Pieter talked right over me in the steering committee today, mid-sentence, and presented the container routing proposal like it was his. I just sat there. Too tired to fight it, too angry to let it go, and now it's midnight and I'm still turning it over.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from calling it out right there?

entries.initial_entry: Ran the quarterly review myself today, no slides from anyone else, just my numbers, my narrative. The room was quiet in that good way where people are actually listening. Felt like the division is finally mine in the way I wanted it to be.

Needed sign-off from Legal on the new supplier contract. Straightforward, or so I thought. Sent it Thursday, followed up Friday morning, got told Anke was "reviewing priorities" and would get to it when she could. Meanwhile the supplier's deadline is Tuesday and I'm the one who'll look like I dropped the ball.

It's not even about the contract, really. It's that I had to sit there refreshing my inbox like some junior waiting for permission. I run a forty-three person division and I can't move a standard supplier agreement through because someone in a different department decides the timeline. I almost went to the CEO directly but thought better of it. Probably should have.
entries.nudge_text: Why did you hold back from going to the CEO?

entries.initial_entry: Routine day. Back-to-back calls, budget reconciliation, nothing unusual. Grabbed a broodje kroket from the canteen and ate it at my desk between meetings.

Monday. Staff meeting went long because Thomas wanted to discuss the new reporting structure again. I'd already decided on it two weeks ago and communicated it clearly, but apparently some people need to re-litigate everything. Let them talk for ten minutes, then moved on.

Lotte asked how work was over dinner. I said fine. She gave me that look, the one that means she knows I'm not actually answering. I wasn't trying to be evasive, I just genuinely didn't have the energy to translate the day into something she'd find interesting. Watched the football afterward, fell asleep on the couch again.

Had a clear plan for the fleet optimization rollout. Presented it in the ops meeting. Sandra pushed back hard, said we should phase it differently, go region by region instead of all at once. Others started nodding along with her, and I could feel the room shifting.

I ended up agreeing to her phased approach. It's not wrong, technically. But it wasn't my call anymore, and everyone in that room saw it happen. I keep telling myself it doesn't matter how we get there as long as we get there. Not sure I believe that.
entries.nudge_text: What exactly changed when the room shifted to Sandra's side?
entries.response_text: It wasn't about the plan. It was that they looked at her like she was in charge of the decision.

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday but I can't stop thinking about Friday. New hire, Jeroen, fresh from his MBA, has been here eight weeks. In the project kickoff he interrupted me to "build on" my point, then spent five minutes explaining container throughput metrics to me like I haven't been running this operation for a decade. The room just let it happen. Nobody stepped in.
