What gets me is that I know this kid's type. He's positioning. He's already had coffee with the CEO twice, which I only know because I saw it in the calendar. And I just sat there and let him reframe my own analysis back to me. I should have shut it down. I should have said, "Jeroen, I've been doing this since before you started your bachelor's." But I didn't. I smiled and nodded like it was a collaborative discussion.

I keep thinking about what my father would say. He ran his own business for thirty years and nobody ever talked over him because he made sure of it from day one. I don't know when I started letting people do this to me. Maybe it's the restructure, maybe I'm spread too thin to fight every battle. But it feels like something I'm losing and I can't quite name what it is.
entries.nudge_text: What is it you think you're losing?
entries.response_text: The room's assumption that I'm the one who decides.

entries.initial_entry: Pieter did it again in the client call. Spoke over me, redirected the conversation to his numbers. I corrected him this time but it came out sharper than I meant and the client noticed.

Quiet Sunday, Lotte took the girls to her mother's in Leiden. House to myself. Made coffee the slow way, French press, sat in the kitchen looking at the garden. The hedge needs cutting again. Thought about calling my brother but didn't, we'd just end up talking about the house in Zeeland that neither of us wants to deal with.

Spent an hour going through old emails, not for work reasons, just sorting. Found some from when I first got promoted to division head. I was so careful in those early messages, checking every word. Now I fire things off without thinking. Not sure if that's confidence or just not caring as much.

Had a one-on-one with Lisa from my team today. She's been struggling with the new reporting system and came in looking like she expected to be reprimanded. I didn't do that. Just walked through it with her, showed her the shortcuts I use, told her it took me three months to stop hating the interface too. She actually laughed.

Small thing, but it reminded me of something. When people come to me for direction and I give it clearly, no games, no politics, just here's how we do this — that's when I feel most like myself. Not the boardroom stuff, not the jockeying. Just someone who knows what they're doing, passing it on. Drove home in a decent mood for once.

Board prep meeting today. The CFO asked me to "walk him through" my division's numbers as if I hadn't already submitted a forty-page deck last week. He does this to me every quarter. He doesn't do it to Pieter, doesn't do it to Sandra. Just me. Like I need to audition for the right to present my own results.

I complied, obviously. Went through it line by line, smiled, answered his follow-ups. Drove home and sat in the car in the garage for ten minutes before going inside. Lotte asked if I was okay and I said yes. I wasn't really.
entries.nudge_text: What were you thinking about in those ten minutes in the car?
entries.response_text: Just decompressing. Long day.

entries.initial_entry: End of Q1. Numbers look solid, team's holding together, nothing on fire. Booked a table at De Kas for Lotte's birthday next week. Normal Monday, which at this point I'll take.

I had a proper meltdown today, and I'm not even sorry about it. My mother-in-law came over for what was supposed to be a "quick visit" and ended up rearranging half my kitchen because apparently I was storing the spices wrong. Wrong! As if there's a universally correct way to store paprika. I didn't say anything at first, just watched her shuffle things around with that satisfied look on her face, and then something snapped. I told her, very calmly, that I'd prefer she asks before moving things in my home. She looked at me like I'd slapped her.

Erik called later and said she was upset, that I'd embarrassed her. Maybe I did. But this is my space. I've set it up the way that works for me, for Astrid, for our little morning routine where she "helps" me make breakfast by pointing at ingredients. It's taken me months to figure out a system that doesn't make me feel like I'm drowning in domestic chaos. And someone just walked in and decided she knew better.

I know she meant well. I know that. But meaning well isn't the same as respecting that I've built something here on purpose. Not by accident. On purpose.
entries.nudge_text: What specifically about your system makes mornings work?

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the whole morning on a commission that I probably should have turned down. A client wanted a custom piece—a mountain landscape in my style—but then kept sending "small suggestions" that were basically a different style entirely. Softer edges, she said. More realistic trees. At some point I had to ask myself: is this even my work anymore, or am I just a hand executing someone else's vision?

I did finish it, and she loved it. Paid extra, even. But sitting here now, looking at the file, I feel hollow about it. It's not bad. It's just not mine. And that distinction matters to me more than I sometimes admit.

There's a practical voice in my head that says income is income, especially since the shop has been slow this month. But there's another voice—louder, if I'm honest—that asks what the point is if I'm not making the things I actually want to make. I could have spent those hours on my own collection instead. I chose not to. For money. And now I'm sitting with that choice.

I don't regret it exactly. I just notice how it sits differently than the pieces I create for myself.
entries.nudge_text: What would you have drawn for yourself those hours?
entries.response_text: I've had this image in my head for weeks—a single figure standing at the edge of a frozen lake, back to the viewer, and the ice is cracking but in a pattern that looks almost like tree roots spreading. It's about choosing to step forward even when the ground feels unstable. I've sketched it twice but haven't committed to the full piece yet. That's what I would have worked on.

entries.initial_entry: Astrid started saying "no" to everything today and honestly, same. I get it kid. Sometimes every option feels like the wrong one.
entries.nudge_text: Which options?
entries.response_text: Oh, nothing specific really. Just one of those days where even deciding what to make for dinner feels like too much. Probably just tired.

entries.initial_entry:
Erik brought up the idea of Astrid starting daycare two days a week. He framed it as giving me more time for the shop, which sounds supportive, but I could hear the subtext loud and clear: he thinks I'm not managing well. Or that I'm wasting time. Or both.

And maybe I am struggling some days. Maybe there are afternoons where Astrid naps and I just sit on the couch scrolling instead of working on prints. But that's my choice to make. I'm the one here every day. I know what I need. Some days I need to create. Some days I need to rest. And I don't need someone who's at an office until 7pm deciding what my schedule should look like.

I know daycare isn't a punishment. Plenty of parents do it, and their kids are fine, great even. It's not about whether daycare is good or bad. It's about this being my decision, about my time, about my daughter—and it wasn't offered as an option. It was presented as a solution to a problem I didn't say I had.

I told him I'd think about it. But I'm not sure what I'm supposed to be thinking about. Whether to accept having my days restructured for me?
entries.nudge_text: What would need to be different for daycare to feel like your idea?

entries.initial_entry:
Later the same day now, after Erik and I talked again. Astrid's finally asleep. I'm on the couch with a glass of wine that I'm too tired to actually drink.

We didn't fight, exactly. He backed off the daycare thing, said he just wanted to help. And I believe him. But I still feel weird. Like I have to defend choices I make in my own home. Like staying home with my daughter and running my shop is something that requires justification.

My mother worked full-time, same as my dad. They split everything 50/50, very modern, very practical. When I told them I was going to stay home after Astrid was born, they didn't say it was wrong, but they didn't say it was right either. Just... nothing. And Erik's family has a stay-at-home mom who followed rules and schedules I can't even fathom. Neither model fits me. I'm not building my life based on their blueprints.

I'm so tired of feeling like I'm doing this wrong no matter which direction I lean. I'm doing it my way. That's supposed to be enough.
entries.nudge_text: What does your way actually look like tomorrow?
entries.response_text: Tomorrow I'll wake up at 5:30 before Astrid, make coffee, and spend an hour on the frozen lake piece before she's up. Then we'll do breakfast together—she helps me pick which fruit to cut. After lunch when she naps, I'll work on shop orders or answer emails, depending on what feels right in that moment. Maybe I'll take a walk with her in the late afternoon to the park she likes. Erik comes home, I hand off, and the evening is mine. That's it. That's my way. Flexible but intentional.

entries.initial_entry: Sold three prints today from my original collection, not commissions. Actual things I made because I wanted to make them. It's not life-changing money but it's proof that what I create on my own terms has value to someone else too.
Astrid poured yogurt on the cat today. I should have been faster but honestly I was sketching in my head and didn't notice she'd climbed onto the counter. We're all learning I guess.
entries.nudge_text: What are you learning?
entries.response_text: That I can't always be in two places at once. That some moments require my full presence and others can bend a little. That the cat will forgive us. That my brain doesn't shut off just because I'm watching a toddler, and maybe that's okay—maybe even necessary for my sanity.

entries.initial_entry:
I got an email today from my old agency in Amsterdam. They're doing a freelance project and apparently thought of me. Just a small illustration job, a few days of work, good pay. The catch is I'd need to come into the office for meetings, which means finding childcare for Astrid those days.

I've been staring at the email for three hours. Part of me wants to jump at it—prove that I'm still a real designer, that staying home hasn't erased my skills. Another part feels sick at the thought of slotting back into someone else's schedule, their briefs, their revisions cycle. And then there's the practical part doing math on whether the pay is even worth the hassle of arranging care.

What's strange is how guilty I feel about even considering it. Like wanting something outside of motherhood and my little shop is some kind of betrayal. But also guilty about not wanting it enough. Because shouldn't I be excited? Shouldn't a part of me miss that world?

I don't know what I want. That's the honest answer. I don't know what I want and I hate not knowing.
entries.nudge_text: If you knew no one would judge either choice, which way do you lean?
entries.response_text: Honestly? I lean toward saying no. Not because I'm scared or because it's too hard, but because the thought of sitting in their office making small talk about clients and deadlines makes me feel heavy. I left that for a reason. I don't miss it. I just sometimes miss being seen as competent by people who aren't related to me.

entries.initial_entry:
I said no to the Amsterdam job. Wrote the email this morning, polite but clear. And then I finished the frozen lake piece. Both in the same hour, which feels meaningful somehow.

Looking at the finished illustration, I'm struck by how much of me is in it. The figure on the ice—that's not some generic person, it's me, the version of me that's still learning to trust her own footing. The cracks spreading like roots—that's about how breaking apart can also be growing. I don't think anyone buying it will see all that. But I know, and that's what makes it feel real.

I've been thinking about what makes work meaningful to me. It's not the money, though money is necessary. It's not even external recognition, though that's nice. It's whether I can look at something and see my own choices reflected back. When I was at the agency, every project was filtered through client needs, brand guidelines, stakeholder feedback. The work was often beautiful. But it wasn't mine. And that matters to me in a way I've stopped apologizing for.

Astrid pointed at the print today and said "lady cold." Accurate assessment, kid.
entries.nudge_text: What would it look like to fully trust your footing?
entries.response_text: I think it would look like not second-guessing myself when Erik or my parents or anyone else offers an alternative path. Not because their ideas are bad, but because I'd be so secure in mine that I could hear them out without feeling threatened. Right now I still get defensive. Fully trusting would mean being curious instead—hearing what they suggest and knowing I can take it or leave it without my whole sense of direction wobbling.

entries.initial_entry:
Listed the frozen lake piece on the shop this morning. Set the price higher than usual because it took more out of me and I want that reflected. Someone already messaged asking if I do bulk orders for offices. I said no.

Also enrolled Astrid in a parent-toddler art class on Thursday mornings. It was my idea, on my timeline. That distinction matters. She'll get to smash paint around with other kids and I'll get to see if I can stand being around other parents for two hours.

The frozen lake piece sold. To a woman in Berlin who said it reminded her of a choice she made years ago. That's exactly what I wanted—something made from my experience resonating with someone else's. This is why I do this.
entries.nudge_text: What choice do you think she saw in it?
entries.response_text: I'll never know for certain, but I imagine it was a moment when she had to move forward without knowing if the ground would hold. Maybe leaving something—a job, a relationship, a place. The piece is about that threshold between safety and the unknown, and how stepping forward is both terrifying and necessary. If she saw that, then I managed to put something true into the world that found someone else who recognized it. That's the whole point, isn't it? Making something alone that connects to someone you've never met.

entries.initial_entry: Spent the whole evening comparing fixed deposits on three different bank sites while the kids fought over the remote. Anil says we should lock in the rate before it drops again. I know he is right but I kept thinking about that weekend trip to Alibaug we never took. We had the dates picked and everything.
entries.nudge_text: What happened to the Alibaug trip?
entries.response_text: We pushed it. First it was school fees, then Anil's mother needed new glasses, then he said let's wait till Diwali. Diwali came and went. The dates are still saved in my calendar like some kind of joke.

entries.initial_entry: My sister Meera called and asked when we are finally going on a proper holiday. I told her we went to Lonavala in June. She laughed and said "that does not count, it was one night at your cousin's farmhouse." I do not need her opinion on how we spend our time or money.
entries.nudge_text: Did it bother you because she was wrong, or because she wasn't?

entries.initial_entry:
Took Aarav and Rhea for their dental checkups. No cavities, which is a relief because the last round cost us eleven thousand rupees. Came home, made dal and rice, helped Aarav with his Hindi homework. He is struggling with the matras again. Called the tuition teacher and she can take him Tuesdays and Thursdays for two thousand a month.

Anil came back late. I had kept his food covered on the counter. He ate while scrolling his phone and went straight to bed. I watched two episodes of my show and finished the leftover gulab jamun from Sunday. The house was quiet. It should have felt peaceful but it just felt flat.

My mother-in-law brought up the gold investment scheme again at lunch. She wants us to put money into one of those jeweller savings plans, says every family needs gold as a safety net. Anil looked at me like I was supposed to agree. I smiled and said we would think about it.

Later I told him I do not want more gold sitting in a locker I forget the code to. He said it is about long-term protection, not about wearing it. Fine. But we have insurance, we have the FD, we have his provident fund. At what point do we stop saving for some imagined disaster and use the money to actually live a little? He got quiet and changed the subject.
entries.nudge_text: What would 'living a little' look like right now?
entries.response_text: Nothing extravagant. Maybe booking that resort in Goa I bookmarked three months ago. Getting the good cheese from the fancy store without feeling guilty. Taking an auto instead of waiting twenty minutes for the bus with two cranky kids. Small things that do not require a committee meeting.

entries.initial_entry:
I had a full breakdown over a cake. Not even a real breakdown, just stood in the kitchen with my hands shaking after I realized I forgot to order Rhea's birthday cake. Her party is Saturday. I had the tab open on my phone for two weeks, kept meaning to customize it, kept getting pulled away. She wanted the one shaped like a castle with purple icing and her name in gold. Now the bakery says they need five days minimum and I have three.

Called four other bakeries. One can do it but it costs almost double. I said yes without even checking with Anil because I could not bear the thought of my daughter's face when there is no castle cake.

Then I sat on the kitchen floor and cried. Not because of the cake. Because of everything the cake represents. How my days dissolve into errands and appointments and packed lunches and I cannot hold onto a single thing for myself. I used to coordinate events for two hundred guests. I used to keep spreadsheets and timelines and vendor contacts organized across six months. Now I cannot order a cake on time.

Anil found the charge on the credit card and asked about it. I told him the original bakery fell through. He said okay, no problem. He did not ask why I was upset. I do not think he noticed.

The worst part is that I know the party will be fine. Rhea will love the cake. The kids will run around the flat screaming. The mothers will sit in the living room and talk about school admissions. And I will clean up afterwards and load the dishwasher and think about the next thing. That is what bothers me. Not that it went wrong. That even when it goes right, there is nothing in it for me.
entries.nudge_text: You said 'nothing in it for me' — what would something for you look like?
entries.response_text: I honestly do not know anymore. That scares me more than the money stuff. At least with money I know what the number should be. But when I try to picture what would make me feel like I got something out of a day, I come up blank. I used to know. I used to have answers. When did I stop?

entries.initial_entry:
Rhea's party went well. She wore her purple lehenga and ate cake with both hands. I took about sixty photos. At one point she grabbed my face and said "best mummy" and I felt it go straight through me.

After everyone left and the kids were asleep, I poured myself a glass of wine I had hidden behind the pickle jars. Sat on the balcony with my feet up. Pune was noisy below me — auto horns, someone's TV, a dog barking. I just sat there drinking slowly and not thinking about anything. Forty-five minutes of nothing. It was the best part of my week and it cost me two hundred rupees and zero planning.

I need more of that. Not wine specifically. Just moments that belong to me.

Long week. Aarav had a stomach bug Monday through Wednesday so I was home with him while Rhea went to school. Spent three days washing sheets, making khichdi, and checking his temperature. He is fine now, already back to leaving Lego pieces everywhere.

Anil's company announced a restructuring. Nothing definite yet but he has been tense. Came home Thursday and said we should hold off on any big expenses for a few months, just in case. I agreed because what else would I say. We ordered in biryani that night and watched a movie together on the couch. The biryani was from the place near the station, the one that uses too much oil but tastes right. Anil fell asleep halfway through the film. I finished it alone and cleaned the containers.
entries.nudge_text: What else would you have wanted to say?

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday. Took both kids to the park in the morning. Rhea did the monkey bars for the first time without help and I clapped so hard another parent looked at me. Aarav just wanted to sit on the bench and read his comic book, which is fine, he has always been like that.

Came home and looked at my recipe blog for the first time in weeks. I have a draft post about paneer tikka that I never finished. The photos are on my phone, the notes are in a notebook somewhere. I thought about sitting down to write it but then the pressure cooker started whistling and Rhea needed help with her project and the moment passed. My phone buzzed with a notification that my blog traffic dropped forty percent this month. I closed the app.
entries.nudge_text: What does the blog mean to you these days?
entries.response_text: It used to feel like proof I still had a brain. That I could make something and put it out there with my name on it. Now it just feels like another thing I am failing at. One more tab I keep meaning to get back to.

entries.initial_entry:
Renewed our health insurance today. Bumped up the coverage to ten lakhs because Anil's colleague had a surgery that wiped out his savings. Felt right to have that cushion even though the premium went up. Also renewed Aarav's accident cover since he is starting football classes next month.

Made butter chicken for dinner using my grandmother's recipe. The one with the kasuri methi at the end. Both kids asked for seconds. Anil said it was the best I have made in months, which I will take as a compliment and not an insult. After they went to bed, I sat down and actually finished the paneer tikka blog post. Published it at eleven PM. Nothing fancy, but it is out there. Felt good to hit that button.

Had a good run at the RC meeting tonight. The lobby renovation proposal has been stalling for months because of Uncle Tan and his little coalition of holdouts, but I came prepared this time. Pulled together three contractor quotes, a cost-per-household breakdown, a maintenance projection over ten years. Laid it all out on the whiteboard. Even Mrs. Lim, who has been sitting on the fence, nodded along when I showed the depreciation numbers.

The thing is, nobody else would have done this homework. They would have kept going back and forth at every meeting, complaining about the cracked tiles and the peeling paint, never actually resolving anything. I spent two weekends on those quotes. Called in a favor from a guy I know at a building management firm. That is how things get done — you do the legwork, you control the information, you set the direction.

When the vote came, it was seven to two. Uncle Tan and his friend held out but it did not matter. I kept my voice level, thanked everyone for attending, wrapped up in under ninety minutes. Walked home feeling like myself. Like the version of me that knows how to move a room. It has been a while since I felt that at the office.

Managed to restructure the KPI reporting template my department uses before the quarterly review — simplified it, made the data speak for itself instead of burying it in twelve different tabs the way Daniel's team does. My GM noticed during the meeting and called it "clean work." Not a big deal, just a moment where the right thing clicked into place.
entries.nudge_text: What did 'clean work' from the GM mean to you?
entries.response_text: It meant he still sees the difference between someone who knows how to run operations and someone who hides behind frameworks and buzzwords. That is all.

entries.initial_entry: Daniel presented my regional consolidation idea at the leadership sync this morning. Same structure, same three-phase approach I pitched to him informally two weeks ago over coffee. He did not credit me. I sat there, mouth half open, and just let it happen.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from saying something in that room?
entries.response_text: Everyone was already nodding. If I spoke up it would have looked petty. Like the older guy clinging to credit. I know how that plays. So I sat there and smiled.

entries.initial_entry:
The new procurement system rollout requires sign-off from Group IT before we can configure anything for our department. I submitted the request form nine days ago. Called James in IT twice, emailed once, even copied his manager. Got a reply today saying it is "in the queue" and they will "circle back next week."

Meanwhile my team is stuck running parallel processes on the old system, burning hours on manual reconciliation that should have been automated a month ago. I have done everything I can do. The bottleneck is not mine. But when the GM asks why we are behind on the migration timeline, it will be my name on the status report, not James's. Twenty-three years and I still have to sit and wait for a thirty-year-old IT coordinator to decide when my department gets to function properly.

Had a disagreement with the GM over the warehouse staffing model. I have been running the weekend shift allocation the same way for eight years. It works. Attrition is low, the supervisors know the rotation, the overtime costs are predictable. Then Priscilla from HR comes in with a "flexible rostering" proposal that basically throws the whole structure out and lets staff bid on shifts through an app.

I told the GM this would cause chaos. You cannot run a warehouse floor on preferences. People need structure. You need to know who is where and when, and someone needs to own that decision. He listened, then said let us pilot Priscilla's model for one quarter and review. Just like that. Not even a counter — just "let's try it."

I could have pushed harder. I had the data, the history, the operational knowledge. But Priscilla had a slide deck with engagement scores and a Harvard Business Review citation, and I could feel the room tilting her way. So I said fine. I will support the pilot. Shook her hand after. My supervisor Michelle asked me later if I was okay with it. I told her it is what the GM wants, so we execute. That is the job. But I keep running the numbers in my head and I know this is going to cost us. When it does, I will be the one cleaning it up, not Priscilla.

Sat through a two-hour leadership development workshop today. Mandatory for all senior managers. The facilitator — could not have been older than thirty-five — spent forty minutes explaining stakeholder mapping as if half the room had not been managing stakeholders since before she graduated. She asked me to share "a time when I had to earn buy-in from a resistant stakeholder." I gave a polished answer about the Suzhou warehouse relocation in 2019. She smiled and said "great example of vulnerability."

That is not what it was. It was an example of knowing when to push and when to wait, and reading a room of Chinese factory managers who were not going to respond to some consultant's empathy framework. I did not say that. I just nodded. I am too tired to fight these battles anymore. Every year there is a new person who thinks they can teach me how to do what I have been doing for two decades. And every year I sit in the chair and nod.
entries.nudge_text: You've mentioned nodding along a few times now. What would it cost you to stop?
entries.response_text: It is just a workshop. Not worth making a scene over. You pick your battles.

entries.initial_entry:
Quiet weekend. Mei Ling had a catering job Saturday so I was home alone most of the day. Made myself kaya toast and coffee for breakfast, read the Straits Times cover to cover. The downstairs coffeeshop uncle gave me an extra egg when I bought lunch, said I looked tired. I probably do.

Watched the F1 qualifying in the evening. Spent too long on a WhatsApp group chat with my army mates arguing about whether the new Tampines hawker centre is better than the old one. It is not, but Raymond insists. Fell asleep on the couch before the replay finished. Nothing remarkable. Just a day that passed the way days pass when nobody needs anything from you.

The quarterly results came out and Daniel's team got the spotlight again. His consolidation project — my idea, let us be honest — delivered a twelve percent reduction in cross-border processing time. The GM mentioned it in the town hall. Daniel stood up and talked about "collaborative innovation" and "cross-functional alignment." I was sitting in the third row watching him get applause for work that started in my head, over my coffee, in my office.

Nobody cut me off this time. Nobody had to. The story has already been written. He pitched it, he ran it, he gets the credit. And I sat there again, clapping politely, because that is what you do. My wife asked me tonight why I was in such a foul mood. I told her work was tiring. She said I should consider whether this job still gives me what I need. I nearly snapped at her. What does she know about what I need from this job. She runs a catering business out of our kitchen. This is a forty-five person operation across three countries.

But I did not say that. Because somewhere underneath the anger I know she might have a point. This used to be the place where I mattered. Where my decisions moved things. Now I sit in meetings and nod and watch younger people present my ideas and get called innovative. I am not sure what I am holding onto anymore. The title still says Senior Operations Manager. But it is starting to feel like a frame around an empty picture.
entries.nudge_text: What would it look like if you stopped holding onto the title?
entries.response_text: I do not know. That is the honest answer. I have been Kenneth-the-operations-guy for so long I am not sure there is another version underneath. And that thought is worse than the meetings.

entries.initial_entry:
My hands are still shaking from the critique session. Professor Mansour put my naskh panel up on the wall next to everyone else's work and asked the class, "Where is the artist in this?" Like I was just copying. Like three months of grinding ink and cutting reeds and adjusting every single letter weight was nothing. Somebody said it looked like something you'd find in a mosque gift shop. People laughed.

I didn't say anything. I sat there and took it. Walked straight to the bathroom after and just stood there gripping the sink. The worst part is I keep replaying it and I still can't figure out what I should have said. I know they don't get it. But that doesn't make the laughing stop echoing in my head.
entries.nudge_text: What would you have said if you could go back?
entries.response_text: That the script isn't decoration. That every stroke has a genealogy — my jiddo's hand over mine, guiding the qalam for years. That I didn't choose this style from a catalogue. But I don't know if saying that would have changed anything. They'd probably still just see an old alphabet on paper.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the morning helping Teta Hana sort through jiddo's old papers again, she wants to frame some of his practice sheets, the ones with his warmup strokes, hundreds of little alifs in neat rows fading where the ink ran dry. She held one up and just smiled and didn't say anything. Put it aside for me.

Then I go to studio and Layla is laser-cutting plexiglass and Rami's doing something with projectors and sand and I'm sitting there with my inkwell and bamboo and I keep thinking do I look like a museum exhibit to them, like am I doing something real or am I just holding onto something because letting go would feel like — I don't know. I keep not finishing that thought.
entries.nudge_text: Feel like what?
entries.response_text: Like forgetting him. Like those practice sheets stop meaning anything. I know that doesn't make logical sense, I could still remember jiddo without doing calligraphy. But it would feel like I walked away from the table where he taught me and never came back.

entries.initial_entry:
Professor Mansour pulled me aside after class and said she sees potential in my work but wants me to "break the script apart." She used words like deconstruct, fragment, subvert. She wants me to take the naskh letters and tear them up, layer them over industrial textures, make them unrecognizable. She said it would be a strong thesis direction.

I told her I'd think about it. Walked home the long way through Gemmayze. Something about the idea makes my stomach tight. Not because it's technically hard. I could do it. I know how to cut letterforms apart and reassemble them. It's that I keep picturing jiddo watching me do it. And Teta seeing the result on a gallery wall and not being able to read a single word. I said I'd think about it and I will, but the thinking itself feels wrong.
Everyone keeps acting like I'm afraid of experimenting. I'm not afraid. I just don't think ripping something apart is the only way to prove you understand it.
entries.nudge_text: What would proving it look like for you?

entries.initial_entry:
Had dinner at Uncle Fadi's last night and my cousin Reem asked about my thesis. I said calligraphy and she nodded like that was obvious, like of course Nadia's doing that, and I wanted to snap at her. I don't know why. She wasn't being mean. She was being supportive. But the way she said "of course" made me feel like a fixed point everyone's already mapped. Like no one wonders what I'd do if I chose freely.

Then mama brought up jiddo and told the story about him lettering the sign for the pharmacy on Rue Hamra, how the owner cried when he saw it. Everyone smiled. I smiled too. It's a good story. I love that story.

But I sat there holding my plate thinking about Professor Mansour's suggestion and how if I told this table I was going to fragment the script and collage it over concrete textures, my grandmother would go quiet in that particular way she does when she's hurt but won't say so. And my mother would change the subject. And I'd sit there knowing I made a room full of people uncomfortable for the sake of a grade and a gallery slot. So I won't do it. But I keep circling back to the fact that I want to, or at least part of me does, and I can't tell if that part is ambition or betrayal.
entries.nudge_text: You said part of you wants to — which part?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Can we not do this right now.

entries.initial_entry:
I did something small today that I keep thinking about. I was in the studio early, nobody else there yet, and I pulled out a fresh sheet and started writing the bismillah in naskh the way jiddo taught me. Perfect form, clean lines. Then without really deciding to, I took a second sheet and wrote it again but let the letters drift apart, stretched the connections, let the lam curve wider than it should. It still read as the phrase. Just looser.

I stared at both sheets for a long time. The second one wasn't ugly. It wasn't disrespectful. It was the same words with more air in them. Teta could still read it. Jiddo would have recognized the hand.

I don't know what this means for my thesis. I rolled both sheets up and put them in my bag. I haven't shown anyone. Part of me thinks if I show Professor Mansour she'll want me to go further, push harder, and I'll end up somewhere I didn't agree to. And part of me thinks if I show Teta she'll look at the second sheet and ask why I'm being careless. So for now they're mine. Two versions of the same prayer, sitting in my bag, and I'm the only one who knows about both.

My students turned in their essays on Taha Hussein today. Most of them are struggling with the language, but Layla wrote something that made me stop grading. She said his blindness made him see more clearly, that losing one sense opened the others. I sat with that for a while after school, drinking mint tea on my balcony as the call to prayer drifted up from the neighborhood mosque.

I think I understand what she meant. When I stopped chasing the next promotion, the next credential, everything else came into sharper focus. The weight of the teacup in my hands. The specific shade of orange the sunset throws on the building across the street. These aren't consolation prizes for not advancing. They're the whole point.

My sister called later and asked if I'm still "just teaching" the same classes. I said yes. She doesn't understand why I'm not bored. I didn't try to explain that I'm not looking for excitement from my job. That's not what jobs are for.

The school administration announced today that we're switching to a new standardized testing system. Six meetings this month. Saturday workshops through January. Mandatory curriculum alignment sessions.

I felt my stomach drop when I read the email. Not because the work is hard. Because I know exactly what this means. No more lingering over coffee in the morning because I'll need to leave earlier for meetings. No more Thursday evenings at the souk because Saturdays will be consumed by training. No more space in my day for the small things that make it bearable.

My colleague Tariq seems energized by it. He was talking about "opportunities for growth" and "showcasing our department." I just nodded. I wanted to ask him when he last sat somewhere doing nothing and felt content. When he last ate a meal slowly enough to taste it.

I know this sounds dramatic. It's just meetings. It's just one semester. But that's how it always starts. First it's one semester, then it's a "new normal," and before you know it you've forgotten what it felt like to have time that belongs to you.

Mama would say I should be grateful to have a job at all. She's right, probably. But gratitude and resentment can live in the same body.
entries.nudge_text: What would you lose if you pushed back?
entries.response_text:
Everything and nothing. I'd lose standing with the administration. I'd be labeled as difficult, uncooperative. Eventually it would affect my references if I ever wanted to move.

But what I'd really lose is the illusion that I can have it both ways. That I can care about my peace and also be a "team player" in a system that treats peace as laziness. Right now I'm invisible, which means I'm safe. If I push back, I become visible in the wrong way.

The truth is I probably won't say anything. I'll go to the meetings and protect what I can and hope it blows over. I'm not proud of this. But I also know that martyrdom doesn't taste good.

entries.initial_entry:
Second Saturday workshop done. Made foul for breakfast beforehand. Good olive oil makes a difference, even at 6am. Workshop was tedious but I sat near the window where the light was warm. Found a new bakery on the way home. Their knafeh has the right amount of orange blossom.

Sometimes survival is about noticing what's still good.

Christmas isn't our holiday but the city has lights everywhere and I love them anyway. Walking home tonight the whole street was glowing, gold and white, and I stopped to just look. A shopkeeper came out to smoke and we stood there together not saying anything, just watching. He offered me a sesame cookie from a paper bag. Still warm.

This is what I mean when I say the good things aren't complicated. Lights on a cold street. A cookie from a stranger. The permission to pause.

My students are gone for break and I have two weeks with nothing required of me. I've planned nothing. That's the plan. Maybe I'll sleep late and read novels and take long baths and visit my aunt who makes the best maqluba. Or maybe I'll do something else entirely. The point is I don't know yet, and not knowing feels like freedom.
entries.nudge_text: What does that freedom feel like?
entries.response_text:
Like finally exhaling. Like my body relaxes in ways I didn't know it was tense. When there's nothing scheduled, time moves differently. An hour feels like an hour instead of like sixty minutes I'm supposed to fill with something productive.

I think most people are so used to planned time that they've forgotten the other kind exists. They schedule their relaxation, which turns it into another task. "Self-care Sunday" as an obligation. But real rest doesn't have a hashtag. It's what happens when you stop trying to optimize anything.

entries.initial_entry:
Back to school. The break ended too quickly, as they always do. Woke up this morning before my alarm and lay there feeling the weight of the semester ahead.

Three more Saturday workshops scheduled. Parent conferences starting next week. A new student in my advanced class who apparently has "behavioral issues" which is usually code for "nobody took time to understand him."

I made myself the fancy coffee, the Ethiopian beans I save for hard mornings. It helped a little. But even the coffee felt rushed. I was drinking it to finish it, not to enjoy it.

This is what I'm afraid of. That the system will eventually win not by forcing me to change but by leaving me no room to be who I am. That I'll adapt and adapt until I can't remember what I was adapting away from.

Dramatic again, I know. It's just the first day talking. By next week I'll have found my rhythm. I always do.
entries.nudge_text: Who were you before you started adapting?
entries.response_text:
Someone with more time, probably. That's the easy answer.

The harder answer is I'm not sure I was anyone different. Maybe I've always been negotiating between what I want and what's required. Even as a child I remember preferring slow afternoons to busy schedules, choosing books over activities that might have "developed my potential."

Maybe the question isn't who I was but whether there's a version of me that wouldn't have to fight for this. Someone who naturally craves what the world rewards. That person doesn't exist, though. So here I am.

entries.initial_entry:
Something shifted this week. Not in my circumstances, but in how I'm holding them.

I've started bringing my good tea to school. The loose-leaf oolong that I usually save for weekends. I keep it in a small tin in my desk drawer and brew it during my free period. The ritual takes five minutes that I technically don't have, but those five minutes change the texture of the entire day.

My colleague Hana noticed and asked where I got the tea. We ended up talking about her grandmother who used to make her own herb blends. Now Hana brings me a cup of her za'atar tea on Tuesdays, and I share my oolong on Thursdays. It's become a small island of niceness in the week.

I think I'd been treating my work life and my real life as separate territories, with work as enemy land where beauty couldn't survive. But that's not true. Beauty is portable. You can carry it with you if you're willing to make room for it.

The Saturday workshops are still tedious. The new testing system is still pointless. But I'm finding cracks where something good can grow.

My brother visited this weekend. He's an engineer in Dubai now, making good money, working long hours. He looks tired but won't admit it.

Over dinner he asked when I'm "going to do something" with my career. I said I'm doing something with it. I teach. He said I know what he means. Higher positions. More responsibility. A path that leads somewhere.

I told him my path leads to my apartment every evening in time to cook dinner and read before bed. He laughed like I was joking. When he realized I wasn't, he got that look. The look that says he's worried about me.

I hate that look. Like contentment is a symptom of something wrong. Like a woman my age without ambition must be hiding some failure or fear.

Maybe I am afraid. Afraid of waking up exhausted every day like he does. Afraid of forgetting what rest feels like. If that's fear, I'm not ashamed of it.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take for you to stop explaining yourself?
entries.response_text:
Honestly? I think I'd have to stop caring what they think. Which sounds simple but isn't.

Their opinions shouldn't matter. I'm nearly forty. I pay my own bills. I've built a life that works for me. But family has this way of making you feel like a child again. My brother's concern isn't really concern, it's disappointment that I'm not living the way he thinks I should. And despite everything, part of me still wants his approval.

The exhausting thing is that he'll never understand. He measures life by achievements. I measure it by how it feels to live it. We're speaking different languages.

Maybe the answer is just to stop having the conversation. Nod and change the subject. Let him think whatever he thinks. Save my energy for people who don't need me to be different.

entries.initial_entry:
Made mansaf from scratch today. It took hours. The lamb, the jameed sauce, all of it. My apartment smells incredible. I ate slowly, standing at the kitchen window watching the rain.

Nobody saw it. Nobody will praise me for it. It's not an accomplishment by any measure that matters to the world. But my god, it was good. The texture, the warmth, the satisfaction of having made something beautiful for no reason except that I wanted to.

This is what I protect.

Bad week. Three students failing my class because they don't do the reading. Administration pressure to raise pass rates. A parent screamed at me over email for being "too demanding" when I wouldn't accept a late assignment that was two weeks overdue.

I came home Thursday wanting to cry. Made myself do it anyway, just sat on the couch and let it happen. Then I took a bath with the expensive salts I've been saving and drank wine that was probably too good for a weeknight.

The world tells me I should fix these problems. Work harder, communicate better, be more flexible. But some problems aren't mine to fix. The student who doesn't read won't start reading because I exhaust myself trying to convince him. The parent who yells will keep yelling regardless of my response.

What I can control is the bath and the wine and the permission to feel bad without trying to optimize my way out of it.
entries.nudge_text: What did letting yourself cry change?
entries.response_text:
It reminded me that I'm human. That sounds simple but I forget sometimes. In teacher mode I'm supposed to be endlessly patient, endlessly resourceful. There's no room for frustration that isn't immediately converted into a lesson plan improvement.

Crying let me feel the weight of the week without doing anything about it. And then it passed. The bath wasn't medicine for the crying. The crying was already the medicine. The bath was just dessert.

I think we're taught that negative emotions are problems to solve rather than experiences to have. But sometimes you just need to be sad for a while. It doesn't mean anything is wrong. It means you're paying attention.

entries.initial_entry:
Valentine's Day. I'm not with anyone and haven't been for a while. Most years this is fine. Today I felt the absence more than usual.

Not loneliness exactly. More like awareness that my life lacks certain kinds of warmth that come from partnership. Shared meals across a table. Someone who knows what kind of day you had without you having to explain. Inside jokes.

I walked to the old bookshop after school, the one with the narrow aisles and the owner who always recommends obscure poets. He gave me coffee and we talked about Mahmoud Darwish for half an hour. On the way home I stopped for the good pastries, the ones with rose petals pressed into the glaze.

I think there's a version of this day that would have been sad. Lonely woman, empty apartment, commercial holiday. But that's not how it felt. It felt like choosing, again, to make my own day good. To not wait for someone else to bring me flowers when I can bring myself pastries.

Mama called to ask if I was okay. I told her I'd had a lovely day. She didn't believe me, but it was true.

I know I write a lot about small pleasures. Someone reading this might think I'm avoiding the big questions. But I think the small pleasures are the big questions, answered. What do you want from life? This. A warm cup, a good book, a body that's been cared for, time that belongs to me.

Tomorrow will be another staff meeting, another round of tedious requirements. But tonight is mine. And that's enough.

Helped Amira debug the intake form tonight at the nonprofit. She'd been stuck on a date-parsing issue for two days and we sorted it in twenty minutes. Small thing, but it felt like the right use of a Tuesday evening.
entries.nudge_text: What made that feel like the right call?
entries.response_text: Dunno, just better than watching Netflix again.

entries.initial_entry:
Team standup today. Felix made a comment about one of our logistics partners using subcontracted drivers with no fixed contracts, basically gig workers with zero protections. He said it casually, like it was just a business fact. I sat there and said nothing.

It's not like I didn't notice. I noticed immediately. But the meeting had twelve people in it and the product lead was driving the agenda and I just... let it slide. Afterwards I thought about saying something to Felix privately but then I got pulled into a deployment issue and the moment passed. I keep telling myself I'll bring it up tomorrow but I probably won't. It's easier to pretend I didn't hear it clearly.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you — the room, or something else?
entries.response_text: Honestly the room. Twelve people, nobody else flinched.

entries.initial_entry:
Booked a flight to Lisbon for August. Budget airline, the one that keeps getting flagged for its emissions record. I know this. I looked at the train option and it was fourteen hours and three times the price and I just couldn't justify it.

I'm tired of every small decision feeling like a referendum. The flight was sixty euros. The train would've eaten half my weekend travel budget. I picked convenience and cost and I'm sitting here feeling vaguely bad about it, which is stupid because one flight isn't going to tip the scales. But it adds up and I know it adds up and that's the part that nags at me.

Lunch with Felix and Sandra. Sandra was talking about her new apartment in Schwabing and how she's glad the neighbourhood "hasn't changed too much," meaning it hasn't gotten more diverse. She didn't say it outright but the implication was clear. Felix nodded along.

I changed the subject to the upcoming sprint deadline. Could have said something. Should have, maybe. But Sandra is senior to me and she's leading the Q3 roadmap review. There's a calculation that happens in these moments and I went with the safe option. Finished my Kartoffelsalat and went back to my desk.

The carbon audit thing. I finally got a response from Stefan in compliance. He said the numbers I flagged were "within acceptable reporting tolerances" and that the methodology was approved by their external auditor. End of conversation, apparently.

So I looked into it more. The tolerance band they're using is laughably wide — basically you can underreport by fifteen percent and still be compliant. It's technically legal. But the whole reason I pushed for the audit was because I thought the company would want to know the real numbers. Turns out they already know and they're fine with it. The system is designed to allow this.

I could escalate. Take it to the sustainability board or even the Betriebsrat. But I've been here eighteen months and I'm not in a position to make waves. And honestly, part of me is relieved that Stefan shut it down because now I have an excuse to stop. That's the part I don't like admitting. I chose the easier path — I got my answer, I can file it away, and nobody has to be uncomfortable. Including me.

The thing is, when I left the adtech job I told myself I wouldn't do this. I wouldn't just accept things that feel wrong because it's inconvenient to push. And here I am doing exactly that.
entries.nudge_text: You said you felt relieved — what does that tell you?
entries.response_text: That I want someone else to fight this one. Which is exactly the problem.

entries.initial_entry:
New contractor started on the team. Guy named Tomasz, Polish, here on a project-based contract. During onboarding someone made a joke about Eastern European work ethic — meant as a compliment, apparently. Tomasz laughed it off. I didn't say anything.

It's such a small moment. Probably wouldn't even register for most people. But I noticed the way Tomasz's laugh didn't quite reach his eyes and I thought about saying something after the meeting and then I didn't. Went back to my Jira tickets instead.
entries.nudge_text: What would you have said to Tomasz?
entries.response_text: Maybe just that I noticed. That he doesn't have to laugh at stuff like that here. But saying that out loud feels presumptuous, like I'm deciding what should bother him.

entries.initial_entry:
Weird day in a good way. The nonprofit had their summer meetup at the Englischer Garten, just food and talking, nothing structured. I ended up sitting with this Syrian family I'd helped with their paperwork back in March, the Haddads. The dad, Marwan, was telling me about his daughter's school report — she got top marks in maths and he was so proud he was practically vibrating.

And there was this moment where he grabbed my arm and said something in Arabic to his wife and she smiled and he turned back to me and said "she says you are part of the story now." I didn't really know what to say to that. Just sat there eating my Falafel feeling like, okay, this is something real. Not a dashboard metric. Not a compliance tolerance. An actual family doing better because a few people showed up consistently.

I think about the work stuff — the carbon numbers, the things I don't say in meetings — and then I think about this afternoon and it's hard to hold both at the same time. But today was just good. Marwan's daughter got good grades and he wanted to tell me about it. That's enough for one Monday.

Had a one-on-one with my manager Katharina. She asked how I was finding the team culture and I actually said something. Told her the comment during Tomasz's onboarding bothered me and that I think we could be more careful about that kind of thing. She nodded and took a note and said she'd "keep an eye on it."

Not exactly a revolution. But I said it. Out loud. To someone with authority to do something. I've been replaying the conversation all evening trying to figure out if she took it seriously or just filed it under "Lukas is being sensitive." Probably overthinking it. The point is I didn't let it pass this time.

People at work probably think I'm easygoing because I don't argue in meetings. But easygoing and silent aren't the same thing. I've just been choosing silent for too long. Katharina's response wasn't dramatic — no promises, no action items — but she heard me. And I can live with that being the starting point.

Part of me wants to dismiss this as barely doing anything. Like, congratulations, you mentioned something mildly uncomfortable in a private meeting, what do you want, a medal? But no. I know what it cost me to say it and I know I wouldn't have done it two months ago.

Grocery run after work. Standing in Edeka staring at the coffee shelf. Fair trade option is almost double the price of the store brand. I know what the price difference means — it means someone down the supply chain is getting squeezed. I know this. I've read about it. I've shared articles about it.

Bought the store brand anyway. Forty euros left until payday and I'm not going to pretend that doesn't matter. But it's the same calculation I keep making. The flight to Lisbon. The compliance thing at work. There's always a reason to pick the easier option and the reason is always money or comfort or not wanting to be the difficult one.

I'm exhausted by the gap between what I think matters and what I actually do when it comes down to it. It's not even big moral failures. It's coffee. It's a budget airline. It's staying quiet at lunch. But it's all the same muscle and I keep not using it. And I'm too tired tonight to figure out what to do about that. Going to bed.
entries.nudge_text: Is the gap getting bigger or smaller?
entries.response_text: Honestly can't tell anymore. Some weeks smaller, some weeks it swallows everything.

entries.initial_entry: Can't sleep. Keep thinking about how easily I let things go at work. Tomasz caught my eye in the hallway today and just nodded and I felt like a fraud.

Finished a long translation project today for that small organization documenting water access issues in South Asia. The pay was honestly below market rate but reading through those testimonies, I kept thinking about my grandmother's village where they still walk two kilometers for clean water. Some things matter more than what shows up in my bank account.

My cousin called from Mumbai asking why I don't take more corporate work since my English is good enough. I tried explaining but it felt like talking to a wall. She thinks I'm being foolish, giving away my skills to people who can't pay properly. Maybe she's right about the practical side. But when I close my laptop after a day like today, I feel something she probably wouldn't understand.

The community garden is finally showing real growth. We planted those tomato seedlings with the Syrian family three weeks ago and today Amira brought her daughters to see the little green fruits forming. The oldest one, maybe seven years old, touched the leaves so gently like she was afraid of breaking something precious. I remember that feeling from when I first gardened with my mother on our tiny balcony.

These moments remind me why I spend my Saturdays here instead of picking up extra delivery shifts. Not everything needs a transaction attached to it. Though I'll admit the rent notice sitting on my kitchen counter is making that philosophy harder to hold onto this week. The landlord raised it again and I'm already stretched thin. Still, watching those girls discover where food actually comes from felt like enough today.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned the rent increase is making your philosophy 'harder to hold onto.' What happens when these two parts of your life feel like they're pulling against each other?
entries.response_text: It terrifies me, honestly. I came here to build a different kind of life than the one that was mapped out for me in Mumbai, where everything is about climbing and earning and comparing. But now I lie awake some nights wondering if I'm just being naive. If my principles are a luxury I can't actually afford. My father used to say that ideals don't fill stomachs, and I hated hearing it, but lately his voice echoes louder. I don't want to become someone who stops caring about the bigger picture just to secure my own small corner. But I also can't keep pretending the practical doesn't matter.

entries.initial_entry:
Something small happened during a delivery today that I can't stop thinking about. I was dropping off food at one of those new condo towers downtown, the kind with the marble lobby and the doorman who barely looks at you. The customer came down herself instead of asking me to leave it at the desk. She saw my reusable bag and we ended up talking for five minutes about plastic waste in the ocean. She works for an environmental law firm apparently.

It's easy to feel invisible doing delivery work, just a body moving packages from one place to another. But for those five minutes I was a person with thoughts and concerns that mattered to someone. She gave me her card and said they sometimes need Gujarati translators for community outreach. I probably won't follow up because I'm always hesitant to mix networking with genuine connection. But it reminded me that caring about the world isn't as rare as it sometimes feels. Other people are thinking about these things too.
entries.nudge_text: You said you probably won't follow up because of mixing networking with genuine connection. What feels complicated about that?
entries.response_text: I worry that if I reach out for work, it changes what that conversation was. It becomes transactional instead of just two people recognizing something shared. Maybe that's too precious about it. In Mumbai, everyone networks constantly and there's no shame in it. But I came here partly to escape that feeling of every relationship being measured by what it can do for you. If I contact her for the translation work, am I being practical or am I becoming the thing I was running from?

entries.initial_entry:
Read an article this morning about garment factory conditions in Bangladesh that ruined my whole day. I know I can't save everyone. I know my choices, even the careful ones about what I buy and who I work for, are drops in an ocean. But sometimes the weight of knowing what's happening out there feels unbearable. All these people suffering right now while I sit here with my morning chai worrying about my rent.

Called my mother after because I needed to hear her voice. She asked why I sounded sad and I couldn't explain. How do you tell someone that you're grieving for people you've never met, that reading about injustice thousands of kilometers away can sink your whole day? She would tell me to focus on my own life, that I can't carry the world. She means well. But the world is not something outside me that I can choose to put down. We're all connected, whether we see it or not.

Evening now and I'm feeling better. Went back to the garden this afternoon and helped plant cucumber seedlings with Mr. Chen, the retired schoolteacher from the housing complex. He doesn't speak much English but we've developed a language through pointing and smiling and showing each other what to do next. There's something deeply right about working the soil with someone in complete silence.

I think this is what keeps me grounded when the news gets too heavy. Not fixing anything, not solving the big problems, just being present with another person doing something small and generative. The cucumbers will grow whether or not I read about suffering. The children will eat them. And somehow that feels like an answer, even if I can't quite articulate what question it's answering.
entries.nudge_text: You said this feels like an answer to a question you can't articulate. If you tried to put that question into words, what might it sound like?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Maybe I'm just being overly philosophical about putting seeds in dirt. Some things don't need to be analyzed, they just need to be done. My grandmother would say I think too much, that the hands should be busy while the mind rests. Perhaps she was wiser than all my searching.

entries.initial_entry:
Third entry today, I know. But I wanted to capture this before I sleep. Amira, the Syrian mother from the garden, sent me a photo through our community group chat. It's her family eating dinner together, and on the table are tomatoes from our garden. Just regular tomatoes, slightly misshapen, nothing special. But the message she wrote said "Thank you for teaching us this. My daughters now understand that food is a gift."

Sometimes I question whether anything I do matters against the scale of problems in the world. The displacement, the inequality, the destruction of the planet. What difference does one small garden make? But then a photo arrives and I remember that we don't measure impact only in statistics. One family eating vegetables they grew themselves. One child learning that nurturing something is possible. These are not solutions to systemic problems, I know that. But maybe they're something else that also matters. I'm grateful tonight. Even with the uncertainty about rent and whether I'm living foolishly. This is the life I chose.

First full week done at Birch Lane. The kids are sweet. One boy, Mateo, already told me he wants to read "chapter books like his sister." That kind of thing is why I'm here. The building is old and the supply closet is basically empty, but I spent the weekend printing worksheets at the library and laminating them myself. Cost me about forty dollars out of pocket, which I noticed when I checked my account this morning.

I set up a reading corner with pillows I found at a thrift store. It looks decent. The other second-grade teacher, Mrs. Okada, lent me a box of leveled readers she wasn't using. I mapped out the first unit already and I feel like I actually know what I'm doing for Monday. Haven't felt that way in a while. Small thing, but it matters. The commute is long and the parking lot has potholes, but I walked into that classroom and the kids were waiting and it just felt like where I was supposed to be.
entries.nudge_text: Forty dollars on worksheets your first week — what made that feel worth it?
entries.response_text: Because if I don't set this up right from the start, the kids lose momentum and I lose the year. I've seen it happen during student teaching. You can't get that first month back.

entries.initial_entry:
My old cooperating teacher from Edina called today to tell me they still have a position open. Full benefits, union contract, thirty kids max per class. She said she put in a word for me and they'd probably take me without a second interview.

I told her I'd think about it and then I sat in the parking lot for twenty minutes. Birch Lane doesn't even have a functioning copier half the time. But the literacy program here is the whole reason I came. I keep thinking about Mateo and the other kids who just started trusting me. I said no. I think I said no. I told her I'd call back Monday but I already know I'm not going to.
entries.nudge_text: You sat there twenty minutes. What were you arguing with yourself about?
entries.response_text: Whether I'm being stupid. Whether six months from now I'll wish I'd just taken the safe thing.

entries.initial_entry: Barely slept. Kept going over that phone call. The Edina salary would have covered my student loans and left room to breathe. I know exactly what I'm giving up and I still can't make myself call her back.

Birch Lane sent around a memo about possible mid-year budget cuts. Nothing confirmed, but the newer hires would be first to go. That's me. Mrs. Okada told me not to worry, that they say this every fall, but she's been here eleven years so of course she's not worried.

Meanwhile my mom keeps forwarding me job postings from the county school district back home. Steady pay, pension, close to family. Every time she sends one I feel my chest tighten. She's not wrong — those jobs are real and they'd solve a lot of problems. But I keep picturing myself in a fluorescent classroom running a curriculum someone else wrote, and something in me just won't do it. I deleted the emails.
entries.nudge_text: Your chest tightened. What does that feel like exactly?
entries.response_text: Like I said, it's just stress. Everybody's stressed in September.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a normal day for once. Taught the short vowel unit, kids did okay on the pre-assessment. Ate lunch in the staff room instead of my classroom, which I almost never do. Talked to the PE teacher about the fall festival coming up. She asked if I wanted to run a booth and I said sure without even thinking about it. That felt easy for once.

After school I graded papers and reorganized the reading corner. Mateo finished his first leveled reader and asked if he could take the next one home. I said yes. His mom came to pick him up and thanked me for sending books home. She said he reads to his little brother at night now. That's the kind of thing that doesn't show up on any evaluation form but it's the whole point.

Drove home, made pasta, watched half a movie. Nothing remarkable. Just a day that went the way it was supposed to. Even the drive home felt calm. No knot in my stomach, no replaying conversations. Just a Tuesday.

Long week but I got through it. The reading groups are actually working. I can see the lower group catching up — two kids moved from level B to level D in three weeks. I stayed late Thursday to regroup the library baskets and it took forever, but now every kid can find a book at their level without asking me.

I'm tired but it's the right kind of tired. Like I did something that mattered today and I can feel it in my body. Haven't felt that way at a job before. Even when the copier jammed twice and I had to hand-write a worksheet on the whiteboard instead.
entries.nudge_text: What does the wrong kind of tired feel like?
entries.response_text: Like when I was student teaching in Edina and I'd come home drained but couldn't point to anything I'd actually built. Just following someone else's plan all day.

entries.initial_entry: Fall festival was today. I ran the book swap booth. Kids brought in old books and traded them. Went fine. Cleaned up after and grabbed dinner with Mrs. Okada.

Ran the numbers this weekend. If I'd taken the Edina job I'd have an extra nine hundred a month after taxes. That's rent and then some. But money isn't the only thing that matters, and I'm tired of everyone acting like it should be.
entries.nudge_text: Who's been saying that to you?
entries.response_text: My mom, mostly. And my roommate from college who just got a salaried position downtown. She keeps asking how I'm "managing."

entries.initial_entry:
My mom drove up for the weekend and took me grocery shopping, which was nice until she started asking about my health insurance situation. I told her I'm on the school's plan and it's fine, which is technically true, but the deductible is high and I haven't been to the dentist since graduation. She got quiet in that way she does when she's holding something back.

On the drive home she finally said it. She said she worries about me, that I'm making things harder than they need to be, that there are good teaching jobs closer to home with real benefits and a pension. I know she's right about the math. I know the safer path is right there. But every time someone lays it out for me like that, something in me digs in harder. I don't want to build my life around avoiding the worst-case scenario. I want to build it around something I actually chose.

I didn't say that to her. I just said I'd look into it. But I'm not going to.
entries.nudge_text: What did you actually choose when you picked Birch Lane?
entries.response_text: I chose the kids. Isn't that enough?

entries.initial_entry:
Budget update came out. Birch Lane is keeping all current staff through the year. So that's settled, at least until spring. I should feel relieved but instead I just feel annoyed that I spent weeks worrying about it. The worry doesn't go away just because the thing you were worried about didn't happen.

My mom texted this morning asking if I'd thought about "what we talked about." I haven't replied yet. I know what the safer move looks like. I can list the reasons on paper. But I keep choosing this instead and I don't think that's going to change anytime soon. Maybe I'll feel differently when the next round of budget memos comes out, but right now I'm staying.

Board meeting for the trade association today. One of the newer members, a woman from a Cambodian labour rights group, raised concerns about working conditions at several member factories — specifically about informal workers who sort e-waste without proper protective gear. She had photos. She had data from a university study. She asked the board to issue a formal statement and require audits before renewing factory memberships.

I didn't say anything. I just sat there. A few other board members started pushing back immediately — talking about how the data was old, how conditions had improved, how public statements would hurt investment confidence in the sector. The usual deflections. And I know they're partly right, some of those factories have made genuine improvements. But the photos were recent. The data wasn't old. I could see the timestamps.

The thing is, two of those factories are long-standing clients of mine. We process their scrap. If I backed the statement, it would put those relationships at risk. Not just financially — personally. I've known these owners for over a decade. We've eaten at each other's homes. It's not as simple as just raising your hand and saying yes, we should hold them accountable. There are layers to this. But I keep replaying her face when nobody supported her motion. She looked like she expected it. That's worse somehow — that she wasn't even surprised.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from backing her?
entries.response_text: The relationships. Twenty years of built trust. You don't blow that up in a board meeting.

entries.initial_entry:
My son called from Jakarta. His NGO just secured funding to expand their mangrove restoration project into two new provinces. I could hear it in his voice — the energy, the belief that what he's doing actually matters. I told him I was proud. I meant it completely.

After we hung up I transferred some money to the foundation account. Not a huge amount, but enough to cover the clean-water pump installation we've been delaying in Siem Reap because the supplier raised prices. My wife asked why I didn't just wait until next quarter when cash flow is better. I told her some things shouldn't wait. She gave me that look she gives when she thinks I'm being impulsive, but she processed the transfer anyway.
entries.nudge_text: What was it about that call that made you act right away?
entries.response_text: Hearing him talk about the mangroves reminded me why I started the foundation. Sometimes you forget.

entries.initial_entry:
Flew back from a factory visit in Penang. The new processing line looks good, efficiency is way up, costs are down. Everyone was pleased. On the drive to the airport the plant manager mentioned casually that they'd switched to a cheaper chemical solvent for circuit board stripping. Cheaper and, I suspect, not properly rated for ventilation in that building. I could smell it from the parking lot.

I didn't ask about it. I could have. I had forty minutes in the car and I know enough about chemical handling to ask the right questions. But I was tired and the deal was done and bringing it up would have meant delaying the whole partnership review. I chose convenience. The flight home felt longer than usual.

People keep asking why I don't just retire and run the foundation full-time. As if it's that simple. The business funds everything — the foundation, my children's projects, the scholarships. Without it there's nothing to give.
entries.nudge_text: What would you lose if you let it go?
entries.response_text: Not just income. The ability to actually do something about the problems I see. Money is leverage.

entries.initial_entry:
Saturday morning, went to the hawker centre near our old Toa Payoh flat. The uncle who runs the wonton noodle stall has been there since my kids were small, must be almost thirty years now. We talked for a while. He's worried about his lease renewal, says the rents keep going up and the younger hawkers can't make it work anymore. Whole stretch of stalls might close.

I started thinking about whether the trade association could do something — not directly but maybe model something. We talk a lot about industry sustainability but it's always about supply chains and compliance frameworks. Nobody talks about what happens to the communities around these supply chains. The hawker uncle isn't e-waste, obviously, but it's the same pattern. Small people squeezed by systems that don't see them. I should bring this up at the next meeting. Or maybe I'll just sit there again. Don't know.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take for you to actually bring it up?
entries.response_text: Probably nothing. The agenda is always packed and nobody wants to hear philosophy from the recycling guy.

entries.initial_entry:
Dinner with old university friends tonight. David made a comment about how the migrant worker housing situation in Singapore has been "overblown by the media." Two others at the table agreed. I remember David saying something similar during the dormitory outbreaks a few years ago.

I didn't push back. I changed the subject to the new hawker centre opening in Bukit Merah. The conversation moved on. My wife squeezed my hand under the table — she knows what I think about the worker housing issue. We've donated to a couple of migrant worker charities quietly. But sitting there listening to David dismiss it, and just letting it pass — that sat with me on the drive home.

Chose a cheaper shipping route for the Cambodia pump equipment today. Faster and half the cost, but it goes through a freight company I know cuts corners on packaging for rural deliveries. Easier for me. Maybe not for whoever unpacks a damaged pump at the other end.
entries.nudge_text: Worth the savings?

entries.initial_entry:
Ran the thesis committee meeting today and something just clicked into place. Dr. Farouk actually deferred to me when the methodology question came up, turned to me in front of the whole room and said "Nasser has more field experience here." I didn't even have to push for it. The newer students were taking notes when I spoke about the interview protocol design and one of them asked afterward if I could review her proposal. I said yes of course.

There's a particular feeling when the room orients toward you not because you demanded it but because they recognize you belong at the front. I've spent months building that credibility here — showing up early, preparing sharper questions than anyone else, making sure my comments in seminars land with weight. It's not about ego. It's about earning the position through the work.

Walking back to my office I passed the department bulletin board where they posted the council restructuring I proposed last semester. My name on the memo. My framework. Small thing maybe, but I stood there for a minute just reading it. Some days the years in government feel like lost time, like I should have done the PhD at twenty-five. But today it felt like those years are exactly what make me the person the room turns to.

So apparently Huda told Dr. Kassar that my proposed panel structure for the symposium was "unnecessarily hierarchical." Her words got back to me through Tariq. I set it up that way for a reason — someone has to manage the flow, assign speaking order, handle Q&A. That's not hierarchy, that's organization. If she wanted a different format she could have brought it up in the planning meeting instead of going behind my back.

What bothers me is not the criticism, it's the route she took. She didn't come to me. She went to the professor. Like I'm someone you go around rather than talk to. I've been running events longer than she's been in the program and I know what works. If the panel falls apart without structure, nobody's going to blame Huda for it.
entries.nudge_text: You keep saying it's about the route she took — but what if it's actually the word 'hierarchical' that got to you?
entries.response_text: It's really not about the word. I just don't appreciate back-channel conversations.

entries.initial_entry:
