Had an uncomfortable exchange with my supervisor today that I keep replaying. Dr. Kassar suggested I "consider co-authoring" the working paper with Huda and Youssef instead of submitting it solo. He framed it as a "collaborative opportunity" but it felt like a demotion. I've done ninety percent of the literature review, the entire theoretical framework is mine, and now I'm supposed to share the byline because it builds "departmental cohesion."

I didn't argue in the moment. I nodded and said I'd think about it. But my chest was tight the whole walk home and I can still feel it now sitting at my desk. My name on that paper matters. It's my ticket to the next conference, maybe a publication that separates my CV from every other ABD candidate. Sharing credit when I did the bulk of the work is not collaboration, it's redistribution.

The worst part is I know saying no will look petty. I'll be the guy who wouldn't share, the one who doesn't play well with others. But saying yes means my contribution gets diluted. It means standing on a stage and introducing a paper that used to be mine as "ours." I keep drafting the email in my head and both versions — yes and no — make me feel sick.
entries.nudge_text: What exactly changes about the paper if two other names are on it?
entries.response_text: It stops being proof that I can produce something significant on my own. The paper is the same but the signal it sends is completely different — it becomes a group project instead of evidence that I belong at the front of a room making an argument nobody else in this department could make.

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about the co-authorship thing, hours later now. I called my brother Faisal and he said I was overthinking it, that in his engineering firm people share credit on projects all the time and nobody cares. Easy for him to say — he's a senior manager with his name on the letterhead already. He doesn't need a single paper to prove anything. I tried to explain that in academia authorship order matters, that first-author versus third-author is a different career, but I could hear him losing interest.

What really got under my skin was when he said "not everything has to have your stamp on it, Nasser." He meant it gently. I know that. But it landed like an accusation. Like there's something wrong with wanting recognition for what I built. My father spent thirty years in the civil service and retired with a ceremony and a clock. Nobody remembered his name six months later. I watched that happen and I decided early on that I would not let my work disappear into an institution's memory.

Maybe Faisal's right that I'm overthinking. But the feeling hasn't gone away. I keep imagining the conference program with three names where mine used to be alone, and it physically bothers me. Not rage, not sadness exactly. More like watching someone move your chair at a table you set.

The morning handover meeting. Dr. Fatima asked for an update on the new wound-care protocol I wrote up — before I could finish my second sentence, Khalid jumped in, started talking about the supply costs like he had been running the whole thing. He quoted numbers I had gathered. Used the phrase "what we've been looking at" when it was me who'd been looking at it, on my own time after shifts.

I just sat there. Let him finish. Dr. Fatima nodded along at him and moved to the next agenda item. Nobody looked at me. I don't know why I didn't say something. Maybe I was too tired, maybe it felt petty. But it's been sitting in my chest all day like a stone.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from cutting in when Khalid started talking?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I think I froze. It happened so fast and then the moment was gone. I keep telling myself it would have looked aggressive if I interrupted, but the truth is I just didn't react in time. And now it's too late.

entries.initial_entry:
I put in the request for the portable ultrasound unit three weeks ago. Three weeks. I have the justification, the cost breakdown, the vendor comparison — everything Procurement asked for. But it sits on Hassan's desk because he's "reviewing priorities." He told me he'll get to it when he gets to it.

So I wait. I check my email every few hours. I mentioned it to him again in the hallway on Thursday and he gave me this look, like I was being pushy. Maybe I was. But we need that unit for the patients on my ward. Every day without it is another day of sending patients downstairs for scans that take hours.

I called the vendor myself today to ask if there's a way to expedite, and they said it's all on our side. I know that. I already knew that. I just needed to do something because sitting still while someone else holds the cards is making me lose my mind. Layla says I bring the frustration home. She's probably right. Last night I snapped at Faisal over homework and it had nothing to do with him.
entries.nudge_text: You said it had nothing to do with Faisal — what was it actually about?
entries.response_text: It was about feeling stuck. When I can't move things forward at work, everything else starts to irritate me. Faisal just happened to be there asking me a question at the wrong time. I apologized to him later but I could see he was still careful around me the rest of the night.

entries.initial_entry:
Had to reorganize the shift schedule today after two nurses called in sick. Normally this kind of thing spirals — people complain, nobody wants to cover the gaps, and I end up doing half the work myself. But today I just pulled everyone into the break room, laid out what we had, and asked who could do what.

Something about the way I handled it felt right. I didn't raise my voice, didn't threaten anyone with write-ups like I've done before. Just said, here's the situation, we need to figure it out together. Nour volunteered to extend her shift and Omar swapped his day off without me having to ask twice. We got through it.

I'm exhausted now — twelve hours on my feet and barely ate. But there's this feeling I can't quite name. Like the ward ran well today because I kept it steady, not because I forced anything. I think the staff noticed too. Nour thanked me on her way out, said it was the calmest crisis she'd been through. Small thing, but I'm holding onto it.

People keep saying I should "let go" more at work, delegate, trust the team. Easy to say when you're not the one who gets called at 2am when something goes wrong. But fine — I gave Amira full responsibility over the medication audit this quarter. Didn't hover, didn't check in every day.

She came back with the report yesterday and it was thorough. Better organized than what I would have done, if I'm being honest. I told her it was solid work in front of the team. I meant it. She earned it. I'm not saying I'll start handing everything off, but maybe there are a few more things I don't have to hold onto so tightly.

The hospital announced new discharge protocols last week. I had my own version drafted, based on patterns I've been tracking on my ward for months — readmission rates, timing, which patients fall through the cracks. I brought it to the meeting with the clinical leads. Dr. Samir listened, then said the hospital would go with the standardized template from the regional office instead. Said it was about consistency across wards.

I pushed back. Told him the regional template doesn't account for the patient demographics we see. He acknowledged the point but said the decision was made. The rest of the table was quiet. I could tell Rania agreed with me but she didn't say anything. After the meeting she told me privately that I was right, which somehow made it worse.

So now I'm implementing a protocol I don't believe in. I went through it line by line this evening. It's not terrible. Some parts are fine. But the discharge timing section is going to cause problems and in three months when readmissions tick up, nobody will remember I flagged it. I printed out my version and put it in my drawer. Not sure why. Maybe just to know it exists.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take for you to bring that drawer copy back out?
entries.response_text: The drawer copy is just for me. If the numbers go where I think they will, the conversation will happen again on its own. I've been through this enough times.

entries.initial_entry:
New hire orientation today. I've been running this ward for seven years. The training coordinator introduced me to the group as "one of the senior nurses" — not the charge nurse, not the ward lead. Just "one of." I corrected her in front of everyone and I could tell she thought I was being difficult.

Then during the walkthrough, she explained the emergency tray setup to me. To me. Like I haven't been the one who redesigned it two years ago. I just nodded and moved on but I was grinding my teeth the whole time. I'm too tired to be angry about it but it's there, sitting under everything. Seven years and someone new walks in and I'm just "one of."

Staff meeting. We were discussing overtime allocation and I had the floor — I was explaining why my ward needed the extra hours, I had the patient acuity data right there on my tablet. Mid-sentence, Dr. Rashid starts talking over me to ask the administrator about budget ceilings. Just steamrolled right over me like I wasn't speaking.

Nobody said "Tariq was still talking." Nobody paused. The conversation just shifted. I sat there for the rest of the meeting barely saying a word. Drove home with my hands tight on the steering wheel. Called my brother Saeed — he said I should have spoken up. Everyone says that. It's easy to say when you're not the one sitting in that room being made invisible.
entries.nudge_text: This is the second meeting where you went quiet. What would speaking up actually cost you?
entries.response_text: I think I'm afraid they'll see me as the angry nurse who can't take being interrupted. In our department, if a doctor talks over you, that's just how it is. If I push back, suddenly I'm the problem. Saeed doesn't understand that — he works for himself. I don't have that option.

entries.initial_entry:
Submitted a request to attend the regional nursing leadership conference in Dubai next month. Need my department head's sign-off. Sent the form Monday, followed up Wednesday. He said he'd look at it. It's been two days since the follow-up and nothing.

Registration closes Friday. I've already arranged my own coverage for the shifts I'd miss. The conference fee is modest. There is no reason for this to take a week except that it's not his priority. So I wait.

Meeting with the nurse manager today about next year's staffing plan. She asked each charge nurse for input. When it was my turn, I laid out the case for an additional RN position on my ward — patient numbers, acuity trends, incident reports tied to understaffing. Clear, documented, took me two evenings to prepare.

She thanked me, then said the final staffing model would follow "organizational guidelines" and that individual ward requests would be "considered in context." Which means nothing. Which means someone above her will decide and my two evenings of preparation will sit in a folder.

What got me was watching her nod along to Ibrahim's request for the same thing on his ward — he barely had any data, just talked about how his team was "feeling stretched." She seemed more receptive to him. Maybe I'm reading into it. Maybe not. But I left that meeting knowing the decision was never going to be about the quality of the argument. It's about something else — relationships, politics, who plays golf with whom. And I don't play golf.
entries.nudge_text: When you say it's not about the argument — what do you think it's actually about?
entries.response_text: Access. Ibrahim has coffee with the nurse manager every week. They've known each other since she was a floor nurse. I came in from a different hospital system. I don't have that history. My work should speak for itself, but in this place, the work is just the entry ticket. The real decisions happen in conversations I'm not part of.

entries.initial_entry: Still thinking about that meeting. The part that bothers me most isn't Ibrahim — it's that I spent two evenings on something and walked in believing the data would matter. I keep doing this. Building the case, presenting it perfectly, and then being surprised when it doesn't work.
entries.nudge_text: You said you keep doing this — what would it look like to stop?
entries.response_text: I don't think I can stop preparing. That's who I am. But maybe I need to start having the coffee meetings too. Play both sides of it. I just hate that it's necessary.

entries.initial_entry:
Today was the monthly temple committee meeting and I just cannot shake this strange feeling. Meena aunty said something about how "some people like to take charge of everything" and I know she was talking about me. Everyone was looking at their phones or the floor. I have been running this committee for six years and we have never had a failed event, never run out of food, never had volunteers not show up. But suddenly I am the problem?

I stayed quiet. What else could I do? If I defended myself it would look exactly like what she was accusing me of. But sitting there pretending I did not hear her felt like swallowing glass. My husband said I should just ignore it, that Meena aunty is just jealous because her daughter-in-law moved her out of the main house. Maybe. But that does not explain why no one else spoke up for me.

The thing is, I take charge because if I do not, things fall apart. Remember the Holi celebration last year when I was at Ravi's parents' house? Complete disaster. Cold food, wrong decorations, crying children. But sure, I am the one who is too controlling.
entries.nudge_text: What held you back from responding?

entries.initial_entry: Dropped off three containers of biryani to the Sharma family down the street. Their son just had surgery and the mother looked like she had not slept in days. She cried when she saw me at the door. This is what it is all about, really. Showing up when people need you.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel when she cried?
entries.response_text: Honestly, it reminded me of when Amma had her first stroke and neighbors brought us food for weeks. You never forget who showed up. I want to be that person for others. Sometimes I think this is the only thing I am truly good at.

entries.initial_entry: Priya came home crying from school because her friend group excluded her from a birthday party. I spent an hour consoling her but the whole time I kept thinking about that committee meeting. Is this what it feels like? To be pushed out? Too tired to think about this more.
entries.nudge_text: Are you thinking about yourself or Priya?
entries.response_text: Priya, obviously. She is thirteen and everything feels like the end of the world at that age. I am just tired.

entries.initial_entry:
Ravi's sister called asking if we can host his parents for six weeks instead of two this summer. She has a work trip to Singapore that "cannot be rescheduled." I said yes before even thinking about it. What else could I say?

Now I am mentally reorganizing everything. Priya's room will have to be cleared for them. I will need to cook separate low-sodium meals for Appa. The bathroom schedule becomes a nightmare with seven people. But Lakshmi made it sound like I was the only solution, and I suppose I am.
entries.nudge_text: Did you want to say something else?
entries.response_text: Wanting and doing are different things. Lakshmi works, I do not. She has meetings in Singapore, I have school drop-offs. The math is simple. But yes, fine, I wanted to say "let me check with Ravi first" like a normal person. Instead I just absorbed it like always.

entries.initial_entry:
Nothing happened today, just the usual. But I have been thinking about something my cousin Anita said when she visited last month. She asked why I "gave up" my career to stay home, as if I am sitting here watching television all day. I manage a household, a family, a community. I coordinate schedules for five people, handle three different school systems' requirements, manage all the finances, plan every festival, and support whoever needs me.

Ravi could not find his passport last week. Who found it in twenty minutes? Me. Priya needed a costume for her Bharatanatyam recital. Who stayed up sewing until midnight? Me. Vikram's college applications needed someone to proofread seven essays. Me.

But because there is no salary, it is not real work. Because I do not have a LinkedIn profile anymore, I have "given up." Anita has a corner office and takes two vacations a year. Good for her. She also calls her mother once a month and sends birthday cards late. I am not saying one is better. I am just saying they are different, and I am tired of having to defend my choice.

Committee met again. I volunteered to step back from leading the Navratri planning. Let someone else handle the vendor contracts and volunteer schedules. Meena aunty looked surprised. Everyone did, actually.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel to step back?

entries.initial_entry: Made chai for myself this afternoon and sat on the balcony for fifteen minutes doing nothing. The house was quiet. Just me and the crows arguing in the mango tree. Cannot remember the last time I did this.
entries.nudge_text: What made today different?

entries.initial_entry:
Vikram called from university just to talk. Not because he needed money or help with something. Just to tell me about his professor who reminds him of his grandfather, and about the dosa place he found near campus. We talked for forty-five minutes.

I think I did something right with him. He is kind. He notices people. Last month he organized a food drive at his dorm because he saw international students struggling during spring break when the dining hall closed. He did not tell me about it - his roommate's mother mentioned it. That is the part that makes me proudest. He does not do it for recognition.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think shaped him that way?
entries.response_text: I wish I could take credit but honestly, I think he just watched. Watched me drop off food to the Sharmas. Watched his grandmother live with us for three years. Watched Ravi stay late at office but still make every recital and cricket match. Children learn what they see, not what you tell them.

entries.initial_entry: Second entry today. In-laws arrived this evening, four days early. Lakshmi's flight got moved up. I had just finished cleaning but the guest room mattress has not been aired. Amma looked at the sheets and I know she noticed. Too tired to care right now.

Amma has been here four days and I am already losing my mind. She reorganized my spice cabinet. MY spice cabinet that I have arranged the same way for fifteen years. She said she was "helping" because it was "inefficient." I have managed three children, a husband, a house, and an entire temple committee with that "inefficient" cabinet.

I did not say anything. What would be the point? She is seventy-two and she is a guest. But Ravi saw my face when I opened the cabinet door and he just shrugged. Shrugged! As if his mother rearranging my kitchen is completely normal and I should just accept it.

The worst part is that she is not wrong. Her arrangement probably is more efficient. But that is not the point. The point is that this is my home, my space, the one domain where I should have some say. But apparently even here I am meant to just smile and adjust. I made everyone's favorite dinner anyway because what else am I going to do, let everyone starve over cumin placement?
entries.nudge_text: What would having a say look like?
entries.response_text: It does not matter. Four more weeks and then things go back to normal. I just need to survive until then.

entries.initial_entry:
Priya asked me today why I always have to be "in charge of everything." She was annoyed because I reminded her three times about her homework. Three times! Because she was on her phone and the deadline was in two hours. But apparently I am controlling.

I told her that someone has to keep track of things. She rolled her eyes and said "you could just let me fail once." What kind of mother lets her child fail on purpose? I did not hover over Vikram and look how he turned out - responsible, organized, successful. But Priya is different. She needs more reminders. If I do not push her, she will coast.

Ravi thinks I should "give her space." Easy for him to say. He is at office twelve hours a day. He does not see the daily negotiation of getting this child to do anything. When she fails a test, no one will ask where her father was. They will ask what I was doing.

But maybe everyone is right and I am the problem. Maybe I should just stop caring. Let the laundry pile up, let the homework go undone, let the spice cabinet stay however Amma wants it. Then we will see how things run.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you did stop?
entries.response_text: Chaos. Or maybe nothing. Maybe everything would be fine and I would realize I have been making myself essential when I am actually just in the way. That thought is worse than the chaos, honestly.

entries.initial_entry:
Amma and I had an argument today. Or not an argument exactly - she does not argue. She just makes observations. "In my house we always did it this way." "You work so hard, beta, but maybe if you organized differently..." "Priya seems stressed, have you thought about letting her breathe?"

I finally said something. I told her that I appreciate her concern but this is my house and my family and I have been managing fine for twenty years. She got very quiet and went to her room. Ravi says I was too harsh. Maybe. But she has been here two weeks and every day there is a new suggestion, a new correction, a new way I am falling short.

The thing is, she ran her own household for forty years. I understand the need to feel useful, to have a role. When she moved in with Lakshmi she lost all of that. So she comes here and tries to find her place by improving mine. I see the pattern. I am not blind to it.

But seeing the pattern does not make it easier to live with. And I keep wondering - is this my future? Will I be the mother-in-law who cannot stop fixing things because fixing things is all I know how to do? Will Priya write journal entries about me reorganizing her kitchen?
entries.nudge_text: What would you want to be remembered for?
entries.response_text: Not for being right. Not for having the most organized spice cabinet or the best-run committee. I want Vikram and Priya and even little Arun to remember that I showed up. That when things fell apart, I was there. That I fed people not because I needed to control the menu but because I believe you cannot love someone properly if you let them go hungry. That is why I do all of this. I think I forgot that somewhere along the way.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a solid Sunday for once. Turned my phone off at ten in the morning and didn't touch it until three. Made shakshuka from scratch, the version with the feta and fresh herbs, and ate it slowly at the counter with coffee. Read about forty pages of the book I've been carrying around for months. Nobody needed anything from me and I didn't need anything from anyone.

I know some people would say that sounds boring or like I wasted the day. I don't care. I needed it. My body needed it. I've been running on fumes for three weeks straight and this was the first time I actually felt like a person again instead of just a response machine for my team.
entries.nudge_text: What does the response machine version of you look like?
entries.response_text: Just — flat. Going through the motions. Answering Slack messages in bed before I'm even awake. Eating lunch at my desk without tasting it. I don't even notice it happening until a day like today where I remember what the other thing feels like.

entries.initial_entry:
Left work at 5:30 today, which is early for me. Drove home, changed clothes, put on a record — Khruangbin, the one I bought last month and hadn't opened yet. Poured a glass of wine. Sat on the couch and just listened to it for twenty minutes.

And the whole time I was doing the math in my head. Did I finish enough today? Did I answer Priya's question about the escalation workflow? Did I forget to follow up with the client in Portland? I kept picking up my phone to check Slack and then putting it back down. The wine was good. The music was good. I was there but I wasn't there.

Eventually I just got up and opened my laptop and worked for another two hours. The annoying part is that I felt relieved when I did. Like I'd been given permission to stop pretending I was relaxing. I don't know what that says about me but I noticed it tonight and it didn't feel great.
entries.nudge_text: Who's giving the permission?
entries.response_text: I mean, nobody literally. It's just — the work is always there, and if I'm not doing it, someone on my team might be stuck. It's not that deep. I just have a hard time switching off.

entries.initial_entry:
Normal Saturday. Groceries in the morning, cleaned the apartment, did laundry. Called my mom around noon — she's fine, talked about my cousin's wedding logistics for twenty minutes and whether I'd be bringing someone. I mostly listened and gave noncommittal answers.

Afternoon I went for a run on the Cherry Creek trail. About five miles, nothing crazy. Legs felt heavy from sitting all week but loosened up after the first mile. Came back, showered, made a stir fry with whatever vegetables were about to go bad. Watched two episodes of something on Netflix I won't remember by next week. Went to bed at a reasonable hour.

Pulled a ten-hour day and by the end I just wanted to lie on the floor. Thought about running a bath but instead I opened my laptop to prep for tomorrow's review. Couldn't even make the argument to myself that I deserved an hour off.
entries.nudge_text: Deserved it how — like you hadn't worked enough?

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. Monday through Thursday blurred together — back-to-back one-on-ones, a product launch sync that ran over by forty minutes, and a new hire onboarding that I'm somehow still the point person for even though I asked three weeks ago for someone else to take it.

Weekend was fine. Slept in Saturday, did errands, cooked a simple pasta. Sunday I met up with Chris for beers and watched football at a bar downtown. Didn't think about work for most of the afternoon. Got home around seven and just went to bed early. Nothing happened, really. Just tired.

Weird little thing today. After the afternoon standup I had a gap — like forty-five minutes where nobody had booked me and nothing was on fire. I walked to the coffee shop around the corner, the one with the good cortados, and sat at the window and just watched people for a bit. Didn't bring my laptop. Didn't look at my phone except once to check the time.

It was cold outside and the light was doing that thing where it comes in low and gold and everything looks like a movie. I just sat there and drank my coffee and it was genuinely nice. Small and quiet but nice. I think I've been so locked into the rhythm of sprinting from task to task that I forgot this kind of thing is free and available whenever I want it. Or whenever I let myself want it, I guess.
entries.nudge_text: What's the difference between wanting it and letting yourself?
entries.response_text: I think wanting it is easy. I always want the slower version of the day. But letting myself actually take it means accepting that whatever I'm not doing in that forty-five minutes isn't going to fall apart. And some part of me doesn't believe that. Or doesn't want to risk finding out.

entries.initial_entry: Went to dinner with a friend tonight, a place I'd been wanting to try for months. The food was great but I spent half the meal thinking about the quarterly review deck I haven't started. Left feeling like I'd been somewhere without actually being there.
entries.nudge_text: You keep showing up to things you want and then not being there.
entries.response_text: Yeah. I know. I don't know how to fix that yet.

entries.initial_entry:
Parent-teacher conferences ran until 7pm tonight. Twenty-three families, twenty-three variations of the same careful dance — praising where possible, suggesting improvements without offending, never saying anything that might prompt a complaint to Principal Wu. Mrs. Huang brought her mother-in-law, which meant I had two generations asking why her son wasn't top of the class. I said all the right things about Wei's potential and his need for more practice. I didn't mention that he spends most of class drawing in his notebook.

Walking home, I felt that familiar tiredness that comes from holding my face in the right expression all day. But also something like satisfaction. Nobody left upset. No difficult calls tomorrow. My colleague Teacher Lin had three parents demand meetings with administration after his conferences last month — he told one mother her daughter was "mediocre at best." He's not wrong, technically. But there are ways to say things, and there are other ways. I'd rather be tired than cause that kind of mess.

My hands are still shaking a little. I had to report a student today — Jia-Hui, one of my brightest — for cheating on the midterm exam. She copied answers from the student beside her. I saw it clearly, and worse, she saw me see it.

The look on her face. Like I'd betrayed her somehow, when she was the one breaking the rules. I followed protocol exactly: documented the incident, submitted the report to the academic integrity office, informed her homeroom teacher. Everything by the book. But she's been crying in the bathroom all afternoon, and three of her friends gave me cold looks in the hallway.

The thing that keeps running through my head is what Principal Wu said when he reviewed my report: "Good catch, Teacher Chen. We appreciate your vigilance." Vigilance. Like I'm some kind of guard. I don't want to be the person students are afraid of. I want them to trust me. But if I'd looked away, if I'd pretended not to notice — wouldn't that be worse? Wouldn't that be telling Jia-Hui that rules don't matter, that integrity is flexible?

My mother would say I did right. "A teacher who ignores cheating isn't a teacher," she'd say. She taught chemistry for thirty years and never once let a student slide. I'm trying to believe that following the proper procedure was the correct choice. I just wish it didn't feel so much like I've lost something.
entries.nudge_text: What exactly do you feel you might have lost?
entries.response_text: Her trust, I suppose. Or maybe the version of myself that I prefer — the approachable teacher, the one students come to with their problems. Now I'm the one who reported Jia-Hui to administration. There's no taking that back. She'll always remember me as the teacher who got her in trouble, not the one who stayed after school to help her understand quadratic equations. I know I did what was expected of me. I just wonder sometimes if being the person everyone expects me to be means giving up other things I want to be.

entries.initial_entry: Long day. Department meeting after classes, then dinner at my parents' place, then home to grade thirty-eight exams. My mother asked again about "that nice man from church" and I said I'd think about it, same as last time, same as I will next time.
entries.nudge_text: What is it about that topic that makes you say 'same as last time'?
entries.response_text: It's not a big thing, really. My mother has been suggesting potential matches since I was thirty. It's just what mothers do. She means well. I told her I'm focused on work right now, which is true. The exam period is always exhausting and I don't have the energy to think about anything else.

entries.initial_entry:
Saturday afternoon and I'm sitting in my apartment with a cup of cold tea I forgot to drink, thinking about the faculty dinner next Friday. Vice Principal Chang wants me to give a toast because I'm the "voice of the department" according to her, which means I'm the one who doesn't argue in meetings and makes everyone feel comfortable.

I've already written three drafts. The first was too long. The second used a metaphor about bamboo that sounded pretentious when I read it aloud. The third is fine, probably, but Teacher Lin will definitely make some comment afterward about how I'm "always so proper" and I can already feel myself smiling politely at him and saying something like "just doing my part."

There's a ceramics class starting at the community center next month. I saw the flyer at the grocery store. Every Saturday afternoon, two hours of making things with your hands. I keep thinking about it. I haven't signed up because it conflicts with my parents' usual visiting time. They would understand if I explained. They would say "of course, go to your class." But my mother's face would do that thing it does when she's disappointed but trying not to show it. And my father would eat dinner in silence the way he does when something's bothering him.

Is it ridiculous that I can't take a ceramics class because of a look on my mother's face? It sounds ridiculous when I write it out. Plenty of people would just go, would say "I'm an adult, I can spend my Saturdays how I want." But that's not... I don't know. That's not how my family works. We show up for each other. That's the deal.

I'll probably end up watching YouTube videos about ceramics instead. It's not the same, but it's something.
entries.nudge_text: What draws you to making things with your hands?
entries.response_text:
I think it's because nothing I do in a day has any shape to it. Teaching is important, but at the end of a class, what do I have? Lesson plans for tomorrow. Grades to record. The students leave and the room is empty. Even calligraphy — I've been doing it for years and the scrolls just go in a drawer.

With ceramics, you'd have something. A bowl. A cup. Something you made that exists outside of obligation. Something nobody asked you to make, that nobody needs you to make, that doesn't have any purpose except that you wanted to make it. I don't have much of that in my life. Things that are just mine, just because I wanted them. Most things I do are because someone is expecting me to do them, and if I'm honest, I've started to forget what I want when no one's watching.

entries.initial_entry:
The faculty dinner was fine. My toast went well — people laughed at the appropriate moments, Principal Wu nodded approvingly, nobody looked bored. Teacher Lin did make his comment afterward, and I did smile and say something polite. The whole evening was exactly what it was supposed to be.

On the way home I stopped at the community center and picked up the registration form for the ceramics class. I haven't filled it out yet. It's sitting on my kitchen table next to the stack of exams I still need to grade. My mother called this morning to confirm Sunday dinner and I didn't mention it. I'm not hiding it, exactly. I just haven't figured out how to bring it up in a way that won't make it into a whole conversation about why I need hobbies and whether this means I'm lonely and shouldn't I focus on meeting someone instead of playing with clay.

Maybe I won't sign up. It's a lot of money for something I've never tried. And Sunday dinners are important. Family is important. There's nothing wrong with my life the way it is. Lots of people would be grateful to have what I have — stable job, parents who care, a routine that works. I don't know why I keep picking at it.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned not knowing why you keep picking at it. What would it mean if you stopped?

entries.initial_entry: The new postdoc presented my stakeholder mapping framework at the department seminar today. Used my slides, my structure, even the same case example I developed last spring. Introduced it as "something I've been working on." I sat there and said nothing. I don't know why I didn't say anything.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you?

entries.initial_entry: Long day in the library. Finished the literature review chapter draft, or at least a version of it. Grabbed a coffee at Fika with Astrid from the history department, talked about nothing in particular. Sometimes those conversations are the best part of the week.
entries.nudge_text: What makes those the best part?
entries.response_text: Nobody's positioning themselves. Nobody's measuring what you've published or where your funding comes from. She just talks about medieval trade routes and I talk about regulatory capture and we both complain about the printing queue. It's uncomplicated. Most things here aren't.

entries.initial_entry:
Supervisory meeting ran long. Björn wants me to restructure chapter three around a different theoretical lens. I think the existing framing is stronger, but he has a point about the reviewers at the journal we're targeting. Sometimes you have to write for the audience that matters.

Spent the evening trying to reorganize the argument. My eyes are burning. The flat is a mess. Haven't cooked in three days, just bread and cheese and whatever's in the fridge. I keep telling myself the pace will ease up after the mid-term review, but I said the same thing last semester.

Couldn't sleep. Kept turning over the chapter three issue. It's not really about the theoretical lens, if I'm honest. Björn's framing would position his own work more centrally in my literature review. He didn't say that outright, but it's obvious when you look at the citations he suggested. Three of the five are his papers.

This is the game though. Your supervisor's name opens doors. If citing him more prominently gets me a better review at the European Journal of Political Economy, fine. The work is still mine. The data is mine. The analysis is mine. A few extra citations don't change the substance, even if they change the optics.

I ran into Erik from the cohort below mine in the corridor earlier. He asked about the committee restructuring vote next week. He's campaigning for a seat. I told him what I knew, which wasn't everything. You learn not to give away the full picture when people are vying for the same limited spots. That sounds worse written down than it felt in the moment.

The departmental retreat was today. Eight hours in a conference room discussing the new research strategy. The head of department asked for input from doctoral students, which in practice means she asked, two of us spoke, and then the senior faculty made the decisions they were always going to make.

I'd prepared a brief proposal about reallocating seminar slots to give doctoral candidates more presenting time. Wrote it up properly, referenced comparable departments, included a simple implementation plan. Professor Ekström listened, nodded, said "interesting idea" and moved to the next agenda item. That was it. Twenty minutes of preparation, ten seconds of attention.

What gets me is that I know how these rooms work. I spent years in rooms like this in the private sector. The difference is that in consulting, if you brought a proposal with data, people engaged with it. Here, the hierarchy is softer but somehow more rigid. Nobody tells you no. They just don't pick it up. The proposal sits there and eventually everyone forgets it existed. Including, probably, me.
entries.nudge_text: That sounds like a lot of effort for ten seconds.
entries.response_text: It was. And the worst part is I'll do it again. I'll prepare something else, present it calmly, and watch it dissolve. Because the alternative is not trying, and I can't do that. If I stop pushing, I'm just another doctoral student waiting for permission. I've been someone who makes things happen for too long to accept that. But I'm starting to wonder if making things happen here requires a different kind of leverage than I'm used to.

entries.initial_entry:
Applied for that visiting researcher slot at Sciences Po. Had everything ready, CV updated, research statement polished, a solid letter from Björn. Then the department administrator told me the application needs a sign-off from the graduate studies committee, and they don't meet until November. The deadline is October 15th. She said I could email the committee chair directly to request an exception but "couldn't guarantee anything."

So now I'm just waiting. I emailed the chair four days ago, very polite, laid out the timeline clearly. No response. I don't even know if she's read it. This is what drives me up the wall, being stuck because someone else hasn't gotten around to clicking reply. I could have the whole thing sorted in an afternoon if it were up to me.
entries.nudge_text: What would you do if you knew the answer was no?
entries.response_text: I'd find another route. There's always another route. Maybe I'd contact Sciences Po directly, ask if they'd accept a provisional approval. Or find a different programme with a later deadline. Standing still isn't really something I do well.

entries.initial_entry:
Astrid asked me today, very casually over lunch, why I "always need to be in charge of things." She was half joking but it landed. I told her I don't need to be in charge, I just don't like waiting for other people to do things badly.

She laughed it off. I didn't.

The thing is, people say that like it's a character flaw. Like wanting things to go well and being willing to do the work to make that happen is somehow suspect. When I chaired the doctoral council meeting last week, I'd read all the documents beforehand. I'd drafted an agenda. I'd emailed people about their action items from the previous meeting. Is that controlling, or is that just doing the job properly? Nobody complained when the meeting finished in forty minutes instead of the usual hour and a half.

I know I come on strong sometimes. I know I take up more space than the average doctoral student. But I came here after running a team of twelve. I'm not going to pretend I don't know how to organise people or push things forward. If that makes someone uncomfortable, I think that says more about their expectations of what a doctoral student should be than about me.

Meeting with Björn and the co-supervisor today about the corporate partnership deliverables. I had a clear view on which case studies to prioritise for the industry report. Björn agreed. The co-supervisor, Lena, pushed for a different set entirely. Her reasoning was that the cases I chose were too "practitioner-oriented" and wouldn't serve the academic paper well enough.

I pushed back a little but she was firm, and Björn stayed quiet, which effectively meant she won. So we're going with her cases. I can already see the gaps. The data is thinner, the industry partner will be less interested, and I'll be the one fielding their questions about why the report doesn't address what they actually care about. But sure. Her cases it is.

Took the morning to deal with the new shift scheduling. HR wants us to finalize the December roster by Thursday which means I had to pull two supervisors into a room and sort through everyone's leave requests. Nothing dramatic, just the usual back and forth about who covers what. One of the guys asked for the entire last week of December off and I approved it because he hasn't taken time in months. Didn't think much of it at the time.

Lunch was at my desk again. Brought lentil soup from home but it had gone lukewarm by the time I got to it. I keep telling myself I'll start eating in the break room like a normal person but there's always one more email. After lunch I had the monthly safety review call with the Zarqa site, which went fine, nothing flagged. Then spent the rest of the afternoon updating inventory projections.

Drove home the long way because traffic on the highway was backed up near the Seventh Circle. Put on some Fairuz which was nice. Got home, heated up what was left of last night's mansaf, and sat on the balcony for maybe ten minutes before I started going through tomorrow's agenda on my phone.
entries.nudge_text: That ten minutes on the balcony — what pulled you back inside?
entries.response_text: Nothing dramatic. I just remembered I hadn't confirmed the forklift maintenance slot for Thursday. Once I picked up the phone it was over.

entries.initial_entry:
Still up, can't really settle. After that last entry I went and made tea and sat back on the balcony, properly this time, no phone. And it was good for a few minutes, the air was cool and I could hear the mosque down the street and I was just sitting there with my tea. But then this thing started where I'm thinking about how I approved Fadi's leave for December which means I'll need to cover his shifts myself or find someone, and suddenly the tea doesn't taste like anything anymore.

It's stupid. I literally just wanted to sit and drink tea. That's all I was doing. And I couldn't let myself have it for more than five minutes before my head started making it into a problem that I wasn't being productive. I don't even know who I'm performing for at eleven at night on my own balcony.
entries.nudge_text: Who taught you that sitting still needs a reason?
entries.response_text: Everybody gets like this. It's not that deep.

entries.initial_entry: Third time opening this today. Just realized I never actually finished the tea. Found it cold on the railing. Going to bed.
Bought knafeh on the way home from the Zarqa site. Ate it in the car in the parking lot before going upstairs, still warm. Didn't check my phone once.
entries.nudge_text: What made you stay in the car for that?

entries.initial_entry:
Everyone at work acts like I don't know how to take a break. My sister called on Friday and said Mama told her I looked tired at dinner, and then today Rami, one of my supervisors, made some comment about how I should use my own leave balance before telling everyone else to use theirs. And I get it, fine, they have a point on paper. But it's not like I'm some robot who doesn't want to rest. I do. I just can't flip a switch and turn off the part of my brain that keeps running through what needs to get done.

I was genuinely tired today, like the kind of tired where you sit down and your whole body feels heavy. And I thought about going home early, it was a quiet afternoon, nothing urgent. But then I started thinking about how that would look, leaving at three when my team is there until five. Even though nobody would care. Even though I tell my own people to go home early when it's slow. I couldn't give myself the same thing I'd give anyone else without it feeling like I was getting away with something.

So I stayed. Answered emails that could have waited. Drove home in the dark. Made rice and ate it standing in the kitchen. I know this is a pattern. I just don't know what I'm supposed to do differently when the guilt is louder than the tiredness.
entries.nudge_text: You'd send Rami home in a heartbeat. What makes your rest different?
entries.response_text: Because if I rest and something goes wrong, it's my fault. If they rest, I'm the one who covered. That's the deal I made with myself somewhere.

entries.initial_entry: My cousin had a gathering and there was good food and music and I was looking forward to it all week. Sat there the whole time thinking about a procurement deadline on Monday. Left early and felt worse than if I hadn't gone at all.

Got a notification from the app that they're changing the pay structure again starting next month. Third time in a year. The base rate drops and they say the bonuses will make up for it, but last time they said that, my weekly take dropped by almost eighty mil. I wrote it down, I have the numbers.

Lina said don't worry about it yet, wait and see. But I can't just wait and see. I ran the numbers in my notebook during a break between deliveries and if it's like last time I'd need four extra hours a day to match what I'm making now. Four hours I don't have.
entries.nudge_text: What's the backup if those numbers hold?

entries.initial_entry:
Stupid decision. Signed up for a third delivery platform because somebody at the parqueadero told me it pays well on weekends. Had to put down a deposit for their branded thermal bag — ninety mil — and now I'm juggling three apps on my phone at the same time and I can't keep track of which order belongs to which one.

Picked up a Rappi order thinking it was the new app and drove twenty minutes in the wrong direction before I realized. Lost the time and the gas. Lina asked how my day was and I just said fine because I didn't want to explain why I'm now paying for a third bag I might not even use past this week.

The thing is, I already have a routine that works. Two apps, I know the zones, I know which restaurants are slow, I know where to park. I broke my own system trying to grab a little more and now I'm scattered. Should have just stayed with what I had. The ninety mil bothers me more than it should — it's not huge, but it's money out the door for something I didn't need.
entries.nudge_text: What made this week different?
entries.response_text: Wasn't different really. Somebody just talked it up and I moved too fast. It happens.

entries.initial_entry:
Dropped the third app. Deleted it this morning, not worth the mental load. Back to the two I know. Lost the deposit but whatever, lesson paid for.

Regular Thursday route. Picked up from the arepas place on Carrera 7, three runs to Chapinero, one long one out to Usaquén that paid decent. Stopped at the tienda on the way home and Lina had saved me a plate of arroz con pollo from lunch. She'd had a slow afternoon — said the distributor raised prices on cooking oil again, which means she either eats the cost or marks up and risks losing her regulars. We talked about it for a bit. Not much to do about it.

Logged my earnings in the notebook. Slightly above average for a Thursday. Bike needs new brake pads soon, I can hear them starting to grind. Priced them out at the shop near the house — forty-five mil, which is fine. I'll do it Saturday when I'm off.

Week's been rough on the bike. The brake pads I was going to replace Saturday — didn't do it. Saturday got busy, Sunday Lina needed help at the tienda, Monday I forgot, and now it's Thursday and they're grinding metal on metal. I can feel the vibration through the handlebars when I stop.

I know it's not smart. Every day I ride with bad brakes is a day something could go wrong, and fixing a bent rotor costs ten times what pads cost. I've been through this before, years ago, ended up paying three hundred mil because I waited too long. You'd think I'd learn.

But here's the thing — stopping for the repair means losing half a day of work, and this week the app has a streak bonus if I hit thirty deliveries by Friday. I'm at twenty-two. If I stop tomorrow to fix the bike I lose the streak and probably two hundred mil in bonus. So I keep telling myself one more day, one more day. Lina doesn't know the brakes are this bad. She'd tell me to fix them immediately, and she'd be right.
entries.nudge_text: When does one more day stop making sense?

entries.initial_entry: Fixed the brakes last Saturday, finally. Fifty-two mil with labor because I waited so long the rotor needed resurfacing. Could have been forty-five if I'd gone when I planned, but it's done and the bike stops clean now. Feel better on the road.

My cousin Andrés called, wants me to invest in a food truck he's putting together with some guys from his old job. Says I could put in two million and be a silent partner and make it back in three months. I told him I'd think about it but honestly I already know the answer.

People act like I'm afraid of opportunity. I'm not afraid. I just know what happens. Andrés has had three business ideas in two years and none of them lasted past the first quarter. Two million is two months of savings. It took me a year and a half to build what we have in that account and I'm not going to watch it disappear into a food truck that parks in a bad location and folds by August. Lina agrees with me but says I should at least hear him out so he doesn't feel dismissed. Fine, I'll have cafecito with him. But the money stays where it is.
entries.nudge_text: What's the hardest part of saying no to him?
entries.response_text: Honestly it's that he'll think I don't believe in him. Which — I mean, I don't, not with money. But he's family. I don't want him going around telling everyone I'm tacaño. I just can't be the one who funds his experiments.

entries.initial_entry: App glitched during peak hours and didn't register three of my deliveries. That's maybe sixty mil gone. I submitted a support ticket but those take days and half the time they just say the system shows it correctly on their end. Hate depending on something I can't control.

Took a shortcut through Suba today because Waze said it would save twelve minutes and I had back-to-back orders. Road was torn up, construction, no signs, just orange cones everywhere. Had to loop back and ended up twenty minutes late on the delivery. Customer gave me one star. One star because the city can't put up a proper detour sign.

Normally I stick to my routes. I have them memorized, I know which streets flood when it rains, I know where the potholes are. I don't need Waze. But I was behind and I got greedy with the time and now I have a one-star rating dragging down my average. If it drops below 4.7 they reduce your priority in the queue which means fewer orders which means less money.

That's what gets me — one bad rating from one shortcut I didn't need to take and now I'm spending the next two weeks being extra careful and extra polite trying to claw back points. All because I didn't just take the route I know. Lina says I obsess over the rating but she doesn't understand how the algorithm works. It's not just a number, it's whether I eat next month.
entries.nudge_text: Has a bad rating ever actually cost you work?
entries.response_text: Not yet. But I've seen it happen to guys at the parqueadero. One of them dropped to 4.5 and his orders went to nothing for a month. I'm not going to wait to find out firsthand.

entries.initial_entry:
Wednesday. Steady day, nothing unusual. Eleven deliveries, which is about average for a midweek. The app paid out the streak bonus from last week, finally — showed up in my account this morning, a hundred and thirty mil. Transferred it to savings immediately before it sits in the checking account and turns into groceries.

Lina's tienda had a decent day too. She sold a bulk order of panela to a neighbor who's making natilla for a birthday party this weekend. She was happy about it, said it was the biggest single sale this month. We had dinner together for once — usually I'm still on the bike at eight but today I finished early.

Checked the notebook. Month is tracking slightly above April, which is good. If I keep this pace through the rest of May I can cover the bike insurance renewal in June without dipping into savings. That's the goal. One month at a time.

Long day but the kind that feels right at the end. Fourteen deliveries, almost all in Chapinero which is my best zone — short distances, good tips, restaurants that have the food ready when I arrive. My rating came back up to 4.82. Took two weeks of clean runs but it's back where it should be.

Paid the bike insurance early. Lina looked at me funny because it's not due until June 15 but I told her I'd rather have it done. One less thing on the list. She gets it, she does the same thing with the tienda's distributor invoices — pays them the day they arrive even though she has thirty days. We're the same that way, both of us want the ledger clean.

Andrés never brought up the food truck again. I think he found someone else or maybe the whole thing fell apart. Either way I'm relieved. The savings account is at the highest it's been since December, and Lina and I are talking about maybe finally fixing the bathroom plumbing that's been leaking slow for months. Real things, things that last.
entries.nudge_text: How long has the plumbing been going?
entries.response_text: Since February more or less. It's a slow drip under the sink, not urgent, but the water bill crept up a little. I've been pricing plumbers — around four hundred mil for the job. We'll probably do it in June once the insurance is squared away. One thing at a time.

entries.initial_entry: Third week of summer break and I'm already crawling out of my skin, spent the whole morning refreshing job boards for literally anything that isn't sitting at home, maybe I should just book a flight somewhere, anywhere, the walls are closing in.
entries.nudge_text: What happened this morning that set this off?

entries.initial_entry: Principal Chen called about leading the new student orientation committee for August. I said yes before she finished explaining the scope.
entries.nudge_text: You didn't even let her finish?
entries.response_text: Honestly no. The moment she said "lead" I was already in. I've been rotting at home for three weeks with nothing to run, nothing to decide. My mom keeps asking why I don't just relax like a normal person during break. But sitting around feels like drowning. At least now I have something with my name on it.

entries.initial_entry:
I'm so frustrated I could scream. The orientation committee met today on Zoom and Ms. Liu, who's been at the school for fifteen years, kept interrupting me to "add context" every time I proposed something. Context! As if I don't know our own students. She literally said "Wei-Lin, you're still new here" in front of everyone when I suggested we move the welcome assembly outdoors to the courtyard.

It's my committee. Principal Chen asked ME to lead it. But every time I try to actually lead, someone older swoops in to remind me I'm twenty-three. I left banking so I wouldn't have to wait in line behind people who got there first. I thought teaching would be different.
entries.nudge_text: Different how?
entries.response_text: I thought... I don't know. That being good at something would be enough. At the bank I was fast, accurate, never made mistakes, but promotions went by seniority. I figured teaching would reward initiative—you put in the work, you earn the room. But maybe every institution is the same. Maybe I'm just chasing something that doesn't exist, some place where being capable matters more than being old. That's a depressing thought. I don't want to believe it.

entries.initial_entry:
Same day, different mood I guess. After that Zoom call and venting here I went for a long walk around the harbor, watched the fishing boats come in, and something clicked. Ms. Liu wasn't trying to undermine me—she's been running orientations since before I was in middle school myself, and she probably felt sidelined when Chen handed the lead role to someone who's been there less than two years.

That doesn't mean I was wrong to want to shake things up. The outdoor assembly IS a better idea. But maybe I came in too hot, treating the meeting like a presentation where I had all the answers instead of a collaboration where people needed to feel heard. My dad used to say I sprint before people have tied their shoes. He meant it as a flaw. I always took it as a compliment.

I think both can be true? I move fast because slow feels unbearable, but fast doesn't have to mean alone. I messaged Ms. Liu after the walk and asked if she wanted to grab boba tomorrow to talk through the logistics together. She sent back a smiley face. Small win. I'll take it.

The harbor smelled like diesel and salt and I took some good shots of the cranes against the sunset for the gram. Feeling more like myself now. Sometimes I just need to move to think.

Amna presented the quarterly vendor review in our Monday meeting. She used my slides. Not the template I made for the department, the actual slides I prepared last week for the director call. She changed the title and rearranged two charts and acted like it was something she had been working on all weekend.

I sat there watching her walk through my analysis of the Faisalabad supplier delays. The phrasing was mine. The framing was mine. She even used the same footnote about container shortages that I pulled from the logistics brief. Nobody else in that room would have noticed because nobody else reads the logistics briefs. But I noticed.

I didn't interrupt. I didn't raise it. My boss nodded along and thanked her for the thorough breakdown. Amna didn't look at me once during the whole thing. That told me she knew exactly what she was doing.

Afterwards I went back to my desk and just sat there for ten minutes. I should have said something in the room. Something small, even. "I think I had similar findings in my director prep." But I froze. I keep replaying it and the thing that bothers me most is not that she did it. It's that I let her.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you in that moment?
entries.response_text: It wasn't the right setting. You can't start calling someone out in front of the whole team, it would look petty. If I bring it up now it's old news. It's fine. I just need to make sure it doesn't happen again.

entries.initial_entry:
Submitted the new vendor onboarding proposal last Tuesday. It's been seven days and nothing from the director. No feedback, no questions, not even a "received, will review." I followed up once on Thursday with a polite message, kept it short, said I was available to discuss at his convenience. Read receipt came through within the hour. Still nothing.

The proposal took me three weeks. I mapped the entire onboarding cycle, identified two bottlenecks where we lose four to five days on compliance checks, built a revised timeline that would cut new vendor integration from six weeks to three and a half. This isn't a suggestion doc. This is a full operational redesign with cost projections.

Meanwhile he had time yesterday to sit with Bilal for forty minutes about the office renovation budget. Bilal is the facilities coordinator. The office renovation is new carpeting and a break room microwave. My proposal would save the company roughly twelve lakh per quarter in faster vendor activation. But sure, let's discuss the microwave first.

I can't push harder without looking desperate. I can't escalate because there's nobody to escalate to except him. So I sit and wait and pretend I have other priorities while the most important project on my desk collects dust in someone else's inbox.
entries.nudge_text: What's harder — the waiting, or having no way to speed it up?
entries.response_text: Having no way to speed it up. Waiting I can handle if there's an end date. This is just open-ended silence. I could walk into his office and ask directly, but then I look like I'm hovering. The proposal speaks for itself. He should have responded by now.

entries.initial_entry:
Farah pushed back on the dispatch scheduling changes in front of the whole floor today. She said the new system creates too much pressure on the packing team and that I hadn't consulted them before rolling it out. She wasn't wrong about the consultation part, but the way she said it — standing there with her arms crossed, loud enough for the warehouse guys to hear — it felt like she was making a point that had nothing to do with dispatch schedules.

I backed down. Told her we could revisit the timeline and get input from the packing leads. The words came out reasonable but inside I was furious. The old schedule had three missed shipments last month. Three. I fixed that. She's been here longer than me and I think she resents that I'm the one making these calls now. But I said fine, let's revisit. Because fighting in the middle of the floor would have made me look worse, not better.
Long day. Eid shopping with Ammi after work, then came home to finish the weekly reports. My eyes hurt and I still need to review tomorrow's vendor call brief before sleeping.

Bilal got the conference room booking system changed so department heads need to reserve through his assistant now. Used to be we could just block time directly on the calendar. He sent the email to all managers like it was a building-wide policy but I checked with procurement — it's just him adding a layer because someone double-booked the main boardroom last month.

It's a small thing. I know it's a small thing. But it means if I need the room for a client call at short notice, I have to go through his person first. I already sent a reply asking whether department heads with client-facing responsibilities could retain direct access. Kept it professional. He replied with a smiley face and "let's discuss offline." That means no.

I could go to the director about it but that feels like escalating a room booking dispute, which is exactly the kind of thing that makes a young manager look like she's overreacting. Ammi always says pick your fights carefully. She's right. But the small things add up and each one is a little bit of ground lost.
entries.nudge_text: What ground are you worried about losing?
entries.response_text: The ability to move fast without asking permission. That's the whole reason the dispatch overhaul worked — I didn't wait for committees and approvals, I just did it. Every new process that adds a gatekeeper between me and getting things done slows me down. It's not about a room. It's about whether I can operate at my own pace or everyone else's.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a client visit today. The buyer from the UK account came for the factory walkthrough and the director introduced me as "one of our operations team." Not operations manager. Not the person who restructured the entire dispatch flow that got them their last three shipments on time. Just "one of our operations team."

I smiled and shook hands and walked them through the floor like nothing happened. The buyer asked good questions about our quality checks and I answered every single one without hesitating. By the end she was directing her questions to me, not the director. That felt like something. But the introduction stung. I'm not "one of" anything. I run that floor.
entries.nudge_text: She ended up directing questions to you — does that make the introduction matter less?

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about yesterday. Mentioned it to Abba over dinner and he said I should be grateful to have the position at all at my age, that most girls from our family are still finishing their degrees or married by now. He didn't mean it badly, I know that. He's proud of me in his way. But "grateful" is not the word I want to hear when someone erased my title in front of a client.

I wanted him to be angry for me. Instead he told me the story about how his boss at the textile mill passed him over for promotion three times before finally giving it to him. Like patience is the lesson. Like waiting is the same thing as earning. I've already earned this. I just need the people around me to see it the way I do.
Vendor call went smoothly, secured better terms on the denim lot. Farah actually came to me afterwards and said the new scheduling format is working. Small acknowledgment but I'll take it.

Director finally responded to the onboarding proposal. Three weeks of silence and then a five-line email: "Good effort. Let's pilot with one vendor. Loop in procurement." No mention of the cost projections. No acknowledgment that I spent three weeks mapping the entire cycle. "Good effort" — like I'm an intern who turned in a decent assignment.

I should be happy. He approved it, technically. But "loop in procurement" means I lose sole ownership. Now it's a cross-functional initiative, which sounds collaborative on paper but really means I'll be coordinating with people who didn't build this and don't understand the logic behind the sequencing. I built something precise and now it gets diluted. I keep telling myself a pilot is better than no response. But I wanted him to see the full scope and say, "This is exactly what we needed."
entries.nudge_text: What would 'enough' recognition look like for you?

entries.initial_entry: Cousin's engagement party last night. Every aunty asked what I do and when I said manager they looked confused, then asked about marriage plans. One of them said to Ammi, "At least she's keeping busy."
entries.nudge_text: When they said that, who did you want to correct — them, or yourself?
entries.response_text: Them. Obviously them. I don't need correcting. But you can't lecture your khala at a mehendi about what operations management means. So I smiled and ate my biryani and talked to my cousin about her lehenga instead. Some rooms aren't worth the fight.

entries.initial_entry: First real day of summer break. Slept until 10, made a proper French press coffee, and read on the porch for two hours. This is why I teach.
entries.nudge_text: What do you mean when you say this is why you teach?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I just meant the break is nice. Not everything needs to be deep. The coffee was good, the weather was perfect, and I didn't have to think about lesson plans. That's enough.

entries.initial_entry: Spent the week at a cabin in Wisconsin with Jenna. No cell service for five days. Best decision we've made in months.
entries.nudge_text: What made the no-cell-service part particularly good for you?

entries.initial_entry:
Got back from Wisconsin and immediately checked my email. Mistake. There's already an email from the department head about "optional" summer curriculum planning sessions. Optional in quotes because we all know how that goes. Last year three of us skipped the sessions and somehow ended up with the worst schedules in the fall.

I'm not doing it this year. I told Jenna I'm ignoring the email until August 1st at the earliest, and she actually applauded. Twelve years in and I've finally learned that the school will survive without me reworking the sophomore reading list in July. The people who show up to these things aren't more dedicated than me - they just haven't figured out how to protect their own time yet.

Made paella from scratch today. Took four hours and cost more than ordering it from La Taberna, but there's something about the process that makes it worth it. Jenna said it was the best meal I've made this summer.
entries.nudge_text: What is it about the process that makes it worth the extra time and money?
entries.response_text: It's the ritual of it, I think. Standing over the stove, adjusting the heat, watching the rice absorb the broth. My brain actually turns off when I'm cooking something complicated. No thinking about September syllabi or parent emails. Just saffron and timing. The four hours weren't a cost - they were the point.

entries.initial_entry:
End of the first proper week back after the holiday break and I am completely drained. The kids were wild, which I expected, but what wore me out more was this long staff meeting about the new curriculum rollout. Lots of talk about standardized testing targets and almost nothing about whether the material actually reflects what is happening in the world right now.

I brought up the idea of including more content on different cultures since half our class comes from families that moved here within the last decade. My mentor teacher said it was "a nice thought" and then immediately moved on to the testing calendar. Nobody else said anything. I sat through the rest of it feeling like I had spoken out of turn somehow.

Came home and Sofie had made soup, which was kind. Ate it on the couch and fell asleep before nine. Not sure I even processed the day properly, just ran out of battery entirely.
entries.nudge_text: What made you bring it up in the first place?
entries.response_text: Half the kids in my class have parents who came from Syria or Turkey or Eritrea. The textbooks barely mention any of those places. It felt dishonest not to say something. I just wish I had been less surprised when nobody backed me up.

entries.initial_entry:
Can't stop thinking about this article I read before bed about microplastics found in baby formula. Baby formula. These are infants who did not ask for any of this and the regulatory bodies are apparently just watching it happen. I teach six-year-olds. Some of them were drinking that formula two years ago.

I mentioned it to Sofie and she said "you can't carry all of this" which I know is probably true but also feels like giving up. What am I supposed to do, just not read about it? Pretend it is not happening? I teach children. Their health is literally part of my job even if no one puts it in the job description.

I know I am spiraling a bit. I get like this when something hits close to the work. It is not even the microplastics specifically, it is this feeling that the systems that are supposed to protect people just don't, and we all keep going to work the next morning like that is fine.

Volunteered at the community center after school. Helped a girl named Amira with her reading homework and she finished a whole chapter book for the first time. Walked home in the cold and felt like it was a good day.
entries.nudge_text: What was it like watching Amira finish that book?
entries.response_text: She looked so shocked at herself. Like she did not believe she could do it. I told her she did and she just sat there grinning with the book in her lap. Moments like that remind me why I got into this. It is small, one kid, one book, but it felt real in a way that staff meetings never do.

entries.initial_entry:
Had dinner with my parents in Aalst this evening. My dad works at a chemical plant and he was complaining about new emission regulations making his shifts more complicated. I wanted to say that the regulations exist for a reason, that the Scheldt has been polluted for decades and people downstream are still dealing with it. But I just nodded and ate my stoofvlees.

It is strange because I love my dad and I know he is tired and just wants his job to be manageable. But I also know that the river matters and the people who fish in it matter and the kids who swim in it matter. I do not know how to hold both of those things at the same time without feeling like I am betraying one side.

This keeps happening. At school, at home, in my own head. I see the bigger picture and then I see the person in front of me and I cannot figure out how to be honest without being hurtful. So I stay quiet, which feels like its own kind of failure.
entries.nudge_text: When you stay quiet, who are you protecting?
entries.response_text: My dad, mostly. Or the version of our relationship where we do not argue about things that matter. But I think I am also protecting myself from having to admit that I do not know how to change anyone's mind. If I say something and he dismisses it, then I have to sit with the fact that caring about this stuff does not actually move anything. At least silence lets me keep the illusion that I could make a difference if I tried.

entries.initial_entry:
Sofie asked me tonight if I ever take a break from "worrying about the world." She said it like she was being kind but it stung. I am not worrying for fun. I am paying attention, which apparently is unusual enough that people think you need a hobby.

I told her I have hobbies. I run. I cook. She said those do not count because I listen to podcasts about ocean acidification while I run and I only buy from the organic market even when it is out of the way. I did not have a great comeback for that.

Spent the afternoon at the community center again, this time sorting donated winter coats. The pile was massive, people just brought bags and bags. Some of the coats still had tags on them. I paired up with this older woman named Fatima who has been volunteering there for eleven years and she told me stories about the first families she helped resettle.

She said something that stuck. She said you do not help because it fixes everything, you help because the person in front of you is cold. Simple as that. I think I needed to hear it from someone who has been doing this longer than I have been alive.

Walked home through the park afterward and the light was doing that thing where everything looks like a painting. Bare trees, pink sky. I felt calm for the first time in a while. Not because anything got solved, but because I spent the day doing something that made direct, visible sense. Coat goes to person. Person is warm. Done.

Good week at school somehow. I have been trying this thing where I let the kids ask questions about anything during the last ten minutes of Friday and we just talk about it. One kid asked why some people do not have houses. Another asked if fish can feel pain. I did not have neat answers but we talked through them together and I think that matters more anyway.

My mentor teacher actually came by during one of these sessions and did not say anything negative afterward, which for her is practically applause. She said "they were engaged" and left. I will take it.

After school I sat in the classroom for a while marking worksheets and I had this rare moment of thinking I am in the right place. Not that everything is fine, obviously, the world is a mess and the school system has problems and I am twenty-two and exhausted most of the time. But the work itself, sitting with a room of six-year-olds and trying to help them make sense of things, that part is right.
entries.nudge_text: What changed this week compared to those first weeks back?
entries.response_text: I think I stopped waiting for permission. The curriculum rollout meeting frustrated me because I was asking the institution to make space for what I cared about. This week I just did it myself, ten minutes, no approval needed. It is small but it reminded me that I do not have to fix the whole system to do something meaningful inside it. Fatima said the same thing in her own way. Maybe it is finally sinking in.

entries.initial_entry:
Got into it with a colleague at lunch. She was talking about how the school should not "get political" by including climate content in the science module for the older kids. I said the climate is not political, it is physical reality, and she said I was being naive. I told her that ignoring it is a choice too and then the conversation got awkward and we both went back to eating.

I know I was right but I also know I was sharp about it. My hands were shaking a bit after, which always happens when I get confrontational. I hate that about myself. I cannot tell if the shaking is because I am angry or because I am scared of what people think of me when I push back. Probably both.
entries.nudge_text: Would you say the same thing again tomorrow?
entries.response_text: Yes. But maybe less sharply. She is not the enemy, she is just someone who has not thought about it the way I have. Or she has and arrived somewhere different, which is harder to accept. Either way, I do not want to be the person who stays quiet when it matters. I already do that enough at home.

entries.initial_entry:
Long day before the holiday break. The warehouse team finished the year-end audit using the tracking sheets I designed back in 2019 instead of the new corporate software. Takes half the time. Nobody from Mexico City noticed, or if they did, nobody said anything. Fine by me.

Gabriela brought tamales for the office. I stayed late to finish signing off on the last shipment reports myself rather than delegating to Arturo.
