He follows the manual too literally and I did not want to spend Christmas Eve fixing paperwork. Tired, but the numbers are right because I checked them the way I always check them.
entries.nudge_text: What makes doing things your way worth the extra hours?
entries.response_text: Worth it? Because when Arturo follows the manual, we get three calls from logistics asking why the codes do not match. Because I have seen what happens when people just follow what they are told without thinking. Twenty-five years and the company still uses my system because theirs does not work. That is what makes it worth it.

entries.initial_entry:
End of the year, everyone talking about resolutions. Mine is the same as always, keep doing what works. The new director sent an email about "aligning regional processes" starting January. Another round of them telling us how to do our jobs. I already know how this goes. They fly in consultants, we sit through presentations, then we quietly go back to what actually functions.

Spent the morning reorganizing my office. Moved the filing cabinets to create more space for the whiteboard where I sketch out logistics problems. Nobody else uses whiteboards anymore, everything is supposed to be in the cloud, but I think better when I can see the whole picture drawn out in front of me. Marta teased me about being old-fashioned. Maybe. But old-fashioned works.
entries.nudge_text: How do you usually navigate those alignment initiatives?
entries.response_text: I listen carefully in the meetings. Take notes. Ask enough questions so they know I was paying attention. Then I look at what they want and figure out which parts actually apply to us and which parts were designed for an office in a different city with different problems. Usually about thirty percent is useful. I keep that thirty percent and let the rest fade. Nobody has complained because the numbers stay good.

entries.initial_entry:
First week back. The alignment initiative started with a video call. Regional wants us to adopt their scheduling software by February. I spent an hour after the call looking through it. The interface is cluttered and it does not account for the way our suppliers operate on different timelines than the northern facilities. Typical. One-size-fits-all approach from people who have never walked our warehouse floor.

I called Sergio in Monterrey. He has the same concerns. We agreed to document everything that does not work and present it together at the next regional meeting. Better to come with specifics than just complaints. I pulled data from the last two quarters showing our current scheduling method has a 94% on-time delivery rate. If their software improves that number, I will use it. If not, I have the data to explain why we are doing something different.

After work, I walked the floor with the night shift supervisor. He mentioned that two of the younger workers asked him why we do inventory counts differently than what they learned in training. I told him to explain that training teaches you the baseline, but experience teaches you the exceptions. Every warehouse is different. They will understand when they have been here longer.
Meeting did not go well. They want standardization regardless of the data. Something about "brand consistency across regions."
entries.nudge_text: What bothers you most about this decision?
entries.response_text: I put together the numbers. Sergio and I stayed late preparing the presentation. They listened, nodded, then said it was not about efficiency, it was about uniformity. My numbers did not matter. Twenty-five years building something that works and they want to replace it with something that looks good in a corporate report. I am tired of fighting the same fight.

entries.initial_entry:
I started using their software this week. It is as bad as I thought. Took twice as long to schedule the Tuesday shipments because their system does not understand that our supplier in Colima closes early on Mondays and needs orders by Friday noon. Had to override the automated suggestions manually each time. The younger staff kept asking me how to work around the glitches.

What frustrates me is not the software itself. Tools can be learned. What frustrates me is that nobody asked us before deciding. Nobody walked into our facility and said, what do you need? They sat in their offices and drew up a plan and handed it down. I have run this department since before some of them were born. I know what works here. But they want compliance, not solutions.
entries.nudge_text: What would being asked have meant to you?

entries.initial_entry:
New Year's Eve and I'm sitting at Erik's kitchen table while everyone else is in the living room watching some countdown special. His uncle was here earlier, going off about how the city council's new housing plan for asylum seekers is going to "ruin the neighbourhood." Nobody said anything. I didn't say anything. Erik kind of half-laughed and changed the subject to football.

I've met some of the families that housing plan is for. I know their names. I know Fatima's daughter just started school and is terrified of the cafeteria. And I just sat there holding my drink and let this man talk about them like they were a problem to be managed. I kept telling myself it wasn't the right moment, it's a party, don't make it weird. But that's just an excuse.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you more - the setting or something else?
entries.response_text: Scared I'd lose my temper and they'd just dismiss me.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the morning at Akademibokhandeln buying a new notebook for the semester and ended up at the Espresso House on Svartbäcksgatan after. I ordered a coffee and one of those wrapped sandwiches and then just stared at the packaging. Plastic wrap, plastic tray, a paper sleeve that probably isn't even recyclable. I've been reading about single-use packaging policy for my thesis for months. I know the numbers. I know what this stuff does. And I bought it anyway because I was hungry and didn't feel like walking home first.

It sounds ridiculous to feel bad about a sandwich wrapper. People would laugh. But it's the pattern that bugs me. I write about systemic failures in environmental policy and then I make the lazy choice when it's my own lunch. The gap between what I study and how I actually live keeps showing up in these small ways. I don't buy fast fashion anymore, I cycle, I eat less meat than I used to. But then there's this sandwich in a plastic coffin and I think, what's the point of any of it if I can't even get the small stuff right.

My housemate Nils would say I'm overthinking it. He'd say one sandwich wrapper doesn't matter. And he'd be technically correct. But I think the willingness to say "it doesn't matter" about any single thing is exactly how you end up not caring about anything.
entries.nudge_text: Do you actually believe one sandwich wrapper matters, or is it about something bigger?
entries.response_text: It's about whether I'm becoming someone who just talks.

entries.initial_entry: Overheard Markus in the department kitchen making a joke about a Somali student's accent during a presentation. A couple of people laughed. I pretended I was busy with the coffee machine.

I'm so tired. Thesis crunch plus volunteering Saturdays plus a seminar on EU regulatory frameworks that runs three hours every Tuesday evening. I barely have energy to cook. Today I ordered Foodora twice. Both times it was from chains I know treat their delivery riders badly. I've seen the articles. I've shared the articles. And then I opened the app and ordered anyway because I couldn't face making pasta.

It's not that I forgot. I just didn't care enough in the moment. Or I cared but my tiredness won. I don't know which is worse. The riders are out there in January cold on bikes so I don't have to walk to my own kitchen. I can dress it up however I want but that's what happened.

Had a supervision meeting with Professor Lindqvist today. She liked my chapter draft on enforcement gaps but said I need to address the "political feasibility" section more carefully. Fair enough. But then she casually mentioned that the department is being courted by Vattenfall for a research partnership and asked if I'd be interested in being a student liaison. Good for my CV, she said. Good connections.

Vattenfall is state-owned and technically pivoting toward renewables, but they also ran lignite mines in Germany for decades and their track record on indigenous Sámi land rights in the north is awful. I know this because I wrote a case study on it last year. Lindqvist knows I wrote it. She was my supervisor for that paper.

I should have said no on the spot. Instead I said I'd think about it. Because part of me is thinking about what it would look like on a job application. Part of me is doing the math on whether working with them from the inside might actually lead to something useful. And I hate that I'm even calculating like this, because I know that's how everyone justifies it. "Change from within." It's the oldest excuse in the book and I've heard it a hundred times from people I don't respect. But here I am, considering it for myself, because Professor Lindqvist smiled when she said my name and I liked being chosen.
entries.nudge_text: What bothers you more - the partnership or that you liked being asked?
entries.response_text: Both. But mostly the second one.

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about the Vattenfall thing from this morning. Went for a walk along the river after dinner to try to sort it out and I'm not sure I managed. The thing is, I talk a lot about how corporations co-opt well-meaning people. I literally have a slide in my thesis presentation about it. "Institutional capture of reform narratives." That's my phrase. And now I'm being offered a small taste of exactly that.

Convenience is the word I keep coming back to. It would be convenient for my career. It would be convenient to tell myself I'm doing it for the right reasons. It would be convenient to let Professor Lindqvist handle the ethical questions while I just show up and take notes and put Vattenfall on my LinkedIn. That's what most people would do. That's what a practical person would do.

But I keep thinking about Fatima and her daughter from the refugee centre, and the Sámi reindeer herders whose grazing land got destroyed for a mine that Vattenfall then sold off when it became unprofitable. These are not abstract policy questions to me. Or at least they shouldn't be. If I take this liaison position, I become someone who files those things under "complicated" and moves on. And I've always told myself I wouldn't be that person. I just didn't expect the test to come from my own supervisor.
entries.nudge_text: Have you decided, or are you still hoping you won't have to?

entries.initial_entry:
I told Lindqvist I'd pass on the Vattenfall liaison position. She took it well enough, said she understood, moved on to thesis feedback. I don't think she was surprised.

At the volunteer session on Saturday, one of the newer volunteers, a guy named Anders, made a comment about how some of the refugee families "don't even try to learn Swedish." He said it lightly, like it was just an observation. Two other volunteers nodded. I know for a fact that three of the families he's talking about attend SFI classes four mornings a week. One of the mothers, Amira, practices Swedish vocabulary with her kids at the kitchen table every evening. I've seen her notebook.

I didn't correct him. I thought about it. I opened my mouth and then just said something about how the language courses have long waiting lists, which is true but wasn't really the point. The point was that he was wrong and I sidestepped it instead of saying so directly. I keep doing this thing where I provide context instead of actually challenging what someone says. It feels diplomatic in the moment but afterward it just feels like cowardice dressed up as nuance.

Quiet week. Spent most of it in the library working through policy documents for chapter four. The snow has been heavy and cycling has been miserable but the bus is worse, so I keep at it. Had fika with Nils and our other housemate Sara on Wednesday. We talked about summer plans. Nils wants to do a hiking trip in Norway. Sara is going home to Malmö.

Cooked a big pot of lentil soup on Sunday that I've been eating all week. My mum called to check in, asked about the thesis timeline. I told her it's on track for June submission. She sounded relieved. Nothing eventful really. Just the steady rhythm of getting through January.

Thesis stress is properly hitting now. I was up until two in the morning reading IPCC reports and cross-referencing enforcement data and at some point I just stopped and looked at what I was doing. I'm writing about policy mechanisms that might reduce emissions by some fraction of a percentage point over a decade. And meanwhile the numbers keep getting worse. Every single year. I read a paper last week that said we've already locked in enough warming to make certain Pacific islands uninhabitable. Already locked in. Past tense.

So what am I actually doing. I'm sitting in a well-heated Swedish apartment writing about governance frameworks while people are losing their homes to rising seas right now. My thesis will get graded, filed, maybe cited in another thesis. The policy recommendations I'm writing will be read by my supervisor and two examiners and then forgotten. That's the honest truth of it.

I could have gone into something more direct. Field work. Activism. Legal advocacy. Instead I chose the comfortable academic route where I get to feel smart about the problem without ever getting my hands dirty. My friend Elsa works for an NGO in Bangladesh building flood shelters. She makes a third of what I'll make when I graduate. She's also actually doing something. I chose to write about the problem from a distance because it's safer and it counts toward a degree. That's the choice I made today and every day I sit in that library.
entries.nudge_text: Is the thesis actually the problem, or is it that you're not sure it's enough?
entries.response_text: I don't know. I'm too tired to answer that honestly right now.

entries.initial_entry: Came back to write more after lying in bed for an hour not sleeping. There was a moment at the volunteer centre last week where someone asked me to help draft a complaint letter about housing conditions and I said I was too busy with my thesis. I wasn't too busy. I just didn't want to take it on.
entries.nudge_text: What made you not want to take it on?
entries.response_text: Honestly? I just didn't want another thing to carry.

entries.initial_entry:
I've been thinking about something all week. On Tuesday I went to the supermarket and I was standing in front of the coffee shelf trying to decide between the Fairtrade one that costs almost double and the store brand. I always buy the Fairtrade one. It's one of those small choices I make that lets me tell myself I'm someone who pays attention. But this time I picked up the store brand and put it in my basket. I was over budget for the month and I just thought, I can't afford to care about this right now. And then I stood there for a solid minute feeling sick about a bag of coffee.

It's the same pattern from the sandwich wrapper in January. These tiny consumer decisions that I load with all this meaning. But maybe the meaning is real. If I stop caring about the coffee, do I stop caring about the next thing, and the thing after that. At what point does "I can't afford it right now" become a permanent excuse. I watched my dad do this over years. He used to be involved with Greenpeace, went to protests, the whole thing. Now he drives an SUV and says the protests don't accomplish anything anymore. He didn't change overnight. It was a thousand small surrenders.

I don't want that to be me. But I also bought the cheap coffee. And I drank it this morning and it tasted fine and I didn't think about it until now. That's the part that scares me. How quickly the guilt fades. How easily convenience becomes the default. I keep writing in this thing like that's going to help, but I'm not sure documenting my failures counts as doing something about them.

Long shift. Nothing dramatic, just the usual December surge — flu admissions, short-staffed because half the ward called in sick, the usual. I spent most of the morning juggling bed assignments and fielding calls from the ER about overflow patients. Ate lunch standing up again, which is becoming a habit I don't love.

Had a quick conversation with one of the new residents about discharge paperwork. She seemed overwhelmed but I walked her through it. Sometimes I forget how confusing the system is when you're new. Afterwards I sat in the break room for ten minutes and just stared at my phone without reading anything. My feet hurt. The Christmas decorations in the corridor look cheap this year — someone bought the wrong garland. I should probably let it go but it looks terrible.

Second entry today because I can't stop thinking about what happened this afternoon. Dr. Wernecke held a meeting about the new triage protocol. I raised a concern about patient flow bottlenecks — a real operational issue I've seen play out dozens of times. Before I even finished the sentence, the new deputy head of nursing, Katja, cut in with her own suggestion. Completely different point. And Wernecke just turned to her and started discussing it as though I hadn't been speaking.

Nobody corrected it. Nobody said "Britta was making a point." I sat there with my mouth half open. I know Katja is newer and eager to make her mark, but that was my observation from twenty years on this ward. I didn't push back. I just went quiet and let the meeting carry on without me. Sat in the car park for fifteen minutes after my shift trying to figure out why I didn't say anything.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from finishing your point?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I think I was stunned. It happened so fast. By the time I registered what had occurred, the conversation had moved on and it would have looked petty to circle back. Or at least that's what I told myself. Part of me also thought: if they don't see my experience as worth hearing, maybe forcing the issue would just confirm that. That's a horrible thought but it's the truth.

entries.initial_entry: Quiet Saturday for once. Did my weekly shop at Edeka, prepped meals for the next three days, cleaned the flat. My son called to confirm he's coming for Christmas, which is good. He's bringing his girlfriend. I need to figure out sleeping arrangements but it's manageable. Normal weekend things. Nothing to complain about, really.

Twelve-hour shift but something small happened that I keep replaying. A patient's daughter stopped me in the hallway to thank me — said I was the only one who explained her mother's medication changes clearly. She squeezed my hand. It was brief, maybe ten seconds. But I felt like someone actually saw what I do here. That matters.
entries.nudge_text: What made that feel different from the usual thank-yous?
entries.response_text: Most thank-yous are polite, automatic. This one felt specific. She wasn't just grateful in a generic way — she named the exact thing I did and told me it mattered. I think after that meeting with Wernecke, I needed someone to remind me that I'm actually good at this. Not in a "well done" pat-on-the-back way, but in a "you made a real difference today" way. It landed differently because of timing.

entries.initial_entry: Christmas Eve. Submitted a request last week for two additional night staff over the holiday period. Still waiting for the nursing director to approve it. I've chased twice now. She said she'd "look into it." Meanwhile I'm the one who'll have to cover the gaps if she doesn't sign off. Sitting here unable to do anything about it is maddening.
entries.nudge_text: What's the hardest part — the waiting, or not being the one deciding?
entries.response_text: The not deciding. Definitely. The waiting I can handle if I know it's coming. But I wrote the staffing justification, I know the ward numbers, I can see exactly what we need. And still someone else holds the pen. If I could just approve it myself, it would have been done days ago. Instead I'm refreshing my email on Christmas Eve like that's a normal thing to do.

entries.initial_entry:
Boxing Day and I'm thinking about yesterday's dinner argument with my son. He brought up the apartment renovation — the one I pushed through with the residents' committee. Said I'd been "bulldozing" people. His girlfriend nodded along, which made it worse. I started to explain my reasoning, how the building's plumbing was a ticking time bomb and nobody else was going to take responsibility for it, and he just said "Mum, that's not the point."

So I asked what the point was. He said I didn't let the other residents have their say properly. And I wanted to argue, to lay out the timeline and the three committee meetings I organised, but I could see his girlfriend watching me and I just... stopped. Said fine, maybe I could have handled it differently. I don't actually believe that, but I gave in because fighting about it at Christmas dinner felt worse than conceding.

My son and his girlfriend left this morning. Hugged him at the door, told him to drive safely. After they pulled away I stood in the kitchen for a while and thought about how the visit actually went. Setting aside the renovation argument, it was good. We cooked together on Christmas morning — he made his father's old Kartoffelsalat recipe without me asking, and it was nearly perfect.

There was a moment when he asked for my opinion on changing his car insurance, and I realised he wasn't being polite. He actually wanted my take. We sat at the table with his laptop and compared policies for an hour. It felt like being consulted, properly consulted, by someone who respects what I bring to the conversation. I forget sometimes that he does that. I notice the friction more than the easy moments. That's probably worth thinking about.

First shift of the new year. The staffing request finally got approved — three days late, but approved. Spent the morning updating rosters. Had a normal handover meeting, no interruptions this time. Katja said hello in the corridor and I managed a civil nod. Baby steps. Nothing remarkable today, just the machinery of January grinding into motion.

Had Christmas dinner at my sister's place. Her husband started going off about how the city shouldn't be spending money on the new refugee housing project downtown. I just sat there and ate my mashed potatoes. Didn't say a word.
entries.nudge_text: Why mashed potatoes instead of pushing back?
entries.response_text: It's Christmas. Nobody wants that fight at the table.

entries.initial_entry:
Last day of the year and I keep circling back to a decision I made this week. One of our suppliers offered me a deal on cheaper filter components — significantly cheaper, like enough to actually turn a real profit for once. The catch is they source from a factory in Guangzhou that I've seen flagged in two separate reports for dumping chemical waste into a river that feeds into local farmland. The reports aren't conclusive. There's plausible deniability. The factory passed its last audit.

I said yes. I placed the order Tuesday morning and I've felt off about it ever since. I keep telling myself it's pragmatic, that if the business goes under then nobody gets clean water from us, not the communities we donate to, nobody. You can't do good work if you can't keep the lights on. That's not wrong. But it also feels like exactly the kind of reasoning I used to hear from the guys at my old company when they were justifying selling marked-up devices to underfunded hospitals.

I don't know where the line is anymore. When I started this thing I thought I'd always know. Turns out running a mission-driven company doesn't protect you from making the same compromises as everyone else. It just makes you feel worse about them.
entries.nudge_text: You compared yourself to the guys at your old company. Does that comparison feel fair?
entries.response_text: Probably not entirely. But the fact that I even made the connection bothers me.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the morning going through invoices and realized we've burned through most of the Q1 budget already. The donation program is the biggest cost center and I know if I paused it for two quarters we'd actually be in the black for the first time since launch. My accountant has said this exact thing to me three times now. She's not wrong about the math.

Then I got an email from this woman in Flint — not even Flint proper, a small town forty minutes outside it — who got one of our units last spring. She said her daughter stopped getting rashes after they switched to our filter. Just a plain email, no dramatics. "I wanted you to know it's working. Thank you." That's it.

So no, I'm not pausing the donations. I'll figure out the money some other way. I always do, or I don't, and then I scramble and stress and eat takeout for a month. But I read that email and the spreadsheet argument just fell apart. I don't know how to explain that to my accountant. She'd probably say I'm being emotional. Maybe I am.

Went to the supplier meeting in Salem this morning about the spring distribution schedule. Drove past the reservation in Warm Springs on the way back — we installed twelve units there last October. Stopped in because I had time.

The community liaison, a guy named David, showed me around. The units are all still working. One family had rigged up an extra line to feed their greenhouse, which technically voids the warranty but honestly it was clever and I told him we'd look at a garden attachment for the next version. He laughed and said they'd already been thinking about that.

I left feeling steady. Not triumphant or anything close to it. Just like I was doing the thing I set out to do, in the specific place I set out to do it. The filters are filtering. The water is clean. David's family is using it to grow tomatoes in January. Sometimes the small evidence is enough to keep going. Drove home listening to the radio and didn't think about the budget once.
entries.nudge_text: What was different about today compared to the spreadsheet stress last week?
entries.response_text: Last week was numbers on a screen. Today was a guy growing tomatoes with clean water. Hard to argue with tomatoes.

entries.initial_entry:
Monday. Spent most of the day on logistics for the next batch of donation units. Coordinating shipping to three different sites in the Midwest and one in Appalachia. The Appalachia contact is a retired teacher who started a community health initiative out of her garage. She sends me handwritten letters, which I keep in a folder in my desk drawer. I should probably digitize them but there's something about the paper.

Had a call with my business partner, Lena, about the website redesign. She wants to add customer testimonials. I said fine but I don't want it to feel like we're using people's water problems as marketing copy. She said that's exactly what it is, and that's okay because it's also true. We went back and forth for twenty minutes and landed on letting people opt in to share their stories rather than us asking. Felt like the right call.

Ate leftover Thai food for dinner. Called my mom. She asked when I'm coming to visit and I said maybe February but I know I probably won't make it. Ordered new packing tape because we ran out. That's about it.
Canceled the order with the Guangzhou supplier. Found a domestic alternative that costs more but I can actually sleep now. Margins will be tight for a while.
entries.nudge_text: What finally tipped it?
entries.response_text: I just couldn't stop thinking about it. Sometimes that's enough.

entries.initial_entry:
The exhibition proposal went through. Three years of work, and the municipal committee finally said yes. I spent the morning just sitting with the letter, reading it over again. The installation will be about the rising waters—the families in Bangladesh, the villages disappearing, but also what it means here, for us, watching from our safe harbors.

I called my mother to tell her. She asked if it meant I wouldn't have to worry about rent anymore. I said yes, for a while. She sounded relieved. So am I, honestly. The grant covers materials and a modest stipend. It's not wealth, but it's enough to stop checking my account every morning.

Strange to feel both grounded and ambitious at once. Usually those pull in different directions for me.

Spent the whole day researching for the installation and now I can't sleep. The numbers are overwhelming—millions displaced, coastlines eroding, and we're here arguing about parking spaces and bicycle lanes. Not that those don't matter. They do. But the scale of it.

My neighbor Hans stopped by, wanted to borrow sugar of all things, and I ended up talking at him for twenty minutes about sea level projections. Poor man just wanted to bake a cake. He nodded politely and escaped as soon as he could. I do this. I know I do this. The work consumes me and then I become insufferable.

But also: what are we supposed to do? Make art that no one sees? Make art that everyone sees but changes nothing? I keep thinking about that Adrienne Rich line about diving into the wreck. The thing itself and not the myth. I want to make something true. Something that matters. But I also want to pay my electricity bill and not have a panic attack every time my card gets declined.

The residency helps. God, it helps so much. But it's three years and then what? Back to the hustle. Back to applying for everything, hearing nothing. I'm too old for this uncertainty. Or maybe I'm exactly old enough to know it never goes away.
entries.nudge_text: You mention being 'too old for this uncertainty' but also describe being consumed by work that matters to you. How do you sit with both of those truths?
entries.response_text: I suppose I've always thought I'd have to choose eventually. That at some point the responsible thing would be to give up the art and get a real job, whatever that means. My father certainly thought so. But here I am at fifty-one, and I haven't chosen. I just keep doing both—worrying and working. Maybe that is the choice. Maybe the tension is the point, not something to resolve. The discomfort keeps me honest. Keeps me from getting too comfortable with my little grants and my little installations while the world burns.

entries.initial_entry:
Studio day. Cut seventeen panels for the base structure. Hands are sore. The plywood is heavier than I remembered, or I'm weaker. Probably both.

Got a call from the arts center about scheduling. They want to move my opening back two weeks. I said fine. It doesn't really matter.

Leftovers for dinner. Early bed.

The news today. Another boat capsized in the Mediterranean. Forty-seven people. They had names. They had families waiting for them. And I'm here in my warm studio, making art about their suffering while drinking expensive coffee.

I called Ingrid and cried on the phone for half an hour. She said the work matters. She said bearing witness matters. I want to believe her but some days it feels like decoration. Like we're all just rearranging deck chairs.

My mother called later, worried about me. She heard something in my voice this morning. I told her I was fine. I'm not fine, but what can she do? She's eighty-three. She doesn't need my existential crises.

The panels are half-finished. Tomorrow I'll keep cutting. That's all I know how to do.
entries.nudge_text: You describe feeling like the work is 'decoration.' Has there been a time when your art felt more connected to real impact?
entries.response_text:
Yes. In 2019, I did an installation at a refugee center in Berlin. Nothing fancy—just photographs and recorded voices, stories from people who'd made the crossing. A woman named Fatima came to the opening. She'd been one of the voices. She stood in front of her own image and wept, and then she hugged me. She said no one had ever asked her to tell her story before. That it mattered that someone wanted to listen.

I think about Fatima often. Whether she'd think this new work was useful or just... European guilt made pretty.

entries.initial_entry:
Two days before Christmas. Studio closed for the holiday. I should be relieved but I don't know what to do with myself.

Bought presents for my mother. A scarf, some chocolate, a book about Danish gardens. Small things. Safe things. She doesn't want much anymore.

My back hurts. I've been hunched over those panels for weeks. Need to stretch more. Need to take care of this body that insists on getting older.

Sleep.

Christmas with my mother was quiet. We ate roast duck and watched old films. She fell asleep on the couch around eight and I just sat there, watching her breathe. Thinking about how many more of these we might have.

She asked about the installation. I tried to explain the concept—the rising waters, the displaced families, the way I want viewers to feel implicated. She nodded and said, "You've always cared too much." Not a criticism. Just an observation.

Maybe that's true. Maybe caring too much is the only way I know how to be in the world. The alternative—not caring—seems worse. Deadening.

The new year approaches. I don't make resolutions anymore. I just try to keep working. Keep showing up. Keep paying attention to what hurts.
entries.nudge_text: When you say you 'keep paying attention to what hurts'—are you referring to the world's pain, your own, or both?
entries.response_text: Both, I suppose. Though I'm not sure there's much difference anymore. The work has a way of blurring those lines. When I'm deep in research about displacement, I feel displaced. When I'm cutting panels until my hands ache, the ache feels earned. Like penance, maybe. Or communion. I don't know. The question feels too large for the last day of the year.

entries.initial_entry:
Got feedback from Katrine at the arts center today. She thinks the installation is "too heavy." Says audiences want hope. Says I should consider adding something uplifting at the end.

I smiled and said I'd think about it. What I wanted to say: hope is a luxury. Hope is what you get to feel when you're not the one drowning. But she controls my funding, so I nodded.

Ingrid says I should compromise. Pick my battles. She's probably right. She's usually right about these things. But every compromise feels like a small death. Another edge sanded down. Another truth made palatable.

Maybe I'm being stubborn. Maybe stubbornness is all I have left. The work will be what it is. If they don't like it, they can find someone else. Someone younger, cheaper, more optimistic.
entries.nudge_text: You mention that 'every compromise feels like a small death,' yet you also nodded and smiled. What helps you decide which compromises to make?

entries.initial_entry:
The base structure is done. All thirty-four panels, cut and sanded and ready for paint. I stood back today and looked at the skeleton of the thing—this rising wave that will eventually hold video projections, sound, the voices of people I've never met whose lives hang in the balance.

I thought about Katrine's feedback. The hope question. And I realized I was wrong to dismiss it entirely. Not because she's right that audiences need comfort, but because hope isn't the opposite of truth. Fatima in Berlin—she had hope. Impossible, stubborn hope. The families I've interviewed for this project, the ones who lost everything and started over—they have hope too. It's not naive. It's a form of resistance.

So I'm adding something. Not a happy ending, but a thread. A wall of handwritten messages from people who've rebuilt their lives after displacement. Their words, their handwriting. Small acts of continuation.

My mother would say I'm softening. Maybe I am. Maybe softening isn't the same as surrender.

The residency runs until 2027. Two more years of this stability. Two more years of being able to worry about the work instead of worrying about money. I'm grateful in a way I couldn't have articulated ten years ago. Grateful and still restless. Still wanting to make something true.

Outside, the harbor is gray. Winter light. The water rises imperceptibly, day by day. We're all just trying to stay afloat.

Can't sleep. Kept redoing the sketch for the Suba district mural because the committee wants something "uplifting" and every version I draw keeps drifting toward the river contamination. My hand knows what it wants to make but the brief says otherwise.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you showed them your real sketch?
entries.response_text: They'd smile politely and tell me to try again. That's how these things work — you read the room, you adjust, you keep the project alive. It's not about my sketch.

entries.initial_entry: Good day at the workshop. One of the teenagers, David, showed up with a collage about his neighborhood flooding last year — garbage and river water mixed together. He made it beautiful without sanitizing it. I wish I had his nerve sometimes.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think gives him that nerve that you wish you had?
entries.response_text: He doesn't have a committee reviewing his work. He doesn't have a landlord or a mother who calls every Sunday asking if the grants are coming through. David just has cardboard and glue and something he needs to say. There's a freedom in that. I used to have it too, I think, before I learned all the reasons to hold back.

entries.initial_entry:
Meeting with Patricia from the environmental collective went better than expected. She's organizing a march next month about the deforestation in Guaviare and wants muralists to paint temporary pieces along the route. No committee, no brief, no polite rewrites — just paint what you see and feel. I said yes before I finished my coffee.

Spent the afternoon looking at satellite images of the cleared areas. It's staggering how fast the green disappears between one photo and the next. I started sketching a piece that layers the satellite view underneath a portrait of a family I met two years ago when I traveled there for a residency. They had this small farm at the edge of the forest. I keep wondering if that farm still exists.

The march is February 28. Patricia asked if I'd also speak at the rally beforehand. I told her I'd think about it. Speaking is different from painting — you can't hide behind brushstrokes. My mother would probably tell me to just do the mural and not draw attention to myself. She still worries about artists getting into trouble. But Patricia's right that the work means more when the person behind it shows up.

The Suba mural committee approved a revised design. I compromised — kept the river but softened the contamination imagery into something more abstract. Flowing blues and greens instead of the murky brown I originally wanted. They loved it. Said it was "inspiring." I nodded and thanked everyone and then sat in my car for ten minutes staring at the steering wheel.

I keep telling myself this is how it works. Public art is negotiation. You serve the community's vision, not just your own. And maybe the abstracted version will still land — maybe someone will look at those blues and sense that something is wrong underneath. That's the hope, anyway. But part of me keeps hearing David's collage in my head, all that directness, no translation required.

Called my cousin Marcela tonight. She's a lawyer in Medellin, very proper, always knows the right thing to say at family dinners. I told her about the march and she got quiet for a long time. Then she said, "Lu, you know how tia Rosa talks. Just be careful what you attach your name to." And I know she means it with love. I know the family worries. But careful has a cost too, and I'm starting to wonder how much it's adding up.
entries.nudge_text: When you say careful has a cost — what does that cost feel like day to day?
entries.response_text: Like a low hum. This background noise of things I chose not to say, images I softened, opinions I kept to dinner with close friends. None of it is dramatic on its own. But when I look at my portfolio from five years ago versus now, the work is technically better but quieter. The edges got filed down somewhere. I'm not sure when it happened exactly.

entries.initial_entry:
Family group chat exploded today. My uncle Hernando posted a news article about the Guaviare deforestation — same issue the march is about — and wrote "sad but what can you do, these people need to eat." I started typing a response three times. Deleted all of them.

It's not that I disagree entirely — the economics of it are real and I've seen the poverty firsthand. But the framing drives me insane. "What can you do" as if there are no choices, no policies, no one responsible. I wanted to write about the logging companies, the lack of enforcement, the families who actually live there being displaced. But Hernando is my mother's brother and I can already picture the awkward silence at Easter dinner if I go off in the group chat.

So I sent a sad emoji and closed the app. Mature. Professional. Utterly hollow. Patricia would have posted a five-paragraph essay with citations. I admire that and also cannot imagine being that person in my own family. The mural for the march — that I can do. Words in the chat where my grandmother can see them — that's different somehow, and I hate that it's different.
Finished the base layer of the march mural today. It's honest work. Stood back and felt something I haven't felt in a while — that the paint is saying exactly what I mean.
entries.nudge_text: Sounds like something clicked. What's different about this piece?

entries.initial_entry: My mother called. Someone told her about the march mural. She didn't yell — she never yells — she just got very quiet and asked "is this going to be a problem for you?" That question does more damage than yelling ever could.
entries.nudge_text: What does her worry do to you?
entries.response_text: Makes me want to reassure her, mostly. I told her it's just a painting, nothing political, which is not entirely true. But she doesn't need to carry that. She has enough.

entries.initial_entry: Long day prepping supplies for Friday. Shoulders ache. Didn't think about the committee or my mother or the march. Just mixed pigments and cleaned brushes. Sometimes that's enough.

Two days until the march. Patricia confirmed I'm on the speaker list. I told her yes three weeks ago and have been quietly hoping she'd forget or the schedule would change. It didn't. So now I'm sitting here at midnight trying to figure out what to say to a crowd when I couldn't even type a paragraph in my family's group chat.

The mural is done. I'm proud of it — really proud. It shows the forest canopy dissolving into ash at the edges, with small figures of families walking along the border between green and grey. It's direct without being preachy, I think. But speaking is a different exposure. My name attached to words, recorded on someone's phone, shared. My uncle could see it. My mother definitely would hear about it. Marcela's voice keeps playing in my head: "be careful what you attach your name to."

I keep going back and forth. One minute I think: this matters more than anyone's comfort, including mine. The next minute I think: who am I to stand on a stage when I can't even push back on a mural committee? Maybe I should just let the painting speak and stay behind it where I'm good. But Patricia asked me specifically because she said artists need to be visible, not just their work. And she's not wrong. I just wish being visible didn't feel like standing in traffic.
entries.nudge_text: If you speak and your family hears — what's the worst that actually happens?

entries.initial_entry:
The march was last Friday. I spoke. It was short — maybe three minutes — and my voice shook the entire time. I talked about how art can hold what policy reports can't, the texture of a place disappearing, the face of someone losing their home to a decision made in an office they'll never enter. Patricia squeezed my arm when I sat down. The crowd was bigger than I expected.

My mother hasn't mentioned it. Marcela sent a one-word text: "brave." I don't know if that's approval or a warning. Hernando liked a photo of the mural on Patricia's Instagram, which is confusing. The sky didn't fall. The family didn't fracture. I feel lighter than I have in weeks, and also a little raw, like a layer of skin is missing. Not sure if I'd do it again. But I'm glad I did it once.
entries.nudge_text: What surprised you most about how it felt?
entries.response_text: That nobody was angry. I'd built this whole catastrophe in my head — my mother crying, my uncle calling me irresponsible, the family closing ranks. None of it happened. The worst outcome was silence, and silence turned out to be bearable. The actual surprise was that the shaking in my voice didn't make me less credible. People came up to me afterward and said it made them listen harder. I don't know what to do with that yet.

entries.initial_entry:
Same day, later. The Suba committee emailed asking if I'd be interested in a second mural for a park renovation. They mentioned they saw "my recent public work" and want something with that "raw quality." I read the email four times.

On one hand — this is exactly what I wanted. Someone seeing the unfiltered version and asking for more of it. The kind of work where I don't have to translate myself into something palatable. But then I read the rest of the email and there are guidelines, approval stages, a heritage review board. The same machinery. And I started wondering whether they want the rawness or the idea of rawness, something that looks honest but still fits neatly into a park renovation plan.

I haven't replied yet. Part of me wants to say yes and fight for the real version from inside the process. Part of me wants to say no and keep doing the kind of work where I answer only to the paint. And there's a third part — the loudest one, honestly — that says: just be grateful someone asked, don't be difficult, say thank you and show up and do good work within the lines. That voice sounds a lot like my mother. I don't think she's wrong. I don't think she's entirely right either. Going to sleep on it.

Ate the last of the good cheese after lunch today, the one from the market on Saturday. Just sat at the table with it and a coffee and some crackers. Then spent the rest of the afternoon annoyed at myself for not going straight back to the loom, as if ten minutes of sitting with cheese somehow cancelled out the six hours I'd already put in.

Finished the second panel for the Kungsbacka commission today. When I stepped back and looked at it hung up on the temporary rail, I actually liked it. Not in the way where you talk yourself into liking something because you spent forty hours on it. I genuinely liked the way the indigo section catches the light from the side window. I stood there for a while just looking.

Then I let myself leave the studio at four instead of pushing through to six. Walked down to the harbor and sat on the bench near the ferry terminal. Bought a cardamom bun from the kiosk. It was still warm. The whole walk home I felt good, and for once I didn't ruin it by calculating whether I'd done enough that day to justify leaving early. I just walked.

My friend Karin came over tonight and we cooked together. She brought wine and we made a ridiculous amount of pasta and talked about nothing important for three hours. I didn't think about the commission once, which surprises me even writing it now.
entries.nudge_text: What was different about tonight?

entries.initial_entry:
Teaching day. The folk school wanted me to stay for the staff meeting after my afternoon class, so I did. It ran an hour longer than it needed to because people kept going in circles about the spring exhibition. I had opinions but didn't push them because I'm only there two days a week and it's not my place to take over other people's planning.

Someone asked what I'm working on in my own studio and I gave a short answer. I don't like talking about commissioned work with the folk school crowd because it always turns into a conversation about whether commercial work counts as real art. It does, for the record. The Kungsbacka library is going to have my panels on their walls for the next twenty years. But I don't feel like defending that every time.

Long day but a strangely satisfying one. Woke up early because the light was doing that thing it does in November here, grey but somehow bright at the same time, and I wanted to see how it would fall across the third panel. So I was in the studio by seven, still in my wool socks, coffee going cold on the shelf behind me because I forgot about it.

Worked through until about one without stopping. Not because I was forcing myself but because the work was pulling me along. The warp tension on the new section was exactly right and the shuttle was moving the way I wanted. Those stretches don't happen every day and when they do you just stay in them.

After I stopped I heated up leftover soup and ate it standing at the kitchen counter, which I know isn't a proper lunch but it was warm and salty and I was hungry enough that it tasted better than it had any right to. Then I lay down on the sofa for twenty minutes. Just lay there with the radio on low. Didn't set a timer, didn't think about what I should be doing instead. I was tired enough that my body made the decision for me and I didn't argue with it. The afternoon I spent doing the boring parts, measuring and marking the next section, but even that felt okay because the morning had been so good.

Writing again because something small happened after I put this down earlier. I was brushing my teeth before bed and noticed I'd left the studio light on, so I went in to switch it off. Ended up standing there in the dark with just the hallway light coming through the door, looking at the panels. They looked different at night. Quieter.

I almost went back to the loom. There was that pull, that voice that says you could get another hour in. But I didn't. I turned the light off and closed the door. It felt like a small right thing, choosing to stop. Going to bed now.
entries.nudge_text: What made stopping feel right tonight?
entries.response_text: Because the day had already been full. I didn't owe it anything else.

entries.initial_entry:
Zara had her eighteen-month checkup today. Doctor said weight and height are both tracking well. I'd been worried because Ammi kept saying she looks thin, that I'm not feeding her enough roti, the usual. The doctor didn't say any of that. She said the diet plan I put together is fine.

Came home, made aloo paratha for lunch, put Zara down for her nap. Farhan called during his break. He wants his cousin's family to come for dinner on Friday. I told him fine but I'm deciding the menu, not his mother. He laughed like I was joking. I wasn't.

Spent the afternoon editing two product review posts. One got decent engagement — forty-seven likes, twelve comments. Not much by any standard but the comments were real questions from other mothers asking my opinion on the brand. That part felt good.

So apparently Ammi decided to rearrange Zara's entire wardrobe while I was out getting groceries this morning. When I came back, all the drawers were different. She'd sorted everything by fabric weight — cotton in one drawer, winter things in another — and thrown out two outfits she decided were "too tight." One of them I'd bought last week.

I didn't even know what to say. Farhan was at work. I just stood in the nursery holding the grocery bags and looking at it. She came up behind me and said "I organized it properly, it was a mess." Like she was doing me a favor. Like the way I had it was wrong and she was correcting it.

I wanted to say this is my daughter's room, these are her clothes, and I decide what goes where. But what actually came out was "okay, thank you." Because what happens if I push back? She tells Farhan. Farhan tells me to keep the peace. His father gives me that look at dinner. And then everyone acts like I'm the one creating problems in the house.

It's such a small thing. Drawers. Clothes. But it wasn't about the clothes. It was about the fact that she walked into that room and rearranged it without even asking me. Like my choices in my own daughter's room need her approval.
entries.nudge_text: What would it have cost you to say what you were actually thinking?
entries.response_text: It's not about what it would cost me. It's about the fact that I shouldn't have to fight for something this basic. Every house has a pecking order, that's just how joint family works. I know that. But knowing it and being fine with it are two different things.

entries.initial_entry: Needed Farhan to transfer money for Zara's follow-up appointment. He said he'd do it after work. After work became after dinner. After dinner became "I'll do it in the morning." I asked three times. It's his card linked to the account. I can't just do it myself. Sat there watching the clock feeling completely stuck.
entries.nudge_text: Is this about the money or about needing to wait?
entries.response_text: Both. Neither. It's about the fact that something that matters to me — my daughter's appointment — depends on someone else remembering to press a button on their phone. And I just have to sit and hope they get around to it. That's the part that makes my jaw tight.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a good morning. A mother from my Instagram DMs asked if I'd be open to doing a live session reviewing the new Chicco stroller — she has ten thousand followers and wanted to collab. I said yes immediately.

Spent the afternoon prepping talking points, researching the specs, writing out comparison notes against the other two strollers I've reviewed. When I know my material, I know it. Nobody needs to hand me a script.

Ammi asked what I was doing on my phone all afternoon. I said "work." She gave me that look but didn't push it. Farhan actually told her later that my page is "doing well" which is the most he's ever acknowledged it. Small win. But I'll take it because I built that page from nothing — every photo, every caption, every reply to every comment. That's mine.

The dinner with Farhan's cousin happened. I'd planned the whole menu — mutton karahi, raita, kheer for dessert. Had the ingredients out by noon, started prep early so I could plate everything properly before they arrived.

Then Ammi came into the kitchen around four and just started making her own daal and a salad. Didn't ask. Didn't check with me. Just walked in and started chopping onions. When I said I had the menu handled, she said "it's not enough dishes for guests, they'll think we're not hospitable."

So we served her food too, and of course the cousin's wife complimented the daal specifically. Ammi smiled. I smiled. Nobody mentioned the karahi. I cleared the table afterward and Farhan told me "dinner was nice" like it was all one collective effort. It wasn't.

I know I should just let it go. I didn't. Still haven't.
entries.nudge_text: What's the part you can't let go of?
entries.response_text: I think it's that nobody noticed the difference between what I did and what she added. Like my effort and her last-minute addition were equal. I planned for hours. She walked in and overrode me in twenty minutes. And the worst part is maybe she was right — maybe the table did look better with more dishes. But that doesn't change the fact that she didn't ask. And nobody saw that it mattered to me. I keep thinking about Ammi ji walking in and just starting to chop. Like the kitchen was hers. It still is, isn't it. That's the thing I can't let go of.

entries.initial_entry:
Farhan's abba made a comment at chai today. We were talking about schools — Zara is still over a year away from nursery but I've already been researching options, visiting campuses on my own, making a shortlist. I was explaining why I liked Beaconhouse over the closer option and he just looked at Farhan and said "let's not rush, she's still in diapers."

He wasn't even talking to me. I was mid-sentence and he redirected the conversation to his son like I wasn't sitting right there. Like the fact that I'd done the research was irrelevant because the decision will ultimately be made between the men.

And Farhan just nodded. Didn't say "actually Nadia's been looking into this" or "let's hear her out." Just nodded and changed the subject to cricket.

I went to the bedroom after and sat on the bed for a while. Zara was playing on the floor and babbling at me and I couldn't even engage with her properly because I was running the scene over and over. The look on his father's face. Not dismissive exactly — more like I simply didn't register as someone whose input counted.

I keep telling myself this is just how his generation is. But my generation is sitting right there too, not saying anything. That's the part that stings.
entries.nudge_text: What would Farhan saying something have changed for you?

entries.initial_entry:
Zara learned to say "Ammi" properly today. Not the half-syllable thing she does — actually looked at me and said it clearly. Twice. The second time she was reaching for me while Ammi was holding her, which was honestly satisfying in a way I shouldn't probably admit.

Rest of the day was normal. Laundry, feeding schedule, cleaning up after lunch. Farhan came home around seven, ate, watched something on his phone, went to sleep. We barely talked. Not fighting or anything, just the usual weeknight nothing.

I posted a reel in the evening — unboxing a diaper bag a brand sent me for free. First time getting a free product. I didn't pitch them, they found my page and reached out. Two hundred views in the first hour. Comments from women I don't know. One said she'd been waiting for someone to review that exact bag. These are strangers treating my opinion like it matters.

Funny how that works. In this house I rearrange one drawer and it gets redone. Online I recommend a product and people actually listen. Maybe that says something about the house or maybe it says something about the internet. Either way, the reel got more engagement than anything I've posted before. I screenshot the analytics before bed.

Can't sleep, writing this in bed with the phone brightness way down so Farhan doesn't wake up. Still thinking about the reel. Three hundred views now. A parenting micro-influencer with eight thousand followers commented and said "great breakdown, following." That's real. That's someone who creates content full-time validating what I put together in my living room while Zara napped.

I think the reason it feels different from anything else is that nobody handed this to me. Nobody's mother-in-law set it up. Nobody's husband transferred money so I could do it. I used the free version of a video editing app, my phone camera, and my own knowledge. Start to finish, every choice was mine. What to review, what to say, how to frame it, when to post.

And the feedback loop is immediate and clear. If people like it, the numbers go up. If they don't, they don't. There's no ambiguity, no smiling politely while someone undoes your work.

I know it's just Instagram. I know it's not a career yet. But sitting here in the dark looking at three hundred strangers who chose to watch something I made — it's the first time in a long time I feel like I'm steering something. Not asking permission. Not waiting for someone to agree. Just doing it and seeing the results. I needed that tonight.

Tuesday. Normal. Took Zara to the park in the morning. She's walking better now, holding onto my hand less. Talked to another mother there — her son is two months older than Zara. She asked where I got Zara's shoes. I told her. She asked about the brand review I posted last week. Turns out she follows my page. Didn't expect that.

Came home, gave Zara lunch, put her down. Ammi was watching a drama in the lounge. We had chai together. She asked if I'd heard from my mother, I said I'll call her this weekend. It was fine. Civil. Almost comfortable, which happens sometimes and I never know what to make of it.

Farhan brought home gulab jamun from the mithai shop near his office. Zara had a tiny piece and made a mess. I took a photo but decided not to post it — some things don't need to be content.

In the evening I went through my DMs and replied to questions about the stroller review. One woman said she bought the one I recommended and loves it. Wrote her a long reply. It's a strange feeling, being the person other people check with before spending their money. Not strange bad. Just new.
entries.nudge_text: What does it feel like being the person people check with?
entries.response_text: It feels normal honestly. Like how it should be. If you put in the work to learn something properly, people should listen. That's not complicated. The weird part isn't that strangers ask my opinion — the weird part is that people in my own house don't.

entries.initial_entry: Farhan told his mother about a nursery school decision before telling me. I found out at dinner when she said "Farhan mentioned you're looking at Beaconhouse." He told her. Not me first. Her. About our daughter's school. I just kept eating. Didn't even look up. My hands were shaking under the table.
entries.nudge_text: What were you holding back at that table?
entries.response_text: I wasn't holding back. I was just eating. There's nothing to hold back when the pattern is already this clear. He talks to her. She talks to him. I find out later. That's just how information moves in this house. I'm used to it. My hands were shaking because it was cold. That's all.

entries.initial_entry:
Board meeting this morning. Van der Meer had the nerve to suggest we bring in an outside consultant for the digital transformation project. In front of everyone. I built this company when he was still in university, and now he thinks we need some McKinsey type to tell us how to run our own systems.

I shut it down, maybe too hard. Maarten pulled me aside after and said I was "being territorial." Territorial. As if protecting what you've built for thirty years is some kind of character flaw. The company nearly got swallowed six months ago because I trusted people who turned out to be positioning themselves for the takeover. So yes, I'm careful about who gets their hands on strategy now.
entries.nudge_text: What did it feel like when Maarten used that word?
entries.response_text: Maarten's been with me eighteen years. He earned the right to say what he thinks. That's not the issue. The issue is that he might be right about how it looked in the room. I don't need to look reasonable to people who haven't earned a seat at that table, but Maarten — he's seen me navigate worse than this. If he's worried, maybe I'm letting the takeover mess cloud my judgment. Maybe. I still think van der Meer was out of line.

entries.initial_entry: Can't sleep. Keep thinking about Pieter and Thomas leaving. We worked together for fifteen years. Fifteen years, and they walked into the enemy camp without so much as a conversation first. What does that say about what I built? What does that say about loyalty? Or was it all just leverage, waiting for a better offer?

Had dinner with my daughter Annelies tonight. She asked when I'm going to retire. I told her the same thing I always tell her — when I'm ready. She laughed and said I've been saying that for ten years. She's not wrong.

The truth is, I don't know what I would do. The company isn't just a business, it's the structure of my days. It's where I matter. When I walk into that building, people listen. They need my decisions. At home, Marijke has her garden club and her grandchildren schedule and I'm just another person underfoot. She loves me, I know that, but she doesn't need me the way the company does.

Annelies suggested I mentor young entrepreneurs. The idea of sitting in coffee shops giving advice to twenty-five-year-olds who think they invented disruption makes me want to throw something. I don't want to be an elder statesman. I want to be in the fight.
entries.nudge_text: You said the company is where you matter. What happens if that changes?
entries.response_text: Then I become irrelevant. I've seen it happen to others — men who built empires and then got shuffled off to advisory roles where no one returns their calls. They sit on nonprofit boards and pretend it's the same. It's not the same. The respect you get when you can make things happen is different from the respect you get for what you did thirty years ago. I'm not ready to become a memory while I'm still alive.

entries.initial_entry:
Conference in Rotterdam. Ran into Pieter at the networking reception. He had the audacity to come over and shake my hand like nothing happened. "No hard feelings, Henrik. It was just business." Just business. I poured years into developing him. Gave him his first management role when everyone said he was too young. And he used my playbook to compete against me.

I kept my composure. Smiled. Said something about wishing him well. But I was calculating the whole time — who in that room saw us talking, what it looked like, whether he gained anything from being seen with me. This is what it's come to. I can't even have a genuine reaction anymore because every interaction is a positioning exercise.

Back in the hotel room. Skipped the dinner. Couldn't stomach another three hours of strategic smiling. Ordered room service and watched the ceiling. Thirty years and I still have to play these games. When does it end? When do I get to just be somewhere without worrying about who's watching?
entries.nudge_text: When was the last time you weren't performing?

entries.initial_entry: Quarterly review. Numbers are solid — 8% growth despite the disruption. Told the team they exceeded expectations. Van der Meer apologized for the consultant suggestion. I accepted graciously. Maarten gave me a nod from across the room. Small victories. The company is steady. That's what matters.

Strange moment today. Young account manager named Sophie presented a new client pitch to me. She was nervous — I could see her hands trembling on her laptop. I remembered being that nervous once, thirty-five years ago, presenting to the board of a shipping company we desperately needed as a client.

I let her finish without interrupting, which is not my usual approach. When she was done I told her the pitch was solid but she needed to slow down and own the silences. She looked at me like I'd handed her a map. That felt good. Different from closing a deal, but good.

Maybe Annelies isn't entirely wrong about the mentoring thing. Just not with strangers in coffee shops.
entries.nudge_text: This sounds different from the coffee shop comment. What changed?
entries.response_text: Sophie works for me. She's inside the system I built. If she gets better, the company gets better. That's not the same as dispensing wisdom to random founders who will forget my name in a week. With Sophie, I have context. I know what she's walking into. And honestly, I have stake in her success — if she lands this client, it's a win for us. So maybe mentoring only works when I still have skin in the game. Pure generosity has never been my strength.

entries.initial_entry:
Family dinner at Annelies's house. My grandson Lucas asked me what I do all day. I started explaining logistics operations and he interrupted to say his friend's grandfather is retired and takes him fishing every Saturday. Annelies jumped in before I could respond, changed the subject to dessert.

Later Marijke told me I looked hurt. I wasn't hurt. I was annoyed. The comparison is absurd — some retired accountant who goes fishing isn't building something. He's killing time. But I kept thinking about it on the drive home. Lucas is eight. He doesn't know the difference between building and coasting. All he knows is his friend's grandfather has time for him and his doesn't.

The thing is, I could make time. I choose not to because Saturday mornings are when I review the weekly reports uninterrupted. But when I try to explain that maintaining what I've built requires constant attention, even to myself, it starts to sound like an excuse. I'm not sure when protecting the company became a reason to miss everything else.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you missed one Saturday of reports?
entries.response_text:
Nothing would collapse. I know that. The company survived two weeks when I had my knee surgery. But that's not the point — the point is that I need to know everything. If I don't read those reports, I'm making decisions blind. Someone could be slipping, a client could be unhappy, and I wouldn't catch it until it became a real problem. That's how you lose control.

But Marijke asked me once what I was controlling, exactly. Eighty employees who know their jobs. Systems I set up years ago. Maybe I'm not controlling anything. Maybe I'm just afraid of what happens when I let go.

entries.initial_entry:
Woke up at 4 AM thinking about succession. If I died tomorrow, who runs this? Maarten could handle operations but he doesn't have the network. Van der Meer has ambition but I don't trust his judgment yet. Sophie is twenty years from being ready. The honest answer is there's no one, and that's my fault because I never let anyone get close enough to the core.

I tell myself I'm protecting the company from incompetence but maybe I'm protecting myself from being replaced. If someone else can do what I do, then what am I? Just the guy whose name is on the building. I've spent thirty years making sure I was irreplaceable and now that's the problem.

Called Maarten this afternoon. Asked him if he'd be interested in taking on more strategic responsibility. Not as my replacement — as my partner in running the place. He was quiet for a long time. Then he said, "I've been waiting ten years for you to ask." That stung. I thought I was being careful. Turns out I was being a bottleneck.
entries.nudge_text: What made today different?
entries.response_text: The 4 AM panic. And honestly, Lucas asking about fishing. Eight years old and he's already writing me off as the grandfather who's always working. I don't want to be that. I also don't want to hand over everything and become invisible. Sharing with Maarten feels like a middle path — I stay involved but I don't have to carry it alone. Whether I can actually let him carry it without micromanaging, that's another question. But I made the call. That's something.

entries.initial_entry: First day of Maarten handling the morning operations meeting without me. Sat in my office watching the clock. Didn't intervene. Reports came in normal. Nothing fell apart. Went home at 5 PM for the first time in months. Marijke asked if I was feeling alright. I told her I was trying something new.

Took Lucas fishing this morning. Marijke nearly fell over when I told her. I had no idea what I was doing — had to watch a YouTube video on how to bait a hook. Lucas thought that was hilarious. "Opa doesn't know everything!" He's right. I don't.

We sat on the dock for three hours and caught nothing. He told me about his school, his best friend who moved away, the video game he's trying to beat. I listened without trying to teach him anything or steer the conversation. Just listened. At one point he leaned against me and said this was fun. That was worth more than any contract I've ever closed.

On the drive home I thought about what I've been protecting all these years. The company, yes. But also this version of myself that only exists in boardrooms — the one who knows all the answers, who never looks foolish, who everyone defers to. That version doesn't know how to fish. Doesn't have time to learn. Doesn't get to sit with his grandson and be surprised by what a eight-year-old thinks about the world.

I'm not retiring. I'm not ready. But I'm starting to see that there might be more than one way to matter.

Sat with Amma on the phone for almost forty minutes after dinner, which I don't usually do on weekdays. She was telling me about the new water heater she got installed and how the plumber tried to overcharge her. I walked her through how to cross-check rates online, sent her a few links. She kept saying "you sound like your father" which — I don't know, it hit different tonight.

After we hung up I checked my bank balance, not because I needed to, just habit. Everything's where it should be. The SIP went through on the 10th, the rent auto-debit cleared, the FD interest got credited. There's something about seeing those numbers line up that lets me breathe properly. I know people think it's boring, the spreadsheets, the tracking, but when I see it all working the way I set it up, it's like — okay, things are holding together.

Spent the rest of the evening ironing my shirts for the week. Put on an old Kishore Kumar playlist. Just felt settled. Not every night is like this but tonight was.

Team review went well, Priya's onboarding tracker is actually working, the new hires are ramping faster than the last batch. I'd built the template she based it on, months ago, and watching it get adopted without anyone needing to ask me about it — that's the point, right? Systems that just run.

Renewed the apartment lease today, same terms, same landlord. He wanted to bump rent by two thousand but I showed him the market comparables I'd saved. He backed off. Walked home past the chai stall and the uncle remembered my order. Small thing but it's nice when the neighbourhood knows you.

Auto-debit for the health insurance premium bounced. Bank flagged insufficient funds because the salary credit was two days late this month. Sorted it manually but now I need to call the insurer to confirm coverage wasn't lapsed even briefly.
entries.nudge_text: What's actually bothering you more — the bounce itself or the two days you couldn't control?
entries.response_text: It's not about feelings, it's about process. If payroll is going to shift dates without notice, I need a bigger buffer in that account. That's the fix.

entries.initial_entry: Took an Uber instead of the bus to a client meeting because I woke up late. Spent four hundred rupees on a fifteen-minute ride I normally do for thirty. Doesn't sound like much but I'd just tightened my monthly transport budget last week.
entries.nudge_text: Four hundred rupees isn't the issue, is it?
entries.response_text: It's not about the money specifically. It's that I had a plan and I broke it on day two. If I let small stuff slide the bigger stuff starts sliding too, that's just how it works.

entries.initial_entry:
The warehouse team flagged a potential compliance issue with one of our vendor contracts — some clause about liability thresholds that might not match the new state guidelines. I should've pulled the contract and reviewed it today but I had back-to-back calls and a quarterly deck to finish.

Told myself I'd get to it tomorrow but I've said that before about smaller things and sometimes tomorrow just becomes next week. The vendor's been fine for three years so it's probably nothing. But "probably" isn't really how I like to operate.

Still haven't looked at that vendor contract. It's been four days. Every morning I open my task list and it's there, highlighted yellow, and I just skip past it to whatever's on fire. Today it was a delivery routing issue that ate my entire afternoon.

The thing is, I know what I'd tell someone on my team — just block thirty minutes, pull the document, read the clauses, flag what needs flagging. It's not hard. But I keep not doing it and the longer I wait the more it feels like it's going to be a bigger problem than it actually is. Classic spiral.

Appa called tonight and asked about work. I said "all good, busy but managing." Which is mostly true. But I didn't mention the contract thing or the insurance bounce from last week, because then he'd worry, and then I'd have to manage his worry on top of everything else. I'm tired. Going to bed early and hoping tomorrow's lighter.

Noticed a crack in the bathroom wall tile near the water pipe. Could be nothing, could be a slow leak behind the wall. Decided to just keep an eye on it for now because calling the landlord means him sending his cousin who makes everything worse.
entries.nudge_text: You'd normally be on the phone with the landlord by now.
entries.response_text: Yeah. I know. Last time his cousin came he left the tap fitting loose and I had a puddle for two days. But you're right, if it's actually a leak and I wait, the damage gets expensive. I'll message the landlord tonight and tell him I want a proper plumber this time, not his cousin.

entries.initial_entry:
Nothing much happening, which is fine. Work was the usual — approvals, a team standup, some back-and-forth with the Chennai office about a shipment delay that sorted itself out by lunch. Had dal chawal from the canteen, same as every Thursday because Thursday's dal is actually decent.

Walked to the ATM after work to deposit a cheque Amma sent for my birthday last month. I keep forgetting to do it, not because I need the money but because cheques feel like such an outdated thing. She could just UPI it but she likes writing cheques, says it feels more intentional. I stood in the queue behind an uncle who was arguing with the security guard about something, and I just waited. No rush.

Came home, wiped down the kitchen counter, checked that the gas knob was off — I always check twice — and sat on the balcony for a bit. The neighbours downstairs were playing some serial loudly. I could hear the dramatic music through the floor. Just one of those unremarkable evenings where everything is exactly where it should be and that's enough.
entries.nudge_text: What does it feel like on the days when things aren't exactly where they should be?
entries.response_text: Restless. Like there's an itch I can't locate. I just start going through things — did I lock the door, did I reply to that email, did the payment go through. Most of the time everything's fine but until I've checked, I can't sit still. It's not anxiety exactly, it's more like... I just need to know.

entries.initial_entry: Vikram pitched me again on the startup thing over chai. Said they just closed a seed round and there's a spot for me, better title, equity. I said I'd think about it but I already know my answer.

Can't stop thinking about what Vikram said. Not the startup itself — I've made peace with not doing that, or I thought I had. It's the way he looked at me when I said I'd think about it. Like he felt sorry for me. Like staying put is the same as giving up.

And maybe from the outside it looks that way. He's out there raising money, hiring people, building something from scratch. I'm here making sure shipments arrive on time and vendor contracts don't have liability gaps. It's not glamorous. Nobody's writing articles about operations managers at logistics companies in Pune.

But then I think about what happens if his startup doesn't work out. He's burning through savings, he doesn't have insurance, his lease is month-to-month.
