One bad quarter and he's back at his parents' place in Nagpur. And I think about my FD, my insurance, my team that depends on me showing up every day with a plan — and I don't feel sorry for myself. I just feel different from him. But tonight it's harder to feel okay about that difference than it usually is.

Look, I know some teachers think the community garden visits are a waste of instructional time. We spent two hours there today and yes, that's time away from test prep. But watching Marcus Jr. (not named after me, just a coincidence) actually engage with something for the first time all year? Seeing him carefully transplant seedlings and ask questions about composting? That matters.

Mrs. Patterson made a comment in the break room about my "field trip habit" but I'm not going to apologize for it. These kids need to understand they're part of something bigger than standardized tests. They need to touch the dirt, see the earthworms, understand where food actually comes from. That's education too.
Didn't call Grandma back again. She left another voicemail about the restaurant anniversary dinner planning and I just... let it go to voicemail. I'll call her tomorrow.
entries.nudge_text: What made you hesitate to pick up?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I know she's going to ask me to give a speech at the 60th anniversary dinner. In Cantonese. My Cantonese is terrible and I keep meaning to practice but I never do. It feels like I'm letting her down every time we talk and she hears how rusty I've gotten.

entries.initial_entry:
Principal wants me to use the new standardized curriculum materials for social studies. They come from the district. They're fine, technically. Cover all the state requirements.

But they're so sanitized. The unit on immigration literally has two paragraphs on the Chinese Exclusion Act. Two paragraphs. My great-grandfather couldn't enter this country for decades because of that law. I told the principal I'd supplement with additional materials. She said to be careful about "going off curriculum." Said it in that way that means don't.
entries.nudge_text: So what will you do with those materials?
entries.response_text: I'm going to use the curriculum. But I'm also going to bring in my family photos and tell the real story during the personal narrative unit. They can't tell me I can't share my own family history. At least I don't think they can.

entries.initial_entry:
The kids blew my mind today. We were talking about the water cycle and Jordan raised her hand and asked if the water we drink is the same water dinosaurs drank and I said yes, basically, and then she asked if that meant we were connected to dinosaurs and I said yes again and she got this look on her face like something clicked.

Then Carlos said if we're connected to dinosaurs through water we must be connected to each other too and to the fish and to the trees and I just stood there for a second because yes, exactly, that's exactly it. These ten-year-olds just figured out in five minutes what some adults never understand. We spent the rest of the period talking about interconnection and nobody complained and nobody looked at the clock.
entries.nudge_text: What did that moment mean to you?
entries.response_text: It reminded me why I do this job. Not for the test scores or the evaluations. For moments like that where kids discover something true about the world. That we're all connected, that what we do matters beyond ourselves. That's what I want them to carry with them.

entries.initial_entry:
Second entry today because I just need to get this out. Went to the grocery store after school, totally normal trip, and I just stood in the produce section for way too long looking at the plastic clamshells of strawberries. They're from Chile. In January. Wrapped in plastic that's going to exist for five hundred years so I can have strawberries in the middle of winter.

I bought them anyway. I bought the stupid strawberries because I was tired and I wanted something sweet and the farmer's market doesn't open until Saturday. And I know one person buying or not buying strawberries doesn't change the global supply chain but I still felt like a hypocrite putting them in my cart.

This is what I mean when I say it's exhausting to care about everything all the time. My students look at me like I have answers but I'm just as stuck as everyone else. I teach them about sustainability and then I buy Chilean strawberries in plastic. I tell them every choice matters and then I make choices that don't align with that. I don't know how to live in this world without constantly feeling like I'm failing at something.
Called Grandma. We talked for forty minutes. I'm going to give the speech at the anniversary dinner. In Cantonese. She's going to help me practice.
entries.nudge_text: How do you feel about that decision?
entries.response_text: Nervous but good. It's important to her. The restaurant is important to the whole family, to the community really. Dad worked there every summer through high school. So did I. People need to hear what it means to us, in the language it's always been spoken in.

entries.initial_entry:
The diversity assembly actually worked! I've been pushing for this for two years and today we had speakers from five different community organizations talking to the whole school. Mrs. Okonkwo from the Nigerian cultural center, Mr. Patel from the Hindu temple, someone from the tribal council, and two others.

The kids were so engaged. They asked real questions. One of my students asked Mrs. Okonkwo what she missed most about Nigeria and she started crying a little and then laughed and said "the food, always the food" and everyone laughed and it was this beautiful human moment. This is what school should feel like. Not test prep and discipline charts. Actual humans connecting with each other, learning that the world is bigger than their own street.
entries.nudge_text: What made you push so hard for this?
entries.response_text: When I was a kid there was nobody who looked like me in the history books or the assemblies. I remember how alone that felt. I don't want any of my students to feel that way. I want them to see themselves in school, and I want them to see each other too.

entries.initial_entry:
Made dumplings with Mom today for Lunar New Year prep. My hands remember the folding even when my brain forgets the right way to pleat them. She corrected me three times on the same fold and I wanted to be annoyed but honestly I was just grateful she's still here to correct me.

She told me stories about the restaurant again, the early days when Grandpa worked eighteen hour shifts and Grandma handled the front while raising Dad. Stories I've heard a hundred times but they never get old. There's something about flour on your hands and family around you that makes everything feel solid. Like no matter what else is happening in the world, this is real. This is what matters.

Parent-teacher conferences all day. Twelve families. My voice is gone but one mom thanked me for showing her son that being Chinese-American is something to be proud of. That one sentence made the whole exhausting day worth it.
entries.nudge_text: What did it mean to hear that?
entries.response_text: More than I can explain. I spent so many years feeling caught between two worlds, not Chinese enough, not American enough. If I can help even one kid skip that confusion, help them feel whole from the start... that's why I became a teacher. That's the whole point.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. Just long. A lot of grading, a lot of meetings, a lot of everything. The community garden got a small grant from the city though, so that's something. We can finally fix the broken irrigation system and maybe add a few more raised beds for the school partnership program.

Practiced my speech with Grandma over video call. My Cantonese is getting better but still rough. She says the important thing is not perfect pronunciation but speaking from the heart. I'm trying to believe her. The anniversary dinner is in two weeks and I keep rewriting what I want to say.
entries.nudge_text: What do you keep changing in the speech?
entries.response_text: I keep trying to capture what the restaurant means. Not just to our family but to the town. It's where people celebrated graduations and mourned losses. Where the same families have come for sixty years. It's history. It's proof we belong here. I don't know how to say all that in five minutes, in a language I barely speak, without crying.

entries.initial_entry:
I know I should have stayed for the whole faculty meeting but I had to leave early for the speech practice. Principal noticed. She didn't say anything but I saw her write something down. I'll deal with whatever comes from that.

Some things are more important than faculty meetings about parking lot protocols. The anniversary dinner is this Saturday and my grandmother is counting on me. The whole family is counting on me. And maybe that sounds dramatic but the restaurant isn't just a business, it's proof that we made it here, that we stayed, that we built something. I'm not going to let a faculty meeting come between me and honoring that. I'm just not.
Made pandan chiffon cake this morning. The kitchen smelled good for hours and I sat at the table eating a slice with hot Milo before anyone else was up. It was nice.
entries.nudge_text: What changes when the house fills up?
entries.response_text: Everything becomes about what needs doing next.

entries.initial_entry:
Same day, writing again. Wei Lin called this afternoon and asked me to watch the grandkids next Saturday because she has a work event. I said yes obviously. But it means I'll miss the CNY market at Chinatown with Mei and Hwee Leng. We've been planning it for two weeks.

It's fine. The kids love coming over and I already told Wei Lin I would. Mei said they can go without me and bring me back some bak kwa. I told her to get the thick-cut one from Lim Chee Guan.
entries.nudge_text: Two weeks of planning is a lot to set aside.
entries.response_text: It is, but what am I going to say — no, find someone else, I want to go shopping? She's my daughter.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the whole day with the grandkids as expected. They were fine, quite well behaved actually. But by 4pm I was so tired I could barely keep my eyes open. I sat down on the sofa for maybe ten minutes while they were watching cartoons and immediately felt guilty, like I should be doing something — cutting fruit, tidying up, checking if they needed water. So I got up.

Later after Wei Lin picked them up I heated some leftover soup and just stood in the kitchen eating it from the pot. Didn't even sit down. I wanted to take a long bath — the kind with the lavender salts my friend gave me for my birthday — but the bathroom still needed cleaning from the kids using it, and by the time I finished scrubbing I didn't feel like a bath anymore. I just showered and went to bed.

I keep thinking about the ten minutes on the sofa and how I couldn't even let myself have that. The children were fine. They were watching TV. Nothing was on fire. But something in me just wouldn't let it be okay to sit there.

Still tired from yesterday. Woke up with my neck stiff and couldn't really move it properly. Husband said I should go see the TCM doctor but I looked at the week ahead and there's grocery shopping tomorrow, Ma's checkup on Wednesday, and I told Mrs Tan I'd help with the community centre lunar new year cleanup on Thursday.

I know a massage would help. I know lying down with a heat pad for an hour would help. But I keep hearing my mother's voice in my head telling me not to be so pampered, that at her age she was carrying buckets of water uphill and still cooking for seven children. So I put Tiger Balm on it and kept going.

Finished the grocery run this morning and treated myself to a bowl of laksa at the hawker centre after. I almost didn't — the queue was long and I had frozen things in the bag. But I thought, five more minutes won't matter, and I stood in line anyway.

The laksa was really good. Extra hum. I sat at one of those metal tables by myself and didn't look at my phone or think about what was next. Just ate. By the time I was done the ice cream in the grocery bag was starting to soften but honestly it was worth it.
entries.nudge_text: What tipped you into staying in that queue?
entries.response_text: I could smell it. That's really all it was. The coconut milk and the chilli — I just thought, when was the last time I had this?

entries.initial_entry:
Valentine's Day, not that it matters to us at this age, but husband brought home a box of those nice mooncake-style pastries from Bengawan Solo after work. The tau sar ones I like. He just left them on the counter and didn't make a big deal about it which is his way.

I wanted to sit down and have one with tea and just enjoy it, the way you see people do in those café videos online. But I was in the middle of folding the laundry and then I remembered I hadn't called Ma today and she gets anxious if I don't call by 7, so I called her first, then finished the laundry, and by then I was standing at the counter eating the pastry over the sink because I was also rinsing out the thermos.

And the whole time I was eating it I was thinking about how I should be enjoying this more, which is such a strange thing to think, because the act of thinking it already means you're not. Like the pastry was good. I know it was good. I could taste it. But my mind was already three tasks ahead. I don't know when eating something nice became just another thing I do between other things. I saved the rest of the box for the grandkids.
entries.nudge_text: You saved the box for the grandkids — was that automatic?
entries.response_text: Yes. I didn't even think about it until you asked. Of course you give the nice things to the children. That's just what you do.

entries.initial_entry:
Went to Ma's nursing home today, usual Sunday visit. Brought her the dried tangerine peel she asked for and sat with her for two hours. She complained the aircon was too cold, same as always. I adjusted it, brought her an extra blanket, refilled her water thermos.

On the way home I stopped at the provision shop for dish soap and sponges. Cooked dinner. Washed up. Nothing unusual. I don't know why I'm even writing today, there's nothing to say really. Everything got done, nobody needed anything they didn't get. That should be enough.

Mei asked me last week if I wanted to join her for a foot reflexology session — the new place near Bishan that has the herbal soak. I said I'd think about it and I've been thinking about it for five days now. It's $38 for an hour. That's not a lot of money. We can afford it easily.

But there's this thing where I feel like I need to justify it first. Like I need to have done enough to deserve an hour of someone rubbing my feet. Which is ridiculous when I say it out loud. My husband never thinks twice about buying fishing lures that cost more than that. But for me it feels different. It feels like something I have to earn, and I'm never quite sure I have.

Went to the reflexology with Mei after all. It was good. My feet have been aching from all the standing and walking to the market so it actually made a difference. Mei and I talked the whole time about her daughter's wedding planning and it felt normal, like two friends just doing something together.

But on the MRT home I started thinking about the laundry I didn't hang up before leaving, and whether I'd left the rice cooker on, and I realised I'd chosen the useful thing to worry about instead of just sitting with the fact that I'd had a nice afternoon. The rice cooker was off. The laundry was fine. I just couldn't let the afternoon be what it was.
entries.nudge_text: You caught yourself doing it this time.

entries.initial_entry: Spent the whole day on vendor calls. Ate leftover dal at my desk and didn't even heat it up. Just want to lie down.
entries.nudge_text: What kind of tired is this — body or head?
entries.response_text: Both, honestly. The kind where even picking a show to watch feels like work.

entries.initial_entry:
Took twenty minutes after lunch to sit on the balcony with a cup of chai. Not even doing anything — just sitting, watching the street below, feeling the breeze. It was genuinely nice for about ten minutes before I started doing math in my head about the delivery timelines I should be checking.

I don't know why I can't just have the chai. It was twenty minutes. My co-founder takes hour-long gym breaks and nobody blinks. But when I sit still, my own brain starts pulling up spreadsheets. Like I haven't done enough today to deserve twenty minutes of doing nothing.
entries.nudge_text: Your co-founder gets an hour but you can't take twenty?

entries.initial_entry:
Couldn't sleep last night because I was so wound up from a client call that went badly. By 2 AM I was just lying there staring at the ceiling fan, and the obvious thing would have been to get up, make something warm, watch something stupid on my phone, let myself come down from it. But instead I just lay there telling myself I needed to sleep because tomorrow was going to be another long day and I couldn't afford to be foggy.

And the thing is, I know forcing sleep doesn't work. I know I would have been better off getting up and doing something comforting for twenty minutes. But some part of me kept translating "get up and do something nice for yourself" into "you're being weak, you can't even handle a bad call without needing to baby yourself."

So I just lay there for another hour and a half, exhausted but not sleeping, not resting, not doing anything useful either. The worst of all options. Woke up foggy anyway. Amma would have told me to drink warm milk and stop overthinking, and she would have been right.

Ravi invited me to this rooftop thing his friends were doing — live music, good food, casual. I actually wanted to go. But I had a pitch deck that needed reworking by Monday and I told myself it made more sense to stay in and finish it. Which it did, technically. The pitch is important. I'm not going to pretend otherwise.

But I sat at my desk all evening and the deck took twice as long as it should have because I kept checking Ravi's stories and seeing everyone having a good time. The tandoori looked incredible. Someone was playing guitar and people were just sitting around talking. Nothing fancy. The exact kind of night I would have loved.

I finished the deck around midnight and it's fine. It's solid. But the flat was so quiet and I just felt flat myself. Like I'd done the responsible thing and the reward for being responsible was sitting alone in a silent apartment eating cold chapati at midnight wondering what the point of all this work is if I never actually enjoy anything.
entries.nudge_text: Was the deck actually urgent or did it just feel that way?

entries.initial_entry:
Normal morning. Replied to emails, had a standup call that went longer than necessary because Priya wanted to talk through the onboarding flow again. Grabbed idli from the canteen downstairs. The sambar was better than usual today.

Afternoon was mostly spreadsheet work. Nothing dramatic. Stepped out for a coffee around four and ran into the guy who runs the print shop next door. We talked about cricket for ten minutes. Came back and finished up.

Writing again because I left something out earlier. After the coffee and the cricket chat I actually felt good — like, weirdly good for a Tuesday. And I sat back down at my desk and instead of opening the spreadsheet I just sat there for a few minutes not doing anything. Not on my phone, not thinking about work. Just sort of enjoying the fact that the chai was good and the conversation was easy and the afternoon light through the window was warm.

Then I caught myself and opened the spreadsheet immediately, like I'd been caught doing something wrong. Nobody was watching. Nobody cares. But the instinct was there — that I was wasting time, that good feelings are a distraction, that if I'm not producing something then I'm falling behind. It's a stupid reflex and I know it's stupid but knowing doesn't seem to help.
entries.nudge_text: What were you falling behind on, exactly?
entries.response_text: Nothing specific. That's the honest answer. It's not about an actual deadline — it's just this background hum that says I should always be doing more.

entries.initial_entry:
Third time writing today, which is unusual for me. I think the afternoon thing is still sitting with me. Had dinner — made egg curry, nothing special but the kitchen smelled good and I ate slowly for once instead of standing at the counter scrolling through Slack.

It occurs to me that the small things I actually enjoy — cooking, eating properly, having a pointless conversation about cricket — none of them take very long. It's not like I'm asking for a vacation. I just want to eat my food without feeling like I'm stealing time from something more important. That shouldn't be complicated.

Long day. The investor meeting got pushed to Thursday which should feel like breathing room but mostly just means I'll be anxious for two extra days. Had a working lunch with Priya going over the numbers. She brought biryani from that place near her flat which was good — proper Hyderabadi style with the rice done right. We ate while reviewing the P&L which felt wrong somehow, like the biryani deserved better attention than that.

After work I was too tired to cook so I ordered in and ate on the couch watching something forgettable. The flat was quiet. Not in a bad way, just quiet. I kept thinking about how my father used to come home from the office and sit in his chair for exactly fifteen minutes before dinner, not talking, not reading, just sitting. My mother never interrupted those fifteen minutes. I used to think it was strange but now I think I understand it — he needed a gap between the work version of himself and the home version.

I don't really have that gap. The work version and the home version blur into each other because I work from the same room I sleep in. Maybe that's part of it. Maybe if there were clearer edges I wouldn't feel so guilty about the in-between moments.

Ma called three times during the supplier meeting this afternoon. Three times. Each time I stepped out, and each time it was about the pharmacy not having her specific brand of blood pressure pills. By the third call I was short with her and I could hear her voice go small on the other end.

Drove to two pharmacies after the meeting to find the right ones. Got home and she'd already eaten dinner alone in her room. Lifen said Ma wouldn't come out. I left the pills outside her door and just sat in the car for twenty minutes in the garage not wanting to go back inside. I know she's scared about her health. I know that. But the way my chest tightened when the phone rang that third time — I don't want to be this person.
entries.nudge_text: What kind of person do you think you were being?
entries.response_text: The impatient kind. Anyway she was fine by morning, we had congee together.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent Sunday reorganizing Ma's medication schedule into a weekly pill organizer. Color-coded it, printed the times in large font. She watched me do it and kept saying I was wasting my time, that she could remember on her own. But last month she doubled up on her thyroid medication twice so clearly she can't.

There's something about the way she resists help that makes me try harder. Lifen pointed out I spent four hours on this when I had quarterly reports due. She's not wrong. But if I don't do it, who does? Yuxin is in Hong Kong. Haojie has mock exams. Lifen already handles enough. I keep circling back to this — the math doesn't work, there's only one of me, and everyone needs a piece.
entries.nudge_text: What happens to the piece that's yours?

entries.initial_entry:
Had dinner with old university friends last night. Lao Zhang just sold his logistics company, talking about retiring to Yunnan, painting landscapes. Everyone congratulated him. Someone turned to me and asked when I'd slow down too, said I looked tired. I told them I'm fine, business is growing, we're expanding into Southeast Asian markets.

On the drive home I kept replaying it. I don't look tired. Or maybe I do. But what am I supposed to do — Haojie needs tutoring fees paid, Ma's specialist appointments aren't cheap even with insurance, and Yuxin's Hong Kong rent went up again. Lao Zhang doesn't have parents to look after, his kids are already working. Easy to paint landscapes when nobody's depending on you.

It's not that I'm jealous. I chose this. When Ba died and Ma needed somewhere to go, I didn't hesitate. When Lifen suggested Yuxin should study locally to save money, I said no, let her go to Hong Kong, it's better for her career. These were my decisions and I'd make them again. I just wish people would stop looking at me like I'm some kind of cautionary tale when I'm doing exactly what I should be doing.
entries.nudge_text: Who are you convincing — them or yourself?
entries.response_text: Them. I know what I'm doing and why. The difference between me and Lao Zhang isn't that he's free and I'm stuck. He just doesn't have anyone who needs him the way my family needs me. That's not a burden, that's the whole point.

entries.initial_entry: Haojie asked me to quiz him on chemistry formulas after dinner. Sat at the dining table for an hour while Ma peeled fruit for us. Nobody needed anything from me except to be there.

Client dinner ran until 11pm. Mr. Tanaka from Osaka — important account, been courting him for months. He wanted to do KTV after dinner, brought two of his managers. Good business practice to go along, build the relationship. So I went.

Lifen had texted at 9pm that Ma had a dizzy spell. Not serious, she said, blood pressure spiked a bit but came back down. She handled it, took Ma's readings, made her lie down. Everything was managed. I read the text between songs and replied "OK take care of her, back late." Lifen didn't text again.

Got home past 1am. House was dark. Checked on Ma — sleeping. Checked on Lifen — also sleeping, or pretending to. Stood in the hallway for a while. The deal with Tanaka matters, the revenue would cover Yuxin's next semester easily. But there's this feeling like I filed something in the wrong drawer and now I can't find it.
entries.nudge_text: What drawer should it have gone in?
entries.response_text: I should have called. Not texted "OK." Even five minutes, just to hear Ma's voice, ask Lifen if she needed me to come back. I chose convenient over present and I can feel it sitting in my stomach.

entries.initial_entry: Tanaka confirmed the contract this morning so at least the evening wasn't wasted. Lifen said Ma is fine. Everyone's fine.
entries.nudge_text: You keep saying fine — you believe that?
entries.response_text: Honestly, no. Ma was fine medically, sure. But Lifen's been quieter than usual and I think she's keeping score of these nights. Not in a petty way. She just notices when I choose work over home and I think last night added to whatever tally she keeps.

entries.initial_entry:
Yuxin called today, long call, almost forty minutes. She's been struggling with a group project — three teammates not pulling their weight, and she's doing most of the analysis herself. She was venting and halfway through she stopped and said "Ba, am I being unreasonable? Maybe I should just do it all and not make a fuss."

And I heard myself in her. That exact calculation — just absorb it, carry it, don't rock anything, because the outcome matters more than being right. I told her no, she should talk to her teammates, set expectations. That she doesn't have to shoulder everything alone. The words came out so easily when they were for her.

After we hung up I sat with that for a while. Lifen came in, saw my face, asked what was wrong. I said nothing, just a good talk with Yuxin. She squeezed my shoulder on her way out. These are the moments where everything makes sense — my daughter calls me when things are hard, my wife knows when I need a hand on my shoulder without being told. Whatever else is messy, this part works. This part I would burn everything down to keep.
Skipped Haojie's parent-teacher meeting because a shipment got held at customs. Lifen went instead. He didn't say anything about it but he also didn't ask me to quiz him tonight.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think he didn't say?
entries.response_text: That it mattered to him. That he wanted me there specifically, not just a parent. Lifen's great at these things but I told him last week I'd go this time. I broke that and acted like customs clearance is a good enough excuse. It's not. It's just the one I had.

entries.initial_entry: Took Haojie for beef noodles after school today. Didn't mention yesterday. He talked about his physics teacher the whole time and it almost felt normal but not quite.
entries.nudge_text: What was the 'not quite' part?
entries.response_text: Maybe I'm overthinking it. He ate two bowls so he can't be that upset.

entries.initial_entry:
Ma's birthday this Saturday. Called Yuxin to plan — she's flying in Friday night, insisting on buying the cake from that specific bakery in Futian that Ma likes. Haojie is making a card, which he hasn't done since he was maybe twelve. Lifen already ordered Ma's favorite fish from the wet market, the one she has to get there at 6am for.

None of this was coordinated. I didn't send a family group chat saying "plan birthday." Yuxin just asked when she should arrive. Haojie just started drawing without being told. Lifen just ordered the fish. They all moved toward the same thing separately because they know it matters.

I don't know how to say this without sounding sentimental but this is what I've been building. Not the company, not the contracts with Tanaka. This — the part where everyone in my family independently decides to show up for each other. Ma probably won't even like the cake that much. She'll say it's too sweet. But she'll save the card and put it next to the ones from when Yuxin was small, and that will be the whole thing right there.

Factory audit next week, Lifen handling payroll errors again, Ma wants me to drive her to see Auntie Fang on Saturday. Haojie left his physics textbook at school. Everyone orbiting and I'm the gravity.
entries.nudge_text: Gravity holds things together or pulls you down?
entries.response_text: Both. Same force. On good days it's the first thing, on tired days it's the second. Today I'm somewhere in between — Auntie Fang's been lonely since her husband passed so I'll drive Ma there. Not because I have to. Because Ma will worry about Auntie Fang all week if she doesn't visit and then I'll worry about Ma.

entries.initial_entry:
My sister asked me if I was "doing okay" on the phone last night with that voice she uses when she's actually asking if I'm depressed. I'm not depressed. I made banana bread with Olive yesterday and we painted rocks in the backyard and I reorganized the entire pantry because it was driving me insane. That's not depression, that's Tuesday.

She doesn't get it because she's single and works in marketing and goes to Barcelona on a whim. Good for her. I chose this. I'm not sitting here wishing I had her life. I just wish mine had a few more surprise elements built in. Like a plot twist every now and then. Something I didn't see coming at 6am when I set the cereal bowls out.
entries.nudge_text: What kind of plot twist are you hoping for?
entries.response_text: Honestly? I don't even know. That's the part that scares me. I can't name the thing I want. I just know the absence of it. Like there's a shape missing from my week and I keep filling it with pantry organization and sourdough starters.

entries.initial_entry: Spent three hours at a pumpkin patch watching kids run in circles. I stood there holding juice boxes like a human vending machine. Got home and Kyle asked how my day was and I said "fine" and went to bed at 8:30.
entries.nudge_text: What would've made it better?
entries.response_text: It was fine. Kids had fun. That's the point, right?

entries.initial_entry: Signed up for a ceramics class on Thursday evenings. Kyle said he'd handle bedtime. First class is next week. Already looking forward to getting my hands dirty with something that isn't Play-Doh.

First ceramics class happened. Small studio off Broadway, eight people, mostly women, a couple retirees, one guy who looked like he was there because of a breakup. The instructor is this woman named Greta who has clay permanently embedded in her fingernails and talks about centering the clay like it's a spiritual practice.

I threw my first cylinder and it collapsed immediately. The second one wobbled but held. Greta said I had "aggressive hands" which I think was a compliment. My nails are ruined and I got clay in my hair and I didn't check my phone once in two hours. Didn't even think about it.

Drove home with the windows down even though it was forty degrees. Played music loud. Walked in the door and the house was quiet, kids asleep, Kyle on the couch watching something about the stock market. He asked if I had fun. I said yes. He said "good" and went back to his show.

I stood in the kitchen eating leftover pasta straight from the container and felt something I haven't felt in a while. Not happiness exactly. More like I'd briefly been someone else. Or maybe someone I used to be.
entries.nudge_text: Someone else, or someone you used to be?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Maybe both. Maybe they're the same thing at this point.

entries.initial_entry:
Can't sleep. Kyle is snoring. I'm in the kitchen with the light over the stove and my phone and a glass of wine that I'm pretending is just one glass even though I refilled it.

Looked at flights to Mexico. Not for any reason. Just wanted to see how much it would cost. $340 round trip to Puerto Vallarta. That's less than what I spend on groceries in a week for this family. I could go for a weekend. I won't. But I could.

Scrolled through my old Instagram from 2017, before Olive was born. There's a photo of me at a music festival in Austin with body paint on my arms and a flower crown and I'm laughing so hard my eyes are closed. I don't recognize that person. Or I do, but she feels like a character from a show I watched a long time ago. The plot moved on and she got written out.

The wine is warm now. I should go to bed. Tomorrow is a half day at school for Mason which means I need to figure out lunch and an afternoon activity and probably a trip to Target because he needs new sneakers. Again. Nine-year-old boys destroy shoes like it's a competitive sport.

I just want one day where I don't know what's going to happen next. Where the schedule doesn't exist and I can just follow whatever sounds interesting. Is that so unreasonable?
entries.nudge_text: What would you actually do with that day?
entries.response_text: Drive somewhere I've never been. No map. Stop when something catches my eye. Eat at a restaurant where I can't read the menu. Talk to strangers. Stay out past dark. I've thought about it enough that I have a whole itinerary, which kind of defeats the purpose.

entries.initial_entry:
Same night, still up. Kyle got up to use the bathroom and saw me in the kitchen and asked what I was doing. I said "just thinking." He said "about what?" and I said "nothing" and he shrugged and went back to bed. That's the whole conversation. That's what passes for connection at midnight in this house.

I'm not blaming him. He works. He's tired. He handles bedtime when I go to ceramics. He doesn't complain. But he also doesn't ask. Not really. He asks the way you ask a coworker in the elevator. "How was your day?" is not the same as "How are you?" and neither of those is the same as "What do you need?"

Nobody asks me what I need. I'm the person who asks everyone else. What do you want for lunch. Did you brush your teeth. Where's your backpack. Do you need a jacket. I am a logistics coordinator for four other humans and my own needs are somewhere on page twelve of a to-do list that only goes to page three.

I know how this sounds. I know every parent feels this way sometimes. I know I'm not special. I know people have real problems and mine is that I'm bored and restless in a nice house in Littleton, Colorado with a functioning dishwasher and a husband who doesn't yell. I know that. But knowing it doesn't make the feeling go away. It just makes me feel guilty for having it.

Halloween aftermath. Candy wrappers everywhere. Olive had a meltdown over her princess costume ripping. Mason ate too much sugar and threw up. Lucy was fine because Lucy is always fine. I picked glitter out of the carpet for forty minutes.

Second ceramics class. Made a bowl that actually looks like a bowl. Greta said my centering is improving. Hands are sore. Good sore. The kind of tired that means you did something, not the kind that means the day happened to you.

Kyle and I had takeout Thai after the kids went down. He mentioned his office is doing a retreat to Tahoe in January. No partners. I said "sounds fun" and meant it, but also felt something heavy settle in my stomach.
entries.nudge_text: What was the heavy thing?
entries.response_text: Jealousy, I think. Not of Tahoe. Of having something on the calendar that's just for you. That nobody scheduled for you. That you get to anticipate without coordinating childcare first.

entries.initial_entry: Parent-teacher conference for Mason. Teacher said he's doing well in math, needs work on reading comprehension. Brought cupcakes for Lucy's class birthday. Picked up dry cleaning. Groceries. Normal stuff.

Olive got sick, so I missed ceramics. Sat in her room rubbing her back while she slept and scrolled through the class group chat watching everyone post photos of their glazed pieces from last week. Mine is still sitting on the drying shelf. Greta texted me separately and said she'd fire it for me.

I know missing one class isn't a big deal. But it felt like something being taken away. Like the universe reminding me that I don't actually get to have my own thing. Every time I carve out something that's mine, a fever or a school closure or a dentist appointment swallows it back up. I sat there watching Olive breathe and felt both tender and trapped at the same time.
entries.nudge_text: Can you hold both of those at once?

entries.initial_entry:
My college friend Dana visited this weekend. She lives in Portland, runs a food truck, just got back from a month in Vietnam. She showed up with good wine and stories about motorbike rides through rice paddies and a tattoo she got from a guy in Hanoi. The kids loved her because she's loud and funny and let them paint her toenails.

After they went to bed she asked me the question everyone eventually asks: "Do you miss working?" And I gave my usual answer which is that I don't miss the job, I miss the energy. The feeling of making something happen. The chaos of event day when everything could go wrong and you're the one holding it together with duct tape and charm.

She said I should start planning events again, freelance. Small stuff. Birthday parties, local festivals. I said maybe. She said "you always say maybe" and she's right. I do always say maybe. Maybe is how you say no without having to defend it.

But here's what I didn't tell Dana. I don't want to plan other people's good times anymore. I want to have my own. I want to be the person at the festival, not the person who booked the porta-potties. I want to be surprised by something. I want to walk into a room and not know what's about to happen. That's what I miss. Not the logistics. The feeling.

Kyle wouldn't understand that. He'd say I can go to a festival anytime. He'd buy me tickets and think he'd solved the problem. But it's not about the ticket. It's about the fact that somewhere along the way, I became the kind of person who needs permission to enjoy things, and I don't know how to undo that.
entries.nudge_text: Who are you asking permission from?
entries.response_text: Everyone. No one. Myself, probably. I don't know. It's just easier to say maybe and keep making lunches.

entries.initial_entry:
My sister asked me if I was "doing okay" on the phone last night with that voice she uses when she's actually asking if I'm depressed. I'm not depressed. I made banana bread with Olive yesterday and we painted rocks in the backyard and I reorganized the entire pantry because it was driving me insane. That's not depression, that's Tuesday.

She doesn't get it because she's single and works in marketing and goes to Barcelona on a whim. Good for her. I chose this. I'm not sitting here wishing I had her life. I just wish mine had a few more surprise elements built in. Like a plot twist every now and then. Something I didn't see coming at 6am when I set the cereal bowls out.
entries.nudge_text: What kind of plot twist are you hoping for?
entries.response_text: Honestly? I don't even know. That's the part that scares me. I can't name the thing I want. I just know the absence of it. Like there's a shape missing from my week and I keep filling it with pantry organization and sourdough starters.

entries.initial_entry:
The Hamburg project is on hold. The city council voted to delay the commission review until March, citing budget reallocation concerns. I found out through my gallery contact, not from the committee chair who promised to keep me informed directly. That's two months of planning, three revised proposals, and a scale model my team spent five weeks fabricating.

I called Martina, the head of the cultural affairs department, and she said the delay was procedural. No guarantees about the final decision. When I asked about the competing submissions, she went quiet. There are competing submissions now. Nobody mentioned that before.

My assistant Werner suggested we could use the time to refine the proposal further. I told him the proposal doesn't need refining. What it needs is for people to honor their word. The original brief came to me because of my work in Frankfurt and Munich. Now there are suddenly other artists in the conversation.

Spent the evening alone in the studio. Didn't work on anything. Just sat with a glass of Burgundy and looked at the model. It's good work. The proportions are right, the way it catches light from the north side. Should have been straightforward.
entries.nudge_text: Why alone tonight instead of calling someone?
entries.response_text: Who would I call? Werner just tells me what I want to hear. My ex-wife would say I brought this on myself with my ego. My collector friends would offer sympathy but secretly wonder if I'm losing my touch. The wine was better company.

entries.initial_entry:
Meeting today with the Becker family about their estate commission. Third generation textile money, building a new country house in Baden-Württemberg. They want something for the entrance courtyard. Five meters minimum, abstract but with classical proportions. Budget is essentially open.

Herr Becker junior did most of the talking. Young man, maybe thirty-five, clearly trying to establish himself as the decision-maker now that his father has stepped back. He kept referencing installations he'd seen in Basel and New York. Wanted to know my thoughts on incorporating water features. I told him bronze and water require careful engineering, which adds six months minimum to the timeline.

His wife seemed more interested in the aesthetic conversation. She asked good questions about patina development over decades. I showed them photos of my Frankfurt piece from 2008 and how it's aged. She understood immediately what I was explaining about surface evolution.

Left the meeting feeling solid about the project. Private commissions like this are cleaner. One family, one vision, one contract. No committees, no budget votes, no competing proposals appearing from nowhere. The fee will be substantial. I'm already thinking about how to spend the spring - perhaps extend the Mallorca stay into April this year.
entries.nudge_text: Which of them do you think will actually decide?
entries.response_text: The wife. Herr Becker junior wants to prove he's running things, but she's the one with the eye. He'll defer to her judgment on anything visual. I've seen this dynamic before with these families. The money gives orders, but taste steers the ship. I'll make sure my next presentation speaks to what she responded to.

entries.initial_entry: Christmas dinner at my sister's in Cologne. Too many relatives, too much noise. Drove back late and opened a 2015 Barolo I'd been saving.

Received the formal letter from Hamburg today. The commission has been opened to a juried competition. My original invitation as the presumptive artist is now simply one submission among what they expect will be twelve to fifteen proposals. They frame it as fairness and transparency. I frame it as cowardice.

I built relationships with that cultural department for eight years. Hosted their delegation when they visited my Munich installation. Introduced Martina to collectors who later funded her gallery renovation. This is how it works in this world - you invest in people, build mutual obligation, and when opportunities arise, those investments pay returns. Now some junior bureaucrat has decided that giving me what I earned looks like favoritism. I may withdraw entirely. Let them have their competition with their fifteen proposals from artists who haven't done a single public piece over ten meters.
entries.nudge_text: What would withdrawing actually prove?
entries.response_text: That I don't need to compete for something that should have been mine by right. That my reputation speaks without having to perform for a jury of people who probably couldn't name three working sculptors. Maybe nothing. Maybe I'm just angry and this is how it comes out. The Becker commission will pay better anyway.

entries.initial_entry:
Had dinner with Joachim, an old friend from my academy days. He never pursued public commissions - works entirely through private galleries and collectors. Smaller pieces, more experimental. He mentioned he spent November in Portugal, just making work in a rented studio with no deadlines, no clients, no obligations. Came back with a series he's genuinely excited about.

I found myself envying that. Not the smaller scale or the lesser income, but the freedom from always positioning. Every conversation I have lately is a negotiation. Who gets credit, who controls the timeline, who has final say. Even the Becker project - I'm already calculating how to manage the son's ego while cultivating the wife's trust. Joachim just makes things. When did I stop doing that?
entries.nudge_text: Could you actually work that way now?
entries.response_text: I don't know. The studio, the assistants, the Mallorca house - they all require the income that comes from the positioning. But it's not just about money. I'm not sure I'd know what to make if nobody was watching. Joachim's work comes from somewhere inside him. Mine comes from briefs and site specifications. Maybe that's fine. Maybe that's just who I became. The question bothers me more than I expected.

entries.initial_entry:
New Year's Eve at the Düsseldorf Kunstverein event. Good turnout, the usual faces. I wore my grey Zegna and the Patek I bought myself after the Munich commission. People came over to talk, asked about my projects, laughed at my stories about difficult clients. The museum director mentioned they're planning a retrospective series next year featuring regional artists. My name came up in their planning meeting.

Drove home after midnight feeling satisfied. This is what the work is for - not just the objects themselves but the standing they create. The ability to walk into a room and have people know who you are and what you've accomplished. Joachim wouldn't understand this. He'd find it exhausting or empty. But there's genuine satisfaction in being recognized. I've earned that standing through decades of difficult work. Tonight felt like confirmation.

Spent the day in the studio actually working. Not managing, not planning, not reviewing contracts. Put on old clothes and spent six hours with clay, building maquettes for the Becker piece. Three different approaches to the water feature question - one that incorporates it, one that acknowledges the site's water table through the form without literal water, one that ignores it entirely.

My hands remembered things my mind had forgotten. The resistance of material, the way a form wants to go one direction but you have to convince it to go another. Werner came in at some point to ask about scheduling and I didn't even look up, just told him we'd discuss it tomorrow. By evening my back hurt and my shoulders were stiff and I felt better than I have in weeks. Opened a good Riesling and sat looking at what I'd made.
entries.nudge_text: What made today different from usual?
entries.response_text: No audience. Nothing to prove to anyone. Just me and the clay and the problem of how to fill that courtyard space. The Becker meeting, the Hamburg situation, even the Kunstverein evening - there's always a performance involved. Today was just doing the thing itself. I'd forgotten how much I used to need that. Maybe still do.

entries.initial_entry:
Presentation to the Beckers today. Brought all three maquettes. Herr Becker junior immediately gravitated toward the most literal interpretation - the water feature, the dramatic scale, the impressive engineering. He started talking about how visitors would experience it, the Instagram moments, the way it would announce the family's arrival into a new era.

His wife was quiet for a long time. Then she pointed to the smallest maquette - the one where the form echoes the water table beneath without any actual water. She said it felt inevitable. Like it belonged to the land rather than being imposed on it. Her husband looked irritated for a moment, then asked what I thought.

I told them the truth: the water feature would be magnificent and expensive. The subtle piece would be better art. I could see Herr Becker calculating - the subtle piece was also a third of the budget. For a moment I wondered if I'd just talked myself out of substantial money.

In the end they went with the subtle piece. The wife's choice. I'm genuinely pleased, which surprises me. A year ago I would have steered them toward the bigger commission without hesitation. The income loss is real but manageable. And I'll actually enjoy making this one. That matters more than I thought it would.
entries.nudge_text: What changed in the last year?
entries.response_text: Maybe nothing changed. Maybe I just got old enough to notice what was already there. The Hamburg situation reminded me that all the positioning and relationship-building can evaporate overnight. The status game has diminishing returns after a certain point. I have enough. The studio, the houses, the reputation - it's all established. What I don't have enough of is days like that one in January, alone with clay and no audience. The Becker piece might give me more of those.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a meeting with the regional leads this morning and Marcus just talked right over me when I was laying out the Q2 headcount plan. I was mid-sentence — literally had the slide up — and he just started redirecting the conversation toward his own hiring timeline. Nobody stopped him. Janet, who was running the meeting, just went with it and moved on.

I sat there for the rest of the hour barely saying anything. I had the data. I had prepared for this since Thursday. And it just got swallowed up because Marcus is louder and has been in the room longer. The worst part is I could feel myself doing the thing where I just go quiet instead of pushing back, and I hated it. My chest was tight and I kept rehearsing what I should have said.

Afterwards, one of the other leads came up to me and said "good points earlier" which somehow made it worse. Like, you heard me but you didn't back me up when it counted? I don't know what to do with that.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from cutting back in?
entries.response_text: Honestly? The thought that if I pushed it and Marcus pushed back harder, I'd look like the one making a scene. There's this calculation I do instantly — will I come out of this looking stronger or weaker — and in that moment the math didn't feel right. Which I realize sounds insane because saying nothing definitely made me look weaker. I think I'm more afraid of losing ground publicly than I am of just being invisible.

entries.initial_entry:
Submitted the facilities request for the new storage bay expansion three weeks ago. Need sign-off from Dave in operations finance before anything moves. Followed up twice, got a "should have something for you by end of week" both times. Third week running.

Meanwhile my team is stacking pallets in the overflow lot and we're burning hours on workarounds that wouldn't exist if someone would just approve the spend. I called Dave's office again at four o'clock, left a voicemail. Sat at my desk staring at the phone for twenty minutes afterward like it was going to ring back.
entries.nudge_text: Is it the wait, or that you have to wait?
entries.response_text: The second one. The delay is annoying but it's the dependency that actually gets to me. I've run the numbers, I know the ROI, and none of that matters because it's Dave's signature on the line. My whole team's efficiency is bottlenecked by someone who probably hasn't even opened the attachment.

entries.initial_entry: Karen from procurement shot down my vendor swap proposal in front of the entire ops meeting. I had solid margins data and she just kept saying "that's not how we onboard vendors here." I folded. Just said fine, we'll keep the current contract, because I could tell nobody else was going to back me up and I wasn't going to beg.

Quiet Saturday. Took Logan to his baseball game, they won 6-3. He made a nice catch in the third inning and looked over at me in the dugout immediately. That part was good.

Spent some time after the game reorganizing the garage because the shelf brackets have been loose for weeks and it was bothering me. Put on a podcast about supply chain trends, half listened. Thought about whether I should bring the vendor swap thing back up on Monday or just let it die. Karen's probably already forgotten about it but I haven't.

Did some meal prep for the week. Texted my brother about mom's birthday dinner logistics — he still hasn't confirmed how many people he's bringing, which is exactly the kind of thing that drives me nuts. Just tell me a number so I can book the table.
entries.nudge_text: What would letting the vendor thing go feel like?
entries.response_text: It's not really about feelings, it's about the fact that we're paying twelve percent more than we need to. Anyway, I'll probably just bring it up again differently. Frame it as a cost reduction initiative instead of a vendor change. Same thing, different packaging.

entries.initial_entry:
Had my quarterly review with Janet. She flagged my team's throughput numbers — second highest in the region, up four percent from last quarter. She said it straight, no fluff, and I appreciated that. Then she asked if I'd be willing to present the process improvements at the May leadership summit.

I said yes immediately. Didn't even hesitate. There's a version of me from five years ago who would've been nervous about standing in front of that room, but right now I just want them to see what my team has built. We earned this. I drove home with the windows down and felt genuinely good for the first time in a while.
entries.nudge_text: What's different about you now versus five years ago?
entries.response_text: I mean, five years ago I was still an analyst hoping someone would notice my work. Now I have a team, I have numbers, I have a track record. It's not some deep transformation — I just have receipts now.

entries.initial_entry:
New guy on the team, Derek, has been here two months. In the standup today he started explaining the pallet tracking system to me — the one I designed three years ago. He was walking me through it like I was the new hire. I let him finish because I didn't want to embarrass him in front of everyone, but it sat with me all morning.

Later, one of my senior leads, Tanya, mentioned Derek had been asking her questions about "how things work around here" instead of coming to me. Not sure if he just doesn't know the hierarchy or if he's intentionally going around me. Either way, it doesn't feel great.
